By Christopher M. McDonald, Esq., and Laura B. Lawson, Esq.
U
nlike some professions, allegations of unethical con-
duct against engineers are infrequent. But, when al-
legations of unethical engineering conduct are made,
engineers must be prepared to protect themselves and their
engineering licenses.
This column describes two types of legal ramiﬁ cations that
may result from allegations of unethical engineering conduct:
disciplinary proceedings and civil litigation. (Criminal pros-
ecution also may be a legal ramiﬁ cation if the alleged unethical
conduct is serious enough, but criminal prosecution is beyond
the scope of this article.) This column also sets forth a plan
of action for engineers to follow should they be subject to a
disciplinary proceeding and/or civil litigation based on al-
legations of unethical engineering conduct.
Disciplinary Proceedings
Deﬁ nition of Unethical Engineering Conduct
g
g
Each state has an administrative licensing entity that is
responsible for issuing engineering licenses and disciplining
engineers who abuse the license by engaging in unethical
engineering conduct. In general, there are four main acts of
unethical engineering conduct that may lead to disciplinary
proceedings. These include gross negligence, incompetence,
misconduct in professional activities and unprofessional con-
duct. The courts generally deﬁ ne these terms as follows:
Gross negligence is extreme lack of care and disregard for
the rights of others so as to imply intentional behavior or im-
proper conduct that is greater in kind or in degree than ordinary
negligence.
Legal Ramifications
Of Unethical Engineering
Incompetency is the inability to discharge a required duty.
Misconduct is malfeasance or mismanagement.
Unprofessional conduct is conduct violating the standards
of professional behavior that are established through profes-
sional experience and codes, including conviction of a crime,
drug use, fraud, violation of a professional trust and false
advertising.
Sanctions for Unethical Engineering Conduct
g
g
If the state’s licensing entity ﬁ nds that an engineer engaged
in unethical engineering conduct, the entity may impose one or
more of the following punishments on the engineer: licensure
revocation, licensure sanction, license probation, censure,
reprimand and monetary ﬁ ne. Generally, with a reprimand or
censure, the state’s licensing entity issues a formal statement
that the engineer engaged in unethical engineering conduct.
This statement may occur in private (private censure) or
may be published in a public document (public reprimand).
Typically, the engineer’s license is not affected directly by a
censure or reprimand.
If the state’s licensing entity imposes a license probation,
the engineer may keep his/her license and continue to practice
engineering but only under strict observation by the state. If
an engineer’s license is suspended, the engineer loses his/her
license and the privilege to practice engineering for a set
time period.
Licensure probation and suspension can last for a few
months or up to several years. With licensure revoca-
tion, the engineer loses his/her license and the privilege
to practice engineering indefinitely. Often, even after a
licensure revocation, an engineer may apply to have his or
her license reinstated by the state’s licensing entity after
several years.
If the state’s licensing entity ﬁ nds that an engineer engaged in unethical
engineering conduct, the entity may impose one or more of the following
punishments on the engineer: licensure revocation, licensure sanction, license
probation, censure, reprimand and monetary ﬁ ne.
The information contained in this article represents the opinion of the
authors. It is not intended to, and does not, constitute legal advice, nor
does it represent the opinion of ASHRAE or any of its bodies.
© 2005, American Society of Heating, Refrigerating and Air-Conditioning Engineers, Inc. (www.ashrae.org). Reprinted by permission from
© 2005, American Society of Heating, Refrigerating and Air-Conditioning Engineers, Inc. (www.ashrae.org). Reprinted by permission from
ASHRAE Journal, (Vol. 47, No. 9, September 2005). This article may not be copied nor distributed in either paper or digital form without
ASHRAE’s permission.
42
ASHRAE Journal ashrae.org
September 2005
Disciplinary Proceeding Process
Disciplinary Proceeding Process
p
y
g
p
y
g
p
y
g
It may take several months to complete an entire disciplinary
proceeding—from an initial allegation of unethical conduct
to a ﬁ nal determination. Typically, any individual may assert
allegations about an engineer’s unethical conduct to the state’s
licensing entity. This entity will investigate the allegations
and, if they have merit, will ﬁ le a formal disciplinary com-
plaint against the engineer. Due process requires the formal
complaint specify the basis for allegations with a reasonable
degree of particularity so the engineer can prepare a defense.
If the engineer contests the formal complaint, then a process
called discovery commences. During discovery, the parties
attempt to determine the factual basis, if any, for the allega-
tions. Discovery includes interrogatories (written questions
and answers), depositions (oral questions and answers under
oath in front of a court reporter) and requests for documents
from the engineer.
After the discovery process is complete, the engineer may
settle the case by accepting an agreed upon sanction. If there
is no settlement, then an administrative hearing is conducted,
which is similar to a mini-trial, with direct examination
and cross examination of witnesses. After the hearing, an
impartial decision-maker, which is usually a panel of other
engineers, determines the facts, applies the law, and enters
a ruling.
If the panel determines that the engineer engaged in un-
ethical engineering conduct, then the panel administers a
sanction. The engineer may appeal the ruling or sanction to
the court. The court will evaluate whether the licensing entity
(1) violated the constitution, (2) acted outside of its jurisdic-
tion, or (3) entered a ruling not supported by evidence. If it
did, then the court will reverse the ruling or sanction entered
by the panel.
Plan of Action for Disciplinary Proceedings
If allegations of unethical engineering conduct are made
against an engineer, the engineer should immediately take two
actions (1) notify his or her professional liability insurance
company; and (2) hire a lawyer with experience in handling
administrative law matters.
Most insurance companies require immediate notiﬁ cation of a
potential claim against an engineer. However, even with timely
notice of a potential disciplinary proceeding, some professional
liability insurance policies will not pay for an attorney to defend
the engineer in an administrative disciplinary proceeding. To
ensure coverage for this type of defense, an engineer should
carefully review the language of his or her current professional
liability insurance policy. Even if the insurance policy does not
cover disciplinary proceedings, the engineer should still notify
the insurance company of the proceeding because the company
p
y
y p
g
g
will probably want to observe any proceeding that might affect
will probably want to observe any proceeding that might affect
future civil litigation (see section on interplay).
If the engineer’s current professional liability insurance policy
does not cover the cost of a lawyer to represent the engineer
in a disciplinary proceeding, the engineer should consider
purchasing a new policy, a rider for the current policy, or an
umbrella insurance policy that provides this coverage. Without
it, the engineer will need to pay for an attorney out of pocket
to defend the engineer in the disciplinary proceeding. This
can be costly, but it is never wise to proceed in a disciplinary
proceeding without legal representation.
Legal defenses a lawyer may assert in a disciplinary proceed-
ing include statute of limitations, denial of due process, and
other constitutional issues, but legal defenses are not usually
successful in disciplinary proceedings. Factual ﬁ ndings gen-
erally are the key to defending disciplinary proceedings. As
previously stated, a panel of engineers usually makes the ﬁ nal
determination in a disciplinary proceeding as to whether an
engineer engaged in unethical conduct. These fellow engineers
often will give their professional peer the beneﬁ t of the doubt.
Therefore, if the panel ﬁ nds unethical engineering conduct
and recommends disciplinary action, there is probably good
evidence to support it.
Unless no evidence of unethical conduct exists, it may be
wise to consider settling a disciplinary proceeding. The panel
may be more lenient and impose a lesser sanction if the engi-
neer accepts responsibility and explains what led to the alleged
unethical engineering conduct. However, before entering into
any settlement agreement, the engineer must evaluate how a
potential settlement will affect his or her license and how a
settlement may affect subsequent civil litigation based on the
same unethical conduct (see section on interplay).
Civil Litigation
A second legal ramiﬁ cation that may result from allegations
of unethical engineering conduct is civil liability. Engaging in
unethical engineering conduct does not automatically give rise
to civil liability, but an engineer should be prepared for this pos-
sibility. A lawsuit against an engineer for unethical engineering
conduct may include claims for negligence, intentional tort and
breach of contract. All three claims may be based on the same
unethical conduct, but a plaintiff can only recover once for the
alleged damages incurred.
Negligence
g g
As a professional, an engineer owes a duty to the client to act
within the “standard of care,” which means the engineer must
exercise the “same degree of skill, learning and care ordinar-
ily exercised and practiced by other engineers under the same
or similar circumstances.” If the engineer fails to comply with
this duty and such failure causes damage to a third party, then
the engineer is liable for negligence. If an engineer engages
in unethical engineering conduct, this often indicates that the
The information contained in this article represents the opinion of the
authors. It is not intended to, and does not, constitute legal advice, nor
does it represent the opinion of ASHRAE or any of its bodies.
September 2005
ASHRAE Journal
43
g
p y
,
y
engineer did not comply with the standard of care, thereby
engineer did not comply with the standard of care thereby
committing negligence.
Breach of Contract
If an engineer enters into a contract with a client and fails
to comply with one of the contract terms, an engineer often is
liable for breach of contract. All contracts include an implied
term that requires an engineer to act with “good faith and fair
dealings.” If an engineer engages in unethical conduct, this
may constitute noncompliance with the “fair dealings” con-
tract term and lead to liability for breach of contract.
Intentional Torts
An intentional tort is conduct that is typically more egre-
gious than negligence. In negligence cases, the engineer usu-
ally fails to comply with the standard of care unintentionally.
In an intentional tort case, the engineer usually acts intention-
ally in a manner to harm others. If an engineer intentionally
engages in unethical conduct, such as fraud, this often will
give rise to liability for an intentional tort.
If an engineer is sued for an intentional tort based on un-
ethical conduct, the engineer’s professional liability insurance
policy will usually not pay to defend the engineer in the lawsuit
or pay for any damages awarded against the engineer. This
is because professional liability insurance policies typically
only cover liability arising from “professional services,” which
intentional torts do not qualify as. If the engineer is sued for
an intentional tort rising out of unethical conduct, the engineer
often will have to pay out of pocket for an attorney and any
damages awarded against the engineer.
Punitive Damages
g
Punitive damages are awarded above and beyond those
damages necessary to compensate the injured party. Puni-
tive damages are intended to punish the engineer for willful,
wanton, improper and unethical conduct. Punitive damages
usually are not awarded in negligence actions, but they may be
if the negligence was willful or wanton (i.e., unethical). Puni-
tive damages usually are not awarded in a breach of contract
action, even if the breach resulted from unethical conduct.
But, punitive damages often are awarded in intentional tort
cases that arise out of unethical conduct.
If a jury awards punitive damages against an engineer, the
engineer must usually pay for these damages out of his or her
own pocket. The public policy for most states prohibits an
insurance company from covering or paying for an award of
punitive damages. In this way, the economic burden of punitive
damages, awarded for intentionally egregious conduct, falls
directly on the engineer who acted wrongly, in an attempt to
deter future wrongful conduct.
y
p
g
One way to avoid punitive damages is to settle a case before it
One way to avoid punitive damages is to settle a case before it
goes to trial. With such settlement, the insurance policy applies
and the insurance company should pay the settlement amount.
If an insurance company refuses to consider settlement, thereby
exposing the engineer to a potential punitive damage award for
which the engineer will be personally liable, the engineer may
consider suing the insurance company for bad faith.
If a disagreement over settling a case arises between the
engineer and the lawyer hired by the insurance company to
defend the engineer, it is a good idea for the engineer to hire
his or her own personal lawyer to encourage the insurance
company to evaluate settlement. If circumstances dictate, the
engineer’s personal lawyer can negotiate with and if necessary
ﬁ le an action against the insurance company for bad faith if
it does not settle the case and protect the engineer from a po-
tential punitive damages award, for which the engineer will be
personally responsible. However, the engineer should be aware
of the effect a settlement may have on his or her license and be
aware of the interplay a settlement of civil litigation may have
on a disciplinary proceeding (see section on interplay).
Plan of Action for Civil Litigation
g
If an engineer is sued for negligence, intentional
tort, or breach of contract based on unethical engineering
The information contained in this article represents the opinion of the
authors. It is not intended to, and does not, constitute legal advice, nor
does it represent the opinion of ASHRAE or any of its bodies.
Advertisement formerly in this space.
44
ASHRAE Journal ashrae.org
September 2005
conduct, the engineer should consider the following plan
conduct the engineer should consider the following plan
of action:
• Contact your professional liability insurance company, who
will retain an attorney to defend you.
• If a question exists about insurance coverage for
an intentional tort or punitive damages, retain a personal
attorney.
• At your attorney’s direction, bone up on the applicable
ethics code for your deposition. Your attorney should
prepare thoroughly for your deposition, but the more you
know about the claims against you and the ethical rules,
the better off you are. It is easy to be lulled into thinking
that your attorney will take care of this whole “mess,” but
it is your license at stake, and you should take defending
your case seriously.
• Look for an expert on engineering ethics. Your attorney
will retain the expert and prepare him or her for deposi-
tion, but you are the one with engineering knowledge. If
you can help your attorney locate an expert in engineering
ethics, you should.
Civil litigation based on unethical engineering conduct
raises issues that are absent in cases without an ethical com-
ponent. If allegations of unethical engineering conduct are
made against an engineer, the engineer must be aware of these
concerns and take care to protect against them.
Interplay Between and Potential Consequences
of Disciplinary Proceedings and Civil Litigation
Interplay Between Disciplinary Proceedings and Litigation
p
y
p
y
g
g
The same unethical engineering conduct often will result in
both a disciplinary proceeding and civil litigation. Typically,
the discovery process is shorter in the disciplinary proceeding,
so the proceeding often is resolved before the civil litigation
is resolved. The following analysis is based on this assump-
tion, but even if the reverse is true, and the civil litigation is
resolved before the disciplinary proceeding, the same analysis
still applies.
While a disciplinary proceeding and civil litigation may
be based on the same alleged unethical engineering conduct,
the two actions require different elements and have different
standards of proof. Therefore, there is generally no binding
effect between a disciplinary proceeding and civil litigation
based on the same unethical conduct. If a licensing entity ﬁ nds
that an engineer engaged in unethical conduct, this does not
automatically mean the engineer also will be found liable for
negligence or vice versa.
But, some states do have statutes that speciﬁ cally allow a
prior ruling against a professional in a disciplinary proceed-
ing to be binding in any subsequent civil litigation and vice
versa. Furthermore, even in those states where the outcome
The information contained in this article represents the opinion of the
authors. It is not intended to, and does not, constitute legal advice, nor
does it represent the opinion of ASHRAE or any of its bodies.
of the disciplinary proceeding is not binding in the civil
of the disciplinary proceeding is not binding in the civil
litigation, the fact that a disciplinary proceeding took place,
including any statement made therein, and the outcome of
such proceeding may be admissible as evidence in subsequent
civil litigation. Some states, however, have statutes that keep
disciplinary proceedings conﬁ dential and prohibit admission
of any evidence about a prior disciplinary proceeding in any
subsequent civil litigation.
If evidence of a disciplinary proceeding is admissible in
civil litigation, a jury is more likely to ﬁ nd the engineer li-
able and award excessive damages, even if the jury is not
bound by the decision of the licensing entity. Because of this
signiﬁ cant risk, an engineer should consider settling a disci-
plinary proceeding, if there is a chance that civil litigation
may follow, so that evidence of the disciplinary proceeding
cannot be introduced into trial. For the settlement to have its
intended affect, the settlement agreement must make clear
that any statement made or fact stipulated to during the settle-
ment negotiations, and the fact that a disciplinary proceeding
was even ﬁ led or settled, are not admissible or binding in any
subsequent civil or criminal litigation. The settlement agree-
ment must indicate that the agreement was entered into solely
for the purpose of settling a dispute and not as an admission
of liability.
Potential Consequences
q
The engineer should consider the potential consequences of
settling a disciplinary proceeding or civil litigation for reasons
set forth previously. These consequences are often equal to
those that follow a ﬁ nding of unethical conduct by a licensing
entity or a judgment against the engineer in civil litigation.
For instance, if an engineer attempts to obtain a license in a
second state, the second state often will ask not only whether
an engineer has been sanctioned by a licensing entity or had
a judgment entered against him or her, but may also inquire
as to whether the engineer has ever settled a lawsuit or disci-
plinary proceeding in another state. An afﬁ rmative response
could prevent licensure in the new state.
In addition, any state that offers reciprocal licensing may
revoke such license if the engineer has a ﬁ nding of unethi-
cal conduct or judgment entered against him or her, or if the
engineer settles a lawsuit or disciplinary proceeding. Because
of these potentially serious consequences of a judgment, a
disciplinary sanction, or settling any disciplinary proceeding
or civil litigation based on unethical engineering conduct, all
such allegations should be taken seriously and handled aggres-
sively with an attorney familiar with the state law at issue.
Christopher M. McDonald, Esq., is a partner at Shook, Hardy
& Bacon L.L.P., in Kansas City, Mo., and a member of ASHRAE
Technical Committee 1.7, Business, Management and General
Legal Education. Laura B. Lawson, Esq., is of counsel at Shook,
Hardy & Bacon L.L.P., in Kansas City, Mo.
S 1 0 B u i l d i n g f o r t h e Fu t u r e | A S u p p l e m e n t t o A S H R A E J o u r n a l
S e p t e m b e r 2 0 0 5
A
ddressing today’s energy and environmental concerns is
equally the responsibility of building owners, designers, and
contractors. It is through this team effort and a common vision for
conscious and efﬁ cient living that sustainable building practices
can be successful.
The U.S. Green Building Council (USGBC) has developed a concise
system in Leadership in Energy and Environmental Design (LEED®)
that allows the industry to be guided in the best practices in sustain-
able design and operation. Relying heavily on industry standards, such
as those developed by ASHRAE and other professional organizations,
designers and owners can be guided in best building practices.
By Ronald K. Herrmann, P.E., Member ASHRAE
About the Author
Ronald K. Herrmann, P.E., is director of systems
development at T.E.C. Systems in Long Island
City, N.Y.
Building Automation
And LEED Credits
®
Application of BAS to LEED Credits
The LEED rating system sets a series
of goals that must be met to obtain cer-
tiﬁ cation points. (The levels of certiﬁ ca-
tion are: Certiﬁ ed: 26 – 32 points; Silver:
33 – 38 points; Gold 39 – 51 points; Plati-
num 52 – 69 points.) However, the rating
system does not always set the methodol-
ogy by which those goals should be met.
Implementation of a building automation
system (BAS) is not required by LEED
but can be an effective tool in obtaining
LEED points and allowing the owner/op-
erator to better maintain the facility.
BAS technologies can be applied to
approximately 40% of the criteria of the
USGBC’s LEED-NC, proposed Version
2.2 Green Building Rating System. This
© 2005, American Society of Heating, Refrigerating and Air-Conditioning Engineers, Inc. (www.ashrae.org). Reprinted by permission from
ASHRAE Journal, (Vol. 47, No. 9, September 2005). This article may not be copied nor distributed in either paper or digital form without
ASHRAE’s permission.
S e p t e m b e r 2 0 0 5 B u i l d i n g f o r t h e Fu t u r e | A S u p p l e m e n t t o A S H R A E J o u r n a l
S 1 1
article describes the application and appropriateness of using
BAS technologies to achieve sustainable building goals.
Sustainable Sites
Credit 4.3—Alternative Transportation, Low Emitting and
Fuel Efﬁ cient Vehicles
A BAS can monitor or control functions associated with the
alternative fuel refueling stations located at the site. Alternative
fuel options include ethanol, natural gas, propane, hydrogen,
biodiesel, electricity, methanol, and p-series fuels.1 Electric
vehicles (EV) and compressed natural gas (CNG) are the most
available choices. The BAS can monitor, track and control
fueling equipment in the following manner:
Monitoring Applications:
• Monitor when fueling stations are in service and trend
usage patterns.
• For sites with on-site storage (e.g., propane tank), the BAS
can notify suppliers when deliveries are required.
• Monitor and alert security during unauthorized use.
• Submeter electric kW consumption for measurement and
veriﬁ cation or sub-billing purposes.
• Leak and/or fault detection.
Control Applications:
• Enable/disable systems so they are only active during
certain times.
• For electric systems, incorporate into load-shedding and
energy management routines for peak-load reduction
strategies.
• Electric or natural gas systems can be shed by local utility
companies as a part of a utility service area load curtail-
ment program.
• Fuel station activation on a user-by-user basis through card
access, keypad, or other personal identiﬁ cation method.
• Emergency shutdown for security or other reasons.
Water Efﬁ ciency
Credit 1.1—Water Efﬁ cient Landscaping, Reduce by 50%
The BAS can monitor water use and/or control water consum-
ing systems. Water efﬁ ciency for landscaping can be accom-
plished in several ways. One method is to use high-efﬁ ciency
irrigation strategies. These strategies include micro-irrigation
systems, moisture sensors, clock timers, and weather database
controllers.2 The BAS can monitor and control the irrigation
system as follows:
Monitoring Applications:
• Rain sensor can monitor the presence of “natural” irriga-
tion.
• Moisture sensors can monitor the moisture level and de-
termine the necessity for irrigation.
• Water ﬂ ow meters can monitor and trend actual irrigation
water use to understand and verify use patterns.
• For rainwater reclamation systems and water recycling sys-
tems, storage and holding tank levels can be monitored.
• Weather data can be obtained via a BAS Internet connection
for environmental condition monitoring and trending.
• Soil moisture levels, water use and outdoor environmental
conditions can be trended and analyzed to verify if the
original landscaping design requirements are being met.
Control Applications:
• The BAS can control a large number of irrigation zones
facilitating individualized control scenarios to be applied
to areas based on vegetation requirements, location and
sun load, area soil composition, area moisture level, and
use schedule.
• In irrigation applications, zone control often is considered
as ON-OFF control. The BAS can apply variable ﬂ ow tech-
niques to zones based on application and requirements.
• Operating schedules, weather data, current soil moisture
content, water source supply levels and zone vegetation
requirements can be dynamically analyzed by the BAS.
Consequently, a control algorithm can maximize irrigation
efﬁ ciency.
Credit 2—Innovative Wastewater Technologies
The reduction and/or reuse of potable water have a positive im-
pact on potable water conservation and reduces wastewater load
on municipal treatment facilities. The BAS can monitor potable
water use and wastewater treatment systems to ensure their proper
operation, and that conservation goals are being met.
Monitoring Applications:
• Monitor, trend and alarm potable water use.
• Monitor, trend and alarm the operation of wastewater treat-
ment and recovery systems.
Addressing today’s energy and environmental concerns is equally the responsibility
of building owners, designers, and contractors. It is through this team effort and a
common vision for conscious and efﬁ cient living that sustainable building practices
can be successful.
S 1 2 B u i l d i n g f o r t h e Fu t u r e | A S u p p l e m e n t t o A S H R A E J o u r n a l
S e p t e m b e r 2 0 0 5
Control Applications:
• Automatically notify service personnel as efﬁ ciency
of wastewater treatment system declines and service is
required, or non-treatable waste pickup is required.
Credits 3.1 and 3.2 Water Use Reduction, 20% Reduction &
30% Reduction
Using water efﬁ cient ﬁ xtures and appliances can have a major
impact on the reduction of water use. Water system use can be
monitored and veriﬁ ed by the BAS.
Monitoring Applications:
• Monitor, trend and alarm water use.
Energy and Atmosphere
Prerequisite 1—Fundamental Building Systems Commis-
sioning
Building commissioning provides documented conﬁ rmation
that building systems function in compliance with criteria set
forth in the project documents to satisfy the owner’s operational
needs.3 One of the steps in the process includes veriﬁ cation and
functional performance testing of the systems within a building.
The BAS is one of the systems that must be veriﬁ ed for proper
operation to ensure its compliance with the design intent.
Sustainable building practices bring to the forefront the
importance of proper commissioning of the BAS. Not only do
building control systems have to operate properly to allow for
the efﬁ cient operation of the connected electrical and mechani-
cal systems but must be veriﬁ ed and accepted as an accurate
testing system. Since the BAS is inherently a monitoring and
control system of other systems within the facility (lighting,
HVAC, power, etc.), it is an extremely valuable tool in assisting
the commissioning agent in verifying the operation of other
building systems. The BAS has certain capabilities that make
it a valuable tool. The BAS has:
• Sensing capability that allows it to gather real-time data.
• Control capability that allows it to inﬂ uence building op-
erations and index equipment into modes of operation.
• Logging and trending capability to record system op-
eration over time and allow for visualization of operating
proﬁ les.
• Alarming allows for automatic annunciation of out-of-
parameter values.
• Reporting of dynamic and archived information.
• Intelligence to run routines that can simulate operating
scenarios.
Through these capabilities the BAS can assist in the com-
missioning process by:
• Generating reports of monitored and trended data on the
environmental conditions maintained by the HVAC system,
ﬂ ow and use of energy, status of electric equipment, and
any other points monitored by the BAS.
• Indexing controlled equipment into various modes of
operation or adjusting setpoints to simulate changes in
demand.
• The BAS generally has the ability to control the environ-
ment via the HVAC system, it can be used to test and mea-
sure building envelope efﬁ ciency and energy consumption.
By controlling and creating a given indoor environment
along with monitoring the outdoor conditions (temperature,
humidity, sun load, wind, etc.) and energy (Btu) use of the
mechanical equipment, a facility envelope efﬁ ciency test
and facility energy use test can be accomplished.
• The BAS has a dynamic ongoing effect on the operations of
the building systems, it can be used to act as a continuous
test and recommissioning system.
The BAS not only acts as a commissioning tool for the BAS
connected systems but also acts as a facility simulation and data
acquisition tool for other building components.
Prerequisite 2—Minimum Energy Performance
ANSI/ASHRAE/IESNA Standard 90.1-2004, Energy Stan-
dard for Buildings Except Low-Rise Residential Buildings,
establishes minimum requirements for the energy-efﬁ cient
design of buildings. The standard focuses on improving perfor-
mance in two areas of commercial buildings: building envelope
and building systems, and equipment. Building equipment is
comprised of HVAC, water heating, power distribution, electric
motors and drives and lighting. BAS strategies can be applied
in these areas.
1. Section 6.4.3 of 90.1, addressing Controls, deﬁ nes mini-
mum required control schemes.
2. Section 7.4.4, Service Water Heating System Controls,
deﬁ nes mandatory control provisions.
3. Section 9.4.1, deﬁ ning Lighting Control, describes the
minimum requirements for lighting controls.
In addition to the required control functions, the BAS can
perform monitoring functions and advanced energy conserva-
tion control strategies (see “Optimize Energy Performance”).
Monitoring Applications:
• Electric energy monitoring and trending for HVAC, wa-
ter heating, power distribution and lighting applications
(also addressed in Credit 5).
Credit 1—Optimize Energy Performance
Three fundamental strategies to increase energy perfor-
mance are: reduce demand, harvest site energy, and maximize
efﬁ ciency.4 BAS strategies can impact demand reduction and
efﬁ ciency maximization efforts. LEED points can be obtained
for reducing the energy use compared to the baseline building
performance rating per Standard 90.1-2004.
% Energy Cost Savings
Points
(Minimum)
10.5%
1
14%
2
17.5%
3
21%
4
24.5%
5
28%
6
31.5%

8
38.5%
9
42%
10
S e p t e m b e r 2 0 0 5 B u i l d i n g f o r t h e Fu t u r e | A S u p p l e m e n t t o A S H R A E J o u r n a l
S 1 3
BAS strategies can contribute to the overall energy efﬁ -
ciency.
Monitoring Applications:
• Perform real-time energy monitoring for electric, natural
gas, fuel oil and other energy sources.
Control Applications:
• Decrease environmental setpoint tolerances to allow greater
heating and cooling temperature swings, thus, reducing
HVAC load.
• Integrate occupancy sensors or card access systems to shut
down lighting and HVAC for unoccupied zones.
• Implement the following energy-efﬁ cient control strate-
gies:
· Optimized start/stop of HVAC equipment with morning
warm-up/cool-down control.
· Time clock, photocell, and occupancy sensor control of
lighting.
· Time clock and occupancy sensing control of HVAC
terminal units.
· Air and waterside economizer cycle control for HVAC
systems to take advantage of free cooling when outside
air conditions permit.
· Cascaded control loops to reset primary cooling and
heating equipment based on zone conditions. This
eliminates the over-production of primary water or air
services to terminal equipment.
· Multistage or variable capacity control to reduce equip-
ment energy use during part-load conditions.
· Demand-controlled ventilation.
· Central plant water and air reset based on kW optimiza-
tion.
· Load shedding and electrical kW demand limiting.
• Control plug and other loads that generally are not con-
trolled.
Credits 2.1, 2 and 3—Renewable Energy, 5%, 10%, 20%
Solar, wind and biomass power generation are examples of
on-site renewable energy sources. Using renewable energy
sources avoids the negative environmental impact associ-
ated with conventional power generation and does not require
expensive energy transportation infrastructure. The BAS can
meter and perform load control of the on-site renewable energy
sources to assist the owner in evaluation of the efﬁ ciency of the
on-site energy producing systems.
Credit 5—Measurement and Veriﬁ cation
Measurement and veriﬁ cation systems facilitate continu-
ous performance monitoring of building systems. Generally
a BAS, if present in a facility, already has some monitoring
and control functions associated with the systems required to
be monitored as part of this credit. The BAS functions can be
expanded to include the necessary metering data points. Since
measurement and veriﬁ cation activities are intimately related
to commissioning activities, they should be coordinated.5 The
BAS should monitor the following systems:6
• Lighting systems;
• Building electric utility meters;
• Building natural gas utility meters;
• Water meters for domestic hot and cold water;
• Process water meters;
• Chilled water system efﬁ ciency at various loads;
• Cooling load;
• Air and water economizer and heat recovery cycle opera-
tion;
• Boiler efﬁ ciencies;
• Building speciﬁ c process equipment;
• Constant and variable motor loads;
• Variable frequency drives; and
• Air-distribution system pressures and ventilation rates.
Monitoring Applications for Above Systems:
• Monitor and trend building electric and gas consump-
tion.
• Monitor and trend individual equipment energy consump-
tion.
• Monitor and trend equipment status and performance.
Control Applications:
• Calculate and report equipment operating efﬁ ciencies.
Indoor Environmental Quality
Prerequisite 1—Minimum IAQ Performance
Optimal IAQ performance in buildings results in improved
occupant health and productivity.7 To maintain high indoor air
quality, adequate outdoor ventilation is required in accordance
with the minimum requirement of voluntary consensus standard
ANSI/ASHRAE Standard 62.1-2004, Ventilation for Acceptable
Indoor Air Quality. Bringing in sufﬁ cient outside air to maintain
superior indoor conditions while minimizing the energy required
to condition and ventilate the building is a balance that must be
met by the designer and operator. This becomes increasingly
difﬁ cult with the widespread use of energy-efﬁ cient variable air
volume systems. A BAS can ensure that adequate ventilation is
being supplied while energy use is being minimized.
Monitoring Applications:
• Monitor and trend outside airﬂ ow rates.
Control Applications:
• Modulate outside air dampers and ventilation fans to pro-
vide adequate ventilation.
• Coordinate ventilation as required with other operating
criteria such as schedules, actual occupancy, and air quality
measurements.
Credit 1—Outdoor air Delivery Monitoring
Measuring and monitoring indoor CO2 levels provides a good
indication of the quality of the indoor air. High CO2 levels are
generally are an indication of poor IAQ.9 The BAS can provide
continual monitoring, reporting and alarming of CO2 levels
and automatically adjust ventilation rates to maintain CO2 at
optimum levels. The BAS can be used as follows:
Monitoring Applications:
• Monitor and trend CO2 levels in regularly occupied
zones.
• Monitor and trend outside air CO2 to establish reference
values.
S 1 4 B u i l d i n g f o r t h e Fu t u r e | A S u p p l e m e n t t o A S H R A E J o u r n a l
S e p t e m b e r 2 0 0 5
Control Applications:
• Alarm and report on CO2 levels.
• Perform demand controlled ventila-
tion.
Credit 2—Increased Ventilation
Providing increased ventilation im-
proves occupant comfort, well-being and
productivity. For mechanically ventilated
spaces, this requires an increase of at least
30% above the minimum rates required
by Standard 62.1-2004. The use of airﬂ ow
measuring instrumentation can control
outdoor intake systems to ensure the
proper rates are maintained.
Credits 6.1 and 2—Controllability of
Systems, Lighting, and Temperature and
Ventilation
Most workers spend their time indoors
with no ability to adjust their environ-
ment. A lack of individualized control
over lights, ventilation, windows, shad-
ing and temperature control may result
in less than optimal working conditions.
Through individualized control, occu-
pants can maximize their own comfort.
In addition to providing individualized
control for building occupants, the BAS
also can coordinate control sequences to
ensure compliance with primary building
strategies and goals.
Monitoring Applications (on an in-
dividual zone basis):
• Environmental conditions such as
temperature, ventilation rate, and
light level.
• Physical conditions such as oc-
cupancy and window position.
• User desired settings such as
schedule, temperature and light
setpoints, via thermostats, switch-
es or Web interface.
Control Applications:
• Adjust light levels to user’s desired
setting based on schedule, ambient
light level, and actual occupancy.
• Maintain temperature and ventila-
tion levels to user’s desired setting
based on schedule, actual tem-
perature reading, building global
strategies, and actual occupancy.
• For zones with operable windows,
interlock with zone HVAC.
• Adjust shade control to user’s
desired setting based on sched-
ule, ambient light level, building
global strategies, and actual oc-
cupancy.
Credits 7.1 and 2—Thermal Comfort,
Compliance, and Monitoring
The comfort level of building occupants
is highly dependent on the air velocity,
temperature and humidity levels within
a building. Radiation exchange, clothing,
and activity level are other elements that
contribute to human thermal comfort.
However, they are not as controllable on a
building level as are temperature, humidity
and ventilation rate. Through BAS sensing
and control, the temperature, humidity
and ventilation rate can be maintained at
optimal levels for human comfort.
Monitoring Applications:
• Monitor and trend temperature, hu-
midity and ventilation rate.
• For areas of mixed occupancy, use
CO2 monitoring and occupancy
sensing to adjust temperature and
humidity parameters based on oc-
cupancy and activity levels.
Control Applications:
• Alarm temperature, humidity, and
ventilation out of range values.
• Control HVAC system—cooling,
heating, ventilating, humidiﬁ cation
and dehumidiﬁ cation to maintain
indoor environmental conditions.
Innovation and Design Process
Credit 1.x—Innovation in Design
LEED recognizes the development of
new technologies and applications can
contribute greatly to the goals of an energy-
efﬁ cient and environment-friendly build-
ing. Due to the ongoing technological
advances in the building automation
industry, a BAS is ideally suited to be ap-
plied in innovative ways. In addition to the
common monitoring and control applica-
tions of BAS in today’s modern buildings,
BAS functions can be expanded to include
other sustainable applications.
Enhanced BAS Functions
In addition to the common applications
of BAS’s, the adoption of emerging tech-
Advertisement formerly in this space.
S e p t e m b e r 2 0 0 5 B u i l d i n g f o r t h e Fu t u r e | A S u p p l e m e n t t o A S H R A E J o u r n a l
S 1 5
nologies by building management system
manufacturers is allowing designers and
facility owners to obtain added value
from their building systems.
• Tied to other systems on an organiza-
tion’s information system enterprise,
the BAS can perform calculations
that allow the owner to optimize
performance. Data transfer methods
such as XML and OPC facilitate
data exchange among various sys-
tems on an owner’s enterprise. For
example, an owner may elect to
calculate a building’s coefﬁ cient of
performance, using energy use data
as a function of operational data,
such as employee and/or production
information.
• The advances in wireless technology
are emerging in the building automa-
tion industry. Radio-frequency iden-
tiﬁ cation tagging (RFID) is one of
the fastest growing areas of wireless
technology. As RFID technology is
used by building occupants, perhaps
linked to their employee identiﬁ cation
badge, the BAS will have the ability
to automatically adjust building sys-
tems such as lighting and HVAC to
optimum settings for an individual as
they enter their work zone(s).
• Through a connection to the Internet,
a BAS can monitor and control many
more elements than those within the
conﬁ nes of a given building. A BAS
is generally a reactive system. As
monitored conditions change, the
BAS responds to evoke corrective
action to maintain optimum operat-
ing conditions. A BAS can become
a predictive system by gathering
additional data over the Internet
and using the data in its control
algorithms.
• Open protocols have allowed for
streamlined transfer of data between
the BAS and many mechanical and
electrical systems within a building.
This connectivity allows the BAS
to access thousands of data points
in systems that require tuning and
maintenance to provide optimal
performance. As a portal to these
systems, the BAS allows service and
maintenance personnel to remotely
operate and diagnose systems, there-
fore, optimizing performance and po-
tentially avoiding site service visits.
• Recommissioning is an important
aspect of continued sustainable
building operation. Properly con-
figured alarm and annunciation
routines can allow the BAS, in its
simplest form, to be conﬁ gured as
a continuous commissioning tool,
reading and alarming sensors that are
out of tolerance. Advanced strate-
gies can monitor energy input and
system output to calculate operating
efﬁ ciency.
Conclusion
The BAS can play many roles in
the effective and efﬁ cient operation of
a building. Any dynamic aspect of a
building’s operation, which ﬂ uctuates or
is inﬂ uenced by external forces and must
respond or adjust accordingly, is a prime
candidate for BAS technologies.
The coordinated efforts of owner,
designer, contractor and commissioning
agent, as promoted by the philosophies of
sustainable design, allows the value of a
BAS to be realized in many areas of sus-
tainable design and operation. Creative
applications and new technologies will
continue to make the BAS a valuable tool
in achieving sustainable building goals.

By David W. Bridger and Diana M. Allen, Ph.D.
About the Authors
David W. Bridger is an M.Sc. candidate in hydro-
r r
geology and Diana M. Allen, Ph.D., is associate
professor and department chair at the Depart-
ment of Earth Sciences, Simon Fraser University
in Burnaby, BC, Canada.
G
eothermal systems use natural temperatures for heating and
cooling to achieve energy savings. Low temperature geother-
mal systems, whether used for heating or cooling, or a combination,
are known by a variety of names, including ground source heat
pump systems, geoexchange systems, ground-coupled systems,
or earth energy systems.
Open loop systems, in which ground-
water is extracted using a water well and
used for heating or cooling, are a particu-
lar type of low temperature geothermal
system. In contrast, closed loop systems
involve no direct communication with the
groundwater regime and heat exchange
occurs through a set of horizontally bur-
ied pipes (horizontal loop) or pipe circuits
placed in a borehole (vertical loop).
This article focuses on the design of a
particular type of open loop system using
aquifers for energy storage, referred to as
aquifer thermal energy storage (ATES)
systems. We describe the primary appli-
cations and review the subsurface aspects
of ATES systems, including a description
of the thermal properties and heat trans-
port processes that govern the distribution
of heat in an aquifer, identiﬁ cation of
the hydrogeologic properties critical to
ATES system design, and the standard
methods of aquifer characterization for
an ATES project.
Given recent interest by industry in
the design of geothermal systems, this
article provides a current state-of-the-
art review of ATES technology from a
hydrogeological perspective.
Groundwater
Groundwater refers to subsurface wa-
ter found below the water table within
the void spaces of unconsolidated (i.e.,
sands and gravels) and consolidated (i.e.,
sandstone and volcanic rocks) geologic
formations. A groundwater aquifer ex-
ists where geologic formations are suf-
ﬁ ciently permeable to store and yield
large quantities of groundwater.
Temperature ﬂ uctuations at the earth’s
surface inﬂ uence shallow groundwater
temperatures to depths of approximately
10 m (33 ft) (Figure 1
(F
(
). Between depths of
Designing Aquifer
Thermal Energy
Storage Systems

paper or digital form without ASHRAE’s permission.

Aquifer Storage

10 m to 20 m (33 ft to 66 ft) (maximum depth of annual cyclic
variation), groundwater temperatures remain relatively stable at
temperatures typically 1°C to 2°C (1.8°F to 3.6°F) higher than lo-
cal mean annual temperatures.1 Below these depths, groundwater
temperatures gradually increase at a rate of roughly 1°C per 35
m (1.8°F per 115 ft) depth towards the earth’s interior in what
is termed the geothermal gradient. As a result, in areas where
a supply of groundwater is readily available from an aquifer, a
reliable source of low temperature geothermal energy exists.
Open loop geothermal systems use this resource by extracting
groundwater from an aquifer using a water well, and passing it
across a heat exchanger to allow transfer of energy for direct use
in a building’s HVAC system, typically in combination with a heat
pump. The majority of open loop systems subsequently dispose of
the “used” groundwater either by discharging to a surface water
body or injecting back into the aquifer. These types of open loop
systems, known as pump-and-release or pump-and-dump systems,
are relatively simple to implement, and for commercial or institu-
tional applications, offer energy efﬁ ciencies comparable to closed
loop systems at substantially reduced capital costs.2,3
However, these systems have potential for causing environ-
mental degradation due to the long-term warming or cooling of
the environment, as well as a degradation in system efﬁ ciency
or system failure due to excessive warming (or cooling) of the
aquifer.4 This is particularly true in cases where injection of
waste heat is not countered by removal of that heat at a later
time, thus leading to heat buildup. For example, aquifers be-
neath some areas of the Winnipeg, MB, Canada, have warmed
by several degrees due to the continued release of waste heat,
largely from industrial cooling applications.5
Thermal Energy Storage
ATES open loop systems can offer increased energy efﬁ ciency
and long-term cost savings over pump and dump systems and
closed loop systems by using an aquifer as a seasonal storage res-
ervoir for waste or excess thermal energy generated in alternate
(off-peak) seasons or periods of low demand (i.e., solar energy
in summer months; cold air in winter months). ATES systems
operate by transferring heat or cold mass to or from groundwa-
ter via a heat exchanger. The groundwater that is injected back
into the aquifer is heated (13°C to 120°C [55°F to 248°F]) or
chilled (6°C to 12°C [43°F to 54°F]). The groundwater may
retain a temperature higher or lower than the undisturbed ground
temperature, depending on the thermal properties of the Earth
Illustration 1: Various forms of underground thermal energy storage.4
S 3 4 B u i l d i n g f o r t h e Fu t u r e | A S u p p l e m e n t t o A S H R A E J o u r n a l
S e p t e m b e r 2 0 0 5
and ﬂ ow characteristics of the aquifer. During periods of high
heating or cooling demand, water is pumped from the aquifer
and used as an energy source or sink.
ATES systems are one of a number of underground sub-
surface techniques that use the earth as a warm or cold mass
storage medium (Illustration 1
(I(
). Other forms of thermal mass
storage include pit storage, rock-cavern storage, closed loop
pipe, duct or borehole systems in unconsolidated materials or
solid rock, and open-loop gravel-water pit storage.
Closed loop systems, including borehole thermal energy
storage (BTES) systems, offer a good alternative when no suit-
able aquifers are present at a site, or if the cost to determine the
aquifer suitability and drill a well is large. Experience around
the world with these various techniques has shown that with
respect to the storage volume achievable, the ability to transfer
energy, the temperature range, and the efﬁ ciency and cost, ATES
is one of the better methods of underground energy storage over
longer time periods.6
ATES System Design & Applications
The design of ATES systems ultimately
reﬂ ects the variability in the local condi-
tions that are present at the project site
and as a result, different system conﬁ gu-
rations exist. The main components of an
ATES system, however, include a suitable
storage aquifer, a production well or wells
(which may act as pumping or injection
wells), a low cost or free source or sink of
thermal energy (such as waste industrial
process heat or solar heat for heating or
cold outside air temperatures for cool-
ing), a heat exchanger, and a demand for
the energy transfer.7The feasibility of an
ATES system is determined by the suit-
ability of the aquifer to act as a storage
medium for warm or cold groundwater.
The suitability of the aquifer can be assessed through a charac-
terization of the aquifer properties.
Three main ATES system types can be deﬁ ned on the basis of
the form of energy being stored. These are chilled water storage
systems (henceforth, referred to as cold storage), heat storage
systems, and integrated heat and cold storage systems.
Cold storage involves the injection, storage and recovery
of chilled (or cold) water at temperatures between 6°C and
12°C (43°F to 54°F) in a suitable storage aquifer for storage
periods from several hours to several months. One or more
wells can be used depending on energy requirements and the
properties of the aquifer.
Applications of cold storage include primarily air condition-
ing8 and equipment cooling (i.e., computers) in institutional and
commercial buildings, and industrial process cooling. These
systems are best applied when a demand for cooling exists for
signiﬁ cant portion of the year. Presently, many cold storage sys-
tems operate in Europe and North America, particularly in The
Netherlands. The storage efﬁ ciency of a cold storage system is
equivalent to the amount of energy injected into an aquifer (e.g.,
energy injected to the aquifer while cooling a building) divided
by the amount of energy taken from an aquifer (e.g., energy
rejected from the aquifer using a ﬂ uid cooler to passively re-
frigerate the aquifer during cold spells). It should be calculated
over an entire charge/discharge cycle where the aquifer reaches
the same bulk temperature at the end of each cycle, which is
ideal for environmental sustainability considerations. It can
reach 70% to 100% for most long-term cold storage projects.6
The cold storage efﬁ ciency decreases as the heat transfer from
the surroundings to the storage aquifer increases.
Heat storage involves the injection, storage and recovery of
heated water in a suitable storage aquifer. Heat storage systems can
be differentiated on the basis of whether they store low to moderate
temperature heat (10°C to 40°C [50°F to 104°F) or high tempera-
ture heat (40°C to 150°C [104°F to 302°F]). The components, well
conﬁ gurations and storage periods of heat
storage ATES systems are similar to cold
storage systems. Many applications of
aquifer heat storage exist, including space
heating, industrial heating, heating for
agricultural purposes (e.g., greenhouses),
and roadway de-icing and snow-melting.9
Due to convective heat losses (buoyancy
effects) being greater for heat storage than
for cold storage, the storage efﬁ ciency is
typically less than that of cold storage and
ranges between 50% to 80%.6
To counter the effects of higher heat
losses due to convection currents, the
storage wells for high temperature heat
storage are preferably drilled to greater
depths. This minimizes losses by using
the earth above the storage aquifer as an
insulator between the warm aquifer and
the cooler ambient air, and by choosing
a storage medium that is surrounded by warmer materials (due
to the geothermal gradient), thereby reducing losses.
Integrated or combined heat and cold ATES systems may offer
an increased level of efﬁ ciency over cold- or heat-only storage
systems, particularly in large-scale applications. These systems are
most commonly used in combination with a heat pump to provide
heating and cooling in commercial or institutional buildings.
The components of combined systems are virtually the same
as for cold or warm mass storage alone. However, complexity
in the design of aboveground and belowground components
of integrated systems usually is increased. For below-ground
components, the positioning of the wells becomes more impor-
tant as the aquifer is used as a heat source or sink. Speciﬁ cally,
storage of warm and cold mass results in the development of
thermal plumes around the wells and there is increased risk of
heat transfer between them.
From an economic perspective, integrated ATES systems
for combined space heating/cooling applications using a heat

S 3 5
pump, with no other sources of waste heat or chilled water,
may be more sensitive to costs associated with aquifer charac-
terization and other design costs compared to ATES systems
with higher grade waste heat sources. For example, in smaller
applications, it will be challenging to rely on a relatively
small increase in heat pump COP to generate sufﬁ ciently
attractive returns on the incremental capital investment (the
increased complexity of the system, in addition to the effort
for aquifer characterization, may add signiﬁ cant cost to the
project). Nonetheless, in the cooling mode application of an
integrated ATES system, an opportunity may exist for direct
cooling (without the use of a heat pump) where direct cool-
ing might otherwise not be possible. In this case, substantial
energy savings could result.
Aquifer Characterization for ATES
To determine the suitability of an aquifer for thermal energy
storage, a characterization of the aquifer must be performed.
Aquifer characterization for an ATES project typically entails
a detailed assessment of the aquifer in terms of its geology,
physical properties, ﬂ ow characteristics, and water chemistry.
These characteristics are the same as those assessed for most
environmental investigations or water supply studies.
Geology and Aquifer Thickness
gy
q
The physical makeup of the sediments or rocks and depth,
aerial extent, and thickness of permeable and impermeable geo-
logical units both at a regional scale and locally at the proposed
ATES site, will be of primary importance in determining the
nature and distribution of aquifers in the subsurface.
The majority of thermal storage projects use unconsolidated
aquifers as storage media. However, unfractured and highly
fractured bedrock aquifers also can be used for thermal energy
storage. In these aquifers, the mapping of structural features,
such as fractures and faults that strongly inﬂ uence ﬂ uid ﬂ ow,
will be important.
Hydraulic Properties and Groundwater Flow
y
p
The effective porosity and the hydraulic properties (hydraulic
conductivity and speciﬁ c storage) are of primary importance to
the design and evaluation of ATES systems. The effective poros-
ity refers to the system of interconnected void space in the porous
aquifer media and is important in determining the amount of heated
or chilled water that can be stored per unit volume of the aquifer.
The hydraulic conductivity is a measure of the ability of the
porous medium to transmit water. High hydraulic conductivi-
ties are desirable for large ﬂ ow rates of water to be withdrawn
or injected from or to the aquifer with the least amount of
change to the hydraulic and temperature gradients around the
production wells. Speciﬁ c storage is a measure of the water
storage capacity of an aquifer and indicates the amount of
reduction (or increase) in hydraulic head observed at a well.
These properties, along with the hydraulic gradient, control
the velocity and direction of groundwater ﬂ ow or the ﬂ ow
regime in an aquifer.7
Thermal Properties and Ground Temperature Field
p
p
The physical processes of conduction (diffusion) and con-
vection govern the transport and storage of heat in an aquifer.
Conductive heat transport refers to the movement of heat along
a thermal gradient, while convective heat transport refers to the
movement of heat by moving groundwater. In an ATES system,
conductive heat transport occurs due to the temperature gradients
induced by warmer or cooler storage water coming into contact
with the surrounding aquifer water. The conduction of heat in
the aquifer is governed by the aquifer thermal properties: the
volumetric heat capacity (indicative of the amount of temperature
change that occurs when the aquifer media absorbs or loses a
speciﬁ c amount of energy) and the effective thermal conductivity
(the ability of the aquifer media to transmit heat). Since water
has a higher heat capacity but lower thermal conductivity than
rock, the storage of thermal energy in aquifers is best suited to
a high porosity formation to minimize conductive energy losses
and increase the efﬁ ciency of the thermal store.
Convective heat transport results from groundwater move-
ment driven by hydraulic head and temperature differentials,
which exist both locally in the vicinity of the well ﬁ eld (as a
result of pumping/injection of groundwater) and regionally
within the aquifer. Convective heat transport has both advective
and dispersive components.
Advection describes the movement of thermal energy di-
rectly due to the linear ﬂ ow of groundwater through the porous
medium whereas thermal dispersion refers to the microscopic
dispersal or spreading of thermal energy in three dimensions
beyond the regions it normally would occupy as a result of
advection alone.
Dispersion can be viewed as a zone of mixing, which travels in
advance of the thermal energy front deﬁ ned by advection. In addi-
tion, “free” convection also occurs when groundwater movement
is driven by density variations caused by temperature gradients.
In high temperature ATES systems, the effects of free convection
will become important, as the thermal energy will rise due to a
lowered density of the heated water. In lower temperature ATES
systems, including heat storage and cold storage, the density dif-
ferences between the stored water and the ambient groundwater
will be small enough so that free convection is minimal.
Regional groundwater ﬂ ow is an important consideration in
the design of ATES systems as higher groundwater ﬂ ow regimes
can lead to advection or down-gradient ‘drift’ of stored energy
beyond potential recovery regions.10 In the presence of a steep
regional gradient in hydraulic head, which would correspond to
faster groundwater ﬂ ows, a lower permeability aquifer is required
to minimize convective losses (dispersion reduces the thermal in-
tensity of the recovered plume11). In addition, small-scale vertical
and horizontal variations in hydraulic conductivity or heterogeneity
in the aquifer that result from changes in geology are important, as
these will affect the dispersion of the thermal plume.
Groundwater Chemistry
The chemistry of aquifers often represents a signiﬁ cant prob-
lem in the design of ATES systems.12 The primary problems
S 3 6 B u i l d i n g f o r t h e Fu t u r e | A S u p p l e m e n t t o A S H R A E J o u r n a l
S e p t e m b e r 2 0 0 5
related to groundwater chemistry include: (1) the precipitation
of minerals such as calcium carbonate, and iron and manganese
oxides, which result in the scaling of heat exchangers and clog-
ging of wells; (2) the corrosion of piping and heat exchangers
by ambient and heated groundwater; (3) biofouling of the well
intake area; and (4) the clogging of the aquifer as a result of
precipitation of minerals within the aquifer or the transport of
precipitates into the aquifer.
These problems are avoidable if consideration of the potential
for geochemistry problems is considered in advance of the sys-
tem design phase. Many design strategies and water treatment
technologies have been used to mitigate these problems.12
Aquifer Characterization Methods
q
Aquifer characterization for an ATES project typically in-
volves phases of desktop review, drilling, hydraulic testing, and
modeling, although the level of complexity of these character-
ization steps can vary considerably depending on how much
information is known about the local geology/hydrogeology
and how large the ATES system will be.
No speciﬁ c rules of thumb can be used to determine whether
or not a particular site would be suitable for ATES, without
at least some form of prefeasibility study. At this stage, this
exercise need not be exhaustive. A simple desktop review can
be performed as a preliminary data gathering step (pre-feasibil-
ity study) to determine whether an ATES system is feasible. A
hydrogeologist is well-equipped to undertake this type of study.
If the area is well developed and much is known already about
the hydrogeology of the area, then geological/hydrogeological
investigative costs can be kept to a minimum. In uncharted
territory, more effort may be needed.
In a prefeasibility study, information regarding the site ge-
ology and groundwater potential often can be obtained from
existing nearby water wells, previous hydrogeological investiga-
tions, aerial photographs, topographic and geologic maps. The
speciﬁ c questions to be answered would be, is there a suitable
aquifer—one capable of yielding the amount of water required
for the project? If so, how many wells might be needed to meet
the demand, bearing in mind peak demand, average demand, and
the fact that injection wells are often less efﬁ cient at receiving
water. Ultimately, a well (or wells) will have to be hydraulically
tested to ensure that they are capable of producing and receiv-
ing water. The spacing of the wells only can be estimated by a
hydrogeologist in a preliminary sense. A decision then can be
made concerning space availability, piping costs, etc.
If the prefeasibility study suggests that ATES is feasible,
then a preliminary test well should be drilled to conﬁ rm the
information collected to that point. It is not recommended
that a production size well be drilled initially, as these can be
costly, particularly if large diameter wells are needed. A test
well can later be over-drilled to a production diameter. Logging
of borehole cuttings/sediments should be undertaken to verify
the geology, and identify permeable zones and target aquifers.
In unconsolidated aquifers, grain size analyses should be per-
formed on these sediments to select an appropriate slot size for
a well screen. Sampling and analysis of groundwater should be
carried out to assess groundwater chemistry.
Following test drilling, hydraulic testing of the aquifer should
be performed to determine the hydraulic properties of the aquifer,
and to assess the response of the aquifer to pumping and injec-
tion during operation of the ATES system. Constant discharge
pumping tests, step-injection tests, step-drawdown tests and tracer
tests are examples of tests for ATES aquifer characterization,7
although not all of these need to be done. Thermal conductivity
can be calculated using values for known minerals and porosity,
measured in a laboratory using either a needle probe (sediments)
or divided bar apparatus (rock core or chips), or measured in situ
using a formation thermal conductivity testing unit.13
For borehole thermal energy storage (BTES) systems, in situ
measurements of thermal conductivity should be conducted
because these systems rely solely on conductive heat transport.
In cases where thermal conductivity is high, the length of the
loop can be shortened leading to lower costs for installation
of the system.
For ATES systems, thermal conductivity can be estimated
because heat conduction is not signiﬁ cant, and therefore, the addi-
tional expense to perform an in situthermal conductivity test would
not have as signiﬁ cant a payback as for a closed-loop borehole
system. The volumetric heat capacity typically is calculated.
For larger systems, borehole geophysical logging can prove
very useful for identifying geologic units and permeable zones,
particularly fractures in competent bedrock, and to determine
the temperature-depth proﬁ le in the aquifer. A monitoring well
network, installed around the production wells and in background
areas, can be used to determine the hydraulic gradient and direc-
tion of groundwater ﬂ ow at the site, and later used to monitor the
movement of the thermal front during operation of the system.
Maps showing temperature isotherms, lines of equal temperature,
of the thermal plume subsequently can be produced.
The geologic and hydraulic data collected from desktop studies,
site investigations and aquifer testing subsequently can be input
into simulation models that can predict the movement of the ther-
mal plume and calculate the heat balance of the thermal energy
store. These thermo-hydraulic codes provide either analytical
or numerical solutions to heat transport and storage problems
posed by ATES systems. Complex three-dimensional models,
combining groundwater and heat ﬂ ow within the aquifer and
heat conduction to surrounding layers, can be used to calculate
the temperature of the water recovered from the store.
Conclusion
Many ATES systems exist throughout the world and the
technology has been proven to be a viable, cost-competitive,
energy-efﬁ cient technology.6,7,9,14 Careful consideration of the
hydrogeological aspects of the system must be made during the
design phase, however, to ensure a properly operating system.
Widespread application of ATES systems has been impeded
by combination of a lack of familiarity with the hydrogeologi-
cal components of the systems, the perceived high level of risk
and higher initial capital costs involved, and environmental and
S e p t e m b e r 2 0 0 5 B u i l d i n g f o r t h e Fu t u r e | A S u p p l e m e n t t o A S H R A E J o u r n a l
S 3 7
regulatory issues.14 Despite these limitations and concerns, the
number of applications of the technology continues to grow. The
steady increases in energy likely only will add further incentive
for use of aquifer thermal energy storage.
The beneﬁ ts of an integrated design team approach cannot
be overemphasized. The ATES system feasibility and design
considerations should not be contemplated in isolation from the
mechanical and architectural design considerations, nor in isola-
tion of the local hydrogeology. Much can be done architecturally
to favor an appealing load proﬁ le for ATES or other geoexchange
strategies, and much can be done with the mechanical system to
favor geoexchange application (ATES or otherwise). Likewise,
consideration of the aquifer’s potential and its limitations must
be taken into consideration. With an integrated design approach,
multidiscipline sensitivity analyses can be conducted to prioritize
where money should be spent to provide the best and most cost-
effective energy savings gains.

S e p t e m b e r 2 0 0 5
By Terry E. Townsend, P.E., Fellow ASHRAE
A
SHRAE’s involvement with the U.S. Green Building
Council (USGBC) formally began in June 2002 when
the ASHRAE Board of Directors and the USGBC
Board of Directors approved the Memorandum of Under-
standing (MOU) between the two organizations. This MOU
established a Joint Steering Committee, composed of one dues-
paying member and one staff member from each organization.
The Joint Steering Committee usually meets semiannually to
coordinate joint activities.
In August 2003, ASHRAE was invited to become a voting
member of the Sustainability Summit of Professionals. This
group of 14 professional organizations promotes technologies
and professional activities supporting sustainability. In addition
to ASHRAE and USGBC, members include:
• American Society of Interior Designers,
• American Institute of Architects (AIA),
• International Interior Design Association,
• CoreNet Global, Association for Corporate Real Estate
Professionals,
• Carnegie Mellon School of Architecture & Planning,
• Construction Speciﬁ cations Institute,
• Urban Land Institute,
• International Facility Management Association,
• Building Owners & Managers Association,
• Association of Higher Education Facilities Ofﬁ cers,
• Institute of Real Estate Management, and
• Society for College & University Planning.
During the last Joint Steering Committee meeting, USGBC
asked ASHRAE to assume the lead organizational position of
the Sustainability Summit. This is believed to be an excellent
opportunity for ASHRAE to become the primary technical
resource for Sustainability Summit organizations and more
involved in joint development of educational programs.
The biggest annual USGBC event for its members is Green-
Build, which began in 2002. ASHRAE has been involved since
2003 and will have an even greater presence during 2005
GreenBuild since it has a voting member on the program com-
mittee. ASHRAE members are presenting technical programs
requested by USGBC members for 2005 GreenBuild.
Member participation within the other organization’s com-
mittees also increases cooperation between ASHRAE and
USGBC. USGBC members are involved in ASHRAE Standard
Project Committee 90.1 for revising Addendum e, the Energy
Cost Budget Method, as well as in Standing Standards Project
Committees 62 (indoor air quality) and 55 (thermal comfort).
Technical committees with USGBC representation include TC
2.8, Building Environmental Impacts and Sustainability; TC 7.6,
Systems Energy Utilization; TC 7.9, Building Commissioning;
and TC 9.6, Healthcare Facilities.
Likewise, ASHRAE is more involved in USGBC activities,
with voting members on the USGBC Technical and Scientiﬁ c
Advisory Committee, GreenBuild Program Committee and
new USGBC Research Committee. ASHRAE also is slated
to have at least one voting member on each of the USGBC
Leadership in Energy and Environmental Design (LEED®)
Committees—New Construction (NC), Existing Buildings
(EB), Healthcare (HC) and LEED-Residential.
ASHRAE’s Advanced Energy Design Guide series is intended
to give prescriptive guidance on how to achieve 30%, 50% and
up to 70% energy savings toward a net-zero energy use facility
using the year 2000 as the energy consumption baseline. The
ﬁ rst in the series is the Advanced Energy Design Guide for Small
Ofﬁ ce Buildings (AEDG-SO), published in January. The next
AEDG addresses small retail facilities through a collaborative
effort between ASHRAE, USGBC, AIA and the Illuminating
Engineering Society of North America.
Due to extensive support calculations involved in the develop-
ment of each AEDG publication, the USGBC LEED-NC com-
mittee voted to accept (and make retroactive to LEED 2.1-NC)
use of the AEDG prescriptive guidance in lieu of the required
energy calculations in the LEED criteria. This substitution can
earn up to 4 energy credits in LEED-NC submittals.
Even greater opportunities exist with recent and proposed
activities for joint participation between ASHRAE and USGBC.
TC 1.4, Control Theory and Application, formed a new Green
Building Controls subcommittee. TC 7.9, Building Commis-
sioning, is beginning to develop guidance on “Performance
Evaluation of Efﬁ cient, Healthy and Comfortable Buildings.” One
of the main themes in the newly approved ASHRAE Strategic
Research Plan is energy and sustainability. ASHRAE developed
two research programs for TC 9.6 from needs identiﬁ ed during
the 2004 Healthcare Summit. It will provide valuable information
for development of the future AEDG for health-care facilities.
After three years, ASHRAE and the USGBC have made a
uniﬁ ed collaborative effort in the development and implemen-
tation of design tools, construction guidance and operational
metrics for the worldwide sustainability effort. Both organiza-
tions plan to expand collaborative efforts in the most timely and
effective manner because together, we will “make the energy
we use easier on the earth.”
Terry E. Townsend, P.E., is president of Townsend Engineer-
ing, Chattanooga, Tenn. He is president-elect of ASHRAE.
ASHRAE and Sustainability
Special Column
