About the Author
Chillers +
Lighting + TES
Why CFC Chiller Replacement
Can Be Energy-Savings Windfall
A
s we approach the ten-year anniversary of the chloroﬂ uorocarbon
(CFC) ban that took effect in 1996, only 57% of the estimated
85,486 large tonnage CFC chillers in the U.S. and Canada have been
replaced or converted. According to the estimates from the Air-Condi-
tioning and Refrigeration Institute (ARI)1 and the Heating, Refrigeration
and Air-Conditioning Institute of Canada (HRAI),2 by the end of 2004,
approximately 37,000 CFC chillers still operated in North America.
Giuliano Todesco is an energy systems engineer-
ing technologist with Jacques Whitford in Ottawa,
ON, Canada.
By Giuliano Todesco, Member ASHRAE
$3.4 billion to $4.8 billion. In addition,
the cooling and lighting loads in these
buildings contribute an estimated 3,600
to 9,200 MW to the summer peak demand
of North American utilities.
The electricity consumption and peak
electrical demand can be reduced signiﬁ -
cantly by replacing the remaining CFC
chillers with new efﬁ cient plants. The
performance of chillers has improved
signiﬁ cantly in the last 12 years compared
to chillers manufactured in the 1970s
and 1980s. Additional electricity energy
savings are also possible with cooling
These CFC chillers serve an estimated
3.4 billion to 4.7 billion ft² (315 million
to 440 million m²) of commercial ﬂ oor
space with a total electricity consump-
tion of 49,000 to 66,000 GWh/year, and
an annual electricity operating cost of
© 2005, American Society of Heating, Refrigerating and Air-Conditioning Engineers, Inc. (www.ashrae.org). Reprinted by permission from
ASHRAE Journal, (Vol. 47, No. 10, October 2005). This article may not be copied nor distributed in either paper or digital form without ASHRAE’s
permission.
October 2005
ASHRAE Journal
19
load reduction measures such as lighting retroﬁ ts thanks to
considerable improvements in the performance of commercial
ﬂ uorescent lighting systems.
Large commercial chiller replacements that include other
energy efﬁ ciency measures exhibit better economics then
stand-alone chiller replacements, according to the Cool Sense
Program sponsored by the U.S. Department of Energy, Federal
Energy Management Program and Environmental Protection
Agency. This program promotes integrated chiller retroﬁ ts that
emphasize chiller replacements with other energy conservation
measures to achieve greater savings and improve the attractive-
ness of the investment.3
Accelerating the replacment rate of CFC chillers with more
efﬁ cient new plants can beneﬁ t the North American power grid,
which faces limited spare generating capacity and overloaded
transmission grids. Parts of the U.S. transmission infrastructure
for example, are nearing their carrying capacity threshold, and
Ontario’s Independent Electricity Market Operator (IMO) has
forecasted the need for additional generation capacity in the
next 10 years as a result of a 1.7% annual growth in power
consumption.4,5 Commercial cooling is a major contributor to
peak demand in North America, and CFC chiller replacements
plus other load reduction measures are effective demand reduc-
tion options that would provide some relief to the tight North
American electricity generation capacity.
The CFC retroﬁ ts would also help reduce the natural gas use for
summer peaking electricity generation in North America given
that most of the summer peak power is generated using natural
gas turbines. In the last ﬁ ve years, an estimated 200,000 MW of
new generation capacity has been added in the U.S. mostly with
gas turbines, which in turn has contributed to a dramatic rise in
the use of natural gas for peak power generation.6
Reducing Cooling and Lighting Electricity Consumption
The electricity consumption and demand of the remaining
CFC chillers in the U.S. and Canada is shown in Table 1. The
estimates for the U.S. use the CFC chiller statistics from ARI and
the commercial market ﬂ oor space estimates from the 1999 U.S.
Commercial Buildings Energy Consumption Survey (CBECS)
database. The 1999 CBECS database provides ﬂ oor space esti-
mates for commercial buildings larger than 50,000 ft² (4645 m2)
with central chillers.7 Average cooling energy use intensities from
the 1995 CBECS database were combined with the ﬂ oor space
estimates to derive total cooling and lighting energy use.
The estimate of the remaining CFC chillers in Canada and
the electricity consumption and demand are also shown in Table
1. Information from HRAI together with energy use intensity
statistics from Canada’s End-Use Energy Use Data Handbook
were used to derive the values.8
As further shown in Table 1, annual cooling and lighting elec-
tricity use are estimated to range from 26,100 to 35,500 GWh.
From a demand perspective, these two end-uses contribute an esti-
mated 3,600 to 9,200 MW to the summer peak demand of electric
utilities. These are the largest electrical end-uses in a commercial
building, accounting for 54% of the total electricity use.
Large reductions in electricity use can be achieved due to sig-
niﬁ cant improvements in chiller efﬁ ciencies that have occurred
in the last 20 years. As shown in Table 2, the efﬁ ciency of large
centrifugal chillers has improved from an average of 0.8 kW/ton
(COP 4.4) in the 1970s to a low of 0.48 kW/ton (COP 7.3) today.9
Electricity demand savings also can be realized given that com-
mercial air conditioning is a major contributor to peak demand
in the U.S. and some regions in Canada.10 Demand savings can
be large with replacement technologies such as hybrid cooling
plants or thermal energy storage (TES) systems.
Additional electricity savings can be achieved at the time of
the chiller replacement with load reduction measures such as a
lighting retroﬁ t in buildings that still have old T12 ﬂ uorescent
lighting systems. Several studies have estimated that only 30%
of commercial buildings in North America have been retroﬁ tted
to efﬁ cient T8 ﬂ uorescent lighting and electronic ballasts.11,12
Electricity and demand savings as high as 50% are possible
through lighting redesign and conversion to T8 ﬂ uorescent light-
ing. The lighting power density (LPD) in existing commercial
The electricity consumption and peak electrical demand can be reduced signiﬁ cantly
by replacing the remaining CFC chillers with new efﬁ cient plants.... Additional electricity
energy savings are also possible with cooling load reduction measures such as light-
ing retroﬁ ts thanks to considerable improvements in the performance of commercial
ﬂ uorescent lighting systems.

buildings can be reduced from the current market average of
approximately 1.7 W/ft² (18.3 W/m²)13 to levels of 1.0 to 1.3
W/ft² (10.8 to 14.0 W/m²) for most commercial buildings.Lower
LPDs would be possible using the newest high performance T8
ﬂ uorescent systems, which are 15% more efﬁ cient than standard
T8 ﬂ uorescent systems.
ANSI/ASHRAE/IESNA Standard 90.1, Energy Standard for
Buildings Except Low-Rise Residential Buildings, illustrates
the evolution and performance improvements possible with
commercial lighting systems. Table 3 shows the drop in rec-
ommended LPD levels that have occurred between Standard
90.1-1989 and Standard 90.1-2004. As an example, the recom-
mended LPD values for ofﬁ ces have dropped from 1.5 to 1.0
W/ft² (16.1 to 10.8 W/m²) in the most recent standard.

A lighting retroﬁ t that signiﬁ cantly reduces the LPD would
reduce the cooling load and allow the use of a smaller chiller,
reducing the cost of the new chiller and improving the overall
economics. From an internal heat gain reduction perspective,
a lighting retroﬁ t in a 250,000 ft² (~23 000 m²) ofﬁ ce building
that lowers the LPD from 1.7 W/ft² (18.3 W/m²) to 1.0 W/ft²
(10.8 W/m²) would achieve a 50 ton (176 kW) reduction in the
size of the cooling plant.
Table 4 provides a comparison of the cooling load components
based on a DOE-2 simulation using weather data for Chicago. As
shown, the existing building would require a 600 ton (2110 kW)
chiller that could be replaced with a smaller 550 ton (1934 kW)
new chiller after undertaking the lighting retroﬁ t. This represents
a 10% reduction in the size of the cooling plant.
Additional electricity savings from cooling plant auxiliaries
also can be achieved through reduced chilled and condenser

ing energy use during off-design conditions when chilled
and condenser water design ﬂ ows of 2 and 3 gpm (0.036 and

Potential Electricity Savings
Quantiﬁ cation of the energy and demand savings are illus-
trated through a DOE-2 simulation of a 250,000 ft² (~23 000
m²) typical ofﬁ ce building using Chicago weather data.
The base building electricity consumption matches CBECS
energy use statistics of existing large ofﬁ ce buildings. Cooling
and lighting electricity use account for approximately 52%
of the total building electricity use. The building displays a

Table 5 summarizes the results for three
retroﬁ t scenarios. The energy efﬁ cient
(EE) chiller option replacement achieves
a 7.6% reduction in electricity consump-
tion from replacement of the existing 0.75
kW/ton (COP 4.7) CFC chiller with an
identically sized 0.5 kW/ton (COP 7.0)
new chiller. Some electricity savings in
pumping energy also are achieved from
installation of variable-speed chilled
water pumping plus lower chilled water
and condenser water ﬂ ows possible with
the more efﬁ cient chiller. This alternative
achieves a 12% reduction in peak summer
electricity demand.
The second alternative combines the
chiller retroﬁ t and a high-performance
lighting redesign and displays total
building electricity savings of 37.8%
and a summer peak demand reduction
of 33% thanks to a 50% reduction in
lighting LPD from 1.7 W/ft² (18.3 W/m²)
to 0.8 W/ft² (8.6 W/m²). The low LPD
assumes the use of high performance T8
ﬂ uorescent systems that are 15% more
efﬁ cient than conventional T8 systems
while providing the same light output.
The reduction in internal heat gain from
the lower LPD allows the chiller to be
reduced from 600 to 525 tons (2110 to
1846 kW) representing a 12% reduc-
tion in the plant size. Additional VAV
fan electricity savings also are achieved
from the reduction in the internal heat
gain. There is however, a small increase
in space heating energy use.
The third alternative is based on a cool-
ing plant retroﬁ t that includes a TES using
ice storage and a lighting redesign. Much
higher demand savings could be achieved,
but with only a 32.6% reduction in the
building electricity consumption relative to
the base case because of chiller operation
at lower evaporator temperatures. The TES
assumes a full ice storage system that shifts
the operation of the chiller to off-peak
hours using a chiller with a performance
of 0.7 kW/ton (COP 5.0). Such an alterna-
tive would make sense in regions where
demand charges from the electric utility are
signiﬁ cant and preferential off-peak hour
electricity charges are available.
A recent ASHRAE Journal article
stated that cooling plants with TES that
operate during off-peak hours offer elec-
tric utilities better generation efﬁ ciencies
and lower emissions by reducing on-peak
loads and shifting to off-peak hours.14
The previous results illustrate the ben-
eﬁ ts of a chiller retroﬁ t that also combines
lighting and other cooling load reduction
measures. The energy and demand reduc-
tion of the chiller plus lighting retroﬁ t
scenario is more than four times larger
than the chiller replacement option.
Market-Wide Impacts
The cooling and lighting electricity
consumption in commercial buildings
with CFC chillers and T12 ﬂ uorescent
Advertisement formerly in this space.
26
ASHRAE Journal ashrae.org
October 2005
Alternatives
United States
Canada
North America
Annual Potential Electricity Savings
8,800 to 12,300 GWh
2,000 to 2,200 GWh
10,800 to 14,500 GWh
Peak Utility Coincident Peak Demand Savings
At 20% to 50% Coincidence
1,000 MW to 2,400 MW
100 MW to 400 MW
1,100 MW to 2,800 MW
Annual Energy Cost Savings

Reduction in NG Use for Summer Peak Power Generation

Table 6: Overall potential energy savings and demand impact in North America.
lighting can be reduced signiﬁ cantly as illustrated in the previ-
ous section. As shown in Table 6, the electricity savings potential
in North America ranges from 10,800 to 14,500 GWh/year with
overall electricity cost savings of $850 to $1,100 million dol-
lars. These savings are based on replacing the remaining CFC
chillers and simultaneously undertaking T12 to T8 lighting
retroﬁ ts in half of the stock.
Electric utilities also would beneﬁ t from an estimated 1,100 to
2,800 MW reduction in utility summer peak demand. This sum-
mer peak demand reduction also would offset natural gas use for
summer peaking electricity generation given that most of the sum-
mer peak power in North America is generated using natural gas
turbines. In this regard, Amory Lovins15 recently stated that:
During peak demand, reining in consumption, even a small
percentage of users can lead to disproportionate savings.
Saving 5% of U.S. electricity, including peak periods, would
save nearly 10% of total U.S. natural gas consumption.
At a 33% generation efﬁ ciency the reduction in natural gas
consumption from peaking plants would be in the range of 37
to 50 million MCF (40,000 to 54,000 GJ).
Industry Leadership and Market Beneﬁ ts
A signiﬁ cant potential exists to reduce electricity consumption
and electric demand in the existing large commercial buildings that
still have CFC chillers. A chiller replacement plus a lighting retroﬁ t
are probably one of the easiest EE measures to undertake.
Electricity utilities continue to face availability constraints
and natural gas supplies remain tight because of the dramatic
rise in natural gas use for peak power generation. Energy prices
have risen signiﬁ cantly because of this, and energy efﬁ ciency
Advertisement formerly in this space.
October 2005
ASHRAE Journal
27
efforts likely will increase as building owners look at options
to reduce operating costs.
Utilities and government agencies support and continue to
stimulate the rate at which the remaining stock of CFC chillers
are being replaced. A proposed bill to encourage CFC chiller con-
versions through faster tax depreciation of HVAC equipment in
commercial buildings together with existing government programs
and electric utility demand side management (DSM) programs will
help encourage building owners to replace their CFC chillers.
These energy efﬁ ciency retroﬁ ts are an essential and real solu-
tion in the current environment of limited electrical and natural
gas supplies and will beneﬁ t building owners and electric and
natural gas utilities.

About the Authors
L
arge mechanical renovations are always a challenge, especially
L
aL when a building transitions into a new use. Add to that the
L
invariable space and budget limitations associated with such proj-
ects and the challenge becomes even more difﬁ cult.
This was the case at Howard Regional Health Systems in Kokomo, Ind.
The hospital had purchased an existing three-level, off-campus building
to house a new outpatient behavioral health and disaster recovery data
center that would serve as backup to the hospital’s main data center.
A $20 per ft2 ($215 per m2) budget for the mechanical HVAC system
with other critical owner requirements forced designers to consider some
less than conventional alternatives to a traditional VAV scheme.
Ultimately, the design team found a
way to cut installed cost with a unique
reverse heating cooling airside design
while avoiding any penalties in opera-
tional cost. This was accomplished using
dedicated heat recovery chillers (DHRC).
In addition, the design helped meet the
client’s need for an expandable system
within the physical parameters of the
existing mechanical ﬂ oor space.
Thinking Outside of the (VAV) Box
Formerly a bank, the 40,000+ ft2 (3700+
m2) building purchased by Howard Re-
gional had to be completely gutted to suit
its new purpose as a data center and outpa-
tient facility, so engineers were not limited
by existing piping or ductwork. Space and
By Damon G. Greeley, P.E., Member ASHRAE, and Mark Platt, Member ASHRAE
Damon G. Greeley, P.E., is the director of engi-
neering consulting at FreemanWhite Healthcare
Design & Consulting in Charlotte, N.C. Mark Platt
is president of Multistack in West Salem, Wis.
© 2005, American Society of Heating, Refrigerating and Air-Conditioning Engineers, Inc. (www.ashrae.org). Reprinted by permission from
ASHRAE Journal, (Vol. 47, No. 10, October 2005). This article may not be copied nor distributed in either paper or digital form without ASHRAE’s
permission.
October 2005
ASHRAE Journal
29
budget were the primary limiting factors. Because ductwork is
the single most expensive element in most commercial HVAC
systems, one way to save installed cost was to limit the amount
of sheet metal in the design. Since it typically takes about twice
the airﬂ ow to distribute cooling as it does heating, a central air-
distribution system sized for heating rather than cooling is smaller
and less expensive to install. (Required airﬂ ow for cooling vs.
heating typically ranges from 3 to 1 in Mediterranean-like
climates, or the reverse ratio in extremely cold U.S. climates.)
This 2 to 1 ratio was the basis for the reverse heating and
cooling design that was selected for Howard Regional. While
most VAV systems cool air to meet loads in areas requiring
cooling, this system dehumidiﬁ es ﬁ rst and then warms air to
meet heating loads requiring heating. Even though ductwork
is sized for 100% of the cooling airﬂ ow downstream of the
fan coils, it was possible to downsize the main air handler in
addition to the main supply ductwork connected upstream of
the fan coils.
The engineering team knew that the reduction in size of air-
handling equipment would have an impact on installed cost.
Operationally, however, long-term penalties exist for year-round
operation of both boiler(s) and chiller. The antidote to this
penalty, which ﬁ t nicely into Howard Regional’s year-round
requirement for heating and cooling, was DHRC technology.
Such a system would use the condenser side heat of a chiller
(which ordinarily might be thrown away) to offset or completely
replace any boiler generated hot water. In theory, the concept is
wonderfully efﬁ cient since chillers typically produce more heat
than cooling. However, balancing heating and cooling always
has been a stumbling block since condenser water temperatures
are not usually high enough to satisfy a building’s demand for
heat. In many cases, a large chiller would be required to obtain
a relatively small amount of heat.
The recent introduction of modular heat recovery chillers,
capable of producing condenser water as high at 140°F (60°C)
makes the concept much easier to apply, as they can be piped
and controlled to produce the desired evaporator or condenser
temperature.1
Design, Operation and Control
A two-pipe fan coil system with two DHRCs was selected
for Howard Regional’s Main Street campus. The design and
operation is as follows:
Air and Terminal Side (Figure 1
g
(F
(
).) The overall airside me-
chanical system consists of a main outdoor air handler (AH-1)
located in a penthouse on the building rooftop, various terminal
cooling coils, and a second multizone air handler (AH-2) for
the basement zones.
AH-1 includes a pre-temp coil, a cooling coil, and a heating
coil, and provides maximum heating cfm required for each
space on all ﬂ oors, as well as dehumidiﬁ cation. During periods
of full occupation, the unit operates at constant volume. VFDs
were added to the supply and return fan for energy-efﬁ cient
balancing during partial or unoccupied periods. AH-1 provides
all the building’s ventilation requirements, providing supply
temperatures that range from 55°F (13°C) (full cooling) to 90ºF
(32°C) (full heating, required only for perimeter spaces).
The terminal units on Floors 1 and 2 are cooling coils only.
During periods of heat demand, AH-1 provides warmed air
to the cooling coils on these ﬂ oors. These terminal units are
installed with modulating chilled water valves that cool the
heated air down to the appropriate setpoint—the reverse of a
typical VAV system with respect to main duct air temperature
and terminal coil function being cooling in lieu of heating.
Return air from the terminal units mixes with outdoor air
in AH-1. If the reset supply temperature begins to overheat
Because ductwork is the single most expensive element in most commercial HVAC
systems, one way to save installed cost was to limit the amount of sheet metal in the
design. Since it typically takes about twice the airﬂ ow to distribute cooling as it does
heating, a central air-distribution system sized for heating rather than cooling is smaller
and less expensive to install.
30
ASHRAE Journal ashrae.org
October 2005
the space, the space speciﬁ c terminal unit kicks in with ad-
ditional cooling. The overall air/ventilation system operates
the same—winter, spring, summer, and fall during occupied
mode. During partial or unoccupied mode, the main air system
inlet dampers to each space fan coil are closed until a call for
ventilation and/or heating are present.
Chilled Water Side (Figure 2
g
(F
(
).) On this side, the DHRC
evaporator supplies AH-1, the fan coils, and the multizone
unit cooling coil with chilled water. A pre-temper coil in AH-
1 supplements or enables chillers to unload during extreme
outdoor air temperatures. (The maintenance staff requested
50% of the circulated air be ventilation and 50% is recirculated,
keeping the winter season mixed air above 32°F (0°c), so freeze
protection of the chilled water coil in the outdoor air handler was
unnecessary. This was due to the fact that the building would
have no regular maintenance staff.) When mixed air drops to
35°F to 45°F (1°C to 7°C), this cool air will actually drop the
temperature of the return chilled water to 50°F to 60°F (10°C to
16°C), thereby precooling the water entering the evaporator.
g
Heating /Condenser Water Side (
g
(Figure 3
g
(F
(
).) The DHRC
condenser provides all the hot water required for building heat
during daytime operating hours. Although two 1,000,000 Btu/h
(293 kW) boilers are in place, they are only used as backup. The
condenser water is reset from 90°F to 130°F (32°C to 54°C),
depending on the outdoor air handler discharge temperature.
It was anticipated (and has so far proven true) that the building
heating load at the Howard Medical facility would not exceed
the cooling load. This is important, as knowing the relationship
between the heating and cooling load dictates how the system will
be most efﬁ ciently controlled. Applications of DHRC systems
have demonstrated the need to set the control point (chilled or hot
water) to respond to the side of the system that has the lowest de-
mand.2At Howard Medical the controls are set up so that if heat-
ing requirements exceed the concurrent cooling load, the backup
boilers will ﬁ re to add supplemental heat to the low-temperature
heating/condenser loop. If cooling requirements exceed heating,
then the additional condenser heat is rejected to the outdoors via
the dry ﬂ uid coolers. The scheduling of the compressors of each
DHRC is performed by an onboard microprocessor that monitors
discharge pressure cutout, suction pressure cutout, compressor
motor protector, suction temperature and evaporator entering and
leaving chilled water temperature.
Sometimes the building cannot absorb the total heat pro-
duced by the DHRC system, in which case dry coolers are
used to reject the excess condenser heat. Dry coolers were

ASHRAE Journal
33
Like many facilities, Howard Regional had substantial year-
round heating and cooling requirements. This is a pivotal
point for any facility considering a DHRC system. Provided
simultaneous year-round loads exist, the degree of efﬁ ciency
can be determined with the following coefﬁ cient of perfor-
mance (COP) equation:
where
F1 is the ﬂ ow, in gpm, in the chiller evaporator,
T2
T2
T is the temperature of the water entering the evapo-
rator,
T1 is the temperature of the water leaving the evapora-
tor,
F2 is the ﬂ ow, in gpm, in the chiller condenser,
T3 is the temperature of the water entering the con-
denser,
T4
T4
T is the temperature of the water leaving the condenser,
and
W1 is the kilowatt input to the chiller.
In the case of Howard Regional, the COP was determined
for each of the two chillers as follows:
COP (70 ton [300 kW] chiller) =
COP (90 ton (1055 kW) chiller) =
\*
The leaving condenser water temperature (LCW) at How-
ard Regional ranges from 90ºF to 130ºF (32°C to 54°C). As the
LCW temperature goes up, the total heat recovery increases.
However, at these elevated temperatures, the chiller becomes
less efﬁ cient and the kW/ton goes up dramatically, therefore,
the 6.7 COP is actually the worst-case scenario in terms of
overall efﬁ ciency. Generally, the COP for the Howard Medi-
cal DHRC system will range from 6.7 to as high as 11.5. As
condenser temperatures are lowered, the COP increases:
COP (70 ton [300 kW] chiller) =
COP (90 ton [1055 kW] chiller) =
These COPs are as much as four times higher than a tra-
ditional boiler/chiller system. Even though they are based
on the full heat being used (not always the case), the system
has, at the very least, comparable COPs to modern cooling
systems designed for maximum efﬁ ciency.
This evaluation does not include other savings, including
fan or pump energy that would be required to eject the
condenser heat to a cooling tower or closed cooler fan. The
DHRC design might also lower the operational cost of the
cooling tower by reducing the amount of water treatment
and makeup water needed.
The heating and cooling capacities that could be used by
a particular system are a determinate of the actual COP
achieved. Depending on the application, the COP could be as
high as 11 or as low as 3. Proper engineering design calcula-
tions with real-time operations are necessary to determine
the actual system COP.
Coefﬁ cient of Performance
selected instead of a cooling tower or a closed-circuit cooler
because of the poor water quality in Kokomo, which would
have made chemical treatment very expensive. The facility
also avoids the cost of makeup water that would have been
required by a traditional evaporative cooling tower system,
making the system more environment friendly.
Lower Installed Cost Without Penalty
As expected, the reversed heating and cooling system had a
signiﬁ cant impact on the installed cost of the system, resulting
in a 10% reduction in cost to the owner, even with the higher
cost of the DHRC (Table 2 ).
Operationally, the system has proved itself to be equally efﬁ cient
overall to a traditional VAV system. This conclusion is based on a
12-month energy comparison between a simulated VAV design for
Howard Regional and the actual installed system. (Table 1).
While the electrical costs were approximately 32% higher
on the actual DHRC system vs. the simulated VAV system, the
substantial savings in gas (more than $15,000 annually) fully
offset this cost, showing the DHRC system to have a breakeven
efﬁ ciency with the more expensive VAV system.
This data was collected after 12 months of unsupervised
operation without any particular tweaking of the system to help
minimize electrical use. In all likelihood, ongoing commission-
ing would improve electrical efﬁ ciency.
Assuming a facility has year-round simultaneous heating and
cooling loads, a coefﬁ cient of performance analysis will help deter-
mine if this approach is suitable for a given project (see sidebar).
\* The heating and cooling capacities that could be used by a particular system are a determinate of the actual COP achieved. Depending on the application, the COP could
be as high as 11 or as low as 3. Proper engineering design calculations with real-time operations are necessary to determine the actual system COP.

Beyond the Savings
The 10% installed savings without operational penalty
t t
(thanks to the efﬁ ciency of the DHRC) present a limited
snapshot of the overall advantages of the system. For Howard
Regional, many more beneﬁ ts exist:
Expandability. As is the case with most institutional facili-
ties, it was not a matter of if loads would increase at Howard
f
Regional but when. Before construction was completed, plans
existed to remodel the basement and add more equipment to
the disaster recovery data center, further increasing the need
for cooling. A high likelihood of increased internal loads
exists simply by the nature of the data center and continual
need for additional servers, etc. Therefore, it was critical to
the owner that the HVAC system be designed for easy, low-
cost expansion.
In terms of expandability, the chosen design was advanta-
geous. The centrally distributed chilled water loop makes
for convenient availability
and/or ﬂ exibility to address
increased internal loads. The
small footprint and modulari-
ty of the DHRC chillers make
it possible to add modules to
meet increasing loads at rela-
tively minimal cost. It also
allows Howard Regional to
purchase a minimum amount
of chiller capacity, keeping
installed cost down.
Space Efﬁ ciency. The de-
sign allowed for tremendous
economy of space. Prior to the renovation, the existing
penthouse had no central cooling system, and only a single
AHU. After the renovation, the same penthouse housed all the
facility’s heating, cooling and ventilating equipment—with
space left over for additional DHRC chillers.
Practically speaking, the reverse heating and cooling system
simpliﬁ ed very typical stumbling blocks for designers, includ-
ing lower ﬂ oor-to-ﬂ oor heights often found in older buildings
and smaller fan rooms due to lower ventilation requirements
in past years.
Compliance. ANSI/ASHRAE/IESNA Standard 90.1-2001,
Energy Standard for Buildings Except Low-Rise Residential
Buildings, prohibits the simultaneous operation of heating
and cooling systems. As with air or waterside economizing,
the DHRC approach makes it possible to manage concurrent
heating and cooling loads without requiring simultaneous gas
and electric utilities to heat and cool.
This method may also be
more efﬁ cient than airside
economizing, especially in
humid environments found
in the Southeast. This is be-
cause it costs less to produce
heating energy used for de-
humidiﬁ cation using DHRC
than it does boilers, which
are typically the source of
heating for reheat. In shoul-
der seasons, when the outside
air is 60°F to 70°F (16°C to
21°C) with very high humid-
DHRC Two-Pipe
FP VAV
Chillers
$105,000
$57,000
Boilers
$25,000
$25,000
Ductwork
$62,500
$140,000

$150,000
$105,000
Main Air Handler
$57,500
$115,000

—
$40,000

$40,000
—

$30,000
$30,000

$85,000

$555,000
$602,000
Table 2: Systems installed cost comparison.
October 2005
ASHRAE Journal
35
ity, there is a chance that areas of the
building not requiring sensible cooling
may become humid. However, in this
“cool–heat–cool” cycle application, the
heat recovery allows for no more net
energy use than cool-heat (by boiler)
dehumidiﬁ cation cycles, which is dem-
onstrated in the energy analysis.
Verifiable ventilation. Since the
main AHUs and space fan coils are
constant volume, operators can precisely
document the amount of outdoor air
introduced to the building by simple
balancing techniques accounting for the
worst case ﬁ lter pressure drops. During
partial occupancy, when VFDs slow
the main air handler fans, an accurate
ﬂ ow of outside air is maintained via an
airﬂ ow monitoring station that receives
a signal telling it what the outside air
requirement of the occupied ﬂ oor is. In
a typical VAV system, veriﬁ cation of
ventilation requires a higher (and more
expensive) degree of control technology.
Additionally, controlling economizers
necessitates recalibration of enthalpy
sensors every three to ﬁ ve years.
Sustainability. Because this system is
closed on both the condenser and chilled
water sides of the DHRC, it is much less
vulnerable to fouling—a leading cause
of efﬁ ciency loss in aging systems.
Environment Friendliness. The
DHRC system reduces the waste heat
associated with traditional chiller de-
signs. It also eliminates the pollutants
and gases given up by a boiler. While the
operation of the DHRCs also generates
pollutants at the utility plant, these pol-
lutants are closely monitored—not nec-
essarily the case with facility boilers.
Conclusion
Keeping installed cost down almost
always comes with a penalty. More
often than not that penalty is efﬁ ciency.
By combining DHRC with a unique
cost-saving airside design, the engi-
neering team for Howard Regional beat
those odds. The design was 10% lower
in installed cost but equal in overall
efficiency to that of a VAV system.
In addition, Howard Regional has a
space efficient chilled water system
that can be easily expanded as its
loads inevitably increase. It’s a design
approach worth considering for any
facility with simultaneous heating and
cooling loads, especially in a renovation
situation where space and budget are in
short supply.
References
1. Durkin, T. and B. Rishel. 2003.
“Dedicated heat recovery.” ASHRAE
Journal 45(10):18.
2. Durkin, T. and B. Rishel. 2003.
“Dedicated heat recovery.” ASHRAE
Journal 45(10):24.

50
ASHRAE Journal ashrae.org
October 2005
It’s in the Details
Engineering for Low Cost and High Efﬁ ciency
T
he Electronic Arts Phase II building (EAII) serves as the head-
T
Tquarters for Electronic Arts, a video game developer located
TqT
outside San Francisco. It was completed in 2002. The building is
350,000 ft2 (32 515 m2),four stories tall, and includes ofﬁ ces, an
atrium, cafeteria, child care center, and auditorium. There is noth-
ing particularly “sexy” about the mechanical system—no thermal
storage tanks or ground-coupled heat rejection. Yes, it has many
state-of-the-art features such as underﬂ oor air distribution and a
primary-only, variable ﬂ ow, chilled water plant, but this is not why
EAII earned an ASHRAE Technology Award.
What is remarkable about EAII is that it
is an excellent example of how sound en-
gineering principles and attention to detail
can be combined to arrive at a mechanical
design that is inexpensive and exception-
ally energy efﬁ cient. At a time of soaring
By Jeff Stein, P.E., Member ASHRAE, and Steven T. Taylor, P.E., Fellow ASHRAE
construction costs (think Silicon Valley
during the Dot Com Boom) and skyrock-
eting energy prices (Anyone remember
the California Energy Crisis?), the HVAC
construction cost for EAII came in at an
astonishingly low $13.10/ft2 ($141/m2)
and the utility bills for the ﬁ rst two years
of operation were less than half those of
conventional ofﬁ ce buildings.
Energy Efﬁ ciency
According to a detailed DOE-2 simula-
tion model, this building consumes 39.5%
less energy than a standard California
energy code compliant building. Per the
U.S. Green Building Council’s Leader-
ship in Energy and Environmental De-
sign® (LEED) point interpolation table,
the building exceeds ANSI/ASHRAE/
About the Authors
Jeff Stein, P.E., is a senior engineer, and Steven
T. Taylor, P.E., is a principal at Taylor Engineering
in Alameda, Calif. The authors won a 2005 Tech-
nology Award, Honorable Mention, in Category I,
Commercial Buildings (New).
© 2005, American Society of Heating, Refrigerating and Air-Conditioning Engineers, Inc. (www.ashrae.org). Reprinted by permission from
ASHRAE Journal, (Vol. 47, No. 10, October 2005). This article may not be copied nor distributed in either paper or digital form without ASHRAE’s
permission.
October 2005
ASHRAE Journal
51
IESNA Standard 90.1, Energy Standard for Buildings Except
Low-Rise Residential Buildings, by almost 50%. The DOE-2
results were used to qualify the building for a $200,000 utility
rebate (the maximum possible amount).
Utility data have conﬁ rmed that the building is operating
exceptionally efﬁ ciently. Phase I (two large ofﬁ ce buildings
completed in 1998) and Phase II (this building) share a single
electric utility meter. They are also similar in terms of size,
occupancy type, etc. except that most areas of Phase II operate
continuously due to the often nocturnal nature of the video game
programmers. Despite these extended hours, comparing bills
before and after Phase II (assuming that Phase I energy use is
roughly constant) demonstrates that Phase II uses about half the
electricity as Phase I on a unit area basis. Phase I and Phase II
are on separate natural gas meters and Phase II is using about
one-third as much gas as Phase I on a unit area basis. Energy
usage was low enough to qualify the Phase II building for an
EPA Energy Star score of 88 out of 100.
Design Features
The following is a partial list of the energy-efﬁ ciency fea-
tures that account for the exceptional energy performance of
the building:
• Underﬂ oor air-distribution system.The top three ﬂ oors are
served by an underﬂ oor air distribution system with variable
speed fan terminal units for perimeter heating. This system
reduces supply fan energy by eliminating much of the supply
air duct system and all VAV boxes, improves economizer per-
formance by increasing supply air temperatures, and reduces
reheat energy losses by allowing very low minimum airﬂ ow
setpoints and very warm supply air temperatures.
• High-efﬁ ciency chillers. Chillers were selected during the
design development stage using a life-cycle cost bid process
based on detailed DOE-2 energy simulations of multiple
chiller options provided by three major chiller vendors. The
option with the lowest life-cycle cost consisted of two 315
ton (1108 kW) variable speed driven centrifugal chillers and
one 75 ton (264 kW) “pony” screw chiller. The pony chiller
improves off-hour plant efﬁ ciency since the plant runs con-
tinuously to serve a small data center.
• High-efﬁ ciency cooling towers with variable speed driven
fans. As with the chillers, cooling towers were selected using
a life-cycle cost performance bid. The towers with the low-
est life-cycle cost were propeller draw-through type towers
with an efﬁ ciency exceeding 75 gpm/hp (6.34 L/s per kW)
(at Standard 90.1 rating conditions) and with a 7°F (4°C)
approach.
• High chilled water ∆T.
T.
T Coils were selected to provide an
average 17°F (8°C) temperature difference. This reduces
pump energy costs and ﬁ rst costs (smaller piping, pumps,
The Electronic Arts Phase II building outside San Francisco exceeds Standard 90.1 by almost 50%.
52
ASHRAE Journal ashrae.org
October 2005
the fan terminal. In heating mode, the fan runs at minimum
speed (about 15%) until the supply air temperature is 130°F
(54°C). Only then is fan speed ramped up to 30%. Even at
this speed, reheat energy is reduced compared to conventional
systems due to the warm primary supply air temperatures
(~65°F [~18°C]).
• Sequenced economizer dampers. Air handler outside air
and return air dampers are sequenced rather than modulated
simultaneously to reduce pressure drop through the mixing
box during economizer operation.
• Low-pressure drop return air system with variable speed
propeller relief fans. The return air system has a very low
pressure drop since there are no ceilings or return air ductwork
(return air is drawn into architectural return air shafts through
large grilles at each ﬂ oor). The ﬁ rst stage of economizer relief
is simply to open the motorized backdraft dampers on the relief
fans. Trend logs show that the relief fans almost never run even
in 100% outdoor air economizer mode.
• Partially conditioned atrium with radiant heat/cool ﬂ oor
slab and natural ventilation.
• Server rooms cooled by VAV boxes instead of fan-coils.This
design takes advantage of economizer free cooling and the
higher fan efﬁ ciency of central air handlers versus small fan-
coils. Normal VAV zones served by the air handler are shut off
during unoccupied periods allowing central fans to operate at
very low pressure drop, speed, and power to serve 24/7 loads.
• Commissioning. Perhaps the most important energy-efﬁ ciency
feature is the fact that a thorough commissioning process was
executed, including a full battery of prefunctional and functional
test performed by the contractor followed by several days of
functional tests and multiple rounds of post-occupancy trend
review performed by the design engineers. These tests were
able to conﬁ rm that all control sequences were successfully
Figure 1: This shows the cascading control loops used to control the underﬂ oor control dampers.
pump motors, and variable speed
drives).
• Primary-only, variable flow,
chilled water distribution system
with variable speed pumps. This
system saves ﬁ rst cost and energy
compared to a conventional pri-
mary/secondary system.
• Chilled water differential pres-
sure setpoint reset and tempera-
ture setpoint reset. Chilled water
valve position is used to reset in
sequence the chilled water differen-
tial pressure setpoint and the chilled
water supply temperature setpoint.
This reduces chiller energy during
low load periods and pump energy
during all other load conditions.
• Variable ﬂ ow hot water distri-
bution with reverse return and
multiple distributed risers.
• Duct static pressure setpoint re-
set. Static pressure setpoint for the underﬂ oor central supply
fans is reset so that one underﬂ oor static pressure control
damper remains nearly wide open. This allows the setpoint
to drop at low loads, reducing fan energy by about 50%
compared to a ﬁ xed setpoint.1 Similarly, the static pressure
for the overhead VAV systems is reset so that one VAV box
remains nearly wide open.
• Underﬂ oor plenum pressure reset. Figure 1 shows the
cascading control loops used to control the underﬂ oor control
dampers. The underﬂ oor plenum on each ﬂ oor is divided into
four isolation areas with underﬂ oor demising partitions. Air is
injected into the ﬂ oor through modulating control dampers (in
some cases modulating ﬁ re/smoke dampers located at the duct
shaft). Average monitored interior zone space temperature is
maintained at setpoint by resetting the underﬂ oor static pres-
sure setpoint in the range of 0 in. w.g. to 0.1 in. w.g. (0 Pa to
25 Pa). The control dampers are then modulated to maintain
the underﬂ oor static pressure at setpoint. One reason for the
cascading control loop is that it maintains the plenum positive
relative to the space to prevent perimeter fan terminals from
drawing air backwards through the swirl diffusers. Some other
underﬂ oor designs require a relatively high ﬁ xed underﬂ oor
pressure (e.g., 0.1 in. w.g. [25 Pa]) to provide sufﬁ cient pres-
sure for perimeter zone cooling dampers. One problem with
such an approach is that lightly loaded interior zones can be
overcooled. With perimeter variable speed fan terminals and
the cascading control loop, this problem is eliminated.
• Variable speed underﬂ oor fan terminals with ECM mo-
tors. Figure 2 shows the control sequence for the underﬂ oor
fan boxes. One innovative feature of this sequence is that
reheat is drastically reduced compared to conventional VAV
control. In deadband, the fan is shut off and minimum ventila-
tion is supplied by the plenum pressure pushing air through

Figure 3: Static pressure setpoint reset.
implemented. Figure 3, for example, illustrates that the
underﬂ oor duct static pressure reset sequences are working
quite well. During this period, the setpoint was reset from
0.9 in. w.g. down to 0.15 in. w.g. (224 Pa down to 37 Pa)
based on damper position. The actual static pressure closely
tracked the setpoint.
Cost Effectiveness
Two basic approaches were used to keep mechanical costs
down: value engineering and detailed coordination. Value
engineering, in this case, meant using sound engineering
judgment to eliminate the “little things” that so often add
up to big mechanical costs. Here are some examples of
value engineering:
• No return air ductwork;
• 24/7 loads served by VAV boxes, rather than computer room
AC units;
• Extremely close-coupled chilled water plant. The cooling
towers are approximately 15 ft (4.6 m) from the condenser
water pumps, which are 5 ft (1.5 m) from the chillers, which
are 12 ft (3.7 m) from the chilled water pumps;
• High-temperature differences in both cooling and heating
systems, which reduces ﬂ ow rates. Piping was also sized
based on a detailed engineering and life-cycle cost analysis
which resulted in piping close to the pumps (where available
differential pressures are high) to be signiﬁ cantly smaller than
conventional practice.
• Elimination of all manual balancing devices and automatic ﬂ ow
limiting valves in the chilled and hot water systems. Research
has shown that two-way control valves are essentially self-bal-
ancing, particularly with a reverse-return hot water system.2
In addition to engineering judgment, detailed simulation and
life-cycle cost analyses were performed on many of the design
choices including, chillers, cooling towers, variable speed
drives, underﬂ oor fan terminal type, boiler type (condensing,
atmospheric or forced draft), boiler ﬂ ue dampers, and CO2
controls. One of the options evaluated was the use of distributed
hot water risers as opposed to the more common approach of a
single central hot water riser with horizontal distribution. Ac-
cording to the mechanical contractor (both options were bid as
alternates), the distributed riser approach reduced the ﬁ rst cost
by $300,000 compared to a central riser approach.
Detailed coordination meant more than just an “airtight” set
of mechanical plans and speciﬁ cations. It also meant thorough
coordination with other trades so that responsibilities were clear
and there were no conﬂ icts in the ﬁ eld.
For example, careful coordination with the architect and ﬂ oor
contractor was vital to ensure that the supply riser takeoffs on each
ﬂ oor were exactly lined up with the raised ﬂ oor pedestals so that no
unnecessary duct offsets were required. Coordination also meant
maintaining good relationships with the contractors and soliciting
and accepting their ideas on how to cut costs and improve quality.
The general contractor and the mechanical contractor deserve as
much credit as anyone for the success of the project based on their
construction skills, management experience and coordination ef-
forts. They did an excellent job of being team players and avoiding
the adversarial relationships that too often develop on construction
projects. This extra level of coordination and cooperation by the
design team and the construction team resulted in a job that was on
time and had essentially no mechanical contractor change orders,
other than those resulting from owner changes in scope.
The ultimate indicator of the cost effectiveness of the HVAC
design was the project cost. The mechanical cost for this project
was $6.88/ft2 ($74.06/m2) for the core and shell, and $6.22/ft2
($66.95/m2) for the tenant improvements for a total HVAC
construction cost of $13.10/ft2 ($141.01/m2), which was far less
than the cost of typical code-minimum HVAC systems in San
Francisco Bay Area ofﬁ ce buildings at that time (~ $15/ft2 to
$20/ft2 [$160/m2 to $215/m2] total HVAC construction cost).
Conclusion
The HVAC system design at Electronic Arts II has proven to
be energy efﬁ cient and cost effective. The success of the project
had as much to do with the application of old fashioned ideas
like attention to detail, teamwork and follow through as it did
with the application of state-of-the-art technology.
References
1. Hydeman, M., J. Stein. 2003. “A fresh look at fans.” Heating/Pip-
ing/Air Conditioning Engineering May.
2. Taylor, S., J. Stein. 2002. “Balancing variable ﬂ ow hydronic
systems.” ASHRAE Journal 44(10).

ASHRAE Journal ashrae.org

About the Authors
By Michael Lubliner, Member ASHRAE, John Andrews, Ph.D., Member ASHRAE,
and David Baylon, Associate Member ASHRAE
Michael Lubliner is a building science special-
ist at the Energy Program, Washington State
University in Olympia, Wash. John Andrews,
Ph.D., is a guest scientist at Brookhaven Na-
tional Laboratory in Upton, N.Y., and is chair
of ASHRAE Technical Committee 6.3, Central
Forced Air Heating and Cooling Systems. David
Baylon is president of Ecotope in Seattle.
vidence exists1 that the performance
of forced air heating systems with
heat pumps, as actually installed
with ductwork, controls, etc., may differ
markedly from what would be expected
on the basis of standardized tests of the
equipment only. These differences are
usually in the direction of lower than
expected efﬁciency.
Manufacturers of heat-pump equipment
are not responsible for inefﬁcient duct
design, inappropriate sizing, improper
refrigerant charge, inadequate airﬂow, or
inefﬁcient control strategies that cause
resistance backup heat to be used when
it is not needed. Nevertheless, all of these
factors and others as well can cause system
performance to lag behind what might
be expected on the basis of standardized
ﬁgures of merit such as heating seasonal
performance factor (HSPF).
A convergence of interest among state
energy agencies and electric utilities in
the Paciﬁc Northwest has launched six
complementary research projects that
are investigating the relative importance
and frequency of occurrence of system
level problems, such as those mentioned
previously, in heat pumps in the heating
mode. The motivation of this research is
that the state of knowledge for the heat-
ing mode lags well behind that for the
cooling mode.
Utility and state-sponsored residen-
tial energy conservation programs in
the Paciﬁc Northwest historically have
provided incentives for the installation
of high-efﬁciency heat pumps. Roughly
10,000 heat pumps are installed each
year—half in new construction (manu-
factured and site-built homes) and half
in existing homes.2 These heat pumps
typically replace electric resistance base-
board heat and/or older less efﬁcient heat
pump system.
E
© 2005, American Society of Heating, Refrigerating and Air-Conditioning Engineers, Inc. (www.ashrae.org). Reprinted by permission from
ASHRAE Journal, (Vol. 47, No. 10, October 2005). This article may not be copied nor distributed in either paper or digital form without ASHRAE’s
permission.
October 2005
ASHRAE Journal
37
System Level Issues Classiﬁcation
It may be useful to consider the various ways that system
level issues can develop. The term system level issue is broadly
deﬁned here to mean any problem or concern that involves
more than one component or that arises after the delivery of
the components to the job site. System level issues can fall into
any of four categories:
• Inappropriate matching of components to one another or
to the heating load;
• Suboptimal control strategies;
• Out-of-range values for parameters under the control of
the installer; and
• Inadequate system maintenance.
Component Selection. The main system level issues in this
category involve selection and sizing of the equipment and the
sizing, routing, sealing, and insulation of the ducts.
The problems caused in the cooling mode by oversizing of
air conditioners and heat pumps are well documented. The
situation for the heating mode is less clear. It has been argued
that overall heating system efﬁciency can be improved by using
a larger capacity heat pump, because then the balance point
temperature will be lower and less electric resistance backup
heat will be needed. However, oversizing heat pumps can
increase duct and equipment cycling losses, especially
when connected to ducted thermal distribution system
located in unconditioned spaces such as crawlspaces,
attics and/or garages. Investigations related to sizing
issues and duct system cycling losses are needed.
As for duct design, a considerable body of research
over the past two decades has quantiﬁed the beneﬁts
of reducing thermal losses caused by leakage of air
from ducts to the outside and by heat transfer through
the walls of ducts located in unconditioned spaces. This
is especially critical for systems using heat pumps because
their delivered air temperature is typically lower than that in
furnace-based systems.
Besides reducing system efﬁciency, duct losses can exacer-
bate thermal comfort problems caused by the feeling that cold
air is coming from the registers. Heating contractors often refer
to this as cold blow. Locating registers in areas where air is
discharged directly onto occupants and/or improperly selected
registers also can contribute to “cold-blow” complaints.
Control Strategy. Except in relatively warm climates, all
air-to-air heat pumps must rely on some form of backup heat
when outdoor temperatures drop enough to produce heating
loads greater than what the compressor can supply. Almost all
systems installed in the United States use electric resistance
coils installed in the duct to provide the needed supplemental
heat as well as emergency backup heat in case of compressor
failure. Because the efﬁciency of such resistance heating is
low in comparison with that of the compressor system, it is
important to minimize its use.
Control strategy issues can arise in connection with periodic
changes in the setpoint temperature (e.g., night setback) and also
in certain installation practices intended to reduce the number
of customer callbacks. A prime example of such a practice
is to set up the system with one electric resistance element
always coming on whenever the thermostat calls for heat. This
is sometimes done to forestall the perception of cold blow by
raising the delivered air temperature to a level that feels warm
when one’s hand is placed near a register.
Detailed analysis is required to quantify the precise impacts of
various thermostat night setback control strategies for a variety
of heat pump types, climates. A control strategy that proves
beneﬁcial under some conditions might be counterproductive
in other applications of the same heat pump.
ASHRAE Handbook—HVAC Systems and Equipment Chapter
9, “Design of Small Forced Air Heating and Cooling Systems
states3 “Thermostats with programmable temperature control
allow occupants to vary the temperature setpoint for different
periods. These devices can save substantial energy by applying
automatic night and daytime temperature setback for all systems
when used by occupants and appropriately matched to the HVAC
system. With heat pumps, however, night setback of morning
setup can signiﬁcantly increase supplemental heating.4,5,6,7
System Setup. It is normally assumed that a heat pump
will be installed with the correct level of refrigerant charge.
Improper charge has been identiﬁed as an important cause of
poor performance in the cooling mode. Less has been
known about the effect and the frequency of refrigerant
charge problems in the heating mode.
Airﬂow is not as much under the installer’s control
as refrigerant charge, constrained as it is by the air-
moving capacity of the central system fan in compari-
son with the resistance to ﬂow of the duct system and
of coils, ﬁlters, humidiﬁers, and other devices placed
in the airstream. Nevertheless, some control is usually
possible through the provision of multiple speed settings
on the fan and balancing dampers in the ducts.
Maintenance of System Performance Over Time. As
with most things mechanical, heat-pump systems require
maintenance. Periodic cleaning or ﬁlter replacement often is
neglected. Leakage of refrigerant charge and degradation of
duct tape often escape notice as long as the system is able to
maintain the setpoint temperature.
Ongoing Research in the Paciﬁc Northwest
Contractor/Distributor Interviews. A series of 40 inter-
views of HVAC contractors and distributors8 in a broad re-
gional market was conducted to help assess current installation
practices, equipment selection, control of resistance heat, and
customer perceptions of cold-blow. At the time of this writing,
results of the study were expected to be available in fall of
2005. Two related anecdotal comments9 highlight real-world
issues for some heat pump installers. “The relationship between
a heat pump and its homeowner is a complex one.” “Blowing
85°F (29°C) air on somebody and telling them it’s heating their
home is a hard sell.”
Billing Analysis. This study10 involved a utility billing analysis
of 1,400 homes before and after conversions from electric-resis-
tance heating to heat pumps. Improvement of energy efﬁciency was
generally less than what would have been expected on the basis of

ﬁgures of merit such as HSPF. Analysis of another 500 homes in a
Eugene, Ore., utility program evaluated maintenance and operation
HVAC efforts such as checking of charge and airﬂows. The salient
conclusions were that a program of checking and tuning produced
a measurable energy savings, but that addition of refrigerant charge
testing added little to the heating savings.
High-Efﬁciency Heat Pump Demonstrations. Two sepa-
rate studies involved the installation of high-efﬁciency heat
pumps in a total of eight homes in Oregon. One project11 is
testing (in four homes) a cold-climate heat pump design that
includes dual compressors and performs well at low outside air
temperatures. At lower outdoor temperatures, the heat pump
uses a second booster compressor, in series with the primary
compressor, to provide a sizeable increase in heat energy out-
put. At the more extreme low outdoor temperatures (below
20°F [–7°C]), the unit uses an economizer to reclaim the,
Advertisement formerly in this space.
October 2005
ASHRAE Journal
39
otherwise wasted, heat from the liquid line. This technology
has the capability of providing both signiﬁcant energy sav-
ings and demand reduction. The study intends to determine
whether the technology can reliably provide the high efﬁciency
the manufacturer claims with minimal use of auxiliary heat.
The Bonneville Power Administration plans to collect data
from the six houses through spring of 2006 and report results
shortly thereafter.
The other project12 is using three different types of high-
efﬁciency heat pumps with innovative control strategies in
four new installations with an older system as a control.
Factors found to degrade system efﬁciency included use
of resistance heat when it wasn’t needed, unnecessarily
inefﬁcient defrost control, short heating cycles, and duct
leakage. Signiﬁcantly, all but the last are controls problems.
It appears that suboptimal control, whether in the strategy

ASHRAE Journal ashrae.org
October 2005
or in the implementation, can reduce efﬁciency by 50% in
some cases.
At one central Oregon site, before controls were setup for
electric resistance elements to operate when the supply air
temperature did not reach 85°F within a four-minute period
after the heating cycle starts. COP increased dramatically af-
ter the discharge supply air temperature sensor was disabled.9
Full analysis of the data taken in both these projects over the
2004 – 05 heating season is expected to yield further insights.
Preliminary Laboratory Investigation. A research group
in Washington teamed with the mechanical engineering depart-
ment of a major Midwestern university to conduct a preliminary
laboratory investigation.13 The goal was to identify, for a typical
HSPF 7.0 heat pump, the optimal operating performance in the
heating mode over a range of refrigerant charge levels, indoor
airﬂows, outdoor operating conditions, and expansion devices.
Field and laboratory data, along with simulation models were
used to access the impact of installation and equipment factors
on performance and to quantify optimal energy efﬁciency.
The main conclusions of this investigation were the effects
of refrigerant charge were generally not large and some minor
effects were seen with airﬂows below 275 cfm/ton (36.9 L/s
per kW) (although airﬂow measurements in this ﬂow range
during the tests were problematic). Figures 1a and 1b show
the relationship between COP and airﬂow at three outdoor
temperature conditions for both ﬁxed metering 1a and TXV
1b systems with points labeled by percent of full refrigerant
charge. Figures 2a and 2b show the relationship between COP
and refrigerant charge at three outdoor temperature conditions
for ﬁxed metering 2a and TXV 2b systems with points labeled
by air ﬂow rate. Undercharge had some effect at 47°F (8°C)
but not at the lower temperatures.
Field Study. This study involved ﬁeld investigations in 160
existing homes in the four utility areas in Oregon and Wash-
ington State.14 Field tests included duct and building envelope
leakage measurements, estimates of overall heat loss rate,
HVAC charge levels, indoor coil airﬂow rates, characterization
of indoor and/or outdoor resistance heating controls, and an
occupant survey. This research combines a house/duct audit
with evaluation of the heat pump. The ﬁeld sites are selected at
random in speciﬁc utility service territories and include those
involved and not involved in utility heat pump programs. Data
collected will be used to model and estimate performance of
utility heat pump incentive programs.
Results are preliminary but suggest that opportunities for
improving system performance will result from veriﬁcation of
Advertisement formerly in this space.
42
ASHRAE Journal ashrae.org
October 2005
distribution system performance (ducts), optimization of system
controls (thermostats) and veriﬁcation of evaporator airﬂow.
The median measured supply leakage fraction, which is the ratio
of exterior supply duct leakage estimated at operating conditions
to air handler ﬂow is about 9%, similar to results found in recent
NETL research.15 This is the most critical duct performance ﬁgure
in heating dominated climates, such as the Paciﬁc Northwest.
Median measured evaporator airﬂow is 340 cfm/ton (46 L/s
per kW). The bottom quartile of airﬂow in the study starts at
292 cfm/ton (39 L/s per kW), so a signiﬁcant number of cases
exist with ﬂows low enough to noticeably affect capacity.
Refrigerant charge also has received a great deal of attention.
In this study, preliminary refrigerant charge results suggest only
a fraction of cases (about 10%) undercharged.
Two-thirds of heat pumps had pro-
grammable electronic thermostat con-
trols that minimizes back up heat during
morning recovery, (provided occupants
leave them alone). About a third had
operating outdoor thermostats that
lockout electric resistance heat. The
most common setpoint range found
was 35°F to 40°F (2°C to 4°C). Many
Paciﬁc Northwest utility incentive pro-
grams encourage the use of an outdoor
thermostat set to lock out resistance heat
above 35°F (2°C), regardless of type of
indoor thermostat. Further evaluation of
heat pump thermostat controls for utility
program speciﬁcation is ongoing.
ASHRAE Efforts
All of the factors considered previ-
ously—component interactions, com-
missioning, control strategy, and main-
tenance—may be improved with ad-
ditional knowledge. Several technical
committees (TCs) within ASHRAE are
considering heating-mode performance
of heat-pump systems in their research
plans. These include TC 6.3, Central
Forced Air Heating and Cooling Sys-
tems; TC 8.11, Unitary and Room Air
Conditioners and Heat Pumps; and TC
9.5, Residential and Small Building
Applications. One project under con-
sideration would extend the laboratory
investigations of a standard heat pump
(as discussed previously) to the new gen-
eration of high-efﬁciency heat pumps
being developed in response to new fed-
eral HVAC efﬁciency standards.16 This
effort will help utilities and others make
informed decisions on such questions
as to the extent refrigerant charge and
airﬂow veriﬁcation should be included in
their heat pump incentive programs.
Summary of Preliminary Results
Preliminary analysis by those in-
volved in the Paciﬁc Northwest heat
Advertisement formerly in this space.
October 2005
ASHRAE Journal
43
pump research efforts suggests that
energy savings from heat pumps over
electric resistance heating in the Paciﬁc
Northwest is less than what would be
estimated by using HSPF as tested in
the laboratory. These reductions appear
to be primarily as a result of:
• Inefﬁcient control and inappropriate
use of electric resistance supple-
mental heat;
• Thermal distribution system inef-
ﬁciency (i.e., duct air leakage and
conduction losses);
• Poor control of the defrost cycle;
• Short cycling; and
• Inadequate airﬂow.
Analysis of data from ongoing or
recently completed tests should pro-
vide greater insight into the relative
importance of each of these factors.
These results also should help to guide
future research efforts. Continued ﬁeld
investigations, laboratory testing and
heat pump “whole house” modeling
are needed to better determine how
these factors individually and combined
contribute to overall heat pump perfor-
mance. Speciﬁc modeling efforts related
to sizing issues and duct system cycling
losses are underway.
Acknowledgments
The authors would like to thank the
following organizations who contrib-
uted to the Paciﬁc Northwest heat pump
research efforts: ASHRAE Technical
Committee 6.3 research subcommit-
tee, Bonneville Power Administration
Residential Technical Forum, DeltaT,
Ecotope, Oregon Office of Energy,
Northwest Energy Efﬁciency Alliance
Heat Pump Working Group, Northwest
Power Planning Council, Perdue Univer-
sity, Stellar Processes, U.S. Department
of Energy Building America Program
and WSU Energy Program.

About the Author
W
hile the use of wireless sensing technology appears cut-
ting-edge and interesting, it is unclear where its true value
Wt
W
lies for the building community. What beneﬁ ts can be derived from
advanced sensor technology, whether it is wireless or wired? The
technology must somehow reduce operation costs or provide added
comfort to occupants compared to conventional technologies.
Several discussions of the advantages
and disadvantages of wireless sensor
technology for building applications have
been published recently. Kintner-Meyer
and Brambley1 and Kintner-Meyer, et al.,2
demonstrated wireless sensor technology
by instrumenting an ofﬁ ce building with
30 wireless temperature sensors and by
installing a wireless monitoring system
on a rooftop HVAC unit. Wills3 provides a
recent update of the state of the available
technology and discusses advantages and
disadvantages. It is apparent that building
operators and researchers are still waiting
to see whether these technologies can
simplify building maintenance, forensics,
or control to an extent that will justify the
cost and the learning curve needed for
adopting these technologies.
This article discusses one type of wire-
less sensing platform. The sensor network
monitored the energy performance of a
Lessons Learned in
Wireless Monitoring
residential heat pump system and was
later expanded to monitor the perfor-
mance of the water heating system and
the hygrothermal state of a wall. This
demonstration has shed some light on the
intricacies of deploying wireless sensors
and has shown obstacles that may be
faced by building operators or researchers
in adopting these technologies.
Sensor Network Architectures
Sensor networks and wireless tech-
nologies can be characterized in a number
of ways.4 One way to characterize them is
by considering the arrangement of sens-
ing nodes. Figure 1 shows three possible
architectures that can be used.
The ﬁ rst layout depicts a set of sensors
broadcasting messages to a central re-
By William M. Healy, Ph.D., Member ASHRAE
William M. Healy, Ph.D., is a mechanical engi-
neer at the Building and Fire Research Lab at the
National Institute of Standards and Technology, in
Gaithersburg, Md.
© 2005, American Society of Heating, Refrigerating and Air-Conditioning Engineers, Inc. (www.ashrae.org). Reprinted by permission from
ASHRAE Journal, (Vol. 47, No. 10, October 2005). This article may not be copied nor distributed in either paper or digital form without ASHRAE’s
permission.
October 2005
ASHRAE Journal
55
ceiver in a setup commonly referred to as a star network. A bus
topology is shown in Figure 1b, in which sensors send signals
through a common line to the data collection point. This topology
makes sense for wired communications, but it is not relevant to
wireless data communications.
The third network architecture (Figure 1c)
(F
(
is a mesh network,
which is the one used in this study. In this scenario, sensors
can communicate with all other sensors and can relay messages
from other sensors. A central receiving point is simply another
node in this network that is devoted to collecting the data that
are being passed throughout the network. This architecture
has the potential of allowing individual sensor nodes to by-
pass a central station to transmit data only to those nodes that
use the data, thereby minimizing the transfer of unnecessary
information. The cost and complexity of implementing such a
network, however, becomes unbearable with a wired network
because of the challenges in wiring all sensors together. For this
reason, this topology has only become practical with wireless
communications.
For the current application, this topology was chosen for the
following reasons: ease of installation, ability to be expanded
and self-healing properties.
The mesh topology promised to allow for installation without
expert knowledge of the electromagnetic state of the residence.
As designed, sensors could be dropped in the desired location,
and the network would set itself up automatically to route the
signal to its intended destination regardless of radio frequency
(RF) interference or barriers.
Hardware Description
A commercially available development kit was used to imple-
ment this mesh network. The kit consisted of several develop-
ment boards and software to implement a mesh network. A
node that contains the radio and microprocessors that govern
the transmission and reception of signals is placed on the board.
The radio uses Direct Sequence Spread Spectrum in the 902
MHz to 928 MHz band, and the radio power was set at 4 mW
for this application.
The larger board is a product meant for application develop-
ment that accepts analog or digital sensor input and carries out
operations on these inputs. Line power was used in this applica-
tion because of its availability in places where the sensor boards
would be placed. In this respect, the boards are only partially
wireless. Only the transmission of data occurs in a wireless man-
ner. The application board contains a microprocessor with an
analog-to-digital converter, a multiplexer, eight analog inputs,
a 5V output source, and two interrupts that are used to monitor
counters. Sensors are attached to pins on the boards.
This board was designed for developmental purposes. Smaller
boards could be developed and connected to the wireless node
for applications that require small sizes. It is also recognized
that, since the units were connected to electrical outlets, commu-
nication over power line carriers is also possible, but the focus
here was on the transmission of data in a wireless manner.
An identical application board was used to collect data from
all sensors and relay them to a computer where the data are
stored. The board is connected to a computer through a serial
port and has been programmed to act as a data sink so that all
wireless messages received by the antenna are echoed through
the serial port to the computer.
Software Description
In setting up the sensor network, three distinct software
pieces were needed. First, software was needed to coordinate
communications between the sensors. This software was de-
veloped by the hardware vendor. Development of the second
and third pieces of the software was the responsibility of the
application developer.
The ﬁ rst of these sets of code was written to the sensor node
and handled application level instructions. This code regulated
the acquisition of analog signals from the various sensors
and converted those signals to digital messages containing
environmental information. Measurements were obtained and
messages passed to the central station once every minute. Each
sensor node has a two-symbol designation that is announced
with each message.
Following the sensor identiﬁ er, eight data values are sent
corresponding to each of the eight possible channels available
on the board. If no sensor is attached to a particular channel,
a value of –99.9 is sent in that channel place to indicate the
absence of a signal. A sample message is shown below for data
from a board making two indoor (“ID”) temperature measure-
ments and one relative humidity measurement:
Data from ID: 020.1 019.3 039.2 -099.9 -099.9
-099.9 -099.9 -099.9
This software was developed in the C programming language
on a personal computer and was transferred to the development
board through a cable.
The third piece of software needed for the operation of the wire-
less sensor network was host-based software that compiled the data.
The data collection point was programmed to dump received mes-
sages to a personal computer where they were logged to a text ﬁ le.
Software was written on the host computer to process the data.
The application that was selected for deployment of the wire-
less sensor network was the monitoring of a number of critical
energy consumers in a residence along with the moisture levels
of a wall. The disparate goals were selected to investigate the
potential to examine different building phenomena using a single
sensor network. The installation was also carried out in a manner
to simulate the changing needs of a sensor network in a building.
Therefore, different sensors were installed at different times to
investigate the adaptability of the monitoring scheme.
The investigation was carried out in a three-story townhouse.
Figure 2 shows approximate locations of the wireless nodes used
56

October 2005

D.C.

B. Bus Topology

T
l
C. Mesh Topology
Figure 1: Sensor network topologies. (S = sen-
sor location. D.C. = data collection point.)
in this study. This house had an ofﬁ ce in
a third ﬂ oor bedroom in which the data
acquisition computer was located. A util-
ity room on the ﬁ rst ﬂ oor housed the air
handler, water heater, and wall that was
investigated for moisture issues.
Tasks
1. The ﬁ rst task involved the monitor-
ing of a heat pump/air-conditioner unit.
To determine the duct temperatures and
humidities, thermistors and capacitive
RH probes were installed inside the duct
and wires from these sensors were fed
through the duct to the sensor board.
An energy meter was installed in the air-
handler unit to measure the energy
consumption of the blower fan. No
measurement was made of the energy
consumption of the auxiliary heating ele-
ments in the air handler though the author
acknowledges the fact that the energy
performance of the system in the heating
mode cannot thoroughly be characterized
without this measurement.
Separate boards were placed for de-
termining the indoor temperature and
humidity, the outdoor temperature and
humidity, and the energy consumption
of the heat pump compressor and out-
door fan.
2. Three months after installation of the
sensor network to monitor performance
of the heat pump system, sensors were
installed for a new purpose—to deter-
mine the performance of the water heater.
A ﬂ ow meter was installed on the inlet
piping to the water heater, and thermis-
tors were placed in the piping on the inlet
side and on the outlet side to determine
the water temperature.
Additional temperature sensors were
placed inside the insulation jacket of the
water heater near the upper thermostat
and in the utility room to get an estimate
of the ambient temperature to which
standby heat loss would occur. The
electrical power to the water heater was
monitored with an in-line power meter.
Once the sensor boards were turned on,
the network recognized the sensor data
immediately and seamlessly integrated
these boards in the network.
3. The third application was imple-
mented three months after the water
heater sensors were installed. This appli-
cation involved a slightly different focus
than that of the previous two in which
determining the energy performance of
equipment was the aim.
In this application, the aim was to in-
stall a network of sensors to investigate
the hygrothermal state of a wall. Combi-
nation temperature and humidity sensors
were installed at four locations in a wall
facing the building exterior to determine
if any moisture gradient was evident
in the wall. Once again, these sensors
seamlessly integrated themselves into the
existing wireless sensor network.
Lessons Learned
The sensor installation went relatively
smoothly. The setup of the mesh network
was made straightforward by the commer-
cially available hardware and the accom-
panying software that monitored sensor
communications. Setup was surprisingly
simple, even for engineers without in-
depth knowledge of wireless networking
or RF physics, and the network was easily
expanded. While the environment was not
as challenging as a large industrial setting
because of the lack of substantial metal
obstructions and the relatively small space
involved, some lessons have been learned
regarding the installation of a wireless
sensor network in buildings.
• Need for more sensors incorporated
in wireless boards. In this applica-
tion, signiﬁ cant effort went into the
connection of the sensors to the boards
and the programming to process the
signals from the sensors. Currently, it
does not appear that a wide array of
systems are available that contain the
variety of sensors needed for build-
ing studies. To avoid creating custom
solutions for each application, it would
be beneﬁ cial if manufacturers could
equip their wireless nodes with the
sensors that are needed for building ap-
plications. Sensors of interest include
those that measure quantities such as
temperature, relative humidity, electri-
cal power, water ﬂ ow, gas ﬂ ow, air ﬂ ow,
carbon dioxide concentration, smoke
presence, light intensity, and radiant
energy. Ready-made wireless nodes
would speed the adoption of wireless
sensors in building applications, but it
is acknowledged that sensor manufac-
turers will ﬁ rst need to be conﬁ dent
that a sufﬁ cient market exists for such
products.
• Need for more straightforward ways
to program boards. In developing
the sensor application, a significant
learning curve was encountered when
attempting to program the micropro-
cessors. While many engineers may be
comfortable with such programming,
it is presumed that many others would
prefer to stick with a programming en-
vironment that does not require bit-level
operations. Development of integrated
sensors may eliminate the need for low-
level commands, freeing the application
engineer to focus on the higher level
application programming.
58
ASHRAE Journal ashrae.org
October 2005

townhouse with distances between furthest nodes. Open stars rep-
resent locations of the sensor boards while the closed star shows the
location of the data acquisition node (not to scale).

90

Availability
10/6
