Figure 2: Variable speed secondary pumping (1990).
Mixing Chilled Water
All primary/secondary systems have a mix point at the de-
coupler whenever the secondary ﬂ ow exceeds the primary.
Mixing chilled water is almost never a good idea. It raises the
temperature and increases pump energy. Chilled water supply
temperature has to match the coil selection temperature so
cooling coils can dehumidify properly and so the coils can de-
velop the intended ∆T. Although for many hours in the year the
coldest water won’t actually be required for either temperature
or humidity control, if the system can tolerate warmer water,
the better way is to raise the chiller setpoint.
On a related note, raising chilled water supply temperature
as an operating strategy can increase efﬁ ciency, increase ca-
pacity or lower demand. During the summertime, when rais-
ing supply temperature would save kWh and demand, that’s
when the coldest water is needed to dehumidify properly.
All chillers will be more efﬁ cient at warmer evaporator water
outlet temperatures. For example, a 4°F (2°C) rise in supply
temperature will roughly equal a 3% efﬁ ciency increase, some
of which will be given back by increased pumping energy for
all variable speed distribution systems. Caution: if you’re rais-
ing supply temperature to save demand charges, remember
that although chillers have a 3% lower kW/ton at 4°F (2°C)
higher evaporator temps, they also have 6% greater capacity
and a net increase in total power consumption.

Total Head in Feet
From a pump energy standpoint at full load, little difference
exists from a primary/secondary scheme. At all part loads,
variable primary ﬂ ow will use less energy than any primary/
secondary scheme.
From an operational standpoint, we now have a scheme
that makes low ∆T a nonissue. We can “over-pump” the
T
chiller if we need to, and stage chillers on ﬂ ow and load,
rather than just ﬂ ow. This is a far cry from saying that low ∆T
is solved.
• Installed Cost: 0.867 of baseline.
• Operating Cost: 0.937 of baseline.
• Payback: Immediate.
44
ASHRAE Journal
ashrae.org
November 2005
It is a phenomenon that occurs in most chilled water
systems when the return water coming back to the cooling
plant is not as warm as it is designed to be. For example, if
the design called for chilled water supply to the system to
be 45°F (7°C), and the return to be 55°F (13°C), then any
time return water was 54°F (12°C) or cooler, the plant would
be suffering from low ∆T. Usually worse at part loads, it is
frequently seen at full load also. It deﬁ es the fundamentals of
heat transfer, since theoretically, at all part-load conditions,
the ∆T should be above design rather than below it.
T
Why is Low ∆T a Problem?
T
• It limits the capacity out of your chillers. Generally, the
most expensive piece of equipment in a building is the
chiller. In a primary-secondary pumping arrangement
with the primary pumps sized for the chiller capacity at
What Is Low ∆T?
T?
T
the design ∆T, chiller plants will be limited on ﬂ ow rather
than load unless the ∆T is at design.
T
• It wastes pump energy. In a 10°F (5.5°C) ∆T design, if the
T T
water is coming back at 7°F (4°C) ∆T, then the system
is pumping 30% more than it should, and theoretically
using 220% of the pump energy that it should.
• The piping/pumping system may be unable to meet the
building load.
Possible Causes of Low ∆T
• Dirty coil, air-side or water-side;
• Poor coil selection;
• Changing design conditions;
• Laminar ﬂ ow on waterside;
• Coil unequal air distribution;
• Three-way valves;
• System DP above valve shutoff head;
• Piping conﬁ guration, etc.
• Advantages: Save ﬁ rst cost; save operating cost; low ∆T
a nonissue.
• Disadvantages: Increased control complexity (see “Ghost
Energy” sidebar).
1997: The Good Idea Needed Optimizing
Too many small things popped up that negatively impacted
reliability, control and the ability to ﬁ ne tune the system. Flow
meters did not perform as intended due to water quality issues,
piping layout, and instrument accuracy.
Realizing that the most accurate and well-documented oriﬁ ce
in the mechanical room was the chiller barrel, the solution was
to install a differential pressure transmitter across the evapora-
tor barrel and write a simple algorithm to control the chiller
minimum ﬂ ow valve (Figure 5). This allowed more stable
control, more ﬁ nite control at low ﬂ ow and a small increase in
pump efﬁ ciency.
• Installed Cost: 0.864 of baseline.
• Operating Cost: 0.937 of baseline.
• Payback: Immediate.
• Advantage: Better reliability.
• Disadvantages: None.
1999: Pressure-Independent Flow Control Valves (More
Optimizing)
When designing, a myriad of operating conditions need to be
considered that could affect the system, such as full load, part
load, one pump out, startup, shutdown, etc. One particularly
vexing condition is rapid increases in load.
If the chiller plant were operating with pump speed and
minimum ﬂ ow valve in control, a large air-handling unit start-
ing could cause a sudden increase in demand for chilled water.
This might cause a chiller to trip on low ﬂ ow if the minimum
ﬂ ow valve could not open up fast enough and the pump speed
could not increase fast enough.
The initial solution was to slow down the rate at which
air-handling unit control valves open and close to avoid sud-
den ﬂ ow variations. It was felt that slow acting valves would
eliminate most of the problems. However, a better design would
eliminate all of the nuisance trips, to be as operator friendly
as possible.
After much product research in various valve and actuator
styles, an interesting device with its origins in the oil ﬁ eld in-
dustry appeared most promising. A pressure-independent ﬂ ow
control valve (see sidebar “Pressure Independent”) became
Fl
M
Flow Meter
Chiller
Chiller
Chiller Min.
Flow Valve

l

Return
Coil

Chiller

46
ASHRAE Journal
ashrae.org
November 2005
the standard for chiller minimum ﬂ ow protection, and a big
drop in spurious trips was seen. The piping schematic did not
change from the 1997 version, but operation became much
more stable.
• Installed cost: 0.865 of baseline (increase vs. 1997).
• Operating cost: 0.937 of baseline.
• Payback: Never, but, highly worthwhile.
• Advantage: Better reliability.
• Disadvantage: Slight price increase.
2001 and 2002: Two Changes in Two Different Steps,
Both Involving Cooling Coil Control Valves
First, the pump speed differential pressure transmitter (DPT)
was relocated from across the terminal unit branch main (where it
had been from 1990) to across only the most remote control valve.
The theory is that best control and most energy savings would be
seen by monitoring as close to the control device as possible. And
additional energy savings is possible since, in the original scheme,
the DPT setpoint was typically around 25 ft (75 kPa) (coil = 10 ft
[30 kPa]; control valve = 10 ft [30 kPa]; and valves/strainer/pipe
= 5 ft [15 kPa]). In the new location, the DPT setpoint would only
need to be 10 ft (30 kPa), meaning that the variable speed pumps
would spend more hours running at lower speed.
This was an example of what Albert Einstein called a
“schlembe: an improvement that made things worse.” Without
the dampening effect of the coil, piping and the mains, even small
changes in valve position resulted in wild variations in differential
pressure and constant speed swings at the pumps. As with the
chiller minimum ﬂ ow valve, slowing down the valve response
slowed the DPT swings but could not eliminate them.
Conventional control valves could not control accurately or
respond quickly enough to allow reasonable control, hence the
second improvement. The success that was seen with the pressure-
independent control valves (PICVs) in chiller minimum ﬂ ow
service again was seen when PICVs were installed on all the air
handler coils (Figure 6
(F
(
). In addition, with the control valves actu-
6)
6
ally controlling correctly, cooling coil ∆T was now at or above
T T
design, occasionally signiﬁ cantly above design.
Two things were immediately evident: the pumps could be
accurately and smoothly controlled at a much lower setpoint,
hence more energy savings; and low ∆T no longer existed.
T
All previous efforts were about making low ∆T a non-issue,
T
T
this change solved it. With PICVs on all the cooling coils, the
system ﬁ nally obeyed the fundamentals of heat transfer. At all
part-load conditions, return water is consistently above design,
with no change in room comfort level.
From a ﬁ rst-cost standpoint, PICV are about 50% more than
conventional control valves. First-cost is always a concern, as
owners and construction managers look for value engineering
items. On a 100,000 ft2 (9290 m2) building, PICV’s will add
about $20,000 to the cost, which is 0.16% of the total project
cost and 0.7% of the HVAC package. These certainly are not
signiﬁ cant percentages.
From our energy savings point, the ghost energy was elimi-
nated (see “Ghost Energy” sidebar), and control was much
smoother. However, calculated payback was 12 years, prompt-
ing a reevaluation of other parameters to lessen the ﬁ rst-cost
impact. One suggestion was that since low ∆T was no longer an
T
issue, piping mains could be downsized. Building load diversity
was showing up as much greater ﬂ ow diversity. Resizing and
recalculating the mains is worth about half the premium for the
PICV, bringing the payback to six years.
“Serendipity” is ﬁ nding something good that you weren’t
looking for. So, solving the low ∆T was serendipity. However,
T
it presented another concern. With the PICVs on all our cool-
ing coils, the chilled water ∆T was now consistently at 16°F
T
(9°C) and occasionally in the 20°F range (11°C) (12°F [7°C]
design). Most chiller manufacturers will say that, when used
in a variable primary ﬂ ow system, that chiller minimum ﬂ ow
should be 1.5 gpm/ton (0.03 L/s per kW). That equates to
a chiller ∆T of 16°F (9°C), which means that at many load
T
T
conditions, considerable water is being bypassed when the
chiller was heavily loaded. The chiller industry may need to
better deﬁ ne minimum ﬂ ow so a chiller can run fully loaded
with almost any ∆T, without introducing another kind of ghost
T,
T
energy (unnecessary bypass water).
The ﬂ ow-to-position response of valves (called valve au-
thority) is characterized by ASHRAE as either quick opening,
linear or equal percentage. Almost all valves used for tem-
perature control systems are speciﬁ ed as equal percentage.
Chapter 42 of the ASHRAE Handbook—HVAC Systems and
Equipment shows how ﬂ ow in different valve styles reacts
to changes in position.
It also has a chart illustrating a phenomenon called authority
distortion. The chart shows that any valve will only perform
as advertised if the pressure differential across the valve stays
constant, which almost never happens. From full load to part
load, pressure differential across a control valve can vary sig-
niﬁ cantly. For example, 50% valve position may equal 10%
Pressure-Independent Control Valves
ﬂ ow at design differential, or it may equal 50% ﬂ ow at part
load (authority distortion = 0.1, see S42.9, Figure 19).
A pressure-independent control valve automatically and im-
mediately responds to variations in pressure. It is a relatively
recent innovation for hydronic applications. A perfect analogy
is the variable-air-volume (VAV) box. Years ago, when VAV was
in its infancy (a great idea but inconsistent space control), the
invention that made VAV the smart choice for most modern
buildings was making the VAV boxes pressure independent.
The addition of a ﬂ ow ring in the box inlet meant that the box
could maintain constant airﬂ ow into the space regardless of
anything that was happening on the upstream side. A pressure
independent control valve has a similar device at the inlet.
48
ASHRAE Journal
ashrae.org
November 2005
A condensing boiler is one that is built to withstand the
corrosive conditions of cool ﬂ ue gases. It operates at tem-
peratures signiﬁ cantly cooler and at efﬁ ciencies signiﬁ cantly
higher than would be found in conventional boiler systems.
The most important factor of boiler efﬁ ciency is enter-
ing water temperature (EWT), see Figure 6, p. 27.4, 2004
ASHRAE Handbook—HVAC Systems and Equipment. Most
conventional boilers have a minimum EWT low limit of 140°F
(60°C) to prevent condensation on the ﬁ re side. This is one
of the key factors that has pushed HVAC engineers to the
“standard” 180°F (82°C) hot water systems.
Condensing boilers are, by deﬁ nition, meant to condense,
so that the latent heat from the ﬂ ue gas is sent into the hy-
dronic loop, rather than pushed up the stack. Therefore, a
heating system designed around condensing boilers operating
in the condensing mode all the time (135°F [57°C] maximum
leaving water temperature) will be more efﬁ cient in the boiler
room and throughout the system because parasitic losses are
less. It is the difference between component efﬁ ciency, mea-
sured under ideal conditions, and system efﬁ ciency measured
under actual operating conditions. Payback on condensing vs.
conventional boilers typically is two to four years.
Once the heating plant is designed around the lower water
temperatures, the viability and applicability of heat recovery
chillers operating at 120°F to 130°F (49°C to 54°C) condens-
ing temperatures is evident.
Condensing Boilers and Heat-Recovery Chillers
• Installed Cost: 0.872 of baseline (increase).
• Operating Cost: 0.900 of baseline.
• Payback: Six years.
• Advantage: Better control; energy savings; eliminate most
ghost energy and low ∆T.
• Disadvantage: Slight price increase.
2003: Dedicated Heat Recovery Chillers (DHRC) Became
Standard on All Four-Pipe Projects
Heat recovery chillers are not intended to replace the main cool-
ing plant, only to replace summertime boiler operation. They are an
environmentally conscious way of controlling humidity, decreasing
greenhouse gases, and providing domestic water heat.
The ideal location is where it will see the warmest entering
evaporator temperature, which is before the chiller minimum
ﬂ ow bypass (Figure 7
(F
(
). Typical sizing for a 100,000 ft
7)
7
2 (9290
m2) school might be a 200-ton (703 kW) main cooling plant
and a 30-ton (106 kW) DHRC.
Even though it is called a chiller, its summertime function is
to make hot water, and cooling is a by-product. That reverses in
the winter when it makes chilled water with the rejected heat
as a by-product. This is distinctly different than a heat pump,
which has reversing valves on the refrigerant side. In a DHRC,
the evaporator is always the evaporator; the condenser is always
the condenser. The control parameters differ from summer
(condenser control) to winter (evaporator control).
Consider the operating economics of heat recovery chillers
as applied to any concurrent heating/cooling load (the heating
may include domestic water heating or swimming pool water
heating). The utility rates shown are typical of central Indiana
before Hurricane Katrina.
Option 1: Run boilers and chillers.
Chiller: Air-cooled screw at 1.25 kW/ton (0.36 kW/kW).
Electricity at $0.07/kWh = $0.729/100 MBtu.
Boiler: Condensing boiler at 92% efﬁ cient gas at $0.85/
therm = $0.924/100 MBtu.
Total Option 1 = $1.653 for 100 MBtu of heating and
100 MBtu of cooling.
Option 2: It’s cool enough to run economizers, but there is
still a heating load.
Chiller: Off.
Cooling = $0.00.
Boiler: Condensing boiler at 92% efﬁ cient gas at $0.85/
therm.
Heating = $0.924/100 MBtu.
Total Option 2 = $0.924 for 100 MBtu of heating and
100 MBtu of cooling.
Option 3: Heat recovery chiller.
Chiller: Modular scroll at 1.2 kW/ton (0.34 kW/kW)
(55°F [13°C] Evap EAT, 120°F [49°C] Cond LWT); at

ASHRAE Journal
ashrae.org

$0.07/kWh.

Boiler = Off.

At these utility rates, a couple of interesting conclusions can
be drawn from this.
1. Recovered heat will always be cheaper than any boiler or
water heater arrangement.
2. It is cheaper to run the heat recovery chiller than to run the
economizers if you have a need for the rejected heat.
The potential economic beneﬁ ts of heat recovery have been
known for several generations, but a reliable control arrange-
ment and a way to use the low-grade heat recovered from con-
densers were never easy (see “Condensing Boilers” sidebar).
The operating cost justiﬁ cation comes from eliminating
summertime reheat and minimizing the amount of econo-
mizer operation. As illogical as it sounds, the cooling control
sequences should be rewritten to make DHRC the ﬁ rst call
for cooling instead of economizers for VAV and multizone
systems.
If the facility has an indoor swimming pool, the payback is
immediate, since a pool dehumidiﬁ er (arguably the most ex-
pensive and complicated refrigeration device in any building)
Ghost energy refers to unintended and unnecessary energy
usage such as reheating or overcooling for no psychrometric
or comfort beneﬁ t. At least three kinds of ghost energy exist:
ghost cooling, ghost heating, and ghost pumping. The term
originated with the observation that, if low ∆T occurs (which
T
it does), and if the fundamentals of heat transfer say that it
shouldn’t (which they do), then the Btus must be going some-
where (which they are). A leaky preheat valve, for example,
can create signiﬁ cant ghost heating and cooling demand.
Excess ventilation, which comes with a need to dehumidify
or preheat, is also a kind of ghost energy.
Ghost cooling can be caused by authority distortion of
pressure dependent valves, which appears to be a prime
cause of low ∆T. The actuators, especially on quarter turn
valves, are not ﬁ nite enough to react quickly to variations in
system differential. The result is that VAV leaving air setpoint,
nominally at 55°F (13°C), is now running at 54°F or 53°F (12°C
or 11°C) (ghost cooling). No one in the space will notice the
difference, but there would then be an implied reheat load
(ghost heating).
There is ghost pumping for the additional chilled water and
heating water ﬂ ows along with ghost heating and cooling.
Ghost Energy
is no longer required. The same function and same economy
can be had with conventional air-handling devices.
• Installed Cost: 0.999 of baseline (no pool) or 0.830 (with
pool).
• Operating Cost: 0.800 of baseline.
• Payback: 4.6 years (no pool) or 0.5 years (with pool).
• Advantages: Lower operating cost; some cooling redun-
dancy, some chillers available in winter.
• Disadvantages: Potentially higher ﬁ rst cost; control com-
plexity.
2005
The next generations are already on the drawing boards.
Stay tuned.
Conclusion
In the quest for “less expensive to build,” it may seem that
modern chiller plant design has come full circle. When ad-
justed for inﬂ ation, current chiller schemes cost as much as
15-year-old designs unless a pool is part of the project. “Less
expensive to operate” has been achieved, since it now costs 20%
less to operate a comparable building. The “easier to maintain”
is harder to track and quantify, but it is patently obvious that
newer systems are more complicated.
“Increased control complexity” is a disadvantage of almost
every one of the improvements. Much more is required of
the temperature controls people, and the complexity that is
inherent in a more sophisticated design mandates building
commissioning.

Squeezing the Watts
Out of Fluorescent Lighting
Lighting consumes 33% of electricity used in commercial build-
ings. The next columns will discuss energy-efﬁ cient lighting.
By Brian Liebel, P.E., and James Brodrick, Ph.D., Member ASHRAE
Table 1: Comparison of 4 ft (1.2 m) ﬂ uorescent lamp system efﬁ cacies (systems shown are with optimized ballasts).

99.9
Reduced Wattage T8 – 28 W
28

99.7

25

I
n October’s article, we referred to newer ﬂ uorescent light-
ing technologies that make it relatively easy to comply with
ANSI/ASHRAE/IESNA Standard 90.1, Energy Standard
for Buildings Except Low-Rise Residential Buildings. In gen-
eral, there are three areas that we look at for energy-efﬁ cient
lighting—lighting system efficiency, lighting design, and
lighting controls. Since so much of lighting energy efﬁ ciency
is based on the lighting power density (LPD), or W/ft2, we’ll
start by looking at how lighting system efﬁ ciencies reduce the
LPD in buildings. Future issues will explore improved lighting
design and controls.
To adequately describe lighting system efﬁ ciency, we ﬁ rst
must introduce the components: the lamps, ballasts, and lu-
minaires. The term used to describe the energy efﬁ ciency of a
lamp is efﬁ cacy, or lumens per watt. When we consider lamps
that require ballasts, such as ﬂ uorescent and HID lamps, we
must consider the input watts of the ballast that is driving the
lamp to determine the overall system efﬁ cacy. The lamp/ballast
system determines the overall light output from the lamps, and
the optical system of the luminaire redirects the light into the
space and provides the luminaire with its photometric distribu-
tion. The luminaire efﬁ ciency determines how much of the light
gets out of the luminaire and into the space, but has no regard
for the way the light is distributed. It is an often abused value
and it is generally erroneous to use luminaire efﬁ ciency as a
guide for lighting efﬁ ciency, except when comparing luminaires
of similar photometric distributions.
Lighting design starts with selecting a design approach based
on the speciﬁ c application—an ofﬁ ce is lit differently than a
warehouse. Luminaire selection involves a balance of aesthet-
ics, optical performance to meet visual needs, and cost. Once a
basic design approach is selected, the ﬁ ne-tuning of lamp/ballast
combinations and luminaire placement and distribution/ef-
ﬁ ciencies is done—generally with several iterations to get the
right balance for the project. An energy-efﬁ cient lighting design
is one that optimizes luminaire photometric distribution and
system efﬁ cacy to generate the most visually effective light with
the least amount of energy input. An important added consid-
eration in lighting design is the long-term cost impact of lamp
replacement and system maintenance costs.
The workhorse of commercial interior lighting is ﬂ uorescent
lighting, and new ﬂ uorescent technologies offer signiﬁ cant
improvements in energy efﬁ ciency. At this point, there is noth-
ing to be gained by using T12 lamps in any application. The
T8/electronic ballast systems have proven reliable and cost-ef-
fective for 15 years, and more importantly, provide a minimum
of 25% energy savings over T12 systems. They also offer better
light output retention over the life of the system. T8 technologies
have become rather advanced, and along with these advance-
ments come a variety of products that produce incremental
energy savings beneﬁ ts. For example, ﬁ ve different types of 4
,
y
g,
g
g
g
g
,
(
g)
p
y p
© 2005, American Society of Heating, Refrigerating and Air-Conditioning Engineers, Inc. (www.ashrae.org). Reprinted by permission from
ASHRAE Journal, (Vol. 47, No. 11, November 2005). This article may not be copied nor distributed in either paper or digital form without
ASHRAE’s permission.
November 2005
ASHRAE Journal
53
(
)
p
g
p
;
ft (1.2 m) T8 lamps are available: the high-performance 32 W;
the standard 32 W; and the reduced wattage lamps of 30, 28,
and 25 W. Add to this the different color characteristics, and the
variables in lamp selection become quite a head-spinner.
On the ﬂ uorescent ballast side, three types of electronic
ballasts exist—instant start, programmed start and rapid start.
Within these groups, different ballast factor (BF) ratings and
efﬁ ciency grades of standard and extra-efﬁ cient ballasts exist.
The BF is the factor of rated light output that the lamp/ballast
system will deliver.
All of these products have been developed with one goal in
mind—to increase the system efﬁ ciency of ﬂ uorescent lighting
systems. The following list provides some basic guidelines for
selecting ﬂ uorescent energy-efﬁ cient lighting systems.
1. Always use lamps with 82 CRI or better (800 series lamps),
as opposed to those in the range of 75–78 (700 series lamps).
They are more energy efﬁ cient and provide better color.
2. Use high-performance T8 lamps with extra-efﬁ cient ballasts
to achieve maximum energy efﬁ ciency. The high-performance
lamps are 5% more efﬁ cacious, and the use of extra-efﬁ cient bal-
lasts result in approximately 10% additional energy savings.
3. Maximize energy savings by using the variety of ballast fac-
tors available—use low BF ballasts whenever possible (without
sacriﬁ cing visual performance due to the lower light output), or
p
g
use fewer lamps with higher BFs to achieve lower LPDs.
4. Unless the lighting is controlled by occupancy sensors
that result in a lot of on/off cycles during the day, use instant
start ballasts for optimized energy efﬁ ciency.
5. Be cautious with reduced-wattage T8 lamps as they are
generally only compatible with instant start ballasts, and some
are speciﬁ cally matched with ballasts, such that the lamps
may striate or not operate well with non-conforming ballasts.
These lamps should never be used in environments where the
temperature is below 60°F (16°C), and are not recommended
in spaces controlled by occupancy sensors.
The use of extra-efﬁ cient electronic ballasts and higher efﬁ -
ciency T8 lamps add incremental energy savings of about 15%
beyond standard T8 installations. Here are two examples:
Example 1 is a two-lamp ﬂ uorescent luminaire, balanc-
ing lumen output and BF.
• Common approach: (2) 700 series, 2800-lumen
F32T8 lamps with generic electronic standard BF
(0.88) ballast consume 58 W.
• Efﬁ cient approach: (2) 800 series, High-Performance,
3000+ lumen F32T8 lamps with extra-efﬁ cient low
BF (0.77) ballast consume 48 W.
• The results are practically the same light level and a
17% reduction in energy!
Advertisement formerly in this space.
54
ASHRAE Journal
November 2005

Example 2 involves reducing the number of lamps.
Example 2 involves reducing the number of lamps
• Common approach: A 2x4 troffer with (3) 700 series
2800-lumen F32T8 lamps with generic electronic
standard BF (0.88) ballasts consumes 85 W.
• Efﬁ cient approach: (2) 800 series, high-perfor-
mance, 3000+ lumen F32T8s with extra-efﬁ cient
high BF (1.15) ballast consume 73 W.
• The results are practically the same light level, with
one-third less lamps to purchase, and a 14% reduc-
tion in energy!
Another recent innovation in ﬂ uorescent technology involves
the T5 and T5HO lamps. These lamps are only 5/8 in. (16
mm) in diameter, and are good at delivering luminaire optical
efﬁ ciency. Typical T5 and T5HO lamp/ballast combination
system efﬁ cacies are not as good as the T8 systems. However,
one manufacturer recently introduced a high-performance
T5 that looks promising. The T5 lamp systems use different
sockets and ballasts, and the lamps are shorter than standard
4 ft (1.2 m) lamps, making these systems generally much
more expensive than T8 systems. Their best application is in
indirect pendant-hung lighting systems and some high-bay
applications where high operating temperatures are not a
problem.
In summary, the use of new ﬂ uorescent technologies reduces
In summary the use of new ﬂuorescent technologies reduces
LPDs to around 0.90 W/ft2 (10 W/m2) or less in standard ofﬁ ce
applications, well below the Standard 90.1-2004 of 1.0 W/ft2 (11
W/m2) Other buildings using ﬂ uorescent lighting as their main
source will have similar results—about 10% below the 2004
LPD standards. The incremental costs for the extra-efﬁ cient
ballasts and high-performance T8 lamps are relatively small
and are easily made up through the energy savings alone.
The big news, however, is that there will be additional incen-
tive to use these technologies in 2006. Section 1331 of the U.S.
Energy Policy Act of 2005 includes tax credits for equipment
placed into service after Dec. 31, 2005, equipment that reduces
building LPDs by 25% to 40% percent under Standard 90.1-2001
levels. That means the LPD must be between 0.78 to 0.98 W/ft2
(8.4 to 10.6 W/m2) for ofﬁ ces. As noted earlier, this is easily ac-
complished by using these new and improved technologies. The
added factors of improved design techniques and lighting controls
will be covered in upcoming issues… stay tuned!
Brian Liebel, P.E., is principal of AfterImage + Space in
Emeryville, Calif. He is a member of IESNA. James Brodrick,
Ph.D., is a project manager, Building Technologies Program,
U.S. Department of Energy, Washington, D.C.

ASHRAE Journal
ashrae.org
December 2005
T
he chilled water system at Tower Semiconductor, a pure-play
independent wafer foundry in Israel, is a primary-secondary
variable ﬂ ow system consisting of six centrifugal chillers. One chiller
is 1,000 ton (3517 kW) and the other ﬁ ve are 500 ton (1758 kW).
The heat recovery system is a variable primary ﬂ ow.
About the Author
Igor Dubov, P.E., is the HVAC and mechanical
system supervisor at Tower Semiconductor, Migdal
Haemek, Israel.
After the successful completion of
the ﬁ rst stage of the chilled water plant
retroﬁ t project,¹ the facility department
entered the second phase. During the ﬁ rst
part, the emphasis was on the efﬁ cient
operation and proper maintenance of
the chillers, whereas the purpose of the
second phase was the improvement of the
heat recovery system and the auxiliary
equipment energy consumption.
Previous System Operation
Because of the requirements of hu-
midity control in the cleanrooms, hot
water, for reheat, becomes indispensable
throughout the entire year.
Tower’s biggest 1,000 ton (3517 kW)
lead chiller, which was constructed with
an auxiliary condenser for heat recovery,
works continuously.
In 2002, this chiller worked 8,625
hours (98.4% of the entire year); in 2003,
8,692 hours (99.2% of the entire year);
and in 2004, 8,468 hours (96.4% of the
entire year) supplying chilled and hot
water simultaneously. This chiller works
in tandem with a dedicated cooling tower
(50 hp [37.3 kW]).
Even in the minimal cooling load
requirements, the heat rejected from the
chiller exceeds the quantity of the heat
necessary for the hot water system. As a
result, the heat recovery system always
works with the cooling tower in parallel.
Previously, this chiller had two indepen-
dent control systems: one for heat recovery
and one for controlling the temperature of
the water leaving the cooling tower. Both
systems operated simultaneously.
In addition, the condenser water pump
100 hp (74.5 kW) worked continuously
under all load conditions at full speed
(50 Hz), providing approximately 3,000
gpm (189 L/s), which equals 3 gpm/ton
(0.5 L/s per kW), the normal rate in pri-
mary-secondary chilled water systems.
The heat recovery system temperature
was controlled indirectly through the tem-
perature setpoint of the water leaving the
main condenser, by two valves situated in
the condenser water line and operating pro-
portionately. One of these valves supplied
water to the cooling tower and the other
controlled return water back to the main
condenser via the bypass line (Figure 1
(F
(
).
By Igor Dubov, P.E., Member ASHRAE
Heat Recovery,
Energy Savings
For Semiconductor Plant
© 2005, American Society of Heating, Refrigerating and Air-Conditioning Engineers, Inc. (www.ashrae.org). Reprinted by permission from
ASHRAE Journal, (Vol. 47, No. 12, December 2005). This article may not be copied nor distributed in either paper or digital form without
ASHRAE’s permission.
December 2005
ASHRAE Journal
57
The cooling tower provided the temperature control of water
going out. This was done through the variation of the fan’s speed
using a variable speed drive (VSD).
The cooling tower temperature control system consisted of
two operational modes:
• Automatic. A variable setpoint changes automatically
according to an outside wet bulb and the cooling tower’s
capacity curve; and
• Manual. Based on a predeﬁ ned setpoint.
Usually, the variable setpoint controlled the temperature of
the water leaving the cooling tower. Often, in this operational
mode, the temperature was too low and the heat recovery sys-
tem compensated by activating its valves, resulting in the water
ﬂ ow through the bypass valve back to the main condenser.
This situation caused unnecessary energy consumption of the
cooling tower’s fan and the condenser pump, which worked
continuously at full speed.
To achieve a better result in this case, the operators had to
change the cooling tower control mode to manual, with a higher
temperature setpoint. This could result in a certain amount of
energy savings during current ambient and
chiller load conditions. Occasionally under
full-load conditions during hot and humid
days, the temperature of the water leaving
the condenser was too high. This caused
the chiller to work at higher condenser
temperature and pressure, consuming ex-
cessive energy. Increasing the condensing tempera-
ture to 1°F (0.6°C) typically reduces chiller COP
by1% to 2% .² In this case, the hot water supplied to
consumers had a temperature higher than necessary.
New Operational Mode of the Heat Recovery System
The main goal in the second phase of the chilled water plant
retroﬁ t project was to ﬁ nd a new and sophisticated way to pro-
vide optimal energy consumption under all kinds of ambient
conditions and chiller thermal load variations.
The idea was to consolidate three different control systems
into one operational mode with a common temperature setpoint.
This mode consists of: the heat recovery temperature control;
the temperature control of the water leaving the cooling tower;
and the condenser’s water ﬂ ow control. To achieve this, a VSD
was installed on the condenser pump motor.
We evaluated what would be the lowest, but most reason-
able, hot water supply temperature. The result was 88°F – 90°F
(31°C – 32°C).
According to the new conﬁ guration of the control mode, the
common temperature setpoint maintains permanent temperature
of the water leaving the main condenser.
We considered two potential alternatives for operating the
cooling tower’s fan and the main condenser pump: operating in
parallel (the simultaneous sending of the same signal to the cool-
ing tower’s fan and the main condenser pump) and a mixed-mode
operation (Figure 2
(F
(
). The preliminary estimate and ﬁ ne-tuning
of the new control mode proved that a mixed operation of the
cooling tower’s fan together with the main condenser pump would
provide better results with overall energy consumption.
All motor speed (rpm) variations depend proportionally on
frequency changes. Also, the relationship between speed and
ﬂ ow is directly proportional. (Afﬁ nity law for pumps³ and the
same equation is applicable for fans when the impeller’s size
and the air density are constant.4)
Increasing the air mass ﬂ ow through a cooling tower enhances
its heat rejection (the energy balance between the cooling water
and the air in a cooling tower5) and, as a result, decreases the
temperature of the water entering the chiller’s condenser. Simi-
larly, rise of the water ﬂ ow rate through the chiller’s condenser
improves its heat removal. The power requirement of pumps and
fans changes approximately to the third power of the speed.
Considering the previously mentioned reasons, it is obvious
that the operational mode that provides the same heat rejec-
tion, while the summary speed of the condenser pump and
of the cooling tower’s fan is lowered, is preferable. That is,
in this operational mode both pump and fan would consume
less energy.
Finally, we chose the mixed operational
mode because its temperature control,
in comparison to the parallel method,
works more smoothly. In the parallel
operation, two parameters (temperature
and ﬂ ow) of the water entering the main
condenser vary simultaneously, complicating an
accurate temperature control. The new control mode
works as follows (Figure 2). When the current water
temperature exceeds the setpoint requirement, the valve
leading to the cooling tower opens and the bypass valve
correspondingly closes (Figure 1).
When the valve leading to the cooling tower is opened 100%,
the speed of the condenser water pump gradually increases. This
increase in speed changes the water quantity ﬂ owing through
the main condenser from 0% to 25% of its overall rate. This
rate varies from the minimum ﬂ ow of the water speciﬁ ed by the
chiller manufacturer, to the maximum ﬂ ow, which the pump at
full speed can provide.
If these steps don’t ensure the desired temperature, the system
increases the cooling tower fan’s motor frequency from 0% to
25% of the full motor rate. The entire motor’s rate is altered
from the minimum speed, corresponding to 20 Hz, which can
provide a proper motor cooling and gearbox lubrication, to the
maximum speed at 50 Hz. The next sequence of the changes
is: the pump from 26% to 50%; the fan from 26% to 50%; the
pump from 51% to 75%; the fan from 51% to 75%; the pump
from 76% to 100%; and the fan from 76% to 100% of their full
rate. When the water temperature is lower than the setpoint, this
sequence is reversed.
Chilled Water Auxiliary Equipment
The next project goal was to improve the energy consumption
of the chilled water pumps and to avoid exploiting the lead chiller

value (low ∆T syndrome). To prevent unnec-
T T
essary water ﬂ ow in the secondary water loop,
all three-way control valves were converted
to two-way by closing their bypass lines. As
a result, the required pressure difference on
secondary pumps decreased from 38 psi (2.6
Bar) to 22 psi (1.5 Bar).
Although the coils and two-way control
valves function properly, the difference be-
tween the return and supply water tempera-
tures remains relatively constant. The online
chillers maintain the temperature of the chilled
water leaving the evaporators steady. The
setpoint value of the water temperature enter-
ing the chiller was chosen to keep the tem-
perature difference of the evaporator slightly
higher than was designed. To achieve this, a
VSD-controlled primary pump 100 hp (74.5
kW) varies the quantity of the water ﬂ owing
through the evaporator. In this operational
mode, our target was to reduce or prevent the
water ﬂ ow through the bypass line between the
Results and Energy Saving
The retroﬁ t project of the heat recovery and the auxiliary
equipment improvement was completed in the middle of
January 2004.
The data of coefﬁ cient of performance (COP); efﬁ ciency
(kW/ton) of the entire chilled water plant, including the chillers,
the pumps and the cooling towers’ fans; the value of the kW
required by the auxiliary equipment of the entire chilled water
plant and the auxiliary equipment dedicated to the 1,000 ton
supply and the return chilled water loops (hydraulic bridge), while
continuing to provide the required quantity of chilled water.
The 1,000 ton (3517 kW) lead chiller alone was able to provide
the required cooling load in 2002 for more than 1,359 hours (15.5%
of the year); in 2003 for more than 1,424 hours (16.3% of the year);
and in 2004 for more than 2,035 hours (23.2% of the year).
When the main chiller cannot hold the desired temperature of the
chilled water leaving the evaporator, a 500 ton (1758 kW) chiller 4
is energized. This chiller has a higher efﬁ ciency. The annual aver-
age efﬁ ciency of these chillers is represented in Table1.
These chillers together were able to provide the required
cooling load in 2002 for more than 4,620 hours (52.7% of the
year), in 2003 for more than 5,190 hours (59.2% of the year)
and in 2004 for more than 6,172 hours (70.3% of the year).
When operating only these two chillers, the emphasis is on an
efﬁ cient cooling load distribution. This led us to build an addi-
tional control mode. While operating in this mode, according to
the required setpoint, the VSD-controlled primary pump of Chiller
6, varies the quantity of the water ﬂ owing through its evaporator,
consequently the rest of the required chilled water ﬂ ows through
the evaporator of Chiller 4. This setpoint is the value of the desired
cooling tons, which the 500 ton (1758 kW) chiller must provide.
As previously mentioned, the efﬁ ciency of the 500 ton (1758 kW)
chiller is better than 1,000 ton (3517 kW) chiller and, as a result,
we usually deﬁ ne this setpoint above the chiller’s nominal capacity
(500 ton to 550 tons [1758 kW to 1934 kW).
In most cases, this is possible because the temperature of
the water entering the chiller’s condenser is below its designed
speciﬁ cation of 85°F (29.4°C). Lowering the temperature of
the water entering the condenser can considerably improve
the chiller’s capacity and efﬁ ciency. Therefore, under these
conditions the 1,000 ton (3517 kW) chiller supplies only the
emainder of the entire necessary cooling load.

(3517 kW) chiller per 1 ton (3.5 kW) of refrigeration (kW/ton)
was gathered during 2001, 2002, 2003 and 2004 (Table 2). The
kW of energy demanded by the 1,000 ton (3517 kW) chiller’s
auxiliary equipment was estimated on the basis of 1 ton (3.5
kW) of refrigerant provided just by this chiller.
As we can see in Figure 3, the annual efﬁ ciency of the chill-
ers in 2004, in comparison to 2001, was improved by 16.1%,
while the efﬁ ciency of the auxiliary equipment was improved
by 58.1%.
The energy-saving estimate was reached by multiplying the
difference between the entire chilled water plant efﬁ ciencies
(kW/ton), during the same months in 2003 and 2004 by the
summary of the monthly cooling load (ton) in 2004 and the
average electricity price ($0.06/kW) for that period. The same
calculation was done comparing the same months in 2001(the
period before both phases of the retroﬁ t project had begun) and
2004. The ﬁ nal results are represented in Table 3.
Conclusions
This work illustrates the second phase of the chilled water
plant retroﬁ t project. The main emphasis of this phase was on
the improvement of the auxiliary equipment and of the heat
recovery system.
The new operational mode of the heat recovery system
provides optimal energy consumption in all kinds of weather
conditions and in the various chiller thermal loads.
The conversion of the primary chilled water pumping from
a constant ﬂ ow to a variable ﬂ ow offers a strong energy saving
potential. This can increase the return chilled water temperature,
avoiding the low ∆T central plant syndrome, and ensuring the
T
T
chillers’ function at the load is equal and even exceeds their
designed capacities.
As with the ﬁ rst phase, which was ﬁ nished at the end of
2002, the second phase was accomplished with a small capital
investment (about $20,000) and resulted in a saving of 12.3%
of the chilled water plant energy consumption.

About the Authors
Data Centers
Roadmap for
Datacom Cooling
By Christian L. Belady, Associate Member ASHRAE, and Don Beaty, P.E., Member ASHRAE
I
nformation systems (e.g., Internet,
search engines, archival systems) are
all integral parts of the datacom (data
processing and communication) industry.
As a result, the cooling of datacom facilities
has the same potential for overload, change,
evolution, and premature obsolescence.
In some ways, effectively addressing
datacom cooling could be character-
ized as being more difﬁ cult than the
challenge associated with the datacom
equipment and systems themselves. The
typical life cycle or refresh rate of data-
com hardware and software is two to ﬁ ve
years. The typical life cycle of cooling
equipment is 10 to 25 years. Therefore,
the cooling system must be designed
to accommodate at least several major
hardware/software upgrades.
This represents a major challenge
since forecasting the impact of a single
major upgrade event is difﬁ cult enough
but forecasting beyond to the second or
third upgrade can seem impossible. In
non-datacom applications, the cooling
plant simply would be designed for the
current (or Day 1) load with the ability
to expand to the future load.
In the case of datacom, the continued
upward trend in datacom equipment
power densities simply compounds the
problem. These loads are at concentra-
tions that have never been cooled before
in a datacom facility and, therefore, a lack
of historical knowledge or precedence
exist on how to cool. In addition, the
physical location of the load concentra-
tions may vary with each deployment,
making the cooling load a moving target
in both magnitude and location.
Providing expansion capacity at the
plant level may not be the complete an-
swer. How will the datacom equipment be
cooled in the future? Will it be air, liquid,
or a combination, etc.?1
As a result, the planning of new data-
com rooms or even major upgrades has
many unknowns and variables. In addi-
tion, the industry is becoming increas-
ingly obsessed with performance metrics
(especially ﬁ rst cost), so overspending for
the Day 1 build (initial build) seldom will
be accepted enthusiastically.
Although we have become accustomed
to “cookbook” solutions, this is not a
practical approach to such a complex
and variable problem. A datacom cooling
roadmap to consider is one that returns
to fundamental engineering and problem
solving. It is process centric.
This article brieﬂ y addresses the fol-
lowing areas to characterize the datacom
cooling roadmap:
• Planning process (baseline, Day 1
needs and future needs), and
• Providing a framework for a datacom
cooling roadmap.
Too many variables exist, as well as
different personal/corporate preferences,
policies, and strategies for one answer
to exist. At the same time, a movement
towards more standardization and low
cost ﬂ exibility are beneﬁ cial.
Planning Process Part 1: Baseline
Any measurement of the merits of a par-
ticular process requires the establishment
of a strong baseline to measure against.
The baseline involves a clear characteriza-
tion of any relevant existing performance
metrics and historical experience. This
includes the current and past operational
Christian L. Belady is distinguished technologist
at Hewlett Packard, Richardson, Texas. He is one
of the founding members of TC 9.9. Don Beaty,
P.E., is president of DLB Associates Consulting En-
gineers, Ocean, N.J. He is chairman of ASHRAE TC
9.9, Mission Critical Facilities, Technology Spaces
and Electronic Equipment.
© 2005, American Society of Heating, Refrigerating and Air-Conditioning Engineers, Inc. (www.ashrae.org). Reprinted by permission from
ASHRAE Journal, (Vol. 47, No. 12, December 2005). This article may not be copied nor distributed in either paper or digital form without
ASHRAE’s permission.
52
ASHRAE Journal ashrae.org
December 2005
These loads are at concentrations that have never
been cooled before in a datacom facility and, there-
fore, a lack of historical knowledge or precedence
exist on how to cool.
loads and information on existing cooling
difﬁ culties at the current facilities.
Another critical component in estab-
lishing the baseline is to determine the
preferences, policies, strategies, and
concerns of each stakeholder including
their value system. Is there an increased
focus on reliability, redundancy? Does
the ﬁ rst cost have a bigger emphasis than
quality? What are the details associated
with the deployment strategy and the
transitional period?
This information needs to be com-
piled, analyzed and documented as a
part of the owner’s project requirements
(OPR),2 which should be referenced and
adhered to throughout the design phases
of the project.
Planning Process Part 2:
Day 1 Needs
The OPR sets the ground rules for the
roadmap, and the next step is to translate
those rules into a set of design criteria to
establish Day 1 needs. One key aspect to
that is the classiﬁ cation of the Day 1 cool-
ing load. The environmental speciﬁ ca-
tions for a speciﬁ c type of facility can be
obtained based on the classiﬁ cations that
are established in Thermal Guidelines for
Data Processing Environments3 authored
by ASHRAE Technical Committee (TC)
9.9, Mission Critical Facilities, Technol-
ogy Spaces and Electronic Equipment.
In the past, the only information that
was available to determine the cooling
loads for datacom equipment was to
use the nameplate rating. However, the
nameplate rating is determined with a
focus on regulatory safety and is typically
signiﬁ cantly higher than the heat release
of that piece of equipment which can lead
to over-design and stranded capacities.
Thermal Guidelines also establishes
a forum for datacom equipment manu-
facturers to provide concise cooling
information for their products via a
thermal report template. The thermal
report provides actual heat release and
cooling airﬂ ow information for multiple
conﬁ gurations of the same product. It also
graphically indicates the airﬂ ow pattern
through the product.
With datacom equipment manufac-
turers beginning to use this method to
publish their heat release numbers, early
indications show that in some cases, the
actual load at the “minimum” conﬁ gura-
tion of a product is about 40% to 60%
(this could be much lower for large
systems) of the load indicated on its
nameplate based on whether the product
is minimally or fully conﬁ gured.
This not only shows how much lower
and more accurate the heat release num-
bers are, but also highlights the variation in
cooling load that can occur with changes
in product conﬁ guration.4
Other methods also can be used to
plan the distribution of the Day 1 cool-
ing loads. One such method involves
establishing physical zones within the
footprint of the raised ﬂ oor area that have
varying areas and load densities.5 For
example, three zones may be created for
low, medium and high density loads. The
zoning of the area in this manner allows
for a cooling distribution system that is
more tailored to a given zone as opposed
to trying to formulate a design that was
inﬁ nitely ﬂ exible for the entire footprint
of the datacom equipment room.
Planning Process Part 3:
Future Needs
Establishing the future cooling needs
of a datacom facility is the most difﬁ cult
aspect of the roadmap, since it is the
one step that has the greatest quantity of
unknowns and variables. This makes it
also the most reliant on a process-centric
solution.
Once again, the OPR is referred to in
order to determine the owner’s emphasis
and preferences with regards to future
deployment strategies, ﬂ exibility, scal-
ability and tolerance to operational dis-
ruption. The information on these topics
that is extracted from the OPR may not
be succinctly deﬁ ned, but should provide
the underlying principals upon which to
develop the design.
The key to establishing the future
cooling loads is an understanding of the
trends in applications and equipment and
being able to make predictions with some
level of conﬁ dence. The power trends
established by the Thermal Management
Consortium and published by the Uptime
Institute6 have been recently updated and
expanded upon by ASHRAE TC 9.9 in its
Datacom Equipment Power Trends and
Cooling Applications7 to include a greater
delineation of datacom equipment types
and projected loads to 2014.
The application of the trends can vary
based on a given application. The overall
percentages of the types of equipment are
required (e.g., storage servers vs. com-
pute servers vs. communication equip-

Figure 1: Example of datacom cooling roadmap.
ment, etc.) and also an understanding as to whether
changes in future applications may inﬂ uence these
percentages for future deployments.
The trend charts themselves are based on the
maximum predicted loads for a given type of data-
com equipment and so another decision that must be
made is whether the values in the trend charts are to
be considered or whether some percentage reduction
of those values should be considered to allow for the
possibility of using equipment that does not represent
the extreme cutting edge in technology.
A deployment strategy can allow for an understand-
ing of the timing of the deployments and the trend
charts can provide the impact of the deployment from a
cooling load standpoint. However, the incorporation of
the actual changes to the cooling system can be planned
for as well. The questions that need to be answered are:
1. What percentage of the anticipated future cooling capacity
do you install as a part of the initial construction? This option
has the greatest ﬁ rst cost impact but also is the least disruptive
and time consuming future deployment option.
2. What percentage of the anticipated future cooling capac-
ity should I provision for? This option requires some initial
installation and some spatial provisioning for the acceptance
of future equipment. With careful planning, this can provide a
non-disruptive option that does not have a great ﬁ rst cost impact,
but the deployment is not as immediate as the previous option
since some installation is required.
3. What percentage of the anticipated future cooling capac-
ity do I not want to provision for and accept that it will be a
major facility upgrade? This option is the most disruptive but
has little to no impact on ﬁ rst cost. The selection of this option
(or rather the value of the percentage of this option) has to be
weighed against the risk of meeting or exceeding the future
cooling capacity earlier than expected.
Framework for a Datacom Cooling Roadmap
Currently, there is no indication from datacom manufacturers
or end users on where the industry is heading and when they
will arrive there. In other words, an industry roadmap for the
cooling of datacom equipment in the data center is needed.
This was one of the motivating reasons for the development of
TC 9.98 and, as a result, efforts are now underway at TC 9.9 to
develop this industry roadmap.
The goal is to provide a timeline for datacom cooling para-
digm shifts. These shifts are anything that could signiﬁ cantly
impact datacom equipment or data center design for continued
operational success. Some examples of potential industry driv-
ers for these paradigm shifts could be the following:
New data center standards or guidelines. Standards and
guidelines can deﬁ ne operating temperature and humidity
conditions in the data center environment. In addition, these
guidelines could drive how air moves in the data center.
New low power technologies. Breakthrough semiconductor
technologies could lower power. Unfortunately, at this time, no
new technologies are on the horizon that could be commercial-
ized in the next 10 years.
Rapid rise of computing performance. The increase in
performance capabilities in datacom equipment could signiﬁ -
cantly outstrip the demand for performance. Performance per
watt is increasing signiﬁ cantly and end users’ computation
demand is increasing more rapidly. These factors consume all
performance and then some. This drives the need for more or
larger data centers. This could change if the reverse is true and
performance grows faster than the demand. The result would
be fewer computers in the data center but, in the short term,
this is not a likely scenario.
New high density air cooling. Raised ﬂ oor cooling in data
centers is already reaching its limits and at some data centers
density will no longer be adequate. Instead of moving to liquid
cooling at that point, there may be signiﬁ cant opportunity in
using new cooling technologies in the data center to extend the
air cooling limits.
Liquid cooling. Clearly, this is one of the most discussed
paradigm shifts being talked about in the industry. Most every-
one knows if trends continue, liquid cooling is an option that
needs to be considered.
All of these are examples of paradigm shifts that may or may
not occur but as an industry we must try to develop a roadmap
with items similar to these. If we succeed, the industry will
align and navigate through an easy and efﬁ cient transition of
relatively complex paradigm shifts.
Not adopting a roadmap, would create guesswork for all in-
volved. Manufacturers would guess when customers would be
ready for industry shifts, and data centers would be built with
suboptimum solutions based on guessing when shifts would
occur. As an example, if liquid in the data center becomes a real
consideration, when should industry players respond?
Guessing incorrectly by manufacturers and data center
owners would add signiﬁ cant cost and, in fact, in some cases,
54
ASHRAE Journal ashrae.org
December 2005
making the wrong decision could be
devastating to the business. So, as an in-
dustry we have the choice of doing noth-
ing and reacting to change or proactively
driving and inﬂ uencing when change
occurs. This is where TC 9.9 can have
signiﬁ cant impact.
To some extent, TC 9.9’s two pub-
lications3,7 have laid the foundations
for this roadmap. The ﬁ rst document
suggests the alignment of the industry
on common datacom environments and
equipment cooling techniques by 2008,
while the second document projects the
datacom power density growth over the
next decade. The committee is focusing
on how to continue deﬁ ning the roadmap
even further. An example of such a road-
map is shown in Figure 1.
This ﬁ gure shows a graph of server
power data as a function of rack power7
where a typical rack occupies about 7 ft2
(0.7m2) Above the graph is an example
roadmap showing various industry driv-
ers as a function of year that they are
effective. Several drivers already have
been accepted by the industry. The ﬁ rst
was the power trend published by Up-
time6 in 2000 (denoted by Uptime power
trend in the ﬁ gure) that was developed
largely by the same companies that cur-
rently make up TC 9.9 (but prior to the
TC’s existence). Now in its second year,
TC 9.9 also has published an updated
power curve (denoted by ASHRAE
power trend in the ﬁ gure) that will be
valid from 2005 – 2010.
As described earlier, one of the most
important publications of TC 9.9, Ther-
mal Guidelines,3 has provided one of the
foundation elements for the datacom
cooling roadmap. Published in early
2004, alignment is expected after 2008.
However, companies are encouraged to
adopt it sooner, as shown by the transi-
tion period in Figure 1.
In addition, Figure 1 shows generic
industry drivers A, B, C and D for il-
lustrative purposes only. In each case,
the year for targeted alignment will be
projected as well as transitional period
for adoption. The role of TC 9.9 will be
to deﬁ ne some of these generic industry
drivers moving forward. The committee
will need to look at all of the possible
industry drivers in the datacom space,
some of which have been outlined in this
document, and develop consensus across
the complete data center value chain,
which includes infrastructure suppliers
(power and cooling), datacom equipment
manufacturers, consulting engineers and
data center owners/manager. With this in
mind, TC 9.9 has been trying to broaden
its membership to include representatives
from all of these areas.
Summary
With the dynamic nature of the datacom
business, developing a cooling roadmap
is imperative for the industry. Without
such an industry-wide roadmap, the
cooling industry will remain fragmented
and inefﬁ cient. TC 9.9 has initiated this
cooling roadmap through its planning and
publishing of the Thermal Guidelines for
Data Processing Environments3 and the
Datacom Equipment Power Trends and
Cooling Applications.7The ongoing activi-
ties of TC9.9 are continuing towards the
goal of improved industry support and a
cooling roadmap.
References

to the future: liquid cooling—data center
considerations.” ASHRAE Journal 46(12).
2. Stum, K. 2002. “Design intent and basis
of design: clariﬁ cation of terms, structure and
use.” ASHRAE Transactions 108(2).
3. ASHRAE TC 9.9 2004. Thermal Guide-
lines for Data Processing Environments.

center load characterization: connected vs.
demand.” Engineered Systems (1).

and telecom facilities—Part 1.” ASHRAE

sity Trends in Data Processing, Computer
Systems, and Telecommunications Equip-

of data center environmental guidelines.”

Providing Care for
Medical Ofﬁ ce Building
By Jeff Blaevoet, P.E., Member ASHRAE
About the Author
Jeff Blaevoet, P.E., is principal at Guttmann and
Blaevoet Consulting Engineers in San Francisco.
Blaevoet received a 2005 ASHRAE Technology
Award for this project.
T
hermally powered VAV diffusers historically have been used in
Tretroﬁ ts to provide additional zoning for existing systems. The
TrT
Kaiser Geary Medical Ofﬁ ce Building in San Francisco is the ﬁ rst sig-
niﬁ cant new building in the United States to use thermally powered
VAV diffusers as the basis of design. An improved direct digital control
(DDC) approach for reheat control was developed, and a prototype
tested at the factory before construction commenced.
This project demonstrates why medical
ofﬁ ce buildings particularly can beneﬁ t
from this HVAC system type. Other build-
ing types where fully enclosed ofﬁ ces are
needed, such as attorney ofﬁ ces, are also
well suited for this system given.
As the number of reheat coils is greatly
reduced, heating hot water distribution is
also a fraction of a VAV/reheat box sys-
tem, which has cost beneﬁ ts and reduced
liability of water leaks above the ceiling.
Ductwork distribution is greatly simpli-
ﬁ ed, and the extent of powered controls
also is much less.
Where operable windows are provided,
a possibility exists of a window being left
open, which would affect the thermostat
of a VAV box, causing discomfort for the
occupants in the other rooms served by that
box. With self-contained thermally pow-
ered VAV diffusers, the master DDC zone
controllers are much fewer, reducing the
chances of this happening. If it did, the VAV
diffusers would compensate. For these
reasons, as well as the improved comfort
and energy efﬁ ciency afforded, thermally
powered VAV diffusers also have a much
broader application potential.
Project Overview
The Kaiser Geary Medical Ofﬁ ce Build-
ing is a high-rise building constructed in
2000. The $60 million building is eight
stories with an atrium and ﬁ ve additional
ﬂ oors of underground parking. Each ﬂ oor
has an area of 32,800 ft2t2t (3047 m2) and the
building has a total ﬂ oor area of 261,600
ft2t2t (24 303 m2) The facility contains a caf-
eteria, a large data/telecom room, doctor’s
ofﬁ ces, nurse’s stations, a full pharmacy,
exam rooms, waiting areas, a cancer center,
neurology, orthopedics, otolaryngology,
urology, dermatology, podiatry, ultrasound,
radiography and other imaging services.
This outpatient facility also houses proce-
dure rooms for performing minor and non-
invasive medical procedures. The project
program was for 150 provider ofﬁ ces and
all the necessary support spaces.
The project site is located in a mild
weather climate with summer conditions
(0.1%) of 84°F (29°C) DB and 66°F
© 2005, American Society of Heating, Refrigerating and Air-Conditioning Engineers, Inc. (www.ashrae.org). Reprinted by permission from
ASHRAE Journal, (Vol. 47, No. 12, December 2005). This article may not be copied nor distributed in either paper or digital form without
ASHRAE’s permission.
December 2005
ASHRAE Journal
41
Doctors and nurses of-
ﬁ ces are located along
the building perimeter
and architectural interi-
or light shelves provide
indirect natural light for
the perimeter spaces.
(19°C) WB and winter conditions (0.2%) of 41°F (5°C) DB.
The climate has 3,080 heating degree-days.
Architecturally, the building has a rectangular ﬂ oor plate with the
long sides facing north and south for solar control. The ﬂ oor plate
is divided into two halves, east and west, by a three-story atrium.
The envelope is comprised of cement plaster, metal frame
walls with R-19 batt insulation and concrete deck roof with
built-up rigid insulation. A layer of R-19 rigid insulation board
provides thermal isolation between the ﬁ rst ﬂ oor and the parking
garage level. The glazing is a clear single-pane ﬂ oat glass with
88% visible light transmittance.
Doctors and nurses ofﬁ ces are located along the building
perimeter and architectural interior light shelves provide indi-
rect natural light for the perimeter spaces. A solar shade-cloth
provides solar protection and prevents glare on interior work
surfaces. The facility has an overall window-to-wall ratio of
45%, but is 27% below California’s energy code.
Mechanical System Overview
Chilled water is provided to rooftop air handlers from the
facility’s central plant located on the ﬁ rst level of the parking area.
Two 0.48 kW/ton (0.14 kW/kW) 220-ton (774 kW) water-cooled
screw chillers piped in a primary/secondary-pumping scheme are
provided for chilled water distribution throughout the building.
A 50-ton (176 kW) water-cooled reciprocating chiller provides
chilled water to serve the building’s 24-hour loads. Two induced
draft cross-ﬂ ow cooling towers serve the water-cooled chillers.
On the roof are four screened roof-mounted variable-air-vol-
ume (VAV) air-handling units. Since the building is split into
east and west halves, one air handler serves the perimeter of
the east and another serves the interior, while the other two air
handlers serve the west perimeter and west interior, respectively,
to optimize supply air temperature and minimize wasteful reheat.
Chilled water fan coil units serve the data rooms and medical
equipment room loads. Run and standby secondary chilled water
pumps with variable frequency drives and two-way valves keep
pumping costs low. Fiberglass cooling towers are used to resist
the aggressive ocean fog climate. Two boilers provide heat to the
building, one sized at 1.6 million Btu/h (469 000 kW) output and
the other sized at 4 million Btu/h (1.2 million kW) output.
A 15,000 gallon (56 780) water storage tank is provided along
with diesel and electric ﬁ re pumps, combination stand pipes and
Kaiser Geary Medical Ofﬁ ce Building uses thermally powered VAV diffusers.
December 2005
ASHRAE Journal
43
full sprinkler systems. High-rise stair pressurization, vestibule
ventilation, and atrium exhaust systems on emergency power
are included. Double containment fuel oil storage with duplex
pumps in the garage feed the roof-mounted emergency generator
and day tank. Domestic water booster pumps, gas-ﬁ red domestic
water heaters, medical and control air compressors, and medical
vacuum pumps round out this highly serviced building.
Individual Exam Room and Doctor’s Ofﬁ ce Zoning
Medical ofﬁ ce buildings contain many individual doctor’s
ofﬁ ces, exam rooms and special procedure rooms, with dressed
and undressed patients. As medical ofﬁ ce buildings usually are
provided with reheat on interior as well as exterior zones, there is a
signiﬁ cant cost associated with heating hot water distribution, VAV
box hook-ups and controls. Conventional VAV boxes with reheat
typically are zoned at no less than three to ﬁ ve or more rooms per
thermostat. If each room could have its own temperature controls,
the comfort of patients and staff would be enhanced, but how can
that be done given limited construction budgets?
During the schematic design phase, Kaiser expressed interest
in considering VAV diffusers as they had used some in a small
project which had handheld remote controls. The on-board
contractor conducted a cost study that showed that wired or
remote-controlled VAV diffusers were more than the budget.
However, thermally powered VAV diffusers could be provided
for the same cost as a conventional VAV reheat system. This is
because the number of reheat coils is greatly reduced, and they
often are able to be located close together around each shaft,
which reduces the amount of heating hot water piping dramati-
cally. Also, the duct distribution is greatly simpliﬁ ed, reducing
the amount of ductwork that needs to be installed.
What Are Thermally Powered VAV Diffusers?
Thermally powered diffusers have a built-in thermostat and
VAV dampers. The thermostat/actuator is a small brass cylinder
containing petroleum-based wax that responds to room tempera-
ture by expanding and contracting, which in turn modulates a
damper actuator in the diffuser that regulates air volume. Usually,
the damper movement results in a constant discharge velocity,
which improves part load air distribution by maintaining throw
and avoiding cold air dumping that can occur at low discharge
velocities. Room air is induced by the supply air and routed over
the self-contained thermostat to sense room temperature for con-
trol. No external connections (other than sheet metal duct) are
required for operation. Thermally powered diffusers have been
reliably in service for more than 25 years.
Thermally Powered Diffusers With DDC Control
DDC-controlled diffusers are available that require power and
control wiring to operate, but the cost of such systems is usually
prohibitive, and the objective was to provide accurate control of
each room for the same cost as a conventional system with VAV
boxes and reheat. However, in using affordable thermally powered
diffusers, supply air temperature control must be resolved. Simul-
taneous heating and cooling from a single temperature source is
not possible, so each group of diffusers must have the same type
of load—either heating or cooling. In a large new building, this
is most practically accomplished by means of hot water reheat
coils for each exposure and interior space. Individual air-handling
units per exposure and interior space would be better, but they are
usually difﬁ cult to integrate into the building economically. In the
case of this project, Kaiser has a policy of providing separate air-
handling systems for interior and perimeter areas, which provided a
good zoning basis for optimum supply air temperature control.
DDC Master Zone Reheat Control
The main challenge to date has been how to control the reheat
coils. Previous designs have the thermostat in a room with a ﬁ xed
diffuser, but then energy is wasted reheating that zone at full
volume and, worse, supply air temperature can be raised more
than other zones would like. Other designs have the thermostat
in a room with a thermally powered diffuser. Although this can
work in theory by having the room thermostat working within
a wide heating/cooling VAV diffuser dead-band, instability is a
danger, particularly if the initial setup is not maintained.
A conventional DDC VAV reheat sequence is preferred, and
this was accomplished by providing an electrically operated,
DDC controlled master zone VAV diffuser. Full volume reheat
is avoided and damper position feedback is provided to con-
trol the reheat coil. Other beneﬁ ts such as central monitoring,
optimized supply air temperature reset and morning warm-up
scheduling are also provided. Working with a major control
company who has a national purchase agreement with Kaiser,
and a thermally powered VAV diffuser manufacturer, factory
mockups were prepared and tested.
Static Pressure Control
The other critical issue for thermally powered diffusers is
static pressure control. Unlike regular VAV boxes, they must
operate in a static pressure range of 0.15 to 0.25 in. w.g. (37
Pa to 62 Pa). If the duct pressure at the neck of the diffuser
is below that range, less than the rated cfm will be delivered.
Above 0.25 in. w.g. (62 Pa), the diffuser will be noisy. In a
large system, there may be multiple static pressure control
dampers downstream of the reheat coils. Avoiding VAV boxes,
and designing low pressure, low velocity ductwork also has
a beneﬁ cial effect on fan energy use. The four air-handling
units serving the building, have a combined power to airﬂ ow
ratio of 0.58 W/cfm (1.23 W per L/s), which is 53% less than
the ratio of 1.25 W/cfm (2.65 W per L/s) for VAV systems
allowed by California’s energy code.
Implementation and Commissioning
Following the successful mockup and testing of the master
zone DDC with the control and diffuser manufacturers, con-
struction, test and balancing, and commissioning proceeded
smoothly. Balancing VAV diffusers is accomplished by un-
hooking the tension spring that connects the thermostat/actua-
tor and the blades (so the blades are full open constant volume)
setting the static pressure controls to the correct range, and
44
ASHRAE Journal
ashrae.org
December 2005
adjusting the duct-mounted balancing dampers for design
cfm at the diffuser.
Operating Experience
Feedback from facilities staff at the medical ofﬁ ce building
is that the system had few startup problems and has performed
very well since coming into operation. The main problem was
the failure to reconnect some of the actuator mechanisms after
initial balancing had occurred. In the four years of building
operation, no malfunctions of the VAV diffusers have occurred
and the system has been easy to maintain. Most importantly,
complaints from building occupants have been signiﬁ cantly
lower than other similar facilities without individual room
control. Service calls were quickly resolved by adjusting the
diffuser setpoint.
Energy Performance and Environmental Impact
The project design minimizes building energy consumption,
which was projected to be 27% below
California’s energy code, thereby reduc-
ing the environmental impact of elec-
tricity generation. The DOE-2 energy
model showed that the building design
resulted in a savings of 1,859,430 kWh/
year over a standard building designed
to comply with California’s energy code.
Approximately 1.33 lbs (0.6 kg) of CO2
are produced for each kWh of electricity
generated. The 1,859,430 kWh reduc-
tion in energy use corresponds to the
elimination of more than 2,473,040
lbs/year (1.1 million kg/year) of the
greenhouse gas CO2.
Conclusion
The use of thermally powered VAV
diffusers should be applied to new
construction as well as retroﬁ t HVAC
systems (which has been the main
market for the product to date). As
they typically are used for retroﬁ tting
problem systems, and they cannot
solve inherent problems with existing
systems, only provide additional zon-
ing—they perhaps have not been fully
appreciated.
This approach offers improved com-
fort through individual room tempera-
ture control, better air distribution at
part load, simpliﬁ ed duct distribution,
and greatly scaled back reheat piping
with reduced leakage, and associated
mold growth potential above ceilings.
They enhance use with operable win-
dows, have simple, robust controls,
lower energy use, and offer easy com-
missioning and maintenance.
These beneﬁ ts have not been well
publicized, and few new buildings using
this technology have been designed to
date. Thermally powered VAV diffusers
appear to be greatly underestimated, and
should be much more widely used in
new construction.
