Natatoriums
The Inside Story
W
hen you walk into a natatorium wearing street clothes, you probably
Wthink it is really hot and humid. If you are a 6-year-old swimming and
Wt
W
having fun, it isn’t all that uncomfortable. The author has heard children tell
their mothers, “I’m not cold,” when they are almost bright blue and shivering
in natatoriums where the temperature is around 84°F (29°C) and the relative
humidity is about 60%. Older adults and people in therapy require higher air
temperatures as well.
Even at the beginning of the initial design, it is important to know by whom and
how the pool will be used. If it is to be used mostly by children, it is likely that the
space temperatures will be maintained at 84°F to 86°F (29°C to 30°C)—no matter
what the owner may say. Therapy pools need even warmer temperatures.
By Lan Xie, Ph.D., Associate Member ASHRAE, and Kenneth Cooper, Ph.D., Fellow ASHRAE
April 2006
ASHRAE Journal
17
About the Authors
Lan Xie, Ph.D., is mechanical engineer and Kenneth Cooper, Ph.D., is
director of engineering for PoolPak International, York, Pa.
Competition and exercise pool spaces are more likely to be
maintained at temperatures around 82°F (28°C), or lower. This
is driven by the recommendation (2003 ASHRAE Handbook—
HVAC Applications, pp. 4.6 – 4.8) to keep the air temperature
about 2°F (1°C) above the water temperature to help reduce
evaporation from the pool and improve the comfort of bathers
exiting the pool. This will be discussed later.
Although residential space conditions can vary, it is important
for the designer to convince the pool owner not to maintain
the space conditions at normal comfort levels because some
people want to use their indoor pools as places to have parties.
If abnormal natatorium conditions are required, the designer
needs to know what the real conditions will be for proper dehu-
midiﬁ cation equipment selection. Since no one wants to swim
in 73°F (23°C) water, the pool evaporation rate will increase
dramatically as a result of the increased vapor pressure differ-
ential between the warmer pool water and the colder air.
Dealing With Humidity
The relative humidity level in a natatorium normally is
between 50% and 60% year-round for occupant comfort and
to maintain reasonable levels of pool water evaporation. At
typical air temperatures of 82°F to 86°F (28°C to 30°C), the
space dew-point temperature can range between 61°F and 70°F
(16°C to 21°C) as shown in Figure 1. This is much higher
than a typical air-conditioned building designed for 75°F
(24°C) and 50%, where the dew-point temperature is about
55°F (13°C). At the upper end of the comfort zone for indoor
pools, over 75% more moisture is in the air than typically is in
air-conditioned buildings. The designer must be aware of and
minimize condensation on cold surfaces in the natatorium. It
is not always obvious.
To further complicate the issue, the hot, humid environment
doesn’t go away when people go home. You can’t just put a
swimming pool away for the night! Yes, pool covers markedly
reduce the evaporation rate if they are used all the time. Despite
f tf
the best intentions of designers, manufacturers and even pool
operators, such devices are not used consistently over long
periods of time. How is a designer to act?
The continual generation of moisture (24/7) is reduced during
unoccupied periods. While an unoccupied pool only generates
25% to 35% as much moisture as a fully occupied one, the
moisture load never goes away completely. Night setback can-
not be used for a swimming pool because decreasing the air
temperature only increases the evaporation rate from the pool.
Designers and owners must understand that you can never turn
r r
off the natatorium environmental control equipment.
In general, continuous circulation of air must be maintained
24 hours per day. It only takes 20 to 30 minutes for the relative
humidity to increase to 80% to 85% if the dehumidiﬁ cation equip-
ment is shut off in a typical natatorium. To help save energy, shut
off the ventilation air and reduce the load on the dehumidiﬁ ca-
tion equipment during unoccupied times. It is also beneﬁ cial if
the conditioning equipment can take advantage of any outside
air even during unoccupied times when outside conditions are
favorable to help control moisture inside the space.
Indoor Air Quality
Because of the requirements for maintaining safe conditions
in the pool, chemicals are used to control the organisms and
waste products left by swimmers. These chemicals, as will be
discussed later, can produce some irritating airborne byprod-
ucts. One component of maintaining the environment in a
natatorium is ventilation.
ANSI/ASHRAE Standard 62.1-2004, Ventilation for Accept-
able Indoor Air Quality, recommends a ventilation rate of 0.48
cfm/ft2 of pool and deck area (2.4 L/s per m2). Because of the
elevated temperature and humidity levels in a natatorium, this
results in loads on the dehumidiﬁ cation and heating equip-
ment different than normal air-conditioning applications. If
you maintain the space temperature at 84°F (29°C), then any
outside air introduced into the space at temperatures below
84°F (29°C) helps cool the space and increase the heating
load faster as the outside temperature goes down. Similarly,
when the outside air dew-point temperature is below the dew-
point temperature of the natatorium air, it can help to remove
the moisture generated by the pool and occupants. Of course,
when the outside air dew-point temperature is higher, outside
air adds to the moisture load.
Getting Air to the Right Place
The 2003 ASHRAE Handbook—HVAC Applications (pp.
4.6 – 4.8) recommends air exchange rates from four to eight air
changes per hour depending on the use of the pool, occupancy
and equipment type. It has been the authors’ experience that
the design of the air-distribution system is more important
Several proven types of equipment are avail-
able that are designed specifically for nata-
torium applications. A standard, off-the-shelf, air
conditioner should never be used.
18
ASHRAE Journal
ashrae.org
April 2006
than the air exchange rate. The authors know of many systems
with only three air changes per hour and are working just ﬁ ne
with no owner complaints. Proper air distribution is more
important than how much outside air is introduced. Although
it may seem easier to substitute more energy-using outside air
than to design air-distribution systems that properly control
the contaminants in the natatorium, the owner pays for the
increased energy use forever.
It is most important to ensure conditioned air goes where it
is needed rather than assuming it will ﬁ nd its own way (arrows
painted on the wall don’t seem to help much). Deciding where
the air needs to be is not rocket science. Obvious places are
surfaces that can get cold, spectators, a gentle movement across
the pool surface.
The air-distribution system
must be designed so that ef-
ﬁ cient dehumidiﬁ cation and
acceptable air quality inside
the pool can be achieved. Only
increasing the airﬂ ow of the
dehumidiﬁ cation equipment
rarely solves a problem with
condensation or pockets of
poor air quality. Because of
the high dew-point tempera-
tures in a natatorium, more
places exist where conden-
sation can occur as well as
places where there must be
airﬂ ow to maintain air quality.
Some of the considerations for providing proper air distribution
in natatoriums follow.
• Pool Surface. Airﬂ ow over the pool surface and deck area
should be minimized to reduce the drafts on the swimmers
and to reduce the rate of evaporation, which increases
with air velocity. However, it cannot be zero! Substances
off-gassing from the pool will tend to collect at the pool
surface if there is not some small but uniform airﬂ ow over
the surface. These gasses often are irritating to eyes and
may increase respiratory problems. Often, complaints of
uncomfortable conditions in the pool are due to improper
air distribution and chloramines not being swept from the
pool surface.
Almost as often, the dehumidiﬁ cation equipment de-
signer is the one blamed although they did not design the
air-distribution system. Since high supply openings may
be 15 to 30 ft (4.5 to 9 m) above the pool surface, the
designer must be careful to provide proper airﬂ ow at the
pool surface. Proper return air locations can help.
• Supply and return air locations. Ceiling heights in na-
tatoriums normally are greater than regular ofﬁ ce spaces.
Oftentimes, supply air diffus-
ers located near the ceiling
are not adequate to get the
air down to the pool and deck
areas. Sometimes supply air is
introduced at the ﬂ oor level to
blanket the coldest surfaces,
particularly in climates where
a preponderance of heating is
required. Regardless, supply
air grilles should be chosen
to provide sufﬁ cient throw to
blanket the intended surfaces.
Although it may
be easier, it could be a mistake
to locate returns at the same
level as the supply air. Short
circuiting occurs when supply air leaves the conditioned
space without mixing with the space air. This can occur
inadvertently when the supply and return air openings
are not carefully considered and when the throw from the
supply diffusers is not adequate to properly distribute the
air. Strategically locating the returns to minimize short
circuiting as well as provide air movement over the pool
surface is important. Since supply air is most commonly
Commercial swimming pools may be occupied much
longer than ofﬁ ce buildings. It is not uncommon for the
pool to open at 5 or 6 a.m. for early morning lap swimmers,
and remain open until 10 p.m. or later for pool parties and
competition events. During practice sessions, swim teams
churning up the water with nose-to-toe lap swimming
can generate a high rate of evaporation from the pool.
However, few people are in the pool many times during
the day. Conversely, residential pools likely are used only
a few hours each day.
Designers must provide the means to control natatorium
temperature and humidity when the pool is fully occu-
Natatoriums: Long Hours, Varied Occupancy
pied and with a full complement of spectators. However,
most of the time, the level of activity is not nearly that
high. This can cause wide swings in moisture load that
must be accommodated by the dehumidiﬁ cation equip-
ment. For pools with sporadic use, it may be acceptable
to slightly undersize the dehumidiﬁ cation equipment.
Because of the rapid buildup of moisture when activity
increases, it is useful to estimate the type and duration
of the increased activity as well as the building and per-
sonnel tolerance to increased relative humidity when
making the decision whether or not to undersize the
dehumidiﬁ cation equipment.
Figure 1: Dew-point temperatures in natatoriums.
20
ASHRAE Journal
ashrae.org
April 2006
Many reasons exist concerning why odors occur in nata-
toriums. The most common is the pool operator failing to
properly maintain the water chemistry. This may be due to
lack of training, failure of the sanitizing equipment, or to heavy
use of the pool coupled with long operating hours. When
people complain about natatorium conditions, the complaints
are most often about odor, not humidity or temperature.
Commercial pool operators are required to test and
record water quality at least daily and as often as hourly
during occupied periods. Many factors are important to
maintaining good pool chemistry. Only a few are discussed
here. Commercial pool operators must be trained to
maintain all of parameters of the pool.
In the United States, most indoor pools are sanitized
using some form of chlorine such as chlorine gas, sodium
hypochlorite and calcium hypochlorite among others.
Other methods include bromine compounds, ozone,
silver and iodine.
When chlorine is used, a free chlorine residual of between
1 and 3 ppm must be maintained. Because biological matter
always exists in the pool, combined chlorine compounds
(chloramines) form wherever there is chlorine. Chlora-
mines are complex chlorine-nitrogen compounds released
from the pool surface as a gas. It is the off-gassing of the
combined chlorine products that causes the odor—not the
chlorine itself. The National Swimming Pool Foundation’s
Certiﬁ ed Pool–Spa Operator® Handbook (www.nspf.com)
recommends the combined chlorine level be maintained
below 0.2 ppm.
A simple, inexpensive tester for chlorine often is used.
This device relies on the operator comparing the color of
a solution to a color chart. If the combined chlorine levels
are too high, these devices lose their accuracy because the
color comparison becomes more difﬁ cult. More accurate
(and expensive) devices exist. However, many pool opera-
tors do not know about them or cannot afford them. A
quick test for improper pool chemistry is to notice the odor
immediately upon entering the natatorium. If a noticeable
Dealing With Odors
odor exists within the ﬁ rst 10 to 20 seconds, it is prob-
able that the combined chlorine level is too high. After 20
seconds, you become accustomed to the odor.
All pools that have signiﬁ cant use require shocking (super
chlorination) at some time. When the combined chlorine
level reaches 0.2 to 0.5 ppm, shocking is necessary. If the
pool is heavily used, it may need to be done daily. Shocking
a pool requires the operator to increase the free chlorine
in the pool to a minimum of 10 times the measured chlo-
ramine content of the pool. This normally requires the
chlorine content in the water be increased 8 to 10 ppm.
It can take several hours for the pool chemistry to come
back to normal. This cannot be done when anyone is in
the pool for obvious reasons. If this is not done correctly
and breakpoint chlorination is not reached, it can actually
worsen conditions. For a heavily used pool open from 5
a.m. to 10 p.m. or later, this can present a problem. An
operator may not be present during the only time shock-
ing can be done.
Natatorium dehumidification units providing 100%
outside air can be operated in a purge mode that ﬂ ushes
the waste products of the superchlorination process more
quickly, reducing the time required for shocking. During
this mode, it is acceptable for the space conditions to vary
from the setpoint since no occupants are in the space.
Another important criterion is pH. Swimming pools must
be maintained slightly basic, in the range of 7.2 to 7.6 pH.
This is important for two reasons. First, it is not irritating
to swimmer’s eyes. Second, maintaining the pH in this
range allows the other pool chemicals, including chlorine,
to be the most effective. Having a pH below 7.0 can cause
corrosion damage to pool components and reduce the
effectiveness of the chlorine.
Total alkalinity and water hardness are important and
must be balanced with the factors discussed previously.
Scale formation and pH control problems can result from
improper levels.
Off-gassing from pools is not covered in ASHRAE litera-
ture. ASHRAE Research Project 1083, Chemical Off-Gas-
sing From Indoor Swimming Pools, is investigating this.
distributed over the exterior surfaces, returns should be
located on interior surfaces.
Another common problem is to provide one undersized
return air location. Of course, returns are located in out of
the way places since they are noisy and unsightly. Aside
from the potential noise generated by the high air veloc-
ity, it can cause pockets of dead air in the space—think
corners and the edges of the spectator areas as well as the
pool surface itself. Properly sizing the return can almost
eliminate air noise and reduce static pressure loss in the
duct system. A quick inspection of a swimming pool will
demonstrate that there is plenty of open space for properly
sized returns.
If there is a warm pool, whirlpools or kids’ pool (often
kept at a higher water temperature), a return air grille
should be located nearby to localize the impact of the
increased evaporation rate. This should not be the only
return path as it can fool the dehumidiﬁ cation equipment
into sensing that the relative humidity is higher than it
actually is. Sometimes, an auxiliary exhaust fan can be
used to help control evaporation from such areas.
• Spectator areas. Spectators want to be cool and comfort-
able. They don’t understand that the pool space cannot
be maintained at one temperature and the spectator area
at another temperature without a barrier between the two
spaces. An auxiliary air handler might be used to circulate
22
ASHRAE Journal
ashrae.org
April 2006
extra airﬂ ow in the spectator areas during periods of high
occupancy. Careful attention to some of the supply air
diffuser locations also can help. For instance, locating
supply air diffusers to blow dry air into the faces of the
spectators makes them feel more comfortable. Locating
a return air grille at the back of the spectator area will
help direct the airﬂ ow.
• Locker rooms. Don’t go there with the pool dehumidiﬁ ca-
tion system! Locker rooms must have separate ventilation
and exhaust systems. Be careful about open doorways
between the pool and locker rooms. The negative pressure
in the locker room can draw chloramine-laden air into
the locker room, possibly causing corrosion. It is best to
convince the architect that a door is necessary.
Photo 1: Residential pool.
Photo 2: Commercial pool.
Advertisement formerly in this space.
April 2006
ASHRAE Journal
23
• Space pressurization. A slightly
negative air pressure should be
maintained inside the pool area with
respect to adjacent areas of the build-
ing and the outdoors to prevent mois-
ture and chloramine odor migration.
This can be more difﬁ cult than it
seems because often several types of
adjoining spaces exist such as locker
rooms, hallways, common areas and
outside walls. Which space to use?
It is unclear since conditions can
change dramatically during periods
of high use and unoccupied times.
Certainly, the pressure reference
should be coordinated with zones
having a separate exhaust system,
such as locker rooms.
Be careful not to overdo the nega-
tive pressure either. Doors can be
hard to open because it only takes
a small pressure difference due to
the large surface area of the door.
Cracks can make a whistling sound.
Undesirable air from locker rooms
can cause odor problems. Uncon-
trolled air leaking into the space can
increase the load on the dehumidiﬁ -
cation equipment. Cold outside air
drawn in through a crack in a door
can cause frost formation on the
inside of the door even though the
space temperature is 82°F (28°C).
Install the duct system so no condensa-
tion occurs in the ductwork. All supply
and return ductwork seams should be
sealed, including connections to sup-
ply grilles, fans and return air grilles.
Special attention is needed for return
ducts, which have negative air pressure.
When leaks are in return ducts, air from
unconditioned space can be sucked in
and may generate undesirable condensa-
tion, as well as degrade the operation of
the dehumidiﬁ cation equipment. Duct
materials need to be resistant to corro-
sion from airborne chlorides. When the
ductwork is outside of the conditioned
space, insulation must be placed on the
outside of the duct. Seams must be sealed,
wrapped and coated with mastic.
Water, Water Everywhere
With pools of different sizes and many
fun features to entertain swimmers, it is
difﬁ cult to estimate evaporation load.
Many factors drive the evaporation rate.
As discussed earlier, the conditions in a
typical natatorium mainly are determined
by bather comfort. However, desired pool
water temperature and minimizing evapo-
ration rate also are important.
The original evaporation rate equation
developed by Willis Carrier more than
70 years ago, although used for many
years, is not always correct. Evaporation
Rate Equation 1 from the 2003 ASHRAE
Handbook—HVAC Applications (pp. 4.6)
has been modiﬁ ed to better reﬂ ect vary-
ing pool room applications.
wp
wp
w = 0.1A(p
(p
( w– p
–
a)Fa
Fa
F
(1)
where
wp
wp
w
= Evaporation rate of water,
lb/h
A
= Pool surface area, ft2
Pw = Saturation vapor pressure
at surface water tempera-
ture, in.Hg
Pa
Pa
P
= Saturation pressure at
room air dew point,
in.Hg
Fa
Fa
F = Typical activity factor
The 2003 ASHRAE Handbook (p. 4.6)
has a table showing various activity fac-
tors (F
(F
( a
Fa
F ) to be applied for different types
of pools and activities. However, it is
just a Band-Aid™ and does not cover
all water features in common use. Many
attempts have been made to produce a
better correlation, but none has gained
general use. ASHRAE Technical Com-
mittee 8.10, Mechanical Dehumidiﬁ ca-
tion Equipment and Heat Pumps, is
developing a work statement to update
the information in the Handbook.
Increasing the pool water temperature
to equal the space temperature 2°F (1°C)
can increase the evaporation rate by 15%
to 20%. Lowering the relative humidity in
the space by 10% can cause the evapora-
tion rate to increase by more than 30%.
Thus, it is important to know what the
intended natatorium space and pool water
conditions actually will be.
To add to the confusion, Photos 1
through 4 show some typical indoor
pools. A residential pool (Photo 1
(P
(
) usu-
ally doesn’t have much complexity in
Advertisement formerly in this space.
24
ASHRAE Journal
ashrae.org
April 2006
Photo 3: Institutional pool.
Photo 4: Theme pool.
usage or features. A commercial pool (Photo 2
(P
(
) in a motel or
small health facility is similar, although it will have more people
in it occasionally. Both of these natatoriums can have large
glass areas and high sensible cooling loads. An institutional
pool (Photo 3
(P
(
) normally is larger and has more activity and
possibly an occasional large spectator load.
Many theme pools (Photo 4
(P
(
) are built with places that spout
water. Waterslides and waterfalls make it more difﬁ cult to
calculate the evaporation rate, since it is difﬁ cult to estimate
contact surface area between water and air and convection ef-
fect of water movement. Activity factors for such features can
be upwards of two to four or more. No scientiﬁ c studies have
been done in this area.
Outdoor air ventilation rates, recommended by Standard
62.1-2004 for people, pool surface and deck area for acceptable
indoor air quality, can generate high moisture loads during the
summer. However, as discussed earlier, ventilation air can help
to dehumidify as outdoor dew-point temperature drops below
the natatorium dew-point temperature. Generally, it is necessary
to account for the worst case moisture load when sizing the
dehumidiﬁ cation equipment although the maximum moisture
load from ventilation air is not usually present.
Pool covers certainly reduce the evaporation rate when
they actually cover the pool. Long operating hours or people
forgetting to replace the pool cover after use reduce their ef-
fectiveness. Commercial pools often have long operating hours
and may use some of the unoccupied time to clean the pool
by superchlorination (shocking), further reducing the time the
pool is covered. Unless the pool cover is automatic, requiring
no operator intervention, it is likely that the cover will not be
used consistently. In any event, during operating hours, the
pool is not covered and the designer must choose equipment
to meet this load.
All of these factors provide a challenge when it comes to
determining how much moisture to remove. If you design for
the worst case and the pool is not used much, then you run
the risk of greatly oversizing equipment. If you design for
the average case, then you run the risk of the space getting
completely out of control when an extra large load exists. For
smaller, less used pools it can be acceptable for the humidity
and temperature to rise somewhat under unusual conditions
so that the amount of oversizing can be minimized. For larger
facilities, this is less acceptable and the dehumidiﬁ cation
equipment often is called on to work effectively over a large
range of operating conditions.
Where Does the Water Go?
Water vapor in the air changes to the liquid phase, when air
is cooled down below its dew point, which is at 100% relative
humidity. Condensation occurs when liquid water molecules
accumulate, droplets form, and moisture condenses out of the
air. Condensation can damage wood, paper and other materials.
It accelerates rusting of steel and causes paint to peel. In indoor
swimming pools, chloramines (by-products from the pool water
chemistry control system) combine with moisture in the air
condensing on cold surfaces to form a chloride-rich solution
that corrodes most metals, including some stainless steels.
If moisture control is neglected during the design of the nata-
torium envelope and during the supervision of its construction,
problems can occur, including massive condensation; sagging
ceiling tiles; soaked insulation; mold; mildew; corroded ﬁ xtures;
fractured masonry and even eventual structure collapse.
• Cold surfaces. When any surface cools to a temperature
below the surrounding air dew point, moisture condenses
on the surface. Potentially cold surfaces include north exte-
rior walls, windows, window/door frames, and skylights.
Single glazed windows, metal casings of windows and
doors and on rooﬁ ng fasteners create a thermal bridge
between cold weather outdoors and humid air indoors.
Most designers recognize the need for thermal glazing on
windows. Depending on the expected outside temperature,
double glazing might not be sufﬁ cient. More importantly,
most designers do not specify (and hold the speciﬁ cation
when the value engineering starts) window frames with
April 2006
ASHRAE Journal
25
adequate, or any, thermal breaks. We
have seen many installations where
the windows are clear, but the window
frames are covered with moisture.
Another common mistake is in
letting the architect design the
supports above doors and windows
with steel plates or beams that go
completely through the wall with
no thermal break.
Most natatoriums have at least one
emergency exit door to the outside.
Such doors must be selected with
care. Thermal breaks are required
for the door and its frame and, at
minimum, the double-pane windows
as well. Some common places that
are forgotten are the door sill and
locking mechanism. The authors have
seen installations where it looked as if
someone had been spraying the door
with a hose. Another installation had
ice on the inside of the door frame.
Windows that face interior sections
of the building also can form con-
densation, particularly if the window
surface is bathed in air from an air-
conditioning system on the outside
of the window. It usually is best to
use double-pane windows here.
Skylights are similar to windows
except that they are in a much worse
place. Since hot, humid air rises and
the airﬂ ow at the top of the building
may not be as good as elsewhere,
the moisture level near the skylight
will be higher than anywhere else.
Some facilities include operable sky-
lights or moveable roofs for summer
venting. These are mixed blessings
since anything that can be opened is
more difﬁ cult to seal and may have
mechanisms that are problematic to
thermally isolate. As a minimum, the
dehumidiﬁ cation equipment must be
interlocked so the equipment does
not use energy needlessly when the
skylights are open.
Any type of skylight is not a good
idea in a natatorium. If they must
be used, then the designer needs to
be involved with the architectural
design of these features as early as
possible to ensure adequate air cir-
culation. The designer may consider
providing some type of auxiliary
air-moving device to maintain air
circulation over the surface to help
prevent condensation.
•Vapor retarders. The movement
of moisture is determined by the
differences in water vapor pressure
between two spaces. If a portion of
the natatorium has walls, ﬂ oors or
ceilings that are adjacent to interior
spaces, a moisture retarder is re-
quired. Remember, the difference in
the amounts of moisture in the two
air spaces can be large. Do not just
think about the outdoors!
Natatoriums should be built as vapor
tight as possible. Vapor retarders,
which are semi-vapor permeable
materials, should be located im-
mediately behind the interior wall
surface, so that humid air and vapor
will be held inside the space, rather
than moving outward into the cooler
wall cavities. All seams of the vapor
retarder must be sealed instead of
just overlapped. The vapor retarder
must also be sealed to ceiling and
to the ﬂ oor slab to prevent humid
air entering the walls or ceiling
through those seams. All joints
around switches and receptacles
must be sealed to prevent localized
moisture migration. The continuity
of the vapor retarder is important.
The air dew-point temperature is the
key. Since the dew-point temperature of
the air in a natatorium is high by design,
everything else in the building must be
able to accommodate it. It is suggested that
the coldest surface should be at least 5°F
(3°C) above the pool room dew point (2003
ASHRAE Handbook, pp. 4.6–4.8). Main-
k,k
taining all surface temperatures at 70°F to
75°F (21°C to 24°C) on a design winter day
can be difﬁ cult. As mentioned earlier, air
distribution plays an important role.
Dehumidiﬁ cation
Because the majority of pools (in North
America) use a chlorine-based water
chemistry control system (often man-
dated by local health codes), and because
the control of pool water chemistry is not
always adequate, conditions experienced
inside a natatorium dehumidiﬁ er often are
Advertisement formerly in this space.
26
ASHRAE Journal
ashrae.org
April 2006
harsh. This requires the manufacturer to take extra precautions
to protect the equipment to ensure a satisfactory lifespan. Spe-
cially coated or special materials for refrigeration coils and heat
exchangers and interior painting of metal parts are required.
Isolation of operating equipment such as electrical and refrig-
eration parts to minimize corrosion is important. This means
that, in general, standard-duty air-conditioning equipment is not
suitable for natatorium use. Therefore, natatorium equipment
costs more than standard air-conditioning equipment.
Natatorium dehumidiﬁ ers are different from standard air-
conditioning equipment in that they are designed to remove
much more moisture from the air (a sensible heat factor of 0.5
to 0.6 contrasts to a sensible heat factor of 0.8 for standard
air-conditioning units). This has several ramiﬁ cations. First,
the equipment probably is more expensive per unit of cool-
ing. Second, much less sensible cooling capacity is available
than with standard air conditioners. This can dramatically
affect the required equipment size when a space has a high
cooling load.
Natatorium dehumidiﬁ ers are generally higher maintenance
items (although owners don’t want to hear that). Coupled with
the fact that the equipment must operate 24/7, the harsh air
conditions can cause rapid deterioration of the equipment
if regular maintenance is
not performed. Natatorium
equipment has belts, ﬁ lters
and bearings that need to
be inspected and serviced
at intervals that seem too
frequent to most owners.
Newer equipment with direct
drive fan motors and variable
frequency drive speed control
can reduce the maintenance
somewhat. However, a need
still exists for regular visits to
the dehumidiﬁ er. Therefore,
the designer needs to ensure
that the equipment is easily
accessible.
Different fan conﬁ gurations can be used to maintain negative
pressure control of the space, use outside air as an economizer,
control outside air for ventilation, and provide a purge cycle
during regular maintenance of pool water chemistry.
• Return/supply fan conﬁ guration. Two fans are located
inside the equipment cabinet. The return fan is sized to
handle the return duct static pressure, while the supply
fan handles the supply air duct static pressure. Negative
pressure in the space can be maintained easily by changing
the return fan speed, the supply fan speed, or both. An air
mixing box with three dampers generally is used to achieve
100% outside air, or a minimum percentage outside air,
or any percentage outside air for dehumidiﬁ cation when
appropriate. This conﬁ guration normally has two ﬁ lter
sections, one for return and one for supply.
• Exhaust/supply fan configuration. The supply fan
handles both return duct static pressure and supply duct
static pressure. A separate exhaust fan may be located
in the dehumidiﬁ er cabinet or at a remote location. This
conﬁ guration only allows for minimum outside air re-
quirements (typically about one-third of the airﬂ ow) and
requires a second small ﬁ lter area speciﬁ cally for outside
air. The exhaust fan and outside air control dampers must
be coordinated by the dehumidiﬁ er for proper operation.
• Purge/exhaust/supply fan. This conﬁ guration is similar
to the exhaust/supply fan conﬁ guration except that a purge
fan, which normally can handle at least two-thirds of air-
ﬂ ow, is included to obtain approximately 100% outside air.
This conﬁ guration requires a separate ﬁ lter area capable
of supporting full outdoor airﬂ ow as well as a damper
mechanism for control.
Mechanical dehumidiﬁ ers (2004 ASHRAE Handbook—HVAC
Systems and Equipment, p. 47.1) are widely used for natato-
riums. They are speciﬁ cally designed to obtain high moisture
removal capacities, low sensible heat ratio, using a standard
refrigeration cycle. These dehumidiﬁ ers are equipped with
multiple refrigerant condensers so space cooling, air reheat and
pool water heating can be obtained while controlling the space
temperature and dew point in
the natatorium. Mechanical
dehumidifiers are good at
using as much of the rejected
heat as possible. Since evapo-
ration of the pool water causes
the water temperature to drop,
pool water heating normally
is always required year-round.
Using rejected heat for pool
water heating is important.
Where local codes allow, re-
covering moisture condensed
from the air and returning it to
the pool can be cost-effective
because it reduces the amount
of makeup water required. In
a large, active pool several hundred pounds per hour of moisture
can be reclaimed.
A total heating requirement exists comprising the nata-
torium skin losses, ventilation air and the pool water itself
because of continual cooling due to evaporation. Generally, a
requirement exists for auxiliary heat for the pool water as well
as for the air. This is true no matter what type of equipment is
used for dehumidiﬁ cation. However, if the dehumidiﬁ cation
equipment can recycle unused heat from the dehumidiﬁ ca-
tion process back into the pool water, it reduces energy use
substantially. Auxiliary pool water heaters need to be sized
to handle the entire heat loss due to evaporation and cold
makeup water introduction. The auxiliary pool water heater
also might need enough capacity to heat an entire swimming
pool to operating temperature in a reasonable amount of time
Figure 2: Dew-point temperature control.
April 2006
ASHRAE Journal
27
when the pool has been completely re-
ﬁ lled after maintenance or because the
dissolved solids in the pool water have
become unmanageable.
Where the outside air dew point is low
enough, 100% outside air units are gain-
ing popularity. Such units will normally
use a heat recovery device such as a heat
pipe or air-to-air heat exchanger between
the exhaust air and the outside air. When
the outside air dew-point temperature is
above about 60°F to 65°F (15°C to 18°C),
it may not be possible to maintain the
space humidity at acceptable levels, and
some type of auxiliary dehumidiﬁ cation
may be required. When the outside air
temperature is above about 75°F (24°C) it
may not be possible to maintain the space
temperature at acceptable levels. In these
cases some sort of auxiliary cooling coil
must be provided. Auxiliary heat also is
needed for winter conditions.
Air-to-air heat recovery devices can
be used to recover energy from the ex-
haust air to the supply air so the size of
auxiliary heating can be reduced. In con-
sidering such devices, the designer must
be aware of possible problems caused
by the high moisture load of the exhaust
air. If the heat recovery device is too ef-
ﬁ cient, at low outside air temperatures,
condensation of moisture and freezing
can occur in the exhaust side of the heat
exchanger. If the heat recovery device is
not efﬁ cient, then its beneﬁ ts are minimal
and more auxiliary heating is required.
Enthalpy wheels are not used in natato-
riums because no need exists to increase
the moisture in the incoming air.
Natatorium dehumidiﬁ cation equip-
ment is selected primarily to remove the
moisture load. Air circulation rates and
outdoor air requirements are also part of
the consideration. Dehumidiﬁ er manu-
facturers have computer programs to help
the designer select the proper equipment.
Potentially large sensible cooling loads
generated by expanses of south-facing
glass or skylights must be considered.
However, they may cause the dehumidi-
ﬁ cation equipment moisture removal to
be oversized.
The actual amount of moisture in the
air (absolute humidity) is represented by
the dew-point temperature as shown in
Figure 2. In moist pool spaces, the actual
amount of moisture in the air does not
change very fast even with the dehumidi-
ﬁ cation equipment running. On the other
hand, Figure 2 shows that the relative hu-
midity can vary considerably with a small
change in the space dry-bulb temperature.
It is important for the pool dehumidiﬁ er
control system to work on maintaining
proper dew-point temperature and not
relative humidity for best controller sta-
bility. Naturally, the user probably won’t
know what dew-point temperature to
set, so the control system should allow a
nominal space temperature and relative
humidity setpoint with the system con-
trols, making the conversion to dew-point
temperature internally.
Summary
Natatoriums are not just another space
t t
to be air conditioned. This article pre-
sented an overview of several areas that
are important to the successful design of
an indoor swimming pool.
The driving factor is moisture and lots
of it. The designer must be relentless in
ﬁ nding places that moisture might collect
and eliminating them. In addition to being
unsightly, surfaces that are regularly wet
will corrode and may fail prematurely.
Air distribution is another important
aspect of a successful design. The air-
distribution system in the natatorium is
not the place to apply value engineering
to the design.
Although pool water chemistry is be-
yond the control of the designer, it also
is an important aspect of the design. The
designer needs to make provisions for
maintaining the best air quality possible
even if the pool operator doesn’t do a
good job with the pool water chemistry.
Several proven types of equipment are
available that are designed speciﬁ cally
for natatorium applications. A standard,
off-the-shelf, air conditioner should never
be used.
Natatoriums are expensive to build
because of the special design consider-
ations and equipment needed, and they
are expensive to operate. The systems
cannot be turned off, and they require
continual attention for the pool and the
dehumidiﬁ cation equipment.
Advertisement formerly in this space.
68
ASHRAE Journal
ashrae.org
April 2006
By Detlef Westphalen, Ph.D., Member ASHRAE; Kurt Roth, Ph.D., Associate Member ASHRAE; and
James Brodrick, Ph.D., Member ASHRAE
Heat Transfer Enhancement
This is the thirty-ﬁ rst article inspired by a DOE report cover-
ing energy-saving HVAC&R technologies.
H
eat exchanger effectiveness can impact the efﬁ ciency
of vapor-compression cycles used in air conditioners,
heat pumps, refrigeration equipment, and refrigera-
tors. As the refrigerant condensing or evaporating temperature
approaches that of the ultimate heat transfer medium, e.g.,
the outdoor air temperature for an air-cooled condenser,
the vapor compression cycle temperature difference (also
referred to as the lift) decreases. This, in turn, decreases the
pressure ratio across the compressor, increasing its opera-
tional coefﬁ cient of performance (COP) and decreasing its
energy consumption.
In vapor compression cycles, enhancement techniques aug-
ment both refrigerant- and air-side heat transfer. Due to the
more favorable heat transfer characteristics of refrigerants and
liquids relative to air, and the common use of helical grooves
(riﬂ ing) to enhance refrigerant-side heat transfer, air-side
heat transfer tends to limit overall heat exchanger efﬁ ciency,
accounting for two-thirds or more of total heat transfer resis-
tance. Consequently, this column focuses primarily on air-side
heat transfer enhancement techniques.
In the absence of enhancement, most HVAC air-to-liquid
heat exchangers have laminar flow over surfaces due to
the small hydraulic diameters of spaces between ﬁ ns. Heat
transfer in laminar ﬂ ows occurs across a thermal boundary
layer between the heat exchanger surface and the airﬂ ow.
Unlike turbulent boundary layers, which have vigorous
mixing due to turbulent ﬂ ow structures that readily transfer
heat between the surface and the airﬂ ow, calmer laminar
boundary layers have lower heat transfer coefﬁ cients, h.
To overcome these limitations, heat transfer enhancement
approaches augment heat transfer by either causing a transi-
tion to turbulent ﬂ ow, creating vorticity that increases mix-
ing, or restarting the thermal boundary layer to decrease its
thickness. Table 1 describes several different types of heat
transfer enhancement.
In some cases, the heat exchanger operating conditions permit
the ﬂ ow to be tripped from laminar to turbulent ﬂ ow if subjected
to a sufﬁ ciently strong perturbation. The surface downstream
of ﬂ ow transition then experiences higher heat transfer coef-
ﬁ cients because most resistance to heat transfer occurs across
a thin viscous ﬂ ow layer near the wall instead of across the
entire boundary layer. Tripping devices used include surface
obstructions (steps, coils, tapes, three-dimensional shapes),
surface indentations (cavities, dimples), roughness, as well as
upstream turbulence and vorticity.1
Upstream vorticity does not always cause a ﬂ ow to become
turbulent, but its swirling motions can enhance heat transfer by
increasing mixing between the air at the heat exchanger sur-
face and the bulk airﬂ ow. Examples include wavy ﬁ ns, surface
winglets, and other elements that protrude from ﬁ n surfaces
sufﬁ ciently to generate vorticity.1,2,3
At the leading edge of a heat exchanger surface, the thermal
boundary layer is thin and poses little resistance to heat transfer.
As the length from the leading edge increases, so does the resis-
tance to heat transfer. Some designs interrupt the heat transfer
surfaces to enable the boundary layer to restart, increasing h.
Practical examples of devices used to restart boundary layers
include offset strips and louvered ﬁ ns.1,2,3
All of the approaches discussed previously are passive, i.e.,
they do not require additional energy to modify the ﬂ ow. On
the other hand, most increase the pressure drop of the heat
exchanger and, in turn, increase the fan energy consumption.
Consequently, their net effectiveness depends upon the balance
between the reduction in compressor power from increased heat
transfer and the increase in fan power.
Heat transfer enhancements can improve the heat exchanger ef-
fectiveness of internal and external ﬂ ows. Typically, they increase
ﬂ uid mixing, by increasing ﬂ ow vorticity, unsteadiness, or turbu-
lence or by limiting the growth of ﬂ uid boundary layers close to
the heat transfer surfaces.

2006). For personal use only. Additional distribution in either paper or digital form is not permitted without ASHRAE’s permission.
70
ASHRAE Journal

Approach
Description
Enhancement Possible
\*

\*\* Signiﬁ cant enhancement possible in liquid ﬂ ows (from cavitation) or natural convection.
†
See Bergles, 1998.
Slits or offset ﬁ ns interrupt the boundary layer, restarting it, creat-
ing secondary ﬂ ows, and/or generating ﬂ ow unsteadiness\*
Accelerates transition from laminar ﬂ ow to turbulent; also in-
creases turbulent ﬂ ow heat transfer
Ridges or three-dimensional shapes (cube, pyramid, etc.) gen-
erate secondary or unsteady ﬂ ows\*
Surface vibration or sound waves thins or restarts boundary
layer and/or induces secondary ﬂ ows
High-voltage (>1 kV) applied to an electrode near a plate in-
duces secondary ﬂ ows in boundary layer (liquid ﬂ ows only)
Enhancement primarily for multiphase ﬂ ows
Removal of boundary layer restarts boundary layer downstream
Surface Interruptions
Surface Roughness
Surface Protuberances
Forced Flow Unsteadiness
Electrohydrodynamic (EHD)
Boundary Layer Injection
Boundary Layer Suction
50% – 100%
Up to 300%
50% – 500%
Small\*\*
300%+
50% – 500%
Large†
Active Passive
Active approaches use external energy sources to actively
alter the ﬂ ow. Active approaches can enhance heat transfer by
one or more of the three mechanisms described previously. For
example, large pressure ﬂ uctuations imposed acoustically aug-
ment heat transfer by increasing ﬂ ow unsteadiness and, in some
instances, inducing laminar-to-turbulent ﬂ ow transition.1
Energy-Saving Potential
Typically, a refrigerant-to-air heat exchanger for an air
conditioner has a saturated refrigerant temperature approxi-
mately 10°C to 15°C (18°F to 27°F) higher than the ambient
air temperature ﬂ owing through the condenser. By reducing
the temperature difference between the refrigerant and air
temperatures, enhanced heat exchange decreases the overall
temperature lift of the cycle and increases the cycle’s coef-
ﬁ cient of performance (COP). Airside heat transfer enhance-
ments have been used for evaporators, although condensa-
tion and frost formation on the heat exchanger surface if of
evaporators complicates the application of enhancements
to evaporators.
Analyses performed by TIAX to assess the energy-perfor-
mance gains for air-cooled air conditioning and refrigeration
cycles indicate a 100% increase in condenser heat transfer
coefﬁ cient can reduce cycle energy consumption by approxi-
mately 10% to 15% over a range of conditions. On a national
basis, this could reduce the 7 quads consumed by residential
and commercial air conditioners and refrigeration equipment6
by about 0.7 to 1.1 quads. However, this does not account for
additional fan energy consumption, which varies on a case-
by-case basis.
Market Factors
Heat exchanger design and selection cannot be separated
from a system context and reﬂ ects several tradeoffs including:
compressor energy efﬁ ciency gains, heat exchanger airside
pressure drop (fan power), component costs, and component
size. The extent that enhanced heat exchange affects these
design variables as compared with other performance en-
hancement options ultimately affects its ability to penetrate
the market and appear in products. For example, heat transfer
enhancement options may compete with the use of larger
conventional heat exchangers and higher-efﬁ ciency compres-
sors to increase unit efﬁ ciency and meet an EER target for
one product.
Alternately, enhanced heat exchange may be employed
to decrease heat exchanger area and reduce unit cost or
size while maintaining acceptable energy performance.
For example, heat transfer enhancement may prove attrac-
tive for certain unitary or military products because they
allow the manufacturer to maintain adequate performance
without increasing—or while decreasing—the size of the
unit’s enclosure.
Manufacturers may be cautious about introducing novel
heat transfer enhancement approaches into products. If ap-
proaches require appreciable modiﬁ cations to existing heat
exchanger manufacturing processes, manufacturers may have
to incur signiﬁ cant capital costs. To mitigate technical risks of
changing heat exchanger geometry, manufacturers must ﬁ rst
perform extensive laboratory and ﬁ eld testing to verify that
the enhancements perform reliably over the range of expected
operating conditions. Notably, the airside of condensers needs
April 2006
ASHRAE Journal
71
to resist performance degradation from fouling by grease and
dust. This tends to limit the applicability of many potential
enhancement approaches.2
Speciﬁ c heat transfer enhancement approaches have their
own challenges to commercialization. For instance, Electrohy-
drodynamics (EHD) for enhancing water- or refrigerant-side
heat transfer relies upon high voltages (sometimes in excess of
10 kV) to achieve dramatic (300%+) increases in heat transfer
coefﬁ cients.4,5 Implementing EHD has several complicating
factors, including manufacturing complexity (to insert the high-
voltage electrodes in the tubes), safety concerns, and possible
reliability issues.
References

Graw-Hill.
2. Bullard, C.W. and R. Radermacher. 1994. “New technolo-
gies for air conditioning and refrigeration.” Annual Review of

3. Gidwani, A., M. Molki, and M.M. Ohadi. 2002. “EHD-
enhanced condensation of alternative refrigerants in smooth
and corrugated tubes.” HVAC&R Research 8(3).
4. Jacobi, A.M. and R.K. Shah. 1998, “Air-side ﬂ ow and
heat transfer in compact heat exchangers: a discussion of
enhancement mechanisms.” Heat Transfer Engineering,
19(4):29 – 41.

A Tale of Two Codes
W
ith the adoption of the U.S Energy Policy Act of 1992
(EPAct),1 ANSI/ASHRAE/IESNA Standard 90.1-1989, En-
ergy Standard for Buildings Except Low-Rise Residential Buildings,
became the base efﬁ ciency standard for nonresidential building
codes across the United States. Under this act, each state had until
October 1994 to certify to the Department of Energy (DOE) that it
had a building code as stringent as Standard 90.1-1989.2,3 While
many states subsequently adopted codes based on Standard 90.1-
1989 or subsequent versions, approximately 10 states, including
California, already had a building energy code.
California’s code is Title 24, Part 6,
of the California Code of Regulations:
California’s Energy Efficiency Stan-
dards for Residential and Nonresidential
Buildings (Title 24).4 Title 24 at the time
of EPAct’s adoption was deemed more
stringent than Standard 90.1-1989. In the
intervening years, Title 24 and Standard
90.1 have each been through a number
of revisions, each code following a dif-
ferent set of rules in its development and
adoption process.
Although these codes are developed
separately, a fair amount of synergy and
similarity exists. Many members of the
code development process in California
also are involved in the development of
Standard 90.1. This has led to cross pol-
lination between these two documents.
This article presents mechanical
requirements that are signiﬁ cantly dif-
By Mark Hydeman, P.E., Member ASHRAE
© 2006 American Society of Heating, Refrigerating and Air-Conditioning Engineers, Inc. (www.ashrae.org). Published in ASHRAE Journal (Vol. 48, April
2006). For personal use only. Additional distribution in either paper or digital form is not permitted without ASHRAE’s permission.
April 2006
ASHRAE Journal
47
About the Author
Mark Hydeman, P.E., is a principal at Taylor Engineering in Alameda Calif.
He was a member of ASHRAE’s Standing Standards Project Committee
90.1, Energy Standard for Buildings Except Low-Rise Residential Buildings,
through publication of the 1999 version and has been a lead developer for
the mechanical portions of California’s Nonresidential Energy Standards
since the 2001 version.
ferent between these two energy standards in their current
implementation: The 2004 version of Standard 90.1 and the
2005 version of Title 24.
The reader also should be aware that signiﬁ cant differences
exist in the envelope and lighting portions of these two stan-
dards, but these are beyond the scope of this article.
A Tale of Two Codes
If Charles Dickens were still alive (and working in our
ﬁ eld) he might have written “they were the best of codes,
they were the worst of codes….” Building energy standards
like those developed by ASHRAE and California Energy
Commission (CEC) are much maligned and poorly under-
stood. To their critics, they are complicated and expensive
impediments to architecture and development. To their sup-
porters, they are a necessary part of protecting our scarce
resources and a cost-effective investment in our energy
infrastructure.
Title 24 and Standard 90.1 are building energy efﬁ ciency
codes. Although Title 24 and Standard 90.1 were developed
on a similar timeline (Table 1), the history and process of de-
veloping the two was quite different. According to ASHRAE’s
Web site, “ASHRAE is accredited by the American National
Standards Institute (ANSI) and follows ANSI’s requirements
for due process and standards development.”5 The ANSI-ap-
proved process that ASHRAE uses includes requirements to
provide public access to comment through public meetings
and reviews and to seek consensus where conﬂ icts arise.
On high proﬁ le standards such as ASHRAE Standard 62.16
and 90.1, this process can be cumbersome. When Standard
90.1 was referenced in EPAct, the requirements of Standard
90.1 became linked to the market for building products and
services across the United States.
Industry and public interest groups like the American
Council for an Energy Efﬁ cient Economy (ACEEE), American
Gas Association (AGA), Air-Conditioning and Refrigeration
Institute (ARI), Building Owners and Managers Association
International (BOMA), the U.S. Department of Energy (DOE),
Edison Electric Institute (EEI), and the National Resources
Defense Council (NRDC) locked horns on key provisions
in the standard, drummed up public comments to support
their stances and competed for seats on the committee. In
the second public review, the Standing Standards Project
Committee (SSPC) for 90.1 received and processed nearly
60,000 comments.7 Critics of the standard ran inﬂ ammatory
articles in trade journals and the committee nearly buckled
under the process of reading, responding to and resolving all
of the comments.
In contrast to the ANSI/ASHRAE process of standards de-
velopment, California’s process does not require consensus.
It simply requires evaluation of cost effectiveness (as deﬁ ned
under the Warren Alquist Act8) and consideration of public
input. The process as described on the California Energy
Commission’s Web site for the 2008 update to Title 24 is shown
in the sidebar “California’s Standard Process.”
In contrast to the ANSI/ASHRAE process of seeking
consensus, the CEC process is less stringent. The CEC must
provide a forum to solicit public input and must consider
that input in its deliberations. Unlike ASHRAE, the CEC can
publish its code without achieving consensus.10
Although the processes by which these codes are developed
and implemented are different, they share many similarities
in form and content. This is not surprising as many of the
developers participate in both forums. Some examples of the
similarities include:
• A structure that includes mandatory measures and a
choice of either prescriptive requirements or a perfor-
mance approach based on computer simulation;12
• Separate sections for envelope, lighting and HVAC;
• Identical equipment efﬁ ciency requirements;
• Mandatory control requirements including thermostatic
controls, scheduling controls and variable ﬂ ow for hy-
dronic systems; and
• Prescriptive economizer requirements.
Building energy standards like those developed by ASHRAE and California Energy
Commission (CEC) are much maligned and poorly understood. To their critics, they
are complicated and expensive impediments to architecture and development. To their
supporters they are a necessary part of protecting our scarce resources and a cost
effective investment in our energy infrastructure.
48
ASHRAE Journal
ashrae.org
April 2006
Of more interest are the few but significant differ-
ences. An illustrative subset of these that impact the HVAC
design are listed here and subsequently described in the fol-
lowing paragraphs:
• The treatment of Uniform Building Code (UBC) Type I
occupancies;
• Provisions for acceptance testing;
• The base mapping of HVAC systems in the simulation
approach to compliance;
• Requirements for treatment of makeup air for kitchen
exhaust and fume exhaust systems; and
• Prescriptive limitations on electric resistance heat, air-
cooled chillers and centrifugal fan cooling towers.
UBC Type I Occupancies
Title 24 currently has a blanket exception for UBC Type I
occupancies. In California, this is a jurisdictional issue as the
CEC is responsible for the Title 24 regulations, the California
The California Energy Commission’s standards process
as shown on its Web site:
“The ﬁ rst phase of the development process will include a series
of public workshops to communicate to the public what changes
the Commission is considering, to share the results of related
research sponsored by the Commission, utilities, and other
stakeholder groups, and to obtain input from the public.
The second phase, expected in mid to late 2006, will
present draft language for the 2008 Standards based on the
discussions in the ﬁ rst phase and will offer opportunities for
further public input.
The third phase will be the formal rulemaking during which
ﬁ nal proposed language for the 2008 Standards and related
documents will be released for public comment for 45 days
(this language is referred to as “45-day language”). If needed,
substantial changes to 45-day language would subsequently
be released as “15-day language.” This phase will also include
Energy Commission adoption of the ﬁ nal Standards that would
take effect in late 2008. The Commission expects this third
phase in late 2006 and early 2007, with adoption in mid 2007.
Preparation of the Residential and Nonresidential Compliance
Manuals will follow Commission adoption.”9

Acceptance Testing
The 2005 version of Title 24 included a number of acceptance
tests to ensure the performance of mechanical and lighting sys-
tem controls. These requirements deﬁ ne functional performance
tests that take place after construction and before occupancy.
By contrast, Standard 90.1 has only a vague requirement for
“detailed instructions for commissioning HVAC systems” in
projects over 50,000 ft2 (4645 m2) (Section 6.7.2.4). Stan-
April 2006
ASHRAE Journal
51
dard 90.1 doesn’t provide any guidance on how to specify
these tests.
The Title 24-2005 acceptance requirements include the fol-
lowing systems:
• Minimum outdoor air for constant and variable air volume
systems;
• Controls for packaged single zone systems with program-
mable thermostats;
• Duct leakage testing for small single zone units;
• Operation of air-side economizers;
• Demand controlled ventilation (DCV) systems;
• Variable air volume fan controls; and
• Variable ﬂ ow hydronic system valve leakage and pump
controls.
The Title 24 acceptance tests are quite detailed. For example,
the test for minimum ventilation airﬂ ow on variable air volume
systems speciﬁ es the following:
• That airﬂ ow be measured and balanced to within 10% of
the design value;
• That the minimum ventilation airﬂ ow is tested both at
scheduled design supply airﬂ ow and at a minimum supply
airﬂ ow condition;
• That the minimum supply airﬂ ow condition is deﬁ ned as
the greater of either 30% of design airﬂ ow or the sum of
all of the VAV boxes at minimum position; and

• That DCV and economizer controls be disabled during
these tests.
All Title 24 acceptance tests have a similar level of detail.
Documentation for the acceptance tests is submitted to the
building department long after the permit is set but prior to the
issuance of a certiﬁ cate of occupancy. The tests can only take
place after all systems are installed and operational. The building
department is supposed to withhold the certiﬁ cate of occupancy
pending successful demonstration of compliance with the tests.
California implemented these acceptance requirements start-
ing in October 2005. It is too early to assess the actual perfor-
mance of these new requirements. If they prove successful, it
is likely that similar tests will be submitted as a code change
proposal for Standard 90.1.
Simulation Approach to Compliance
Both Title 24 and Standard 90.1 have a simulation approach
to compliance. In Title 24, it is referred to as the Performance
Approach. In Standard 90.1 it is called the Energy Cost Bud-
get Method. In both cases, the project is simulated twice: as a
base case using a building that meets all of the mandatory and
prescriptive requirements, and as a proposed case representing
the proposed design and systems.
As demonstrated in the next example, a fundamental differ-
ence exists in the base case mapping of HVAC systems in Title
24 and Standard 90.1. Standard 90.1 uses the proposed HVAC
system as the basis for determining the budget HVAC system.
Title 24 determines a budget HVAC system based on both the
building type and proposed HVAC system. As demonstrated
in the following example, the use of building type in the Title
24 selection criteria can lead to some inequitable comparisons.
Consider a high-rise ofﬁ ce building served by a water-source
heat pump system.
Using the Standard 90.1 rules (Figure 1 and Table 2), our
example would map to “System 6” a water-source heat pump
system. This can be seen in Figure 1 using the following cri-
teria: condenser cooling source equals water/ground; heating
system classiﬁ cation equals heat pump; and using the system
and occupancy column for single zone nonresidential systems.
Table 2 provides a description of “System 6.” Title 24 on the
other hand (Tables 3 and 4) would compare this to its “System
5,” a four-pipe fan coil system with a central chilled water
plant. This can be seen in Table 3 using the following criteria:
building type equals high rise nonresidential; and system type
equals single zone. Under the Title 24 rules, the determination
of whether it is an air-cooled or water-cooled plant is based on
the size of the load. Since a water-source heat pump system is
far less expensive (and less efﬁ cient) than a four-pipe fan coil
system this is arguably an inequitable comparison.
Title 24 developed its current HVAC system mapping
before Standard 90.1. Due to issues such as the one in the
earlier example, a group is working on proposed changes to
the 2008 version of Title 24 that will be closer to the Standard
90.1 rules.
Makeup Air for Kitchen and Fume Exhaust Systems
Standard 90.1 has two requirements for makeup air that
don’t appear in Title 24. The ﬁ rst (6.5.7.1) requires that make-
up air for kitchen exhaust hoods with a capacity of greater than
5,000 cfm (2360 L/s) come from air that is either tempered
(not fully conditioned) or would otherwise be exhausted due
to ventilation requirements. The second (6.5.7.2) requires that
fume exhaust systems with a capacity of greater than 15,000
cfm (7079 L/s) have one of the following features:
• Designed for variable air volume (supply and exhaust);
• Use tempered air for makeup; or
54
ASHRAE Journal
ashrae.org
April 2006
• Use heat recovery for the makeup air.
Both requirements are being considered for the 2008 version
of the Title 24 standard.
Restrictions on HVAC System Selection
Title 24 has three signiﬁ cant prescriptive restrictions on
HVAC system selection that don’t exist in Standard 90.1.
The ﬁ rst is a limitation on the use of electric resistance heat as
the primary heat (or reheat) source for HVAC systems. Systems
that use gas heating or heat pumps comply with this prescriptive
requirement. Systems that use exclusively electric resistance
heating (or reheat) typically have to show compliance through
the Performance Approach.
The Title 24 requirement allows some limited use of electric
resistance heating under the prescriptive method (such as a
small fan-coil serving a lobby), but it largely restricts the use
of electric resistance heat as the primary heat source for the
building. This has been in Title 24 since the 1990s. A similar
measure was in the second draft of Standard 90.1R and was the
subject of an intensive negative lobbying effort by a consortium
of manufacturers and utility organizations. As a result, the 90.1
proposed restriction on the use of electric resistance heat was
dropped in an effort to achieve consensus.
A prescriptive limitation on the use of air-cooled chillers was
introduced to Title 24 in the 2005 version of the standard. This
requirement is triggered by new plants or plant expansions that
are greater than 300 tons (1055 kW). In either case, air-cooled
chillers are limited to a maximum of 100 tons (352 kW) installed
capacity (for cooling of computer rooms and other 24/7 loads).
Exceptions are made for areas with poor water quality and where
air-cooled chillers are integrated with thermal storage systems.
This requirement was justiﬁ ed through a thorough life-cycle cost
study that included the costs of chemical treatment, maintenance,
housing for water-cooled chillers and water costs.
Under the economic criteria used by the CEC, the energy
savings on the water-cooled systems outweighed the additional
ﬁ rst costs of a water-cooled system. The analysis was thoroughly
reviewed but not refuted by several major chiller manufacturers.
If the Title 24 analysis was updated to include a broader range
of climates, it might be appropriate for Standard 90.1. However,
as has already been demonstrated with the proposed electric
resistance heat provision, a life-cycle cost justiﬁ ed measure can
still fail if strong opposition exists from the manufacturers or
other organized groups.
Also in the 2005 version of Title 24, a prescriptive limitation on
centrifugal fan cooling tower was introduced. This requirement has
exceptions for towers that need acoustical treatment and towers
Advertisement formerly in this space.
April 2006
ASHRAE Journal
55
with ducted inlets and/or discharge. No ex-
ception was provided for towers needing a
low proﬁ le for architectural reasons. Since
centrifugal fan towers use approximately
twice the energy of propeller fan towers
for the same duty (and typically cost less),
this is another provision that likely would
meet the Standard 90.1 cost-effectiveness
criteria.
Conclusion
The development of Title 24 and Stan-
dard 90.1 have been complementary. The
first California building energy code
came directly from Standard 90 and spe-
ciﬁ c provisions have transferred in both
directions since that time.
As discussed in this article a few sig-
niﬁ cant provisions in the 2005 version
of Title 24 could ﬁ nd their way to Stan-
dard 90.1. These include the acceptance
requirements, and possibly the prescrip-
tive limitations for air-cooled chillers
and centrifugal fan cooling towers. Two
important difference in the standards
will naturally limit this exchange: 1) the
standards are designed for different cli-
mates (e.g., Standard 90.1 must deal with
dehumidiﬁ cation devices and controls for
the high design wet-bulb climates east of
the Rockies); and 2) the ASHRAE con-
sensus process might limit the adoption
of measures that target speciﬁ c classes of
equipment such as the prescriptive limita-
tions of electric resistance heat and to a
lesser degree the prescriptive limitation
of air-cooled chillers.
The standards development process
is time consuming and, at times, quite
frustrating. Although the requirements
are usually initiated with engineering
and life-cycle cost analysis they inevita-
bly encounter issues such as availability
in the market, reliability/persistence
and how the measure will affect market
shares among competing technologies.
No matter how bruising the process,
at the end of the day we have results
that have had a positive impact on the
economy and the environment. The
author encourages readers to take part
in the process. Standard 90.1 has a big
table, and there’s always room for good
ideas if you have the inclination and time
to promote them.
