36
ASHRAE Journal
ashrae.org
April 2006
A
ttics or roofs can be designed and constructed to be either
vented or unvented in any hygrothermal zone (Map 1). The
choice of to vent or not vent is a design and construction choice,
not a requirement determined by physics or by building codes. The
model codes allow both vented and unvented roof assemblies. The
applicable physics impacts the design of attic or roof systems as
does the applicable building code, but neither limit the choice.
By Joseph Lstiburek, Ph.D., P.Eng., Fellow ASHRAE
Throughout the balance of this article
the terms attic and roof are used inter-
changeably.
In cold climates, the primary purpose
of attic or roof ventilation is to maintain
a cold roof temperature to control ice
dams created by melting snow, and to
vent moisture that moves from the con-
ditioned space to the attic. Melted snow,
in this case, is caused by heat loss from
the conditioned space. The heat loss is
typically a combination of air leakage
and conductive losses.
The air leakage is due to exﬁ ltration
from the conditioned space (often be-
About the Author
Joseph W. Lstiburek, Ph.D., P.Eng., is a prin-
cipal of Building Science Corporation in West-
ford, Mass.
Photo 1: Rigid insulation
can be installed above the
roof deck, or air-imper-
meable insulation (typi-
cally spray foam [shown
at left]) is installed under
the roof deck in direct
contact with it.
© 2006 American Society of Heating, Refrigerating and Air-Conditioning Engineers, Inc. (www.ashrae.org). Published in ASHRAE Journal (Vol. 48, April
2006). For personal use only. Additional distribution in either paper or digital form is not permitted without ASHRAE’s permission.
April 2006
ASHRAE Journal
37
cause a ceiling air barrier is not present) and from leaky supply
ductwork (often because ductwork located in attics is not sealed)
and from penetrations like non-airtight recessed lights.
The conductive losses are usually from supply ductwork and
equipment located in attic spaces above ceiling insulation (duct-
work is typically insulated only to R-6, whereas ceiling insulation
levels are above R-30). Conductive losses also occur directly
through insulation, or where insulation is missing or thin.
In hot climates, the primary purpose of attic or roof venti-
lation is to expel solar heated air from the attic to lessen the
building cooling load.
The amount of attic cavity ventilation is speciﬁ ed by numer-
ous ratios of free vent area to insulated ceiling area ranging from
1:150 to 1:600 depending on which building code is consulted,
with 1:300 the most common.
Control of ice dams, moisture accumulation and heat gain can
also be successfully addressed by unvented attic or roof design.
Why Two Approaches: Vented and Unvented?
Vented attic and roof construction have a long history of
successful performance. Why change a good thing?
As the complexity of attic and roof assemblies increases, the
difﬁ culty to construct vented assemblies also increases. The
more complex a roof geometry, the easier it is to construct the
assembly in an unvented
conditioned manner. With
complex roof designs, mul-
tiple dormers, valleys, hips,
skylights combined with
cathedral construction with
interior sofﬁ ts, trey ceil-
ings and multiple service
penetrations, it often is
not practical to construct a
vented roof assembly with
an airtight interior air bar-
rier at the ceiling plane.
Additionally, it is more
common to locate mechani-
cal systems and ductwork in attic spaces. When such ductwork
is leaky, signiﬁ cant problems can occur (Figure 1
(F
(
). There are
signiﬁ cant energy and durability advantages to move the thermal
boundary and pressure boundary (air barrier) to the underside of
the roof deck,1 thereby locating these mechanical systems and
ductwork within the building conditioned spaces (Figure 2
(F
(
).
In high wind regions, particularly in coastal areas, wind-
driven rain is a problem with vented roof assemblies. Addi-
tionally, during high wind events, vented sofﬁ t collapse leads
to building pressurization and window blowout and roof loss
due to increased uplift. Unvented roofs, principally due to the
robustness of their sofﬁ t construction, outperform vented roofs
during hurricanes. They are safer.
In coastal areas, salt spray and corrosion are a major concern
with steel frames, metal roof trusses and truss plate connectors
in vented attics.
Finally, in wildﬁ re zones, unvented roofs and attics have
signiﬁ cant beneﬁ ts in terms of ﬁ re safety over vented roof
assemblies.
Approach
The main strategy that should be used when designing roof
or attics to be free from moisture problems and ice dams, along
with control of heat gain or heat loss regardless of ventilation
approach, is the elimination of air movement, particularly ex-
ﬁ ltrating air in cold climates and inﬁ ltrating air in hot and hot,
humid climates. This can be accomplished by the installation
of an air barrier system or by the control of the air pressure
difference across the assembly (depressurizing a building
enclosure reduces the exﬁ ltration of interior air, pressurizing
a roof assembly with exterior air also reduces the exﬁ ltration
of interior air).
Air barrier systems are typically the most common approach,
with air pressure control approaches limited to remedial work
on existing structures.2
Vapor diffusion should be considered a secondary moisture
transport mechanism when designing and building attics. Speciﬁ c
vapor retarders are often unnecessary if air movement is controlled
or if control of condensing surface temperatures is provided.
Vented Design
Vented attics should
not communicate with the
conditioned space. They
should be coupled to the
exterior. Therefore, an air
barrier at the ceiling line,
such as sealed gypsum
board, is needed to isolate
the attic space from the
conditioned space. Ideally,
no services such as HVAC
distribution ducts, air han-
dlers, plumbing or fire
sprinkler systems should
be located external to the air barrier (Figure 3
(F
(
).
The recommended ventilation ratio to provide for vented attic
assemblies when an air barrier is present is the 1:300 ratio (as
speciﬁ ed by most building codes). This is based principally on
good historical experience and simple psychrometrics.3
In vented cathedral ceiling, assemblies a minimum 2 in. (51
mm) clear airspace is recommended between the underside
of the roof deck and the top of the cavity insulation. This is
not a code requirement but ought to be (only 1 in. [25 mm]
is typically speciﬁ ed in the model codes). It is the author’s
experience that typical installation practices and construction
tolerances do not result in airspace of at least 1 in. (25 mm)
and rarely is it clear.
In addition to an air barrier at the ceiling line, a Class II
vapor retarder (see “Recommend Practice” sidebar) should be
installed in Climate Zones 6 or higher (Map 1
(M
(
).
Map 1: Hygrothermal zones.
38
ASHRAE Journal
ashrae.org
April 2006
Figure 1: Ductwork exterior to thermal and pressure boundary.
• Supply ductwork and air handler leakage can be more than
20% of the ﬂ ow through the system.
• Leakage out of the supply system into the vented attic results in
an equal quantity of inﬁ ltration. In cold climates this can lead to
ice dam creation; in hot humid climates, this leads to high latent
loads due to inﬁ ltration into the conditioned space. In all climates
this leads to thermal penalties—increased energy consumption
in the order of 20% of the total space conditioning load.1
• In hot, humid climates condensation on ductwork and air
handlers located in vented attics is common.
Figure 2: Ductwork interior to thermal and pressure boundary.
• Duct leakage does not result in inﬁ ltration or exﬁ ltration (air
change) as ductwork is located within the conditioned space.
• This results in signiﬁ cant energy savings compared to Figure 1.1
Class I vapor retarders (i.e., vapor barriers [see “Vapor
Retarder Deﬁ nitions” sidebar]) can be installed in vented attic
assemblies in Climate Zones 6 or higher (Map 1
(M
(
) but should
be avoided in other climate zones as top side condensation can
occur in summer months during air conditioning periods.
No interior attic assembly side vapor control is required or
recommended in climate zones other than Climate Zones 6 or
higher (Map 1
(M
(
) for vented attic assemblies (note the distinc-
tion, this is not the case for unvented attic assemblies as will
be discussed later). With vented attic assemblies moisture that
diffuses into the attic space from the conditioned space is vented
to the exterior by attic ventilation.
Unvented Design
Unvented attic design falls into two categories: systems where
condensing surface temperatures are not controlled (Figure
4), and systems where condensing surface temperatures are
controlled (Figure 5
(F
(
). The two categories essentially are the de-
marcation between regions where cold weather conditions occur
with sufﬁ cient frequency and intensity that sufﬁ cient moisture
accumulation from interior sources can occur on an uninsulated
roof deck to risk mold, corrosion and decay problems.
The key is to keep the roof deck, the principle condensing
surface in roof assemblies, sufﬁ ciently warm throughout the
year or to prevent interior moisture-laden air from accessing
the roof deck. This can be accomplished in several ways: the
local climate may be such that the roof deck stays warm, or rigid
insulation can be installed above the roof deck, or air-imper-
meable insulation (typically spray foam [Photo 1]) is installed
under the roof deck in direct contact with it.
Where rigid insulation is installed above the roof deck, or
air impermeable insulation (spray foam) is installed under
the roof deck condensing surface temperatures are said to be
controlled.
The climate demarcation is a distinction between regions
where the monthly average temperature remains above 45°F
(7°C) throughout the year and where it drops below 45°F (7°C)
during the year. An additional criterion is also necessary: keep-
ing the interior relative humidity below 45% during the coldest
part of the year.
These criteria were selected for two reasons. First, by keeping
the roof deck above 45°F (7°C), condensation will not occur
unless the dew-point temperature of the interior air exceeds
40
ASHRAE Journal
ashrae.org
April 2006
• Potential for condensation in Phoenix with unvented roof
(see also curve).
• No potential for condensation on the underside of the roof sheath-
ing until interior moisture levels exceed 50% RH at 70°F.
Figure 3: Vented roof assembly.
• Roof insulation thermal resistance at roof perimeter
should be equal or greater to thermal resistance of exte-
rior wall.
• 1:300 ventilation ratio recommended.
45°F (7°C) and this air contacts the roof deck. This interior
dew-point temperature is approximately equal to an interior
conditioned space temperature of 70°F (21°C). at an interior
relative humidity of 45%. These are interior moisture condi-
tions that can be avoided easily with air change/ventilation or
the avoidance of over humidiﬁ cation during the coldest month
of the year in the climate zones speciﬁ ed.
Second, a monthly average temperature was selected, rather
than a design heating temperature, as it is more representative
of building enclosure performance. Short term, intermittent
“spikes” in parameters/environmental loads are of signiﬁ cant
interest to structural engineers and in the sizing of equipment,
but are not typically relevant to moisture-induced deterioration.
Wood-based roof sheathing typical to residential construction
has sufﬁ cient hygric buffer capacity to absorb, redistribute and
rerelease signiﬁ cant quantities of condensed moisture should
intermittent condensation occur during cold nights when
sheathing temperatures occasionally dip below 45°F (7°C). The
average monthly temperatures more accurately reﬂ ect moisture
content in wood-based assemblies.
The temperature criteria also are based on tile rooﬁ ng sys-
tems, not membranes and asphalt shingles. Membrane roofs
and shingle roofs can experience night sky cooling that can
depress roof deck temperatures signiﬁ cantly below ambient
air temperatures. When membrane roofs and asphalt shingles
are used, it typically is necessary to install rigid insulation
above the roof deck or install air impermeable insulation
below the roof deck.
The demarcation between regions that require the control of
condensing surface temperatures and regions that do not can
be obtained by consulting climate information or from Map
1. Both hot, humid and hot, dry climate zones meet the 45°F
(7°C) roof deck criteria. However, the high interior relative
humidities found in buildings located in hot-humid climate
zones during the winter do not always meet the 45% interior
relative humidity criteria. Therefore, the only zone that meets
both of these requirements is the hot, dry hygrothermal region.
Only hot, dry climates do not require the control of condensing
surface temperatures or the use of air impermeable insulation
at the underside of the roof deck (Photo 2
(P
(
). All other regions
require some form of control.
Control of condensing surface temperatures typically involves
the installation of insulating sheathing above the roof deck. In
residential wood frame construction this involves installing rigid
insulation between the roof shingles and the roof plywood or
Figure 4: Condensing surface temperature (underside of roof sheathing) not controlled.

sheathing until moisture levels exceed 40% RH at 70°F when
rigid insulation is not present.
• Rigid insulation is recommended in this roof assembly to raise
the condensation potential above 50% RH at 70°F.
• Ratio of R-value between rigid insulation and batt insulation
is climate-dependent.
Figure 6: Compact unvented roof assembly.
• All insulation located above air barrier membrane.
• Optimum roof design for pool and spa enclosures.
OSB. The installation of the rigid insulation elevates the tem-
perature of the roof deck to minimize condensation.
Figures 4 and 5
d
illustrate the difference between two funda-
mental systems. Figure 4 shows the potential for condensation
of an unvented roof assembly in Phoenix, which is located in
a hot, dry climate zone. This roof assembly has no insulating
sheathing installed above the roof deck.
Figure 5 shows the potential for condensation of an unvented
roof assembly in Dallas, which is located in a mixed-humid
climate zone. Note that this roof assembly has rigid insulation
installed above the roof deck to control the condensation poten-
tial. The thermal resistance of the rigid insulation (thickness)
necessary to control condensation depends on the severity of
the climate. The colder the climate, the greater the resistance
of the rigid insulation required.
Figure 6 shows a roof design that is not dependant on
6 6
controlling interior moisture levels (as the other roof designs
previously discussed). The absence of cavity insulation in this
design yields the highest condensing surface temperature of
all of the designs presented. All of the insulation is installed
over the top of the roof deck. In this particular design, the
condensing surface is the air barrier membrane installed over
the wood decking.
In extreme snow regions, it is necessary to add a vented air
space between the roof cladding (shingles) and the rigid insula-
tion to ﬂ ush heat away trapped due to the insulating value of the
snow (the snow becomes an insulating blanket). This approach
creates a vented-unvented hybrid roof assembly (Figure 7
(F
(
).
7)
7
In these types of unvented roof assemblies (except Figures
6 and 7
d d
), interior vapor barriers (Class I vapor retarders [see
7)
7
sidebar]) are not recommended as these assemblies are expected
to be able to dry towards the interior.
Instead of installing rigid insulation above the roof deck to
control condensing surface temperature, air-impermeable in-
sulation can be installed in direct contact with the underside of
the structural roof deck (Figure 8
(F
(
). Air-impermeable insulations
are typically low density or high density spray foams. Netted
or blown cellulose, ﬁ berglass or rock wool insulation are not
considered air impermeable.

insulation, including any covering adhered continuously to the
bottom side should have a vapor permeance of 1 perm or less (i.e.,

or lower—see sidebar). This can be done by applying

) without any
additional provision for vapor diffusion resistance.
High density spray foam insulation, which is con-
sidered an “air impermeable insulation (air perme-
ance of not more than 0.02 L/s ·m2 at 75 Pa pressure
differential tested according to ASTM E 2178 or E 283, identi-
cal to the deﬁ nition of an air barrier material in the National
Building Code of Canada) can be used in combination with
other insulation systems that are not air impermeable (Figure
(F
(
9). In this particular instance, the high density foam insulation
controls the access of interior moisture to the roof deck by air
movement and by diffusion. This approach is similar in effect
to the approach described in Figure 10 where rigid insulation
is placed above the roof deck.
Effect on Shingle Life
In general, shingles installed on unvented attic assemblies oper-
ate at a slightly higher temperature. This has impacts on the dura-
Photo 2: Netted cellulose. Air permeable insulation in
hot-dry climate
bility of roof assemblies. A 2°F or 3°F (1°C to 7°C) rise in average
temperature is typical for asphalt shingles and a corresponding
10°F (5.6°C) rise in average temperature for sheathing.4,5,6
All other things being equal, applying the Arrhenius equa-
tion,7 expect a 10% reduction in useful service life. This is
comparable to the effect of the installation of radiant barriers.
What is more signiﬁ cant is that the color of shingles and roof
orientation have a more profound effect on the durability of
shingles than the choice of venting or not venting,8 which is
double or triple the effect of venting/non venting.
Inward Moisture Drive
Inward moisture drive through shingles was investigated by
the author over a two-year period in a hot, humid climate in
Figure 8: Air impermeable spray foam insulation.
• In Climate Zones 5 or higher a Class II vapor retarder
is required on the interior of the spray foam layer.
• If high density foam is used in this assembly, a Class
II vapor retarder is not required in Climate Zones 5 or
higher as the high density foam itself qualiﬁ es as a Class
II vapor retarder.
• A thermal barrier is required to separate spray foams
from occupiable spaces due to the ﬁ re performance of
spray foam insulations.
44
ASHRAE Journal
ashrae.org
April 2006
Figure 10: Unvented ﬂ at roof assembly—rigid insulation.
• The thermal resistance (thickness) of the rigid insulation is
climate dependent and moisture load dependent.
• The colder the climate, the higher the thermal resistance
required for the rigid insulation.
• The higher the interior moisture load the higher the thermal
resistance required for the rigid insulation.
• Membrane roofs and shingle roofs can experience night sky
cooling that can depress roof deck temperatures signiﬁ cantly
below ambient air temperatures. When membrane roofs and
asphalt shingles are used it is typically necessary install rigid
insulation above the roof deck or install air impermeable
insulation below the roof deck.
Figure 9: Unvented ﬂ at roof assembly—high density spray
foam.
• High density spray foam insulation does not require an interior
vapor retarder in any climate.
• Membrane roofs and shingle roofs can experience night sky
cooling that can depress roof deck temperatures signiﬁ cantly
below ambient air temperatures. When membrane roofs and
asphalt shingles are used it is typically necessary to install
rigid insulation above the roof deck or install air impermeable
insulation below the roof deck.
Houston with various conﬁ gurations/permeabilities of rooﬁ ng
underlayments and under deck insulation systems. The inward
moisture drive was found to be small—not signiﬁ cant to the
moisture balance of the roof sheathing from a durability long
term service life perspective or to the latent load of the build-
ing or surface condensation. However, the inward moisture
drive was found to alter wood roof deck sheathing moisture
contents sufﬁ ciently to lead to shingle buckling.
The incidence of shingle buckling was not increased or
decreased based on the presence or absence of under roof
decking venting. In other works, venting roof decks does not
decrease shingle buckling.
Inward moisture drive can occur when moisture from the
interior accumulates at the underside of the roof deck (from
both interior spaces in cold climate and from roof venting at
night in hot, humid climates). The solar radiation serves to
drive the moisture inward away out of the sheathing, thereby
dropping the wood moisture content of the sheathing.
Inward moisture drive also can occur due to capillarity and
dew formation. The inward driven moisture causes the roof
sheathing to close as its moisture content goes up ﬁ rst thing
in the morning and then to open back up again later in the
day once the moisture has been driven out of the assembly
(Figure 11).
Vapor Retarder Deﬁ nitions
Class I Vapor Retarder:
0.1 perm or less
Class II Vapor Retarder: 1.0 perm or less and greater
than 0.1 perm
Class III Vapor Retarder: 10 perm or less and greater
than 1.0 perm
Class IV Vapor Retarder: Greater than 10 perm
Test Procedure for vapor retarders: ASTM E-96 Test
Method A (the desiccant method or dry cup method)
Class I:
Sheet polyethylene
Class II: Kraft facing on ﬁ berglass batts
“Membrane” smart vapor barrier
Typical vapor barrier paint
Class III: Typical latex paint
Class IV: Most building papers and house wraps
April 2006
ASHRAE Journal
45
Insulated roof assemblies over unvented attics and other
conditioned spaces should comply with all of the following
conditions:
1. Where wood shingles and shakes are used a minimum
continuous ¼ in. vented air space separates the shingles
or shakes and the rooﬁ ng underlayment placed over the
structural sheathing.\*
2. Either “a” or “b” depending on whether the insulation
applied directly under the structural roof sheathing is air-
impermeable or air-permeable. Air-impermeable is deﬁ ned
as having an air permeance of not more than 0.02 L/s · m2
at 75 Pa pressure differential tested according to ASTM E
2178 or E 283.
a. Air-impermeable insulation: Insulation shall be applied
in direct contact to the interior surface of the structural
roof sheathing. In Climate Zones 5, 6, 7, and 8, where air
impermeable insulation is used, the insulation, including any
coating or covering applied or installed continuously in direct
contact with the interior side of the insulation, must have
a water vapor permeance of 1.0 perm (57.4 mg/s · m2· Pa)
or less, determined using Procedure A of ASTM E 96.
Recommended Practice
Table 1: Prescriptive R-values for rigid insulation above the
structural roof deck when air permeable insulation is used
below the structural roof deck
Climate Zones
Min. R-Value Above
Structural Roof Deck
2B (Tile Roof Only)\*\*
3B (Tile Roof Only)\*\*
1, 2A, 2B, 3A, 3B, 3C
4C
4A, 4B
5
6
7
8
0 (None Required)
R-5
R-10
R-15
R-20
R-25
R-30
R-35
\* Backventing of wood shingles and shakes is required to control decay—liquid
water entry via capillarity and wind-driven rain can become trapped between wood
shingles/wood shakes and rooﬁ ng underlayment.
\*\* Membrane roofs and shingle roof can experience night sky cooling that can
depress roof deck temperatures signiﬁ cantly below ambient air temperatures.
b. Air-permeable insulation: Rigid insulation shall be installed
above the structural roof sheathing as speciﬁ ed in Table 1.
Summary
Vented and unvented attic/roof designs can be used in all hygro-
thermal regions. But, the designs need to be climate sensitive.
Control of ice dams, moisture accumulation and heat gain
can be successfully addressed by using vented and unvented
attic or roof design.
The choice of the venting approach is up to the designer.
Vented attic/roof designs have the advantage of a long, proven
historical track record. However, they work best with airtight
ceiling/attic interfaces and where ductwork and air handlers are
not located within attic spaces.
Unvented attic/roof designs have the advantage of providing
conditioned spaces for ductwork and air handlers. However, they
require different approaches in different climate locations.
References
1. Rudd, A.F., J.W. Lstiburek and N.A. Moyer. 1997. “Measurement
of attic temperatures and cooling energy use in vented and sealed attics
in Las Vegas, Nevada.” EEBA Conference.
2. Lstiburek, J.W. and J. Carmody. 1994. Moisture Control Hand-
book. New York: John Wiley & Sons.
3. Handegord, G.O. and G. Giroux 1984. “An Attic Condensation
Ventilation Model.” Division of Building Research, National Research
Council of Canada, Ottawa, Canada.
4. Parker, D.S and J.R. Sherwin. 1998. “Comparative summer attic
thermal performance of six roof constructions.” ASHRAE Transac-
tions 2:1084–1092.
5. Rudd, A.F. and J.W. Lstiburek. 1998. “Vented and sealed attics in
hot climates.” ASHRAE Transactions 2:1199 – 1210.
6. TenWolde, A. and W.B. Rose 1999. “Issues related to venting of
attics and cathedral ceilings.” ASHRAE Transactions 1:851 – 857.
7. Cash, C.G., D.M. Bailey, et al. 2005. “Predictive service life

tests for rooﬁ ng membranes.” 10DBMC International Conference on
Durability of Building Materials and Components.
8. Rose, W. B. 1991. “More data on shingle overheating with un-
vented roofs.” Energy Design Update (3).

ASHRAE Journal
ashrae.org
May 2006
Insulation
M
ost engineers and architects are familiar with using insula-
tion to reduce heating and cooling loads and control noise
in building envelopes. Insulations used for pipes, ducts, tanks, and
equipment are not as familiar to them. The installed cost of these
materials is usually a small part of the total cost of a project. As
a result, mechanical insulation\* is often overlooked or improperly
speciﬁ ed in commercial and industrial construction projects.
By G. Christopher P. Crall, P.E., Member ASHRAE
In recognition of this, a new chapter
has been added to the 2005 ASHRAE
Handbook—Fundamentals to speciﬁ -
cally address mechanical insulation. The
new chapter, Chapter 26, consolidates
and reorganizes much of the informa-
tion contained in earlier versions of
the Handbook into a single location.
In addition, a significant amount of
new information has been included
to assist designers in specifying these
insulation systems.
The chapter was developed by ASHRAE
Technical Committee (TC) 1.8, Mechani-
cal Systems Insulation. TC 1.8 is a rela-
tively new committee concerned with the
application and performance of thermal
and acoustical insulation systems used on
pipes, tanks, equipment and ducts.
Chapter Overview
Chapter 26 is organized into four
major sections: Design Considerations,
Materials and Systems, Installation, and
Design Data.
\*The term mechanical insulation has been used
to describe these insulation products because
they typically are speciﬁ ed by the mechanical
engineers involved in building projects.
ti
nsulat
I
ion
In
o
s
For Mechanical Systems
© 2006 American Society of Heating, Refrigerating and Air-Conditioning Engineers, Inc. (www.ashrae.org). Published in ASHRAE Journal (Vol. 48, May
2006). For personal use only. Additional distribution in either paper or digital form is not permitted without ASHRAE’s permission.
May 2006
ASHRAE Journal
77
G. Christopher P. Crall, P.E., is a mechanical engineer and principal at
CCrall Consulting in Gahanna, Ohio. He serves as chair of ASHRAE Technical
Committee 1.8, Mechanical Systems Insulation.
The Design Considerations section is probably the most
important section of the chapter and is intended to identify and
provide some guidance on the design objectives involved when
specifying mechanical insulation systems. The design objectives
discussed in the chapter include:
• Energy conservation: minimizing unwanted heat loss/gain
as well as preserving natural and ﬁ nancial resources;
• Personnel protection: controlling surface temperatures to
avoid contact burns;
• Condensation control: avoiding condensation by keeping
the surface temperature above the dew-point temperature of
the surrounding air;
• Process control: minimizing temperature change in process
ﬂ uids where close control is needed;
• Freeze protection: minimizing energy required for heat
tracing systems and/or extending the time to freezing in the
event of system failure;
• Noise control: reducing noise in mechanical systems; and
• Fire safety: protecting critical building elements and slow-
ing the spread of ﬁ re in buildings.
The importance of this section stems from the fact that multiple
design objectives usually exist. Failure to focus on these multiple
objectives can lead to problems with the insulation system.
Chilled Water Piping Example
One example of a project with multiple objectives is specifying
the proper insulation design for a chilled-water piping system. In
a typical project, there are usually at least two distinct design con-
siderations: energy conservation and condensation control. Both
considerations must be addressed during the design phase.
Energy conservation is important, and the mechanical engi-
neer responsible may refer to ANSI/ASHRAE/IESNA Standard
90.1-2004, Energy Standard for Buildings Except Low-Rise
Residential Buildings, to select insulation thicknesses. Standard
90.1-2004 uses the “economic thickness” concept to specify in-
sulation thicknesses for a variety of pipe sizes and applications.
Using the values from the standard is a reasonable solution to
the energy conservation objective. However, the engineer may
not realize that Standard 90.1-2004 does not address the objec-
tive of condensation control. Depending on the location of the
chilled-water piping, additional thickness may be required to
eliminate or minimize moisture condensation on the exterior
surface of the insulation. The thicknesses speciﬁ ed in Standard
90.1-2004 should be viewed as minimum thicknesses.
For cold systems, avoiding surface condensation requires
keeping the temperature of the exposed surface above dew-
point temperature of the surrounding air. Insulation can help by
reducing the heat ﬂ ow to the cold pipe and effectively raising
the surface temperature. This approach is effective if the rela-
tive humidity of the surrounding air is below 90%. Above 90%
RH, the thicknesses required to prevent surface condensation
become impractical, and other approaches are required.
The calculations involved are relatively straightforward, and
are summarized in the Design Data section of Chapter 26. They
can be best understood by considering an energy balance at the
insulation surface. At steady state, the heat ﬂ ux from the ambi-
ent air to the insulation surface must equal the heat ﬂ ux from
the insulation surface to the cold pipe. In equation form:
qsurf = q

be greater than the dew-point temperature of the surrounding

ASHRAE Journal

May 2006
where
Tdp

T = dew-point temperature of the ambient air
By examining Equation 6, we can see that avoiding surface
condensation requires a designer to know the conductivity of
the insulation, the surface heat transfer coefﬁ cient, and the
conditions of the ambient air. By way of illustration, Equation
6 has been solved for some typical conditions and the results
tabulated in Table 1, which shows insulation thickness as a
function of relative humidity of 80°F (27°C) ambient air.
At high relative humidities, the thickness required to prevent
surface condensation increases dramatically, and becomes
impractical above about 90% RH. For conditions where the
dew-point temperature approaches the dry-bulb temperature
(i.e., 100% RH), the thickness required approaches inﬁ nity.
This simple procedure can be used as a ﬁ rst order estimate.
In reality, number of factors
complicate the calculation.
First, for radial geometries,
the thickness calculated by
Equation 6 represents the
effective thickness (roror · ln(roror /
ri)); the actual wall thickness
of pipe insulation required
(roror –ri) will be slightly less.
In addition, the surface heat
transfer coefﬁ cient is not a
constant, but varies as a func-
tion of surface temperature,
air velocity, orientation, and
surface emittance. Likewise,
the conductivity of insula-
tion varies with temperature.
When performing these calculations, it is important to use
the effective thermal conductivity of the insulation material,
which can be approximated by evaluating the conductivity
at the mean temperature across the insulation layer. It is also
important to use the actual dimensions for pipe and tubing
insulation. Standard dimensions of pipe and tubing insulations
are included in Chapter 26.
These complications are readily handled for a variety of
boundary conditions using available computer programs, such
as the North American Insulation Manufacturers Association
3E Plus® insulation thickness computer program, which is
downloadable at no charge from www.pipeinsulation.org.
We have shown that Equation 6 can easily be used to de-
velop a ﬁ rst order estimate of the thickness required to prevent
surface condensation. A more difﬁ cult task is determining
conditions of the ambient air surrounding the cold pipe.
If the pipe is located outdoors, weather data is readily
available to guide the design. For these outdoor applications,
there will generally be many hours per year where the air
is saturated or nearly saturated. A review of typical hourly
weather data for Houston indicates that more than 2,000 hours
per year exist when the relative humidity is greater than 90%.
Even Minneapolis experiences more than 600 hours per year
over 90%RH.
For outdoor applications, no amount of insulation prevents
surface condensation all the time, and these installations must
include “weather barriers” to protect the insulation system from
damage due to surface condensation, even if they are protected
from wind-driven rain.
For indoor environments, the situation is not as straight-
forward. While the systems in most buildings are designed to
control temperature within the comfort range, active humidity
control is rare. Humidity levels will vary depending on the
outdoor conditions, moisture release from occupants and ac-
tivities within the building envelope, and the operation of the
cooling system. At the design stage, it is difﬁ cult to know the
humidity conditions to be expected at the various locations
within the building. This is particularly true within pipe chases
and mechanical rooms that
are sometimes vented to the
outdoor air. The designer ob-
viously must be aware of the
possibility of night/weekend
setback. Extended periods
of system shutdown also are
possible, and have been a
problem for schools, where
restart of cooling systems
often occurs in late summer
or early fall. Because of these
considerations, it is recom-
mended to design for relative
humidities of 75% to 80%,
even for chilled-water piping
in conditioned spaces.
Another important consideration for below ambient piping
is the possibility of water vapor ﬂ ow through the insulation to
the cold pipe. If the operating temperature of the cold ﬂ uid is
below the dew-point temperature of the ambient air, conden-
sation will occur on the cold surface of the pipe, creating a
vapor pressure gradient through the system. While the abso-
lute magnitude of this pressure difference is small (typically
less than 0.5 in. Hg [1.7 kPa]), in some installations it can
act over a large area for many hours per year. The control of
water vapor, therefore, is critical for piping and equipment
operating at below ambient temperatures. These systems
must either use a continuous and effective vapor retarder to
minimize the amount of water-vapor ingress, or incorporate a
means of removing any condensed water from the system. One
means of removing water is the use of a hydrophilic wicking
material to transport liquid water outside the system where it
can be evaporated to the ambient air.
This chilled water example illustrates two distinct design
objectives that must be addressed in most design projects. Some
projects may have additional objectives. Noise control and ﬁ re
safety often are involved. Hopefully, the new chapter will help
focus designers on the many issues.

and insulation with a thermal conductivity of 0.30 Btu · in/(h · ft2 °F).
May 2006
ASHRAE Journal
79
Conclusion
In closing, I highlight some of the information contained in
Chapter 26 that is new to the ASHRAE Handbook.
• The section on noise control includes a discussion of
concepts, terms and test methods speciﬁ c to this subject.
New data on the insertion losses for pipe insulation and
jacketing materials is included in tabular form.
• A section on ﬁ re safety discusses the requirements of the
various model building codes as they apply to mechanical
this section are tables of standard dimensions of pipe and
tubing insulation, both for rigid insulations and for standard
ﬂ exible insulations. The standard insulation sizes have been
available previously in insulation-industry publications, but
have not previously been published in the Handbook.
TC 1.8 is in the process of reviewing and updating Chapter
26. Readers are encouraged to review the new chapter and
provide suggestions to the TC 1.8 Handbook Subcommittee at
tc0108@ashrae.net.
insulations, as well as reviewing the
test methods used to evaluate the
ﬁ re safety performance of insula-
tion products.
• Corrosion of metals under insulation
is a signiﬁ cant issue that is often
overlooked during design. Corrosion
under insulation depends on many
factors, including the environment
and the operating temperature of the
metal. A discussion of the issues and
suggestions for minimizing corro-
sion are included.
• The Materials and Systems section
includes information on the various in-
sulation materials available and a short
discussion of the physical properties of
interest. A summary table is included
that presents key property information
and points readers to some of the many
applicable ASTM Material Standards.
This section also includes a discus-
sion of weather barriers and their
importance in protecting insulation
systems installed outdoors. The func-
tion of vapor retarders and a summary
of the various types of vapor-retarder
systems are discussed in some detail.
• The Installation section provides
information on current installation
practice. A discussion of securing
methods, hangers, and insulation ﬁ n-
ish is provided for each of the major
application areas (pipes, tanks, equip-
ment, and ducts). New information in
this section includes tables of saddle
lengths suitable for supporting insu-
lated pipes. The discussion on duct
insulation summarizes the consider-
ations speciﬁ c to that application, in
addition to providing sample calcula-
tions for temperature rise or drop in
duct systems.
• The Design Data section serves as a
catch-all for data thought to be useful
to designers. Signiﬁ cant new data in
Advertisement formerly in this space.
12
ASHRAE Journal
ashrae.org
May 2006
design is presented, along with lessons
learned.
Energy Study and Estimating
Since the prime contractor is at risk for
promised energy savings, an extensive
energy study was undertaken. For the
project to be justiﬁ ed, a favorable cost-
beneﬁ t during a 15-year period needed
to be proven. A signiﬁ cant number of
spreadsheets were developed for the es-
timating. The following are the highlights
of the energy estimating exercise:
• Parallel energy estimating was
performed independently by the
engineer and proposing contractor.
Results were compared and con-
trasted, with give-and-take between
the two versions, and ﬁ nal outcomes
agreed within a 10% tolerance.
• Twenty-two buildings were selected
to study electric meter charges, natu-
About the Author
Stephen W. Duda, P.E., is director of mechani-
cal engineering for Ross & Baruzzini in St. Louis.
He is a member of ASHRAE Technical Commit-
tees 9.01, 8.02, and Standing Standards Project
Committee 15.
U
ntil 2005, each of the 30+ buildings on Missouri State
University’s campus in Springﬁ eld was air conditioned via in-
dividual water chillers or packaged air-conditioning systems. Many
of the buildings on this 160 acre (65 ha) campus are heated us-
ing steam from a central boiler plant, distributed via a network of
subterranean pipe tunnels, but the air-conditioning systems were
a building-by-building mix of direct expansion packages, centrifugal
chillers, steam-driven absorption chillers, and air-cooled chillers.
The university recently opted to construct a campus district chilled
water system and connect many (but not all) of its buildings, mak-
ing use of new and existing equipment.
The funding mechanism for the project
was tied to future energy savings. The
university chose performance contracting
as the delivery method, with a prime con-
tractor whose compensation is contractu-
ally tied to energy performance. In turn,
the prime contractor hired a consulting
engineer to assist with energy estimating,
and to design the project.
This article details the ﬁ nancial, en-
ergy, and engineering considerations
behind the project. The final system
Chilled Water System
For University Campus
By Stephen W. Duda, P.E., Member ASHRAE
School HVAC
© 2006 American Society of Heating, Refrigerating and Air-Conditioning Engineers, Inc. (www.ashrae.org). Published in ASHRAE Journal (Vol. 48, May
2006). For personal use only. Additional distribution in either paper or digital form is not permitted without ASHRAE’s permission.
May 2006
ASHRAE Journal
13
ral gas use, maintenance costs, water consumption, and
ﬁ rst cost. The remaining campus buildings subjectively
were found to be unsuitable for connection to a proposed
new campus chilled water network, generally because of
distance from the core of the campus.
• Each building was studied twice: once to simulate the existing
system, and a second time for the proposed new system.
• Electric bills were studied for consumption and demand, in a
tiered manner exactly as billed by the local utility provider.
• Electric motors considered in the analysis included the
chiller, chilled water pumps, condenser water pumps, and
cooling tower fans.
• A 5°F (3°C) bin method was used to estimate part load
energy consumption. The number of hours per year in
each bin was obtained from public domain weather data.1
Although the bin method is, by deﬁ nition, not exact, it
was applied identically in the existing and proposed new
scenarios, so the differential between the two is thought
to be accurate within a reasonable tolerance. It did agree
within 10% of the proposing contractor’s parallel study.
It is recommended that before using bin methods, the
reader understand their proper application. Guidance2 sug-
gests bin methods should preferably use both temperature
bins and time periods (e.g., 5°F [3°C] temperature bins
and eight-hour daily time shifts corresponding to general
building occupancy and use).
• The difference in maintenance costs of a new campus
network vs. individual building systems was considered
signiﬁ cant. Therefore, maintenance costs were estimated
in accordance with 2003 ASHRAE Handbook—HVAC
Applications3 using a formula and tables found therein.
• Some of the existing chillers were steam-driven, single-
effect absorption machines. Open-cell cooling towers as-
sociated with single-effect absorption machines lose much
more water via evaporation than those associated with new
electric centrifugal chillers. (Recall that the task of a cool-
ing tower is to reject the energy input into a chiller from
both the chilled water and from the work input or prime
mover. Since single-effect absorption chillers have a much
lower coefﬁ cient of performance, their work input or prime
mover is much greater than with an electric centrifugal
chiller. Thus, their cooling tower must work harder.)
The costs of water consumption associated with cooling
tower evaporation, and the related chemical treatment costs,
therefore, were included in the utility estimate.
• Although none of the existing chillers use gas directly, many
existing chillers use steam-powered absorption. Gas is used
to create the steam, so gas is used indirectly. Natural gas bills
were estimated for both consumption and demand, using the
same bin method described for electric bill estimating. The
gas utility company’s rate structure was accurately embedded
within the mathematics of the spreadsheet.
• None of the proposed new chillers use either gas or
steam, so there was no study of gas bills for the proposed
new system.
• Construction cost was estimated for all costs associated
with the proposed new campus chilled water network,
including construction and professional design fees.
Finally, the annual cost of electric, gas, water and mainte-
nance for the existing systems and the proposed new systems
were converted to present day dollars using the Uniform Series
Present Worth Factor Method.4 The resultant net decrease in
Missouri State University saved energy by constructing a campus district chilled water system.
14
ASHRAE Journal
ashrae.org
May 2006
School HVAC
School HVAC
recurring costs was compared to the estimated ﬁ rst cost of
construction. Time value of money and inﬂ ation are taken into
account, resulting in an analysis that is considerably improved
over a simple payback method. The resulting cost beneﬁ t was
found for each building, and then for the system as a whole.
Energy Study Results
One factor discovered as a result of the energy study is a good
news/bad news predicament. Electric rates in the Midwest are
relatively low. How low? It is not unusual in Missouri to see con-
sumption rates at or below $0.03 per kWh, especially for larger
customers of the power company. This is good news for consum-
ers, as rates are quite low compared to the U.S. East and West
coast. However, this is also bad news for
consulting engineers and others trying to
promote energy conservation in Missouri.
With these rates, energy-savings measures
dependent on payback period and cost-
beneﬁ t analyses are rejected sometimes.
The actual electric rates in this particu-
lar case are $0.0291 per kWh consumption
and $5.80 per kilowatt demand (kWD). Of
the 22 buildings studied, ﬁ ve could not
offer a reasonable payback. These build-
ings are too far off the network, making
construction cost high, or buildings have
a new chiller already in place, minimizing
potential energy savings.
Therefore, 17 buildings became part of
the new campus chilled water network.
Six buildings were conﬁ gured as dis-
tributed producer plants using primary/
secondary pumping. Those six plants
feature a total of eight chillers (ﬁ ve new, three existing) with
an aggregate of 4,000 tons (14 000 kW) of refrigeration.
The ﬁ nal annual energy savings estimate is approximately
$804,000, which is found by summing all energy spreadsheets
calculated for the new system and subtracting those same
spreadsheets calculated for the old systems. The system was

placed on-line in Spring 2006, so no data is available yet to

struction cost was approximately $9,537,000.
New Campus Chilled Water Network
The result of all of the energy estimating and other consid-
erations is a new campus chilled water network, connecting
17 campus buildings and expandable for many others. Six of
these campus buildings were designated as producer buildings,
which generate and feed chilled water into the campus network.
The other 11 buildings are parasite buildings which use chilled
water from the network.
Each producer plant features one or two water chilling units
and a primary chilled water pump per
chiller. One or more secondary chilled
water pumps (with variable frequency
drive) feeds the campus network and pres-
surizes the campus chilled water mains to
setpoint. Additionally, a variable-speed
tertiary building pump feeds chilled water
to that particular building (Figure 1
(F
(
).
During off-peak conditions, fewer
chillers are needed, so some producer
buildings act as parasites (Figure 2
(F
(
). The
campus network takes advantage of load
diversity. For example, academic build-
ings, amenities buildings, and residential
buildings are not likely to experience
peak occupancy all at the same time. As
the system operates, the campus energy
management system learns which combi-
nation of chillers is most energy advanta-
geous at any given time by tracking and
logging results.
The parasite buildings feature a tertiary building pump to
draw chilled water out of the network and into that particular
building. Most of these tertiary pumps already existed, because
nearly every building already had its own chilled water system.
Many of them already included variable frequency drives. Those

Figure 4: Parasite building with backup chilled water ﬂ ow.
that did not received one via retroﬁ t. The few buildings with
three-way control valves at cooling coils will be retroﬁ tted with
two-way valves on an ongoing basis.
One additional energy-savings feature is the bypass around
the building pump. Depending on load conditions and proximity
to a producer plant, the network pressure—as measured locally
by differential pressure sensors— might be sufﬁ cient to force
chilled water through the building at any given time. In this
scenario, the building pump may not be needed and will be shut
off automatically, with ﬂ ow through the bypass (Figure 3
(F
(
).
Because most of these buildings previously had their own
chilled water system, a variety of chilled water supply and return
temperatures, ranges, and temperature differentials were found.
The new network features design peak supply chilled water of
42°F (6°C), return at 58°F (14°C), for a ﬂ ow rate of 1.5 gpm/ton
(0.33 L/s per kW refrigeration). This is typically a few degrees
cooler but a somewhat slower ﬂ ow rate than the conditions typi-
cally provided to those buildings in the past. Selective simulation
of a few such coils show no signiﬁ cant loss of coil performance
at the new conditions, without the use of mixing/blending valves
or other artiﬁ cial supply water temperature adjustment.
Site Piping
An existing network of tunnels carrying steam piping and
other utilities runs beneath much of the campus. In many cases
those tunnels were adequate to house the new chilled water
campus mains as well. However, some areas had no tunnels,
and other areas featured tunnels that were too crowded, so ap-
proximately 5,000 ft (1500 m) of pipe had to be buried directly.
An open-cut trench would have made quite a mess on campus,
forcing the disruption of foot trafﬁ c and removal of mature trees.
The University wanted as little disruption as possible, as the
construction coincided with its 100th anniversary celebration,
when dignitaries and media were attending ceremonies.
Therefore, a directional boring method was selected. The
pipe used in the operation was 10-in. (250 mm) PVC pipe with
a proprietary system of mechanically locked joints, capable of
withstanding several thousand pounds of force pulling against
Consideration was given to construction of an all-in-one
central campus chilled water plant, because of the obvious
advantages in locating the majority of the service, mainte-
nance and noisy equipment in one location. However, as we
found in the energy analysis phase of the project, to make the
cost-beneﬁ t attractive, nearly no new building construction
could be absorbed. Any signiﬁ cant chiller plant construction
cost could have scrapped the entire project.
So the designers had to look for space in existing buildings
to minimize ﬁ rst cost. Suitable space was available, but not all
in one area. Also, ﬁ rst cost could be reduced by using many
existing pumps in these locations. In addition, widely spread
producer plants kept main pipe sizes smaller to improve
cost-beneﬁ t.
Finally, some concern was expressed for heat pickup in
the campus network for the most remote buildings, if all
chilled water was generated in one location. By distributing
the generation of chilled water to several remote loca-
tions, no one building is unacceptably distant from a chilled
water plant.
Why Not One Large Chilled Water Plant?
20
ASHRAE Journal
ashrae.org
May 2006
School HVAC
School HVAC
this pipe to get it in the ground. Parallel bores were made up to
14 ft (4 m) below ground level, separated by 1 to 3 ft (0.3 to 1 m)
of dirt for insulation purposes. Some initial difﬁ culties occurred
with locating underground utilities and some upheaval of the
earth and pavement above. With the region’s soil strata, drilling
was somewhat difﬁ cult, requiring up to three attempts. In the end,
the university and the contractors expressed satisfaction with the
installation,7 and no leaks have been found to date.
Failure Mode Recovery
One of the many challenges faced by the consulting engineer
was the performance contracting delivery method itself. A con-
sulting engineer typically strives to make prudent decisions for
the long-term beneﬁ t of the owner, which might include some
spare capacity or redundant equipment built into the design. How-
ever, in the performance contracting delivery method, all major
ﬁ nancial decisions (beyond obvious code and standard-of-care
requirements) must be directly justiﬁ ed on an energy basis.
Based on energy savings, it made little ﬁ nancial sense to
spend money for demolition, as demolition yields no payback
over abandon-in-place. Similarly, spare or redundant capacity
cannot be justiﬁ ed on an energy basis. Because redundant chill-
ers are purchased (but theoretically never used except when
another fails), they offer no payback. These two ideas on this
project created concern for the failure mode scenario.
The resolution to this dilemma provided a result initially
satisfactory to all interests. The university initially was nervous
that failure of one of the producer plant chillers on a design day
would be intolerable. Because most parasite buildings formerly
had a chiller of their own, and because many of those chillers
are serviceable (albeit older and inefﬁ cient) those chillers
were left in place with normally closed valves (Figure 4
F
). This
scheme allows a backup chiller to be restarted in the event of
a partial failure of the campus network on a design day. In a
lesson learned, however, the university now plans to demolish
several of these backup chillers. They feel the maintenance to
keep them serviceable outweighs the risk of a failure.
Lessons Learned
First, low electric rates in the Midwest tend to reduce the
Why Not Variable Primary Flow?
Primary-only variable flow (VPF) was considered for
this project. One of the primary arguments in favor of VPF
systems is the need for fewer pumps and the need for less
pump ﬂ oor space5 compared to primary/secondary systems.
However, on this project every campus building previously
had its own chiller system, so most of the pumps and pump
ﬂ oor space needed for primary/secondary pumping were
already in place. Furthermore, there was a lack of familiarity
with VPF among the campus facilities staff, vs. a very thorough
understanding of primary/secondary.
One caution about VPF is the need for a more complicated
control strategy if grossly unequal-size chillers are used,6 as
is the case with this new system. Controls would be even
further complicated if VPF chillers were all attempting to feed
and pressurize a district chilled water network at disparate
locations. This controls burden raised discomfort with VPF
on the part of the prime contractor, who in addition to hav-
ing their compensation tied to energy savings performance,
is also a temperature controls system vendor.
For these reasons, VPF was rejected in favor of a more
traditional primary-secondary arrangement.
potential cost-beneﬁ t of attempted energy-saving projects,
making well-intentioned sustainable projects more difﬁ cult to
justify economically.
Second, not all campus buildings could be included on this
network due to lack of cost-beneﬁ t. Some buildings were too
far away, or already had a newer and relatively efﬁ cient chiller
already in place, thereby making connection to the network
ﬁ nancially nonviable.
Third, due to economic considerations, multiple distributed
producer plants tied together in a common chilled water network
won out over single central plant for this project.
Fourth, there exists some unfamiliarity with, and discom-
fort with, variable primary ﬂ ow as an alternative to primary/
secondary systems. VPF was considered for this project but
ultimately rejected.
Fifth, an attempt to provide redundant capacity by virtue of
leaving old chillers in place as standby units was lost when the
university decided to demolish many of them.
Sixth, directional boring for site piping was ultimately suc-
cessful after initial difﬁ culties.

G. Hansen, P.E., Member ASHRAE, contributed signiﬁ cantly
to the energy estimating discussed in this article. Jackson D.
Waterbury, P.E., Member ASHRAE, contributed signiﬁ cantly
to the design of the system described herein.

Systems.” ASHRAE Journal 44(2):25 – 29.

School HVAC
High Performance
School Characteristics
T
o those of us in the design and construction industry, a high
T
oTperformance school is synonymous with a sustainable school
building. However, talk to educators, and they see a high perfor-
mance school as a school where students excel in their studies.
The physical facility and educational outcomes, such as teacher
productivity and test scores, certainly are linked. So in a sense, the
design professional and the educator have the same goals—they
just see them from a different vantage point.
Charles Eley, FAIA, P.E., is a vice-president of
Architectural Energy Corporation in San Francisco.
He is executive director of the Collaborative for
High Performance Schools (CHPS) and is techni-
cal editor of the CHPS Best Practices Manual, the
Advanced Lighting Guidelines: Final Report, and
Standard 90.1-2004 User’s Manual and Standard
62.1-2004 User’s Manual.
By Charles Eley, FAIA, P.E., Member ASHRAE
To facilitate and promote positive
educational outcomes, a high performance
school building provides a comfortable
and healthy environment for teachers
and students. The building itself cannot
guarantee academic performance, but
it can help. If the school building helps
educators teach and students learn better,
then it is a high performance school. The
sidebar at right details what the Collabora-
tive for High Performance Schools (CHPS
[www.chps.net]) considers the important
characteristics of a high performance
school building.
Costs and Beneﬁ ts
The ﬁ rst question school board members
and school facility management personnel
ask is how much do high performance
schools cost. Because school districts
usually are hampered by constraints on
initial cost, construction cost increases can
mean that they are forced to build fewer
classrooms. For this and other reasons, the
long-term or life-cycle cost usually is not
considered by school districts. If it were, the
discounted value of future energy and water
savings from high performance schools
would exceed any initial cost increases.
G. Kats, et al., in a recent study for the
Massachusetts Technology Collabora-
tive1 pegged costs for high performance
schools at 1.5% to 2.5% of construction
cost (excluding site acquisition). An-
other study2 by D. Langdon considered
About the Author
© 2006 American Society of Heating, Refrigerating and Air-Conditioning Engineers, Inc. (www.ashrae.org). Published in ASHRAE Journal (Vol. 48, May
2006). For personal use only. Additional distribution in either paper or digital form is not permitted without ASHRAE’s permission.
May 2006
ASHRAE Journal
61
• Health. A high performance building has good air qual-
ity, resulting in better health. About 20% of our popula-
tion spends a signiﬁ cant amount of time in schools, and
we need effective outside air ventilation as required by
ANSI/ASHRAE Standard 62.1-2004, Ventilation for Ac-
ceptable Indoor Air Quality. Air quality is improved when
interior materials are used that do not release harmful
chemicals that can off gas into the building. Children are
more susceptible to chemical exposures because of their
still-developing respiratory systems, making air quality
especially important. If teachers and students are sick less
often, they are obviously more productive. Absenteeism is
a good benchmark for a healthy school.
• Comfort. High performance schools are more thermally,
visually, and acoustically comfortable. Teachers can teach
better and students learn better if they are comfortable;
if balanced daylight and electric light provide adequate,
uniform, and glare-free illumination for all our visual
tasks; and if the space is quiet.
• Efﬁ ciency. High performance schools are efﬁ cient in the
way they use energy, water, and materials. Efﬁ ciency
translates to lower costs for construction and operation,
meaning that more money can be used for educational
purposes. Of all the features in a high performance
school, energy efﬁ ciency is the best understood. Analy-
sis tools, cost effectiveness criteria, and design strategies
to improve energy use are mature techniques. Material
efﬁ ciency is the newcomer to design. While the science
of life-cycle assessment is emerging, we do not have
complete information about material efﬁ ciency or envi-
ronmental impacts associated with the use of materials
and products.
• Maintenance. Systems in high performance school build-
ings are easy to use and maintain. Users have control over
the temperature, airﬂ ow, acoustics, and lighting in the
building, and are trained on how to most effectively use
the systems. The best high performance schools are based
on simple principles.
• Commissioning. High performance schools are com-
missioned (ASHRAE Guideline 0-2005, The Commis-
sioning Process). Commissioning must begin during
the design phase and continue through construction to
ensure key building systems perform properly and at
the highest levels of efﬁ ciency, air quality and comfort.
Modern construction practice is fragmented with many
specialists on every project. It is easy for equipment
to be installed incorrectly. Commissioning can reduce
these problems.
• Environmentally Responsive. High performance schools
are environmentally responsive. To the extent possible,
existing natural areas on the site are protected and re-
stored. Stormwater runoff is minimized and erosion is
controlled. Construction does not introduce pollutants
or degrade the site. Materials are selected that minimize
the environmental impact related to extraction, harvest,
production, and transportation.
• Security. High performance schools are safe and secure.
While this may be difﬁ cult in rough neighborhoods, the
building can be a positive factor by providing paths for
visual surveillance, limiting access to less public areas
and by controlling entry points. Ideally occupants and
visitors should feel safe anywhere in the building or on
the grounds, yet the school building should be open to
the community, at least in selected areas.
• Good Architecture. Finally, high performance schools
feature stimulating architecture that invoke a sense of
pride and are considered a genuine asset not only for the
owner, but also for the entire community.
Characteristics of a High Performance School
not just schools but all sustainable buildings and concluded
that the cost premium is insigniﬁ cant. However, the study
did not consider additional soft costs for energy modeling or
commissioning.
CHPS estimates that its criteria for a high performance school
can be achieved at a cost as low as $1.50/ft² ($16.15/m2), includ-
ing soft costs. In summary, the costs range between 0% and
2.5% of the construction cost, excluding site acquisition.
However, the economic beneﬁ ts are staggering. Kats estimates
present value of beneﬁ ts associated with sustainable schools are
10 to 20 times the costs. These beneﬁ ts include reduced operating
costs for energy, water and maintenance; reduced absenteeism
and associated costs for substitute teachers; reduced liability;
reduced environmental impact; increased teacher satisfaction
and retention; and increased student learning and teacher perfor-
mance. The last beneﬁ t is perhaps the most important and most
valuable for any school district that looks at the costs and beneﬁ ts
of its programs through a long period of time.
The Building Envelope
Designing a high performance school begins with the building
envelope and in particular the treatment of windows. High quality
low-e windows should be used, but they should be positioned on
the north or south sides of each classroom to reduce direct sun
penetration and glare. Orientation is important but less critical
in multi-purpose spaces, corridors and other spaces where oc-
cupants are not “captive” for extended periods of time. South
windows should be shaded with overhangs, and light shelves
should be considered to increase daylighting penetration into the
classroom. For single-story schools, light from windows should
be balanced by light from skylights, roof monitors or other top
lighting schemes so that uniformity is provided. Tall ceilings
62
ASHRAE Journal
ashrae.org
May 2006
School HVAC
should be provided to enable better daylight-
ing and to help improve ventilation. High
levels of insulation and well-constructed
windows can reduce heat loss and in many
climates eliminate the need for expensive
supplementary perimeter heating systems.
Electric Lighting
Electric classroom lighting should be
designed to supplement daylight on winter
mornings and evening use. One solution is
the integrated classroom lighting system
developed with California Energy Com-
mission funding. The system operates in
two modes. The pendant-mounted luminaires operate in an
indirect mode (two outer lamps are operating) for normal class-
room operation. In this mode, the ceilings and upper walls are
brightly illuminated providing a cheery, bright feeling. When
the teacher needs to use A/V equipment, the classroom lighting
system operates in direct mode, with the center lamp in each
Figure 1: Characteristics of the CHPS classroom. Source: Collaborative for High Performance Schools.
Photographs 1a (left) and 1b (right): The integrated classroom lighting system. In indirect
mode (1a) the ceiling and upper walls are bright and the space feels cheery for general
classroom activities. In the direct mode (1b) glare is reduced on the screen and lighting
is optimized for A/V presentations. Source: Finelite.
May 2006
ASHRAE Journal
63
luminaire operating. In this mode, glare on the A/V screen or
TV monitor is reduced, yet, 40 footcandles of illumination is
provided at the students writing surface for taking notes or
referencing text books. The classroom lighting system has an
integrated occupant sensor with a quiet time switch so that the
lights do not go off when the teacher remains after class to grade
papers or the classroom is otherwise not full of kids.
HVAC and Dehumidiﬁ cation
Heating, cooling, dehumidiﬁ cation and
ventilation are critical in classrooms. Dis-
placement ventilation is a concept that all
school districts should pursue. Not only is
the system energy efﬁ cient, but it is whis-
per quiet, provides great thermal comfort,
and can provide superior air quality.
Acoustics in classrooms is an increas-
ingly important design criterion and
displacement ventilation can reduce
noise levels related to air delivery by 5 to
8 dBA with everything else being equal.
With proper location of equipment and/or
sound attenuation measures, displacement
ventilation can help achieve the 35 dBA
background sound threshold recom-
mended by ANSI S12.60-2002,Acoustical
Performance Criteria, Design Require-
ments, and Guidelines for Schools.
Displacement ventilation and air-con-
ditioning systems deliver cool supply
air near the ﬂ oor instead of blowing it
down from the ceiling. The air is heated
or cooled so that it enters the room at
about 65°F (18°C), considerably warmer
than with a conventional air-conditioning
system. The diffusers are larger so that the
air ﬂ ows into the space at a low velocity.
Since the 65°F (18°C) air is cooler than
the rest of the air in the room, it spreads
by gravity along the ﬂ oor, forming a con-
tinuous layer of cool air at ankle level.
As the cool air is heated by the stu-
dents, the teacher or other warm objects
in the space, it rises to the ceiling, where
it is exhausted. Air movement in the space
is predominately vertical from bottom to
top, so less cross contamination exists
between occupants and each occupant has
a supply of fresh air. The phenomenon of
air rising over a warm object is called a
thermal plume. The heat generated forces
that cause the thermal plume are the same
ones that enable us to lift off the ground
in hot air balloons. The more active the
students, the more heat they produce, and
the volume of air lifted from the ﬂ oor by buoyancy increases.
If they are sitting at their desk, the heat they generate is about
equal to a 75 W light bulb. If it is a dance class or a gym class,
each person generates much more heat.
As heat sources increase, the air volume delivered to the space
must be increased through variable air volume (VAV) damper
control or through some other means so that the temperature in
the occupied zone remains at comfort conditions. A signiﬁ cant
comfort criterion with displacement ventilation is the difference in
Advertisement formerly in this space.
May 2006
ASHRAE Journal
65
School HVAC
temperature between the air at the ﬂ oor and the air at the occupant’s
head. ANSI/ASHRAE Standard 55, Thermal Environmental Con-
ditions for Human Occupancy, tells us that this difference should
not exceed about 3.6°F (2°C) when seated and 5.4°F (3°C) when
standing. Controlling the temperature difference between head
and toe is one of the reasons that air needs to be delivered at 65°F
(18°C) instead of the more typical 55°F (13°C). Considerable
temperature stratiﬁ cation in well-designed displacement ventila-
tion spaces exists; a 10 ft (3 m) or higher
ceiling helps achieve this stratiﬁ cation and
also enables indirect electric lighting and
better daylighting. Ideally the temperature
of air exhausted from the space should be
near 80°F (26°C).
In a typical occupied classroom, about
450 cfm (212 L/s) of outside air needs to
be delivered to keep air fresh and to meet
the requirements of Standard 62.1-2004.
On a temperate day with daylight availabil-
ity, this volume of 65°F (18°C) air is ad-
equate to provide cooling, so the system is
essentially operating at 100% outside air.
On hot days or in classrooms with higher
internal gains, airﬂ ow needs to increase
up to about 1,200 cfm (566 L/s) or more.
Primary temperature control is provided
through volume control, but temperature
reset may be needed when the maximum
or minimum airﬂ ow is reached.
In dry climates typical of the western
United States, it is possible to sensibly
cool outdoor air to 65°F (18°C)without the
need for dehumidiﬁ cation. In the humid
areas of the eastern United States, it is
necessary to subcool outdoor air to remove
moisture and to then reheat it through
recovered heat or to mix it with return air
to achieve the desired 65°F (18 °C).
Summary
A barrier to more high performance
schools is separate budgets for facilities
management, educational programs, and
capital improvements. To justify the cost
of high performance schools, costs and
beneﬁ ts of all three budgets must be con-
sidered. Also to be considered is having
ﬁ nancial instruments shift costs from one
time frame (usually the future) to another
time frame (present time construction
costs). Another problem for school dis-
tricts is ﬁ nding architectural/engineering
teams that have the skills to design to the
school districts’ exacting high perfor-
mance standards. However designers are educating themselves
about daylighting design and other necessary skills.
Many states such as California, Washington, Massachusetts,
New York and New Jersey have aggressive programs to promote
high performance schools. Some school districts, including Los
Angeles Uniﬁ ed School District, which is the second largest
in the United States, have committed to only building new
schools to the CHPS high performance standard. The U.S.
Advertisement formerly in this space.
66
ASHRAE Journal
May 2006
School HVAC
Green Building Council through its Leadership in Energy and
Environmental Design® (LEED) program is actively promoting
high performance schools and is developing a guide for apply-
ing the LEED program speciﬁ cally to schools.
While we are making progress, more needs to be done.
Students, parents, teachers, administrators and the public must
insist all new schools meet the CHPS or LEED standards for
high performance. As engineers and architects, we have a moral
responsibility to produce high performance designs, even if our
clients do not request it. Leadership from within our professions
and pressure from the outside is the winning combination that
will produce more high performance schools.
References
1. Kats, G., J. Perlman and S. Jamadagni. 2005. “National Review of
Green Schools: Costs, Beneﬁ ts, and Implications for Massachusetts.” A
Report for the Massachusetts Technology Collaborative, Capital-E.
2. Matthiessen, L.F. and P. Morris. 2004. “Costing Green: A Com-
prehensive Database and Budgeting Methodology, Davis Langdon.”
3. Heschong, L. 1999. “Daylighting in Schools: An Investigation
into the Relationship Between Daylighting and Human Performance.”
Heschong Mahone Group.
4. Heschong, L. 2002. “Daylighting and human performance.”
ASHRAE Journal 44(6):65 – 67.
l
5. Kuller, R. and C. Lindsten. 1992. “Health and behavior of children
in classrooms with and without windows.” Journal of Environmental
Psychology 12:305 – 317.
Research Results
While common sense is enough for some school districts
to move forward with programs for high performance school
buildings, other school administrators may need to be con-
vinced. For skeptics, a wealth of evidence exists. Important
indicators that sustainable schools help teacher and student
performance are standardized test scores, absenteeism, col-
lege acceptance rates, and teacher retention.
Researchers have made defensible and statistically signiﬁ -
cant correlations between these educational outcomes and
the physical characteristics of school buildings.
L. Heschong was able to link quality daylighting with
increased student test scores in a sample of 26,000 students
in Southern California.3,4
A Swedish study found classrooms without daylight may
upset the basic hormone pattern of children, and this may
inﬂ uence the child’s ability to concentrate or cooperate,
and also eventually have an impact on annual body growth
and absenteeism.5
M. Schneider’s paper, “Do School Facilities Affect
Academic Outcomes?” (www.edfacilities.org/pubs/
outcomes.pdf), provides an excellent literature review of
the connections between school facilities and educational
outcomes. This paper looks comprehensively at lighting,
acoustics, building age, quality and aesthetics, school size,
and class size. The bibliography is extensive and provides
a good source for additional reading.

Charles E. Gulledge III, P.E., is chair of

Construction Specifications Institute. He is
employed at AC Corp. in Greensboro, N.C.
Michael J. King, is member of TC 7.1 and a
fellow in the Construction Speciﬁ cations Insti-
tute and is employed at ARCOM, Alexandria,
Va. Dennis Knight, P.E., is a member of TC
7.1 and is employed at Liollio Architecture,
Charleston, S.C.
HVAC Specifications
For Integrated
Building Design
M
asterFormat is the standard for organizing project manuals
and construction speciﬁ cations in the United States and
Canada. The standard was developed in the 1960s to respond
to a lack of consistently organized information in the construc-
tion world. The standard is jointly produced by the Construction
Speciﬁ cations Institute (CSI) and Construction Speciﬁ cations
Canada (CSC).
By Charles E. Gulledge III, P.E., Member ASHRAE; Michael J. King, Member ASHRAE, and Dennis Knight, P.E.,
Member ASHRAE
The concept of divisions and sections
was developed by CSI in the early 1960s.
For more than 40 years, HVAC engineers
have been developing speciﬁ cations for
construction written under Division 15
within the original 16-division structure
developed in 1963. With the release
of the latest version of the standard,
MasterFormat 2004 Edition (MF04),
t t
Division 15 no longer exists.
Why MasterFormat 95 Changed
The MF04 release reﬂ ects a signiﬁ -
cant expansion of this organizational
MasterFormat™ 2004
