structure. Revisions posted in 1983, 1988,
and 1995 reﬂ ected an update of content
paralleling the dynamics of the construc-
tion world, while maintaining the original
16-division organization. The release of
MF04 expanded the 16-division structure
to 50 divisions. Why was such a signiﬁ -
cant revision required?
• MasterFormat 1995 Edition (MF95)
t
did not cover new developments in
building technologies or all pos-
sible construction scenarios. Many
architects, engineers and owners
developed hybrid specifications
using undeﬁ ned structure, such as
Divisions 17–21. The standard had
lost touch with its user base.
• Select divisions in MF95 were over-
crowded, such as 15 and 16. The abil-
ity to add organizational structure to
keep pace with the dynamics of build-
ing construction was not possible
within the 16-division structure and
ﬁ ve-digit section number scheme.
• MF95 did not have detailed focus
beyond the “turn the keys over”
phase of a project. Factors such as
maintenance, operation, life-cycle
assessment, and facilities manage-
ment had limited coverage.
• Construction outside of the “vertical”
world was not adequately addressed
by MF95. Limited organizational op-
portunity was available to respond to
projects such as roadways, tunnels,
dams, power plants, and a host of
process-speciﬁ c industrial applica-
tions and sectors.
• The development of “smart build-
ings” had outpaced the capabilities
of MF95.
• Concern had been expressed that ele-
ments of MF95 had regressed from
the intention of deﬁ ning work results
logic\* and had migrated towards a
quasi-product classiﬁ cation.
The Expansion Process
The MasterFormat Expansion Task
Team (MFETT) was tasked by CSI to
respond to the documented shortcomings
and concerns provided by the construc-
tion industry (see sidebar, “MasterFormat
“M
“
Expansion Task Team and ASHRAE”).
The MFETT recognized that signiﬁ cant
change was required, so the following
guidelines were established to maintain
structure and direction during the revi-
sion process:
• Maintain the existing structure of
divisions that are not problematic;
• Provide relief for the overcrowded
mechanical and electrical divi-
sions;
• Look beyond typical building con-
struction to address civil, environ-
mental, and process engineering
needs;
• Develop an organizational structure
that has room for expansion in re-
sponse to the dynamics of the built
world; and
• Expand coverage of total ownership
cost perspective features.
The New Organizational Structure
To respond to the industry concerns
and comply with the guidelines created
by the MFETT, signiﬁ cant changes had
to be made to the standard. The 16-di-
vision structure was reorganized and
expanded. Figure 1 shows the structure
of the revised 50-division standard.
From a macro perspective, the new
standard is organized into two main
groups: Procurement and Contracting
Requirements Group and Speciﬁ cations
Group. The Procurement and Contract-
ing Requirements group consists of Di-
vision 00 (Procurement and Contracting
Requirements), which includes project
introductory information, procurement
requirements, and contracting require-
ments. The Speciﬁ cations Group is sub-
divided into ﬁ ve subgroups that focus on
unique construction requirements.
• The General Requirements Sub-
group consists of Division 01
(General Requirements) and in-
cludes work summaries, price and
payment procedures, administrative
requirements, quality requirements,
temporary facilities and controls,
product requirements, execution
and closeout requirements, perfor-
\*Work results1 are permanent or temporary aspects
of construction projects achieved in the production
stage or by subsequent alteration, maintenance, or
demolition processes, through the application of a
particular skill or trade to construction resources.
Figure 1: Structure of the revised standard.

mance requirements, and life cycle activities. Section
01 10 00—Summary, is an important part of the MF04
standard. It is critical that work summaries be properly
identiﬁ ed in Division 01, so bidding contractors have a
clear deﬁ nition as to who is responsible for what work
result. This allows the remainder of the speciﬁ cations
to be mute on a work assignment, leaving that task to
the contractors who will each divide the work differ-
ently. This is a concept that has not been changed by
the new organizational structure. By explicit provisions
in standard forms of agreement and general conditions,
the location of speciﬁ cation content is not a deﬁ nition of
work assignment.
• The Facility Construction Subgroup (Divisions 02-
19) includes the vertical facility construction. The new
divisions parallel Divisions 3-14 of MF95. There are
a few notable changes that have signiﬁ cant impact for
the HVAC&R world. Division 02 (Existing Conditions)
replaces the former division that
focused on site construction ele-
ments. Divisions 15 and 16 have
been removed from the standard
and designated as “reserved for
future expansion.” The MFETT in-
tentionally did not use the 15 and 16
division numbers to avoid confusion
with the signiﬁ cant changes created
by moving and expanding the tradi-
tional ﬁ re suppression, plumbing,
HVAC and electrical subjects.
• The Facility Services Subgroup
(Divisions 20-29) includes the
engineered support systems for the
facility. Here we ﬁ nd the new loca-
tion for the HVAC subjects under
Division 23 (Heating, Ventilating
and Air Conditioning) and electrical
power and lighting subjects under
Division 26 (Electrical). The sub-
group structure provides speciﬁ ca-
tion locations for ﬁ re suppression,
plumbing, HVAC, integrated auto-
mation, electrical, communications,
and electronic safety and security
work results. This subgroup was
created and structured in response
to the overcrowding of the old me-
chanical and electrical divisions.
Plumbing and HVAC speciﬁ cations
have been separated into two divi-
sions. Subject matter gathered in
a few nondescript sections in the
MF95 Electrical division has been
parsed into multiple divisions, so
that building communications, elec-
tronic safety, and security issues can
be addressed more comprehensively
and on an equal footing with other
facility services.
• The Site and Infrastructure Sub-
group (Divisions 30-39) includes
the traditional civil type construc-
tion. The previous site work outlined
in the old Division 2 moved to this
Advertisement formerly in this space.
May 2006
ASHRAE Journal
83
The changes incorporated into MasterFormat 2004 Edition
reﬂ ect a signiﬁ cant shakeup for the construction industry.
The 16-division speciﬁ cation organizational structure that the
built world has relied on for more than 40 years has been
reorganized and expanded threefold.
CSI recognized early in the expansion process that changes
of the magnitude needed to update the standard would
require consensus participation from a wide representa-
tion of stakeholders in the industry. By invitation from CSI,
ASHRAE, American Society of Civil Engineers, Associated
General Contractors, and a number of other engineering and
construction-related organizations were asked to participate
in the MFETT. ASHRAE had two members sitting on this
committee.
The MFETT process spanned almost four years. Four drafts
of the standard were developed and posted for public review
and comment. According to statistics provided by CSI; over
800 organizations were asked to provide comment, over 200
informational presentations were made, over 100 articles
MasterFormat Expansion Task Team & ASHRAE
were published, four Web discussion forums were produced,
and three national symposiums were presented.
As part of ASHRAE’s ofﬁ cial participation and liaison inter-
action with CSI and the MFETT, a speciﬁ cations subcommit-
tee was created under ASHRAE Technical Committee 7.1,
Integrated Building Design. When the ﬁ nal draft of the revised
standard was developed in early 2004, TC 7.1 disseminated
a copy of the draft to all ASHRAE technical committees for
peer review of proposed content. In a three-week window,
TC members were able to generate more than 13 pages of
suggestions for ﬁ nal crafting of the Division 23 content.
TC 7.1 continues to maintain a liaison role with CSI. CSI has
expressed a desire to keep the new version of the standard
current, much the same way ASHRAE provides continuous
maintenance to certain ﬂ agship standards. TC 7.1 will make
recommendations to CSI as requests are received from So-
ciety interests. In this manner, members of ASHRAE have a
conduit to CSI to ensure the standard remains current and
timely in response to the dynamics of the built world.
new subgroup. The subgroup struc-
ture provides speciﬁ cation locations
for earthwork, exterior improve-
ments, utilities, transportation, and
waterway and marine work results.
HVAC work that involves a site in-
tegration element, such as campus
steam or chilled water distribution,
will have work results speciﬁ cations
in Divisions 31 (Earthwork) and 33
(Utilities).
• The Process Equipment Subgroup
(Divisions 40-49) completes the or-
ganizational structure and provides

speciﬁ cation locations for process integration, material
processing and handling equipment, process heating/
cooling/drying equipment, process gas and liquid han-
dling/puriﬁ cation/storage equipment, pollution control
equipment, industry-speciﬁ c manufacturing equipment,
and electrical power generation work results.
Section Numbers and Titles
In response to the overcrowded conditions of MF95, the
MFETT dropped the restrictive ﬁ ve-digit number system and
implemented a six-digit system that, at its base, corresponds to
three levels of classiﬁ cation. The six-digit numbering format
applies to information covered down to Level 3.
MF04 offers three alternative methods of number presenta-
tion. The recommended appearance of the speciﬁ cation number
is to have three pairs of numbers separated by spaces between
Levels 1 and 2 and between Levels 2 and 3. The second method
is for the numbers to be presented with two digits, a space, fol-
lowed by four digits. The third alternative is for all six digits to
be contiguous. To avoid numbers that have spaces (i.e., three
pairs of numbers) from being split at the end of a line of text,
use a “hard” space to separate the numbers. This can be accom-
plished in Microsoft Word® by applying the (ctrl+shift+space)
keystroke combination.
So, what do the levels represent?
• Level 1 is the division level reference.
• Level 2 is a broad scope organization representing big
picture focus areas within the division.
• Level 3 is a medium scope organization of division work
results. Generally speaking, most HVAC speciﬁ cations
will be written to the Level 3 detail.
• Level 4 allows the specifying engineer to write more
narrow scoped speciﬁ cations; promoting more efﬁ cient
retrieval of complex information and detailed integration
between work results organization and construction sup-
port data. Level 4 is created by adding a decimal point and

work results. Pumps would be a single line item discus-
sion in a comprehensive shopping list of systems;
• A Level 2 speciﬁ cation (23 20 00 or 23 21 00) would
provide a broad scope description of HVAC piping and
pumps or hydronic piping and pumps;
• At Level 3 (23 21 23), all types of hydronic pumps can
be included in one section, or the section number and
title can be used to specify only base-mounted centri-
fugal pumps.
• As the number of different types of pumps increase for
a project or master speciﬁ cation section, it may become

more narrow scoped sections and use numbers and titles

two digits to the right of the Level 3
number.
• Level 5 is a special level to be used
for internal purposes or special ap-
plications and is created by adding
a second decimal point and alpha-
numeric text string to the right of
a Level 4 number. The addition of
an alpha-numeric text string allows
the speciﬁ cation writer to organize
ofﬁ ce master speciﬁ cations based
on variable client factors such as
client type, construction features,
control options, and procurement
options. Level 5 might also be used
for drawing keynoting. While Level
5 structure is a powerful organiza-
tional tool, specifications should
not be written to Level 5 for use in
a project manual. When an ofﬁ ce
speciﬁ cation master is used for a
project, the second decimal point
and alpha-numeric text string should
be removed allowing the specifi-
cation to be presented at Level 4
or higher.
Table 1 shows a complete level penetra-
tion down to Level 5 for a hypothetical
base-mounted centrifugal pump. Speci-
ﬁ cations for a base-mounted centrifugal
pump can be included in any of the levels
mentioned in Table 1. The choice is up
to the speciﬁ cation writer depending on
the complexity of the project or master
speciﬁ cation. For example:
• A project could have a Level 1 speci-
ﬁ cation (23 00 00) that presents a
simple narrative of the entire HVAC
• Each of the different types of hydronic pumps could
be further subdivided at Level 5 by identifying com-
mon groups of attributes for each pump type. For
example, 23 21 23.16 could be further subdivided into
“23 21 23.16.allbronze” to describe an all-bronze con-
struction variation.
A pattern of assigning section numbers on intervals of three
(i.e., 13, 16, 19, 23, 26, 29, etc.) has been used throughout
the standard. MFETT intentionally structured assignment of
numbers to allow room for future material in the standard and
to accommodate customization by the user. Customization
of section numbers and titles is available to the speciﬁ cation
writer, but such creativity must be exercised under certain
prescribed control.
• Division (Level 1) numbers and titles are ﬁ xed and should
not be altered;
May 2006
ASHRAE Journal
85
• New Level 1 numbers and titles should not be added.
• Reserved sections should not be used;
• Titles and numbers assigned to Levels 2, 3, and 4 should
not be revised; and
• Levels 2, 3, and 4 may be expanded to accommodate
project requirements by creating descriptive titles and
assigning numbers within the numbering structure that
are not already assigned. Such expansion should follow
prescribed numbering/naming conventions currently in
the standard.
Division 23 Structure
Each of the divisions in the Facilities Services Subgroup has
an organized collection of common information at the beginning
of the division. This is a tool that will allow the speciﬁ cation
writer to extract repetitious information from multiple sections
and locate it into one-time common descriptions. Table 2 depicts
the front-end structure for Division 23.
Section 23 01 00 provides an opportunity to deﬁ ne op-
eration and maintenance activities that are the true basis of
the work result. Sections for
facility fuel systems, HVAC
piping and pumps, HVAC air
distribution, central heating
equipment, central cooling
equipment, central HVAC
equipment, and decentralized
HVAC equipment have been
established. These are broad
categories and align with the
Division 23 Level 2 main
titles. Maintenance and opera-
tion activities supporting the installation of speciﬁ c equipment,
or work results, should be identiﬁ ed in the applicable Level 3
or 4 speciﬁ cation sections, in accordance with CSI Section-
Format criteria.
t
Section 23 05 00 provides an opportunity to deﬁ ne common
work results that apply to the entire division. This strategy
embodies the CSI principle to “say it once and in the correct
place.” This technique was previously used in the mechanical
and electrical divisions of MF95 and was formerly known as
“Basic Mechanical Materials and Methods.” In MF04, the
common work results organizational strategy is used in all divi-
sions. Highlighting 23 05 13—Common Motor Requirements
for HVAC Equipment as an example, a speciﬁ cation section
is deﬁ ned to cover the common or default motor requirements
for all HVAC work with motorized equipment, negating the
need for repeating these “common motor requirements” in all
of the motorized equipment-speciﬁ cation sections. Unique or
special motor requirements can be identiﬁ ed in the equipment
speciﬁ c speciﬁ cation section.
Section 23 06 00 provides an opportunity to include equip-
ment schedules for HVAC equipment that may be common to
more than one title. Sections are deﬁ ned for broad categories
paralleling the Division 23 Level 2 main titles. Schedules for
individual titles should be located within the individual Level
3 or 4 speciﬁ cation sections, or placed on the drawings.
Control system speciﬁ cations dedicated to Division 23 work
have been moved forward in the division. The MF95 version of
Division 15 had control speciﬁ cations towards the end of the divi-
sion. As this work result truly applies across the entire division, it
has been moved into the common focus area of the division.
The remaining structure of Division 23 has been organized
into eight, Level 2 categories that provide a broad scope orga-
nization of the division. Brieﬂ y summarized, these categories
describe fuel systems, piping and pumps, air distribution, air
cleaning (ﬁ ltration), heating plants, cooling plants, central
HVAC systems, and supporting HVAC equipment. Sections
23 90 00—23 99 99 have been left unassigned at this time. Table
3 indicates the remaining Level 2 structure of the division.
Division 25 for Smart Buildings
Control solutions that look beyond the HVAC environmental
control function and seek to include ﬁ re alarm, lighting control,
energy monitoring, security,
and networks can now be de-
ﬁ ned and speciﬁ ed in Division
25 (Integrated Automation).
Two application strategies
must be considered:
•
The first strategy is to
specify each control system
(instrumentation and controls
for HVAC, lighting controls,
security access, and more) in
their respective divisions and
include only the integrating equipment, specialties, and
software in a section in Division 25; and
• Alternatively, one could include all the control system
speciﬁ cations and the integration requirements in Divi-
sion 25. The choice is up to the specifying engineer and
the decision is inﬂ uenced by product and system selection
and project complexity.
The Project Manual
Application of MF04 will change the manner in which the
project manual is assembled and how the table of contents
must be structured. The use of groups, subgroups, and 50
divisions creates an information communication challenge.
To maintain a consistent structure and to accommodate indi-
vidual project customization, the following layout guidelines
are recommended:
• Group headings should be listed, whether used or not;
• All subgroup headings should be listed, whether used
or not;
• If any division of a subgroup is used, all divisions of the
subgroup should be listed. Divisions not used, including ones
designated as future, should be labeled as “Not Used”;

86
ASHRAE Journal
ashrae.org
May 2006
• If no divisions of a subgroup are used, then the entire
subgroup can be designated as “Not Used”;
• Sequential clusters of “Not Used” divisions may be ag-
gregated; and
• Only sections used within a division need be listed.
Implementation
Switching to MF04 for project speciﬁ cations is a deci-
sion that must be made on an individual project basis. The
specifying engineer faces a number of conversion factors.
For example:
• What are the “date certain” conversion time tables man-
dated by clients?
• What resources are available in-house to complete the
conversion?
• Are outside team members committed to conversion? and
• Will differing client mandates dictate dual format
methodology?
Whatever the appropriate timetable turns
out to be, it is recommended that mixed,
or hybrid, manuals using both MF04 and
MF95 structure be avoided. Mixing of the
schemes on a single project increases the
risk of errors, omissions, and invalid cross
referencing among sections.
Implementation means not only chang-
ing the numbers and titles at the beginning
and ending of the speciﬁ cation section, but
also all the cross referencing that occurs
within the section. Some subjects that
were in the old Division 15 now are sepa-
rated into two or three sections. Although
some common requirements exist for the
subjects in these new divisions, each is
uniquely structured for the requirements
of the respective division.
The Road Ahead
As with any useful guideline or standard,
MF04 will require continuous maintenance
to remain relevant and viable as new and
improved technologies, standards, and
means and methods of construction are
developed and introduced into the mar-
ketplace. MF04 provides the ﬂ exibility to
accommodate such changes while main-
taining a rigorous and highly organized
structure that will be essential as complex
Integrated Building Design techniques and
Building Information Modeling systems
emerge to improve the built environment.

School HVAC
Reduced Outdoor Air
For Auditorium
Standard 62 IAQ Procedure
I
f you don’t use the air puriﬁ cation option in the IAQ Procedure
in ANSI/ASHRAE Standard 62.1-2004, Ventilation for Accept-
able Indoor Air Quality, you may miss out on a non-traditional but
cost-effective design that reduces heating, cooling loads, duct
sizes and ﬁ rst costs.
The IAQ design option results in outdoor air (OA) reductions for
most commercial spaces down to 5 cfm (2.4 L/s) OA per person,
and are based at full occupancy 24/7. When gas claeaning tech-
nology is used with high-efﬁ ciency ﬁ lters, you use less energy and
have a healthy and more comfortable space.
able Indoor Air Quality, IAQ procedure
that combined the use of gas-phase clean-
ing and high-efﬁ ciency particulate ﬁ lters
for reduced OA. For greatest savings, an
energy recovery unit (wheel) was added
for energy recovery of the exhaust air.
This project was designed as an ar-
chitectural showcase. It is a one-story
insulated brick building with a built-up
insulated metal deck roof with single ply
membrane and rooftop units.
The Ohio Building Code (OBC) Sec-
tion 403.2, “Mechanical Ventilation,”
requires designers to use Standard
62. The code has the option to use 15
cfm/person (7 L/s per person) of OA to
be supplied per the standard’s Ventila-
tion Rate Procedure. Or, you may use
Standard 62 to calculate the required
outside air with air-cleaning systems to
clean gasses, vapors and improve the
job of removing particulates. In 1999,
In Ohio, Brunswick School ofﬁ cials
were planning a new auditorium in
1998 and wanted the best IAQ with
reduced OA. They also required the air-
handling systems use the least amount
of energy for heating and cooling
the space.
In 1999 the air-handling system’s de-
sign was based on the ANSI/ASHRAE
Standard 62-1999, Ventilation for Accept-
By Peter F. Johnson, Member ASHRAE
Peter F. Johnson is sales/service manager for PIER
Associates in Akron, Ohio.
About the Author

2006). For personal use only. Additional distribution in either paper or digital form is not permitted without ASHRAE s permission.
May 2006
ASHRAE Journal
55
the OBC did not allow a “design reduction in OA on a three-
hour occupancy rule.”
Several types of gas-phase technologies (chemical cleaning)
are available. The two methods considered were bipolar ioniza-
tion vs. packed bed granular activated carbon media. Bipolar
ionization was selected as the more cost effective of the two
because its annual maintenance cost is low. The bipolar cleaning
section is combined with a high-efﬁ ciency particulate phase
ﬁ lter to complete the air-cleaning system.
This project’s OA requirement was reduced from 15 cfm to
7.5 cfm (7 L/s to 3.5 L/s) per person using chemical cleaning. This
level of OA only required 80% ﬁ ltration efﬁ ciency (MERV 11)
for particle removal. Based on the maximum occupant density of
1,000 people, the IAQ program requires calculations of the con-
centration for all chemical contaminants to be below accept-
able limits. Contaminants generated by occupants, odors
contributed from the building and undesirable fumes in
the outdoor air are all handled with this design.
Software was used to quickly run zone-by-zone
calculations of the chemical concentrations of all
contaminants. This software program is based on
ASHRAE formulas.1 It offers the option to consider different
systems’ performance and efﬁ ciency. It then models the level
of cleaning for the type of system chosen in each zone. In a
few minutes, a best combination of variables can be obtained
to satisfy code.
Energy Efﬁ ciency
More than a 50% reduction in outside air was achieved vs. the
original design’s potential outcome. As a direct result, substantially
smaller cooling and heating systems were used, and smaller air-
ﬂ ows and ductwork were needed. A 27-ton (95 kW) cooling load
reduction was achieved (from 71 tons down to 44 tons [250 kW
to 155 kW]). For heating, the load reduction was 1,194 MBtu/h
(350 kW) reduced down to 261 MBtu/h (76.5 kW), which is a net
931 MBtu/h (273 kW) reduction in heating. These ﬁ gures include
the energy reduction from an energy recovery wheel unit on the
reduced OA ﬂ ow (Figure 1
(F
(
). This site does not separately meter
utilities to this building. Therefore, energy savings calculations
are based on the projected utility costs (Figures 2
(F
(
and 3
d d ).
With the IAQ procedure and reduced OA, the $9,268 project
utility costs are reduced to $8,541, a $727 annual energy reduction
with chemical cleaning and energy recovery. This low amount is
o the short number of hours the system operates, i.e.,
due to
o times per week for three hours occupied at 80% and
two
six hours Monday through Friday at 3% occupancy.
s
A major equipment supplier’s modeling program
was used to compare standard rooftop units based on
the Ventilation Rate Procedure vs. the IAQ Procedure
t
ith energy recovery ventilators.
w
Indoor Air Quality and Thermal Comfort
It doesn’t matter if odors come from inside the building
from people, furniture, cleaning chemicals or from the outside.
The supply air to the space always is controlled for odors,
mold and particle removal. IAQ is improved simply because
ventilation with high efﬁ ciency (MERV 11) and chemical
cleaning for reduced OA vs. low efﬁ ciency ﬁ lters (MERV 5)
The design of the Brunswick School Performing Arts Center in Brunswick, Ohio, reduces the amount of outside air needed.

and more OA (as would have been in the standard design) is
a cleaner environment.
Pressure differential sensors are across the sets of ﬁ lters to alert
the building management system when units are dirty and need
changing. Both sets of ﬁ lters have long, useful lives because of
the low number of operating hours. At the start of the job, air
balancing was performed without any difﬁ culty on this innovative
approach. Ceiling air supply and underﬂ oor return air openings
are used. This design allows for a high ventilation efﬁ ciency to
be used in calculation. To be conservative, we used 0.8 ventila-
tion efﬁ ciency. In addition there is less noise. One hardly hears
the air movement.
Moisture, mold and humidity in the space are easier to control
with reduced OA. No HVAC complaints have been made about
system operation during the last four years.
Local building inspectors and commissioning agents had not
seen another project with reduced OA design based on the IAQ
Procedure. We expected they would have questions about accept-
able CO2 levels. A Standard 62-1999 Interpretation2 answered
most of their questions. Still, ﬁ eld veriﬁ cation of the common
chemicals was important for verifying compliance.
Testing was done with high quality, calibrated portable test
equipment to verify that concentration levels are below ASHRAE
limits. CO2 was below 2,000 ppm (max CO2 was 5,000 ppm) and
ammonia was below 2 ppm—the odor threshold level of ammo-
nia. Ammonia was one of the challenging chemicals in designing
controls in this space. All chemical concentrations are dealt with
Advertisement formerly in this space.
May 2006
ASHRAE Journal
57
School HVAC
Original Plan per Ventilation
Procedure
Designed in 1999, three air-han-
dling units required a total of 15,000
cfm (7079 L/s) outside air with 30%
DS filters (MERV 5).
Optimized per IAQ Procedure
With the use of bipolar ionization
and 80% DS filters (MERV 11), the
outside air is reduced to 7,500 cfm
(3540 L/s). Enthalpy wheel energy
recovery systems were added to save
additional energy.
Benefit
The added cost of the bipolar ion-
ization and MERV 11 filtration was
less then the cost of the larger heat-
ing and cooling load per the standard
design. The net result was a lower
total air-handling unit cost. The annual
energy savings are an added benefit
where the client’s electrical and gas
costs are reduced based on the op-
eration of smaller equipment.
Ventilation Rate
Procedure vs.
IAQ Procedure
and are below odor or exposure levels. The
key was the amount of OA, the chemical
cleaning and level of ﬁ ltration.
Innovation
The IAQ Procedure allows for a large
OA reduction and resulting energy sav-
ings. Although the IAQ Procedure has
been available since 1981, it has been
tedious to calculate all the zone-by-zone
chemical calculations to verify compli-
ance with the standard. With new software
from a manufacturer, project designers
can quickly model the zone’s performance
and calculate all chemical concentrations.
System optimization can be done easily
by applying the IAQ strategy for reduced
OA and its associated energy saving.
Calculations take only a few minutes per
zone. The ﬁ ltration efﬁ ciency needed also
is displayed to show the necessary level of
MERV ﬁ lters to install.
Operation and Maintenance
Operationally, total airﬂ ow was de-
creased, reducing noise and improving
auditorium aesthetics. Site personnel
are pleased with the operation of the
chemical cleaning system and that it is
simple to maintain.
Filter changes are routine and scheduled
when the air-handling units show a pres-
sure drop across the ﬁ lters. With the IAQ
design and the high efﬁ ciency ﬁ lters, the
ﬁ nal ﬁ lters are changed out about every two
years. This design has not been a major ex-
tra cost or labor requirement on the site.
Bipolar ionization was selected over
granular activated carbon design because
of signiﬁ cantly less operating costs, i.e.,
no media change out required. In addi-
tion, a reduced air pressure drop occurs
with bipolar design over carbon media.
Bipolar required less fan horsepower.
Bipolar has less than 0.1 in. w.g. (25 Pa)
of static drop with bipolar tubes vs. 0.8
in. w.g. (200 Pa) with carbon.
The bipolar tubes work on gases and
do not collect dirt.Therefore, there is less
maintenance. The tubes have 30,000 hours
of operational life before needing to be
tested. Replacement is simple and only
takes a few minutes each. A remote panel
monitors if a failure occurs in the system.
Cost Effectiveness
Bipolar was the least expensive design.
This project came in with lower capital
equipment cost with the air cleaning strat-
egy than the standard rooftop design. The
chemical cleaning strategy allowed smaller
rooftop units to be selected with the result-
ing lower electrical consumption.
Although the chemical cleaning units
needed slightly larger horsepower fan mo-
tors with the higher efﬁ ciency ﬁ lters, over-
all the site beneﬁ ted with better IAQ, which
resulted in less exhaust air. The net result
is smaller duct sizes and chiller size that
contributed to more ﬁ rst cost savings. A
$60,000 savings in ﬁ rst costs was achieved
with the bipolar chemical cleaning and re-
duced size of the energy recovery ventilator
over a standard rooftop design.
Recognition
The Ohio School Facility Commission
(OSFC) has recognized the value of the
Advertisement formerly in this space.
58
ASHRAE Journal
ashrae.org
May 2006
School HVAC
IAQ Procedure for reduced OA designs using the chemical air
cleaning approach. In April 2004, OSFC incorporated the IAQ
design option into their master Ohio School Design Manual,
Section 15722 for “Chemical Air Cleaning.” The Brunswick
School project was the ﬁ rst of this design in Ohio. This proj-
ect and others helped state engineers see the value of the IAQ
Procedure.
Summary
More projects should consider using the Standard 62.1-2004
IAQ Procedure because of the reduced energy, improved envi-
ronment and ease of maintenance. Do not wait until you have
a problem space to apply the IAQ Procedure, or before utility
costs force you to look for more savings options.
References
1. 1999 ASHRAE Handbook—HVAC Applications, chap. 44, “Con-
trol of Gaseous Indoor Air Contaminants.”
2. Interpretation IC 62-1999-05 of ASHRAE Standard 62-1999.

School HVAC
By Siang Hwa Lek, P.E., Member ASHRAE, and Zaw Min
Energy Efficiency
For Tropical Campus
T
he National Institute of Education (NIE) is part of the Nanyang
Technological University in Singapore. Efﬁ cient use of energy
TTT
is achieved through energy-efﬁ cient lighting and air-conditioning
systems, an integrated building management system, and extensive
use of natural ventilation and natural lighting. Features include a
VAV air-conditioning system along with natural ventilation, daylight-
ing, and an integrated building management system.
The NIE campus consists of low-
rise buildings ranging from two to six
stories and is built on a 16 ha (40 acre)
site with a triangular plateau carving
out a central plaza as shown in the in
Photograph 1.
Extensively used screens are a
key element of the building façade
providing both for weather protection
and aesthetics.
The campus has been developed to
achieve a tropical green setting and
environment, with each building having
its own courtyard, a main entrance water
feature and landscape, central plaza, a
large 1.2 ha (3 acre) preserved existing
forest to the northeast of the site and
a created forest to the west of the
library/canteen building (Photograph
2). To further enhance the cooling and
greening of the environment, eco-ponds
have been incorporated.
Energy Efﬁ ciency
The tropical climate means that the
NIE requires year-round cooling. Daily
temperature range from 23°C to 34°C
(73°F to 93°F) with a daytime temperature
typically from 28°C to 31°C (82°F to
88°F) throughout the year. Electricity is
the sole energy used in the campus. One
year’s electricity consumption data from
December 2003 to November 2004 is
given in Table 1.
• Air-Conditioning System. A vari-
able air volume (VAV) system is
used for most areas. The fans of
the air-handling units (AHUs) are
controlled by a variable speed drive
(VSD). They do not run at full speed
all the time, saving energy. The chill-
ers use R-134a refrigerant and have
an efﬁ ciency of 0.60 kW/ton (0.17
About the Authors
Siang Hwa Lek, P.E., is a vice president of the
M&E Engineering Division of CPG Consultants in
Singapore. Zaw Min is an executive engineer in
the Development and Estate Department of the
National Institute of Education in Singapore.
© 2006 American Society of Heating, Refrigerating and Air-Conditioning Engineers, Inc. (www.ashrae.org). Published in ASHRAE Journal (Vol. 48, May
2006). For personal use only. Additional distribution in either paper or digital form is not permitted without ASHRAE’s permission.
May 2006
ASHRAE Journal
49
Photograph 1: The National Institute of Education in Singapore features include a VAV system, natural ventilation, daylighting, and an
integrated building management system.
kW/kW) of refrigeration. In addition, automatic tube clean-
ing machines are installed to ensure continuous cleaning
of the condenser tubes by circulating brushes through the
condenser water circuit. The cumulative effect of these
provisions enables the chiller and the air systems to operate
at very high efﬁ ciencies and thus saves energy.
• Lighting System. Using daylighting, the average light-
ing load for the campus is 10 W/m2 (108 W/ft2) This
This
is much lower than the requirements of ANSI/
ASHRAE/IESNA 90.1-2001, Energy Standard
for Buildings Except Low-Rise Residential
Buildings, at 20 W/m2 (215 W/ft2). Lighting
in common areas such as external corridors,
staircases and canteen is controlled and oper-
ated by the integrated building management
nt
system (IBMS). Motion detectors are integrated w i t h
the lights for toilets, basement parking lot and selected
internal corridors. Electronic ballasts and energy-saving
lamps are used throughout the campus. Unnecessary
high-power induction lamps and decorative lighting have
been avoided. One-hundred and eighty software-driven
electrical power monitoring meters monitor energy con-
sumption of different areas and produce audit reports for
continuous monitoring/improvement.
• Integrated Building Management System. The IBMS
monitors and controls the mechanical and electrical (M&E)
systems and optimizes start/stop of chillers. Other energy
features of the IBMS include time and event scheduling
to operate air-conditioning and lights.
• Natural Ventilation and Lighting. With the linear
building design, the average depth of the typical ﬂ oor plates
for lecture rooms and staff ofﬁ ces is no more than 12 m
(40 ft) from an external wall or window. This maximized
the use of daylighting. Skylights have been incorporated
the us
r the elevator lobbies on the top ﬂ oors. Glass doors
for
acing the exterior of the buildings are used to take
fa
advantage of natural lighting.
The canteen has been designed with an open
concept letting in daylight and using natural
ventilation. As a result, artificial lighting is not
v
required for the canteen during daytime operating
req
hours. Aluminium sunscreens have been extensively used
for all the single-loaded arcades and corridors. The louvered
sunscreens cut off direct sunlight into the corridors and rooms
but allow the free ﬂ ow of air.
Indoor Air Quality
The occupants in the air-conditioned spaces are protected
with high efﬁ ciency air ﬁ lters comprising the primary ﬁ lters and
secondary ﬁ lters having a ﬁ ltration rating of MERV 12 (Mini-
mum Efﬁ ciency Reporting Value) based on ANSI/ASHRAE
52.2-1999, Method of Testing General Ventilation Air-Cleaning
50
ASHRAE Journal
ashrae.org
May 2006
School HVAC
Devices for Removal Efﬁ ciency by Particle Size. This ensures
that at least 90% of 3 to 10 µm dust particles and 80% of 1 to
3 µm dust particles are removed.
Innovation
In an effort to conserve potable water, artiﬁ cial grass ﬂ ooring
(AstroTurf) is used for an outdoor hockey pitch in the campus. The
artiﬁ cial grass still requires watering to soften the ﬂ ooring before a

Table 1: One year of electricity consumption data.
game. Compared with natural grass, Astro-
turf needs much less water for its upkeep. A
nearby water tank collects rainwater, which
is used to water the pitch. Only when the
rainwater storage is insufﬁ cient due to a
continuous dry spell or consecutive heavy
use of the pitch is makeup water from a
potable source required.
Operation and Maintenance
The senior management of the NIE is
committed to the energy conservation
initiative in the Campus. The Develop-
ment and Estate Department (DED) con-
tinuously monitors the energy trends and
implements new techniques and policies to
conserve energy. The senior management
supports and enforces energy conservation
measures, such as no extension of provi-
sion of air-conditioning beyond normal
ofﬁ ce hours for an individual staff room.
• Maintenance Practice. The DED
has an in-house energy management
group, which works closely with the
maintenance contractors for continu-
ous improvement in energy conser-
vation initiative. The maintenance
contractors are required to carry out
proper and regular preventive main-
tenance for all M&E systems. The
regular servicing and breakdown
maintenance are strictly monitored
and enforced by the DED.
Standard operating procedure is imple-
mented for the control and monitoring of
all major plant operations. Maintenance
contractors must check through the IBMS
to ensure that all AHUs (except 24-hour
units) have been automatically switched
off by the IBMS at the end of each day.
Procedure for the DED staff and mainte-
nance contractors is to walk through and
shut down any AHU for the common fa-
cilities if they were found unoccupied, al-
though the facility may have been booked
through the facility booking system.

Figure 2: Total energy consumption chart for past four years.
• Facility Management System. The setpoints for the air-
conditioning system have been updated and revised to
meet the new requirements and to ensure that the systems
are running optimally. Changes of chilled water setpoints,
operation parameters of the dehumidiﬁ er and AHU have
been made. For example, the original chilled water set-
point is raised from 6.7°C to 7.7°C (44°F to 45.9°F) (day
operation) and 8.5°C (47.3°F) (night operation) without
compromising the thermal comfort of the occupants and
the AHUs’ off-coil temperature is set at 15°C (59°F) with
space conditions adjusted to 24°C (75°F) at 70% RH.
• Continuous Improvement. After operating for two years,
VSDs were installed for primary chilled water pumps to
regulate the supply of chilled water based on the feedback
from the IBMS. With this alteration, no need exists to by-
pass any excessive chilled water. A VSD also is installed to
the condenser water pumps and cooling towers with return
temperature control sensor for night operation and to some
Advertisement formerly in this space.

ASHRAE Journal
53
School HVAC
Photograph 2: A miniature secondary forest
was created to the west of the library/canteen
building to block the western sun from the
canteen area, which is naturally ventilated.
Rare jungle species of plants and trees were
planted at random to give the forest a natural
secondary forest look.
big capacity constant air volume
AHUs with return air temperature
feedback control for them to func-
tion as single-zone VAV system.
Upgrading of the IBMS software
provides direct integration with
facility booking system. In this in-
tegration, common facilities such as
lecture theatres, tutorial rooms and
sports hall are installed with a card
access system and interfaced with
online Web-based facility booking
system to control the electro-mag-
netic locking device, lighting and
air conditioning according to the
booking hours.
• Adjustment of Operating Hours.
The energy management group
studied the chiller operating load and
adjusted the operation sequence of
the chiller system from three chill-
ers (2 × 800 refrigeration tons [2813
kW] and 1 × 400 refrigeration tons
[1407 kW]) to two chillers (2 × 800
refrigeration tons [2813 kW]) and
operating time from 7 a.m. – 7 pm
to 6:30 a.m. – 6:30 p.m. Substantial
energy saving has been achieved
by switching off the night chiller
at midnight. At the same time, only
the chilled water pump is running
(without chillers) until 6 a.m. by
taking advantage of low load at
night and the high energy storage
capacity of the primary chilled water
mains. All operating parameters of
24-hour air-conditioning spaces are
not affected.
Cost Effectiveness
The original design and continuous
improvement measures implementation
have both contributed towards saving in
energy.
• Savings in Energy Cost from the
Original Design. The energy cost
has been signiﬁ cantly reduced with
the incorporation of original light-
ing and air-conditioning design and
system selection such as IBMS,
efﬁ cient lighting and chillers, au-
tomatic tube cleaning system, VAV
system and effective modular de-
sign of fan-coils for the campus.
• Further Savings through Continu-
ous Improvement. Further savings
is achieved through continuous
monitoring of energy use, review
and improvement of operations.
Adjustment and ﬁ ne-tuning of chill-
ers operating mode and hours have
achieved an energy saving of 45,000
kWh per month. The result is shown
in the chiller energy consumption
trend curves in Figure 1. The addi-
tion of VSD to various equipment
has resulted in a savings of 130,000
kWh per month. Furthermore, the
good maintenance practice imple-
mented for the campus M&E sys-
tems has proven to be effective and
signiﬁ cantly reduced breakdowns of
systems. This has resulted in saving
of maintenance cost. Energy savings
are shown in Figure 2, where the
total energy consumption for the
campus is compared on a month-by-
month basis for the past four years.
Environmental Impacts
To further improve the cooling and
greening of the environment, the follow-
ing had been incorporated to the overall
design of the campus.
Conclusion
The challenge in Singapore is to design
and maintain an energy-efﬁ cient and sus-
tainable building in a tropical climate. The
use of modern technology and innovative
design coupled with the ﬁ rm commitment
of the senior management of NIE will en-
sure that the energy-efﬁ ciency efforts will
be sustained on a long-term basis.
Advertisement formerly in this space.
40
ASHRAE Journal
ashrae.org
May 2006
School HVAC
O
ne of four high schools in the Blue Valley School District (lo-
cated in a southern suburb of Kansas City, Kan.) experienced
several indoor air quality (IAQ) issues associated with increasing
student population and limitations of the existing mechanical
systems. The district decided to research the problem at the Blue
Valley North High School (BVNHS) and have the design team ex-
plore new and innovative design solutions. The designs dramatically
improved the learning environment, so the district has duplicated
the solutions throughout the Blue Valley School District.
James E. Megerson, P.E., is vice president of
Larson Binkley Inc. in Leawood, Kan., and prin-
cipal in charge of LBI’s institutional projects. He
won a 2006 ASHRAE Technology Award for this
project. Chris Torline, P.E., is a manager of busi-
ness development with LBI. They can be reached
via jim.megerson@larsonbinkley.com or chris.
torline@larsonbinkley.com.
The Opportunity
BVNHS was built in 1986 when build-
ing codes only required 5 cfm (2.4 L/s)
of outside air per occupant compared
to current ASHRAE standards of 15 to
20 cfm (7 to 9.4 L/s). The school had a
history of complaints about poor IAQ.
Several studies by the U.S. Environmental
Protection Agency, an industrial hygien-
ist, and consulting engineers concluded
that an inadequate amount of outside air,
coupled with poorly performing HVAC
systems, were creating indoor conditions
that were not conducive to learning.
In addition, several systems within the
building were inefﬁ cient. Therefore, the
opportunity for energy conservation was
a signiﬁ cant factor driving the design and
construction of this project.
To ensure students and staff would not
be disturbed during the school year, the
project was constructed in two separate
phases during consecutive summer breaks.
The 270,000 ft2 (25 000 m2) building was
divided into two nearly equal phases that
required the project to use two separate
systems during the interim school year.
Phase 1 included the library, courtyard,
classroom wings, and boiler plant. Phase
By James E. Megerson, P.E., Member ASHRAE, and Chris Torline, P.E., Associate Member ASHRAE
About the Authors
Strategies for
Improving IAQ
© 2006 American Society of Heating, Refrigerating and Air-Conditioning Engineers, Inc. (www.ashrae.org). Published in ASHRAE Journal (Vol. 48, May
2006). For personal use only. Additional distribution in either paper or digital form is not permitted without ASHRAE’s permission.
May 2006
ASHRAE Journal
41
2 included the administration area, commons, gymnasiums,
kitchen, and theater.
To complete the project renovations during the short time
frame when school was out during the summer, each replace-
ment rooftop unit was coordinated to use adapter curbs to take
advantage of the existing roof penetrations.
Answering the Challenge
The district’s primary goal was enhancing the educational
environment by eliminating IAQ complaints and controlling
the comfort level of heating, cooling, and humidity in each
y in each
space. Because of the IAQ issues that needed attention,
the designers offered completely redesigned building
systems. Blue Valley School District viewed the situ-
ation as an opportunity to upgrade the performance
of the entire building, rather than just attempting to
patch the failed system.
The new systems were designed to be efficient
ent
to reduce annual utility costs. It was predicted that the
amount of outside air brought into the building would need
to be increased, which would increase the amount of en-
ergy required for heating, cooling, and dehumidiﬁ cation.
Therefore, it became critical to design building systems that
would operate as efﬁ ciently as possible to offset increased
energy consumption.
Another important consideration was to design the system so
building personnel could maintain it well. Air ﬁ lters needed to
be accessible and a strict ﬁ lter change-out policy developed and
followed. The energy management system needed to be pro-
grammed to control the systems properly, and it had to be user-
friendly to allow for daily use and ongoing modiﬁ cations.
The Solution
The original HVAC system consisted of fourteen rooftop
units that were designed to provide 55°F (13°C) supply air to
97 fan-powered mixing boxes. Seven of the rooftop units were
97 fan pow
signed as constant volume units. The remaining seven
des
were variable air volume units with discharge damper
w
volume control. Hot water reheat coils located at
each of the fan-powered mixing boxes provided
heating to the space. Ventilation air was set based
upon 5 cfm (2.4 L/s) of outside air per occupant.
u
The new air-distribution system is a displacement
ventilation design (Table 1). Using a displacement ventilation
system increases the ventilation effectiveness of the buildings
air distribution as well as reduces energy use. Preliminary
analysis indicated that one-third less energy would be needed to
heat, cool, and dehumidify outdoor air ventilation. Variable air
volume rooftop units, using variable frequency drive technology
and return air bypass, provide 65°F (18°C) supply air (Figure
(F
(
Blue Valley North High School in Kansas was retroﬁ tted with a displacement ventilation system and DDC to solve IAQ complaints.
42
ASHRAE Journal
ashrae.org
May 2006
School HVAC
Figure 2: Displacement ventilation air-distribution system.
1). Cooling is provided by direct expansion cooling coils in the
rooftop units. Hot water coils are located in the rooftop units
to heat the mixed air to 65°F (18°C) during the winter months.
Most internal classrooms when occupied require cooling during
the winter. Heating of perimeter
classrooms is accomplished by
hot water coils in variable air
volume terminal units. To meet
ANSI/ASHRAE/IESNA Standard
90.1, Energy Standard for Build-
ings Except Low-Rise Residential
Buildings, the system controls are
set up to prevent simultaneous
heating and cooling.
To efficiently deliver condi-
tioned air to the spaces, variable air
volume rooftop units were installed
which use variable frequency drives. The new variable air volume
systems replaced inefﬁ cient systems, some were constant volume
and some were variable air volume using discharge dampers.
Air is distributed into the space via low velocity diffus-
ers located at the ﬂ oor level of each room (Figure 2). Close
coordination between the owner, architect, and engineer
ensured that the displacement diffusers were placed in
unobtrusive locations.
By delivering 65°F (18°C) air at low velocity, near the ﬂ oor in
the space, the air within the room is allowed to stratify, rising as it
is warmed and contaminated by building occupants and internal
loads. Return air registers located in or near the ceiling allow the
air to return through the plenum
to the rooftop unit. Exhaust fans
located on the roof are controlled
with variable frequency drives to
relieve pressure while maintaining
a positive static building pressure
of 0.05 in. w.g. (12.5 Pa). These
exhaust fans were located strate-
gically to relieve a portion of the
warmer, contaminated stratiﬁ ed air
before reaching the rooftop units,
avoiding that load on the system.
Boiler Plant
Two original heating hot water boilers, which had a total output
of 6,400 Btu/h (1876 W), were removed. New load calculations
were performed based on several factors of the new system, and
ﬁ ve new equally sized pulse combustion boilers were installed.
Because of new equipment and control strategies, only four
boilers were needed to satisfy the heating load, which allows
one boiler to be available for redundancy. A new boiler control

Hot Water Heating Coil
Figure 1: Schematic of variable air volume rooftop unit.
Smoke Detector
Typ.
Typical displacement ventilation diffusers located within the common areas and classrooms.
44
ASHRAE Journal
May 2006
School HVAC
rooftop unit is set at 65°F (18°C). If the
relative humidity sensor in the return air
duct rises above 55%, the compressors
of the rooftop unit are controlled to pro-
vide 55°F (13°C) supply air leaving the
cooling coil. The bypass damper, which
permits return air to bypass the cooling
coil, is then modulated open. This allows
the warmer return air to mix with the
coil leaving air to maintain a constant
65°F (18°C) supply air temperature.
Once the supply air temperature has
remained below 55% relative humidity
for 10 minutes, the unit is switched back
into normal control.
Lighting System
The original primary lighting system
throughout Blue Valley North High School
consisted of 2 ft by 4 ft (0.6 by 1.2 m),
three-lamp, ﬂ uorescent ﬁ xtures containing
T12 lamps and magnetic ballasts. The new
lighting system that was installed included
2 ft by 4 ft (0.6 by 1.2 m), three-lamp,
ﬂ uorescent ﬁ xtures containing T8 lamps
and electronic ballasts. Occupancy sensors
were installed to control the new lighting
ﬁ xtures in every classroom, hallway, and
other miscellaneous spaces to reduce the
lighting hours of operation.
The lighting retroﬁ t was a signiﬁ cant op-
portunity to reduce the energy consumption
of the lighting system as well as the heating
load produced by the lights, potentially
offsetting the additional energy required to
treat additional outdoor air introduction.
Indoor Air Quality Results
Because the building had a long history
of occupant complaints, the indoor air
quality and the occupant thermal comfort
were major factors when considering the
design of the new building systems. To
system incorporating a reset schedule was
provided to allow the new pulse combus-
tion boiler system to operate efﬁ ciently.
Pulse combustion boilers, in conjunction
with a boiler management system to reset
heating water temperature, create a higher
boiler plant seasonal efﬁ ciency.
Building Automation System
The district struggled to maintain the
existing building automation system.
Several generations of control systems
were installed within the facility, which
caused difﬁ culty with maintenance and
upkeep. Additionally, some of the exist-
ing control system consisted of electric
controls, and some equipment contained
original pneumatic controls. The existing
system’s programming and the myriad of
control types made maintenance difﬁ cult,
which directly resulted in complaints re-
garding occupant discomfort and IAQ.
The new Web-enabled energy manage-
ment control system allows maintenance
personnel to monitor and troubleshoot
issues from any Internet connection. En-
ergy-efﬁ cient control strategies include
outside air economizer cycle, dehumidi-
ﬁ cation bypass strategies, CO2 and out-
door air monitoring stations, unoccupied
night setback temperature control, and
optimized building warm-up cycles. By
designing and controlling the system to
provide 65°F (18°C) supply air to the
spaces rather than the traditional design
of 55°F (13°C), 350 additional hours are
available for free cooling.
Humidity is controlled by using a
return air bypass at the rooftop unit.
A relative humidity sensor in the com-
mon return air duct of each rooftop unit
monitors humidity. During the cooling
season, the supply air temperature of the
Table 1: Traditional mixing system vs. displacement ventilation.
Traditional Mixing System
Displacement Ventilation

• 60°F – 65°F Supply Air Temp.
• Overhead Diffusers

• Cools/Heats Only One-Third to Two-Thirds
Of Volume

Advertisement formerly in this space.
May 2006
ASHRAE Journal
45
School HVAC
ensure superior IAQ, each room is equipped with a dry-bulb
temperature sensor and a CO2 sensor.
To bring ventilation rates up to meet current code, the amount
of outside air was increased from the original design of 25,404
cfm up to 51,635 cfm (12 000 L/s to 24 300 L/s) for the new
design. This results in a required increased cooling capacity of
approximately 95 tons (334 kW).
The displacement ventilation system was chosen because of
eration throughout the design process. The district desired a
system that could be adequately maintained to ensure that the
building continued to perform as designed.
Several systems and equipment were used in the design to
reduce operation and maintenance requirements. The original
system contained 97 fan-powered mixing boxes that contained
individual air ﬁ lters. The new system was designed with single
duct variable air volume terminal units, with hot water heating
its ability to effectively deliver ventilation
air to the building occupants. The diffusers
are designed to distribute air at 50 fpm
(0.25 m/s), which results in an extremely
quiet system and meets ANSI’s Standard S
12.60-2002, Acoustical Performance Cri-
teria, Design Requirements, and Guide-
lines for Schools. The new system reduces
noise and eliminates drafts. As the air rises
to the ceiling, it is returned to the rooftop
unit or exhausted from the building. This
air delivery system is more effective than
a mixed-air system at providing ventilation
air to the occupants because the air within
the space is not continually mixed from
ceiling to ﬂ oor, and contaminants are not
recirculated. Because of this, displacement
ventilation has an allowable ASHRAE
ventilation effectiveness of 1.2 as opposed
to 0.8 of a mixed air system.
CO2 sensors were installed in each zone,
which are used to allow the outdoor air ven-
tilation rate to reduce when the space condi-
tions drop below 1,100 ppm. This allows
for an additional reduction in the amount of
ventilation air brought into the space when
the building is not 100% occupied, further
reducing energy consumption.
Ambient CO2 levels in Kansas City
range between 400 and 450 ppm. ANSI/
ASHRAE Standard 62, Ventilation for
Acceptable Indoor Air Quality, states
that CO2 levels should be maintained at
less than 700 ppm above ambient condi-
tions. Typical CO2 levels at the school
well exceeded 1,500 ppm with the old
systems. After the new displacement
ventilation and CO2 monitoring control
system were installed, CO2 levels rarely
exceed 1,000 ppm. The perception of IAQ
improvements is positive, and has greatly
reduced occupant complaints.
Improvements in O&M
The continued performance of the new
building systems was a critical consid-
Advertisement formerly in this space.
46
ASHRAE Journal
ashrae.org
May 2006
School HVAC
tion. Also, it converted a middle school to displacement ven-
tilation, and is in the design phase to convert a second middle
school and two elementary schools this summer with four other
major conversions planned in the future.
The success of the IAQ improvements and occupant comfort
continues to validate the design beneﬁ ts of implementing a dis-
placement ventilation system in either new or existing schools.
The design application for school use results in achieving a goal
of an enhanced educational environment for students and teach-
ers with an added bonus of a system design that can be replicated
with success in school districts in other municipalities.
coils installed on perimeter zones. This eliminated 97 recircu-
lating fans and 97 air ﬁ lters.
Two heating hot water boilers and one domestic hot water
heater that were nearing the end of their useful life were replaced
with new, efﬁ cient equipment. This eliminated an increasing
number of repairs to the aging equipment and provided new
equipment warranties to the district. The old boilers had heat
exchanger surfaces rated at twice the capacity of the burners,
presumably to allow for future growth. This caused condensa-
tion of the ﬂ ue gas, which created maintenance issues every
year with tube sheet separation because the boilers were not
designed for this operating condition.
The new pulse combustion boilers have
few moving parts, which help to reduce
maintenance cost. Because these boilers
are designed to condense, they tolerate
very low return water temperature, there-
by eliminating the need for a primary and
secondary pumping loop.
All of the existing pneumatic and
electric control systems, which consisted
of several generations of control equip-
ment, were replaced with a common
direct digital control system. The new
system uses LonWorks® protocol to allow
interoperable communication throughout
the equipment. A Web-enabled graphical
user interface allows for user-friendly
diagnostics and changes to the system
by district personnel.
Conclusion
By recognizing and deﬁ nitively address-
ing the deﬁ ciencies their old systems had
developed, the Blue Valley School District
and the design team were able to design
and construct building systems that in-
creases the comfort of the occupants and
provides superior indoor air quality. The
use of highly efﬁ cient equipment and con-
trol systems provides for energy savings
above what additional energy was required
to condition the increased amount of venti-
lation air. The reduced maintenance of the
equipment and the user-friendly energy
management control system allows for
easy monitoring and equipment diagno-
sis which increases the effectiveness of
sustained system operation.
Since implementing the displacement
ventilation system at BVNHS, the Blue
Valley School District has constructed
three new schools using displacement
ventilation with underﬂ oor air distribu-

ASHRAE Journal

School HVAC
Ground-Source
Heat Pumps
Energy Efficiency for Two Canadian Schools
Editor’s Note: This article discusses some of the speciﬁ c technologies at École du
Tournant in Québec. For an overview of the school’s technologies and design, see
“Head of the Class for No GHG-School” on Page 22.
G
round-source heat pumps (GSHP) are an efﬁ cient space-
conditioning technology that helps decrease electricity use
and reduce peak demand. With school boards facing increasingly
tight budgets, this technology offers an effective solution to re-
duce heating and cooling energy costs and enhance classroom
comfort. It also ensures longer and safer service life for the
equipment and improves the building’s general appearance.
Two GSHP systems were implemented in two secondary schools
in Québec with, respectively, horizontal and vertical ground heat
exchangers. The purpose was to demonstrate their energy perfor-
mances in a cold climate to extend their application throughout
the Canadian educational buildings.
By Vasile Minea, Ph.D., Member ASHRAE
The GSHP systems use solar energy
stored in soil. As in traditional water closed-
loop heat pump concepts, these systems
offer opportunities to save additional en-
ergy since the heat recovered from zones
requiring cooling can be transferred to
zones with simultaneous heating demands.
They also offer the possibility of using other
free, inexhaustible and ecological energy
sources such as direct solar energy and
exhaust air waste heat. The main issue in
Canada concerns optimizing the mechani-
cal design and controls strategies of the
GSHP systems to reduce the capital costs
and increase market acceptance.
Multi-Energy GSHP System With
Horizontal Ground Heat Exchanger
One objective of this particular GSHP
system was to ensure a maximum level
Vasile Minea, Ph.D., is scientist researcher for the
Hydro-Québec Research Institute, LTE Laboratory
in Shawinigan, PQ, Canada.

2006). For personal use only. Additional distribution in either paper or digital form is not permitted without ASHRAE s permission.
May 2006
ASHRAE Journal
29
of energy autonomy by recovering and efﬁ ciently managing the
building internal gains, ground and direct solar energy sources,
and the heat recovered from the oil-ﬁ red backup generator.
System Description
A 167-ton (584.5 kW) ground-source heat pumps system with
distributed brine-to-air heat pumps, one of the rare institutional,
closed-loop systems with horizontal ground heat exchanger, was
installed and monitored in a school near Montreal (École Profes-
(É
(
sionnelle de Saint-Hyacinthe) (Photograph 1
(P
(
). This school with
500 to 700 students has a total surface area of 11 426 m2 (122,989
ft2). About 60% of this ﬂ oor area (the north side of the building,
which includes classrooms, ofﬁ ces and a two-story common area)
is heated and air conditioned by 35 brine-to-air heat pumps (12.2
to 21 kW [3.5 to 6 tons]) connected to a reverse closed-loop and
a horizontal ground heat exchanger.1 Eleven of these heat pumps
provide air conditioning to the peripheral zones and the others,
to the central zone of the building (Figure 1
(F
(
).
Two primary energy sources are used for heating and cooling:
electricity and natural gas. Natural gas is used as a second stage in
the fresh air preheating system, while the electricity is used by the
heat pumps’ compressors, blowers and backup coils, brine circulat-
ing pump and peripheral baseboards. Three particularities should
be mentioned: a 618 m2 (6652 ft2t2t ) solar wall on the south side
of the building, that recovers solar energy to partially preheat the
fresh air (4720 L/s [10,000 cfm]); a ground heat exchanger (69.8
m/ton [229 ft/ton]) that includes two sections totaling 32 loops
buried in a damp clay: the ﬁ rst, buried at a depth of 1.83 m (6 ft)
and the second, at 1.22 m (4 ft); and a backup, oil-ﬁ red generator
that supplies power to the GSHP system at outdoor temperatures
lower than –12°C (10.4°F) (during winter peak periods), or in
emergency situations. Waste heat from the exhaust gases and the
engine’s cooling ﬂ uid is recovered and stored into a 100 m3 (3,530
ft3) storage tank. A three-way valve alternates the geothermal ﬂ uid
ﬂ ow between the ground heat exchanger and the storage tank. So,
when necessary, the stored heat is used for space heating.
Construction Costs
The actual cost of the complete GSHP system, based on the
contractor cost schedule, was $589,316 in 1996 U.S. dollars
(Table 1). This ﬁ gure includes horizontal ground heat exchangers,
heat pumps, internal piping, ductwork, circulation pumps, pas-
sive solar wall, preheating unit, back-up boiler, electrical power
distribution, storage tank and energy management system costs.1
The speciﬁ c construction costs of the actual multi-energy GSHP
system was of approximately 86 US$/m2 (7.99 US$/ft2) for a
surface of 6856 m2 (73,798 ft2) supplied by the geothermal heat
pump units. For economic comparison purposes, a conventional
30 680 L/s (65,000 cfm) central HVAC system with a 525 kW
(150 tons) water chiller, two 0.5 W (1.8 Btu/h) natural gas boilers
and 35 induction terminal units was considered as an alternative
system. The conventional system also includes cooling towers,
ventilation ducting and insulation, heating piping, circulation
pumps and a central DDC system (Table 1). The labor costs were
established based on Means Mechanical Cost Data and Means
Plumbing Cost Data. The cost of the actual GSHP system was
26.3% less than the cost of the conventional system.1
Soil Temperatures
At a depth of 1.83 m (6 ft), the lowest temperatures of the un-
disturbed soil, at about 10 m (32.8 ft) distance from the ground
HEX, were measured in April (4°C [39.2°F]), about two months

after the period when the minimum outdoor temperatures were
at their lowest. The ground temperature has decreased by ap-
proximately 1°C (1.8°F) per 0.3048 m (1 ft) of depth during the
cold periods of the year, and increased by about 2°C (3.6°F) per
0.3048 m (1 ft) between May and August. The soil temperature
reversals took place in April and October, respectively, when
the temperature of the undisturbed ground was practically the
same (around 4°C [39.2°F]) in the ﬁ rst 1.83 m (6 ft) of depth.
On the other hand, the maximum temperature of the closed-
loop brine (25% by weight water/methanol) was 29.6°C (85.3°F)
in July, while its lowest temperature was measured in March
(5.3°C [41.5°F]) (Figure 2
(F
(
). Finally, the temperature T between
T
the heating transfer medium inside the ground heat exchanger and
the soil at a distance of 152.4 mm (6 in.) from the buried pipes had
varied between 1°C (1.8°F) and 3°C (5.4°F) in winter (heating
mode), to about 6°C (10.8°F) in summer (cooling mode).
Energy Consumption
The heating energy accumulated in the heating transfer
medium (brine) was provided by the soil, internal gains, heat
pumps’ compressors, the brine circulation pump and the heat
reclaim loop of the backup boiler. The rest of the heating en-
ergy was supplied by other sources as ventilation fans, passive
solar wall, natural gas-ﬁ red boiler, and peripheral electrical
backup baseboards. Between November and March, the heat
transferred from the ground heat exchanger represented 39%
of the building total equivalent heating demand. The seasonal
coefﬁ cient of performance (SCOP) of the heat pumps was 3.5.
During this period, a number of heat pumps operated in cooling
mode about 7.5% of the time, with a seasonal energy efﬁ ciency
ratio (SEER) of 19.2.
Most part of the total annual electrical energy consumption of
the GSHP system (Figure 3
(F
(
) was assumed by the heat pumps’
compressors, blowers and electrical back-up heating coils (74%).
The brine circulating pump and the outdoor air makeup fan
consumed together 19% of the GSHP annual electrical energy
consumption, and the peripheral electrical backup baseboards
7%. During the ﬁ rst monitored winter, when the system was
balanced and adjusted several times, the building’s equivalent
energy consumption, including electricity and natural gas, was
about 0.69 GJ/m2/year (192 kWh/m2), only 8% lower than the
average consumption of all provincial schools.1

Operating Experiences
During the ﬁ rst heating season, the energy performance of
the ground HEX was relatively good, but not fully satisfactory.
A major improvement aimed at alternating its operation with the
storage tank was undertaken. The geothermal ﬂ uid was alterna-
tively diverted by the three-way valve to the ground HEX or to the
heat exchanger, depending on the actual brine return temperature.
This allowed the soil to better recover its thermal capacity and
avoid any unnecessary heat rejection from the system. As a result,
the heat recovery capacity of the ground HEX was improved by
more than 20% during the second heating season. On the other
hand, the closed-loop circulation pump, largely over-designed, ran
continuously even during the night and weekends. That explains
the high energy consumption of the brine pump, although it
normally should represent no more than 6% to 7% of the GSHP
system total electrical energy consumption. In addition, inside
the open, two-story covered common area, a largely glassed-in
space located on the north side of the building, the occurrence
of a high level of vertical air stratiﬁ cation was observed because
of an inadequate supply and return air ducting. Four ceiling fans
were ﬁ rst installed, but they proved to be inadequate to make
the temperature uniform and to maintain a satisfactory level of
comfort. Later, infrared natural gas radiators were installed and
the problem was solved.
On the other hand, the local electricity utility (Hydro-
Québec) had put an end to its winter load shedding policy,
one year after the beginning of the project. Consequently, the
heat recovery from the backup generator was performed only
during the periodical testing, or in emergency situations such
as a severe ice storm.
A few years later, an extension was built onto the school covering
almost the same surface area. A section of the new building is heat-
ed by concrete radiant ﬂ oors with hot water as thermal ﬂ uid. The
same horizontal ground heat exchanger is used with the addition
of a number of vertical boreholes for heating demand peaks.

The ﬁ rst characteristic of this decentralized GSHP system
recently implemented (2003) at École du Tournant a secondary

school located in Montreal region is its extreme design simplic-
ity. This minimized the construction and maintenance costs in
addition to an absence of noise and outdoor disturbances, and
longer heat pump’s technical life vs. most traditional rooftop
Advertisement formerly in this space.
May 2006
ASHRAE Journal
35
units.2The system uses solar energy by direct recovery through
passive solar walls for makeup air preheating and reduces the
greenhouse gases emissions because no fuel is used. Improved
control strategies, including barometric pressure measurement
and natural light and CO2 detection sensors, also are used.
System Description
The two-story school with 2682 m2 (28,869 ft2) in total ﬂ oor
surface and 220 students, is equipped with a decentralized
GSHP system with a reverse return closed-loop (Figure 4).
Backup electrical coils (110 kW) cover the heating demand
peaks, but no device exists to reject the excess heat (such as a
cooling tower). A pump station circulates the geothermal ﬂ uid
in the closed loop, which is connected to twenty-ﬁ ve 2.6 to 35
kW water-to-water heat pumps (0.75 to 10 tons), totalling 203
kW (58 tons) in nominal cooling capacity. The ground heat
exchanger contains 18 vertical U-tubes in boreholes of 119 m
(390 ft) in depth and 2196 m (7,203 ft) in total length.
