Speciﬁ c Collector Area (m2/kW)
Single-effect absorption systems8 have a thermal COP in the
range of 0.50 – 0.73 of which LiBr/H2O systems average 0.66
and H2O/NH3 systems average 0.60. Double-effect absorption
systems can reach a COP of 1.3 when the driving temperature
is sufﬁ ciently high. In the evaluated projects, the driving tem-
perature range is 60°C to 165°C (140°F to 329°F). The majority
operate below 97°C (207°F), using ﬂ at plate solar collectors.
Based on our survey, adsorption systems8 have a lower thermal
COP, averaging 0.59. However, since adsorption systems can oper-
ate at a lower driving temperature than absorption systems (52.5°C
to 82°C or 127°F to 180°F), and accounting for working conditions,
they show a relatively high average value of CEF (0.38), which is
Advertisement formerly in this space.
Advertisement formerly in this space.
18
ASHRAE Journal
ashrae.org
June 2006
June 2006
ASHRAE Journal
19
higher temperatures but at a lower collector efﬁ ciency. Com-
pound parabolic concentrators can reach 97°C to 165°C (207°F
to 329°F). The variation of the system’s thermal COP with the
driving temperature is illustrated in Figure 2. As expected, the
COP increases with the driving temperature.
The average speciﬁ c solar collector area for the evaluated
projects8 was 3.6 m²/kW (136 ft2/ton), ranging from 0.5 to 5.5
m²/kW (19 – 208 ft2/ton). Adsorption and absorption systems
typically use more than 2 m²/kW (76 ft2/ton) and usually lower
than 5 m²/kW (189 ft2/ton). Overall, H2O/NH3 systems require
larger speciﬁ c collector areas than LiBr/H2O systems. As a
result, the installations usually are more expensive (Figure 3
F
).
The initial overall cost of the evaluated projects8 per installed
cooling capacity in kW averages about 4000 Euro/kW ($5,050/
kW), excluding the cost for distribution networks between the
system and the application and the delivery units.
Auxiliary Equipment
The average auxiliary electrical power (for fans and pumps)
as a percent of the cooling capacity is about 22.5%.8 The aver-
age speciﬁ c water consumption is 5.3
kg/h per kW of average cooling capac-
ity (41 lb/ ton-h). The majority of the
projects consume 4 to 6 kg/h per kW
cooling capacity (30.9 to 46.4 lb/ton-h),
depending on the technologies used.8
The ﬁ gures indicate that a considerable
potential exists for future improvements
since large differences exist in auxiliary
energy and water consumption.
Guidelines for Design, Control &
Operation
Each technology has speciﬁ c char-
acteristics that match the building’s
HVAC design, loads and local climatic
conditions.5 A good design must ﬁ rst
exploit all available solar radiation and
then cover the remaining loads from
conventional sources.
Proper calculations for collector and
storage size depend on the solar cooling
technology used. A hot water storage
may be integrated between solar collec-
tors and heat-driven chiller to dampen
the ﬂ uctuations in the return temperature
of the hot water from the chiller.
Storage size depends on the applica-
tion. When cooling loads mainly occur
during the day, then a smaller storage will
be necessary than when the loads peak in
the evening. Heating the hot water stor-
age by the backup heat source should be
strictly avoided. The storage’s only func-
tion is to store excess heat of the solar
system and to make it available when
sufﬁ cient solar heat is not available.
Absorption
The single-effect machine gives best
results with a heat supply temperature of
80°C to 100°C (176°F to 212°F). Double-
and triple-effect machines require higher
supply temperatures. Therefore, if one
selects these machines, then a higher

ASHRAE Journal
ashrae.org
June 2006
June 2006
ASHRAE Journal
21
temperature collector is required. However,
with a double-effect system, if the solar-
supplied temperature drops below 100°C
(212°F), the performance will drop sharply,
below that of a single-effect system.
Most large-scale applications (300 kW
[85 ton] and up) use LiBr/H2O as working
pair, and produce chilled water at about 6°C
to 7°C (43°F to 45°F). The COP is rela-
tively higher than for H2O/NH3. However,
LiBr systems must be water cooled, usu-
ally requiring a cooling tower, while NH3
systems can have an air-cooled condenser.
Due to the large vapor volume of the water
refrigerant, the LiBr/H2O chillers usually
have large physical dimensions. For small
cooling loads and for applications where
it is not possible to use water cooling, an
H2O/NH3 system is preferred.
In hot and sunny climates, the required
solar collector area is approximately 3 to
4 m2 per kW cooling (114 to 152 ft2/ton).
Higher heat supply temperature for multi-
effect chillers require higher cost evacu-
ated tube or concentrating collectors, and
may need a high temperature storage (i.e.,
thermochemical).
In LiBr/H2O systems, the refrigerant
freezes at 0°C (32°F). Thus, care must
be exercised while the machine is idle,
especially during winter. Another prob-
lem is crystallization of the LiBr solution
at high concentrations, which may result
from high generator temperatures or from
inadequate temperature control at other
parts of the machine. Thus, the heat sup-
ply temperature from the solar collectors
or heat storage must be adequately con-
trolled. The cooling water temperature,
particularly to the absorber, must also be
monitored. The chiller capacity may be
controlled by increasing the heat supply
temperature or decreasing the cooling
water temperature, since both increase
the capacity as well as the COP.
A fuel-ﬁ red boiler usually covers the
need for a backup system to heat the de-
sorber of the heat-driven chiller. However,
caution should be exercised since the col-
lectors connected in series with a backup
boiler can turn into a heat sink instead of a
heat source during periods with low solar
radiation availability. Alternatively, one
may use a backup conventional chiller
alongside the solar-assisted machine, but
this would require an additional full-size
chiller, which would be idle for long peri-
ods. A viable design solution for a single-
effect absorption cycle is to incorporate an
auxiliary desorber, which is powered by
the backup while the original desorber is
powered by solar heat. The weak solution
goes ﬁ rst to the solar-powered desorber
where it is concentrated as much as pos-
sible with the available solar heat and then
proceeds to the auxiliary desorber where it
is concentrated further using heat from the
backup source. The vapor from both de-
sorbers is then supplied to the condenser.
Adsorption
Due to the periodic nature of an ad-
sorption cycle, ﬂ uctuations occur at all
Advertisement formerly in this space.
temperature levels. For stable operation, using heat storage
is recommended at least at the high temperature level and on
the chilled water side. This makes the control more stable and
avoids, for example, that high temperature peaks are transferred
into the primary cycle of the solar collector. Variable speed
pumps are required to adjust the volumetric ﬂ ow of the heat
transfer medium through solar collectors and provide the desired
outlet temperature that matches the operating conditions.
Commercially available adsorption chillers use a constant
period between switching from adsorption of the one compart-
ment to desorption and vice versa. Typical time periods range
between 6 – 7 minutes per half cycle. However, under part-load
conditions, extending the cycle time can increase the COP, be-
cause the adsorption potential of the sorptive materials is better
exploited. This is only possible when the loads are below the
chiller capacity. The COP of an adsorption chiller may increase
by reducing the cooling water temperature.
To avoid freezing in the evaporator, it is necessary to install a
chilled water storage, thus increasing the thermal inertia of the
system. This requires proper control of the chilled water pump
that should operate for about 10 minutes after completing the
heating cycle of the adsorption chiller, to avoid regenerated
sorption material causing continued evaporation of water in the
evaporator and cooling below the freezing point.
Desiccants
Liquid desiccant systems can be cheaper than solid desiccant
wheels when comparing commercially available products. Liq-
uid desiccant systems can store cooling capacity by means of
regenerated desiccant. Thus, the hygroscopic salt solution may be
concentrated when solar energy is available, stored, and then used
to dehumidify process air at a later time. When both situations oc-
cur simultaneously, which is often the case (AC is required when
solar radiation is available), desiccant can ﬂ ow directly from the
desorber to the absorber, bypassing the storage. A good control
system should allow for these various operation modes.
The salt solution storage is the most compact cold storage. It
requires no thermal insulation and can be applied indeﬁ nitely.
Storage may be enhanced by increasing the concentration dif-
ference between concentrated and diluted solution by cooling
during the absorption process. This requires a special design
of the absorber, which is no longer adiabatic, and is similar to
that in a closed absorption system. The dehumidiﬁ cation pro-
cess can be operated while concentrated desiccant exists and
independent of the availability of driving heat for regeneration
at the same time. If both hot water storage and solution storage
are available, one should always use the solution storage before
using the heat storage, which is subject to heat losses.
A potential problem in liquid desiccant systems is crystal-
lization of the solution at high concentrations, which may result
from high temperatures in the desorber or from inadequate
temperature control at other parts of the machine. Therefore,
the heat supply temperature from the solar collectors or storage
must be adequately controlled. A higher supply temperature
generally increases the capacity and the COP.
Conclusion
Further research and development activities are necessary
to stimulate market integration and reduce the cost of using
solar-assisted air conditioning in buildings. Solar cooling has
a strong potential for signiﬁ cant primary energy savings. The
investment may become more ﬁ nancially attractive by maximiz-
ing the use of the solar system throughout the year, for both
cooling and heating.
References
1. Klein, S.A. and D.T. Reindl. 2005. “Solar refrigeration.” ASHRAE
Journal 47 (9):S26–S30.

About the Authors
Mark Bowman, P.E., was a senior project engineer
at E-Cube, and currently is a senior engineer at Bow-
man Consulting in the Seattle area.Jack S. Wolpert,
Ph.D., is president for E-Cube in Boulder, Colo., and
contributed to Guideline 1-1996, The HVAC Com-
misssioning Process, and was a member of GPC 14P,
Measurement of Energy and Demand Savings.
Post-Occupancy
Commissioning
issioning
Facility Information Systems for
T
he process of building commissioning and facility optimization
is evolving with advancements in facility information technol-
ogy. This article presents a case study of a project at the David
Skaggs Research Center in Boulder, Colo., where building automa-
tion system (BAS) data and spreadsheet analysis techniques were
used to identify operational problems, opportunities for increased
efﬁ ciency and other improvements in the post-occupancy phase
during the ﬁ rst year following construction.
By Mark Bowman, P.E. and Jack S. Wolpert, Ph.D., Member ASHRAE
The optimization promised a simple
payback of less than one year, and in-
creased the facility quality by providing
an improved ability to serve occupant
needs more efficiently and reliably.
While this facility improvement pro-
cess was applied in the context of new
construction, it is equally applicable for
commissioning (retrocommissioning)
and recommissioning existing facilities
where comparably short paybacks have
been demonstrated. The results also
indicate that further advances in facility
information technology will make these
diagnostic techniques and beneﬁ ts more
widely available, and hold the potential to
fundamentally change the way buildings
are commissioned and operated.

June 2006
ASHRAE Journal
25
\* The ability to monitor the electrical distribution system was envisioned as a
valuable tool for this work. Unfortunately, it was not fully operational and was
only able to be used for some speciﬁ c measurements. Subsequently, it was made
fully operational, enabling the facilities staff to track electrical performance.
\*\* The authors have been using the term “Post-Occupancy Commissioning”
(POCx) for the past decade to distinguish this phase of work from the con-
struction phase efforts. While we recognize and support that comprehensive
commissioning should include both design and initial occupancy phase work,
Background
The U.S. General Services Administration (GSA) built
the David Skaggs Research Center for the National Oceanic
and Atmospheric Administration (NOAA) Environmental
Research Laboratories, the National Weather Service, the
National Geophysical Data Center and other federal agen-
cies. The facility has 720 ofﬁ ces with operable windows, 20
conference rooms, several computer centers, and nearly 100
wet and dry laboratories. It is 900 ft (274 m) long with three
ﬂ oors above grade and one ﬂ oor below grade, and has a total
of 371,500 gross ft2 (34 500 m2).
Two gas-ﬁ red hot water boilers with a steady-state efﬁ ciency
of 82% and a capacity of 6,685,000 Btu/h (1,959 kW) each at
altitude (5,400 ft [1646 m]) provide environmental heating and
year-round domestic hot water. Three water-cooled centrifu-
gal chillers with a full-load efﬁ ciency of 0.63 kW/ton (0.18
kW/kW) and a capacity of 470 tons (1,653 kW), refrigeration
each provide chilled water for cooling the entire facility. Two
small air-cooled reciprocating chillers serve as backup to the
central cooling system for a computer room.
Five central air-handling units, each with 100% outdoor
air economizers, preheat coils, cooling coils and two 75 hp
(56 kW) variable-pitch vaneaxial supply fans are in parallel.
Two units are equipped with laboratory exhaust heat recovery
systems. Most of the more than 1,100 air terminal units are
variable air volume with some constant volume reheat units
for the perimeter.
All of the major plant equipment, mechanical systems, ter-
minal units, core lighting and exterior lighting (among other
systems) are connected to and controlled by a direct digital
control (DDC) building automation system with more than
4,600 points and a graphical operator interface at the front-end
computer. Additionally, the electrical distribution system is
equipped with about 100 power monitoring devices to detect
electrical faults, monitor harmonic distortion and measure and
record electrical energy consumption.\* Each device monitors
20 electrical power parameters or more, including instanta-
neous demand and total energy use.
These devices are networked to a central operator’s terminal
for system monitoring, alarm reporting and trend logging.
These two systems constitute what the authors term “facility
information systems.”
During design, the A/E team along with the commission-
ing agent enabled GSA to undertake a signiﬁ cant initiative
to reduce construction and life-cycle costs for the facility by
implementing a number of highly cost-effective energy and
water-efﬁ ciency measures. One of these measures included
new construction-based, comprehensive building commission-
ing led by one of the authors. Fundamental commissioning
work consisted of installation (prefunctional) checks, startup,
test and balance veriﬁ cation and functional performance tests
for all major mechanical, electrical, ﬁ re/life safety, BAS
controls and water-conserving lavatory systems. Following
ASHRAE Guideline 1-1996, The HVAC Commissioning
Process, detailed training and system manuals also were
completed during commissioning. Once the construction-
phase commissioning work was completed, the focus shifted
to post-occupancy commissioning and optimization (POCx)\*\*
with the following purpose and scope:
• Establish smooth and reliable operation of major sys-
tems, including central heating, cooling and air-handling
systems;
• Identify any problems and/or warranty issues with pro-
posed corrective action
• Identify and propose opportunities for increased efﬁ cien-
cy improvements and other operational improvements;
and
• Establish baselines to document impacts of corrective
action and improvements; and benchmark for future op-
erational management.
The overall goals and beneﬁ ts of the construction commis-
sioning and POCx process included: 1) improved occupant
comfort (temperature and indoor air quality); 2) sustained
and increased energy and environmental efficiency; 3)
reduced maintenance burden and costs; and 4) extended
equipment life.†
Methodology
The basic strategy of POCx process was to use existing
facility information systems to monitor, record and analyze
operational aspects of the building mechanical systems and
use this information to further improve building operation.
The primary tools used to perform these functions were
the BAS and a spreadsheet program. The centralized power
monitoring system would have been a valuable tool, but
was not made fully operational until after the POCx process
was completed.
Of the 4,600+ points connected to the BAS, approximately
200 were identiﬁ ed as key variables (major mechanical sys-
tems only, no terminal units—planned for subsequent efforts)
many commissioning projects often end with project acceptance and do not
include this follow-up work.
† These goals and beneﬁ ts are consistent with those promoted in ASHRAE
Guideline 1-1996, The HVAC Commissioning Process, as well as the more recent
Model Commissioning Plan and Guide Speciﬁ cation, Version 2.05 developed
by Portland Energy Conservation, Inc. under contract to the Federal Energy
Management Program (FEMP). (Note that ASHRAE Guideline 0-2005, The
Commissioning Process, was not available at the time of this work).
26
ASHRAE Journal
ashrae.org
June 2006
and were trended continuously and collected in 15-minute
intervals. These data were automatically stored at the central
operator’s terminal and were retrieved weekly by the com-
missioning provider via remote access for analysis. Major
mechanical systems involved in this process included the
boilers, chillers and related auxiliaries, ﬁ ve large air-handling
units and miscellaneous systems.
The authors developed a series of semi-automated spread-
sheets to import the BAS trend data, graph meaningful
combinations of points and create diagnostic data summary
tables. These graphs and tables were analyzed manually to
verify current operation and detect areas for improvement.
They also created a permanent record of system operating
characteristics, system performance and document signiﬁ cant
events, such as equipment and system failures, which could
then be used for future benchmarking.
Any detected problems or warranty issues were recorded
in a spreadsheet tracking form for the entire commissioning
process. After corrective action was applied, follow-up moni-
toring and ﬁ eld observations verifed that the problems were
corrected before they were moved to the resolved portion of
the spreadsheet tracking form.
Results
More than two dozen signiﬁ cant problems and improvement
opportunities were identiﬁ ed. Of these, about 50% related to
controls and system operation, 25% appeared to be design
related issues and 25% were warranty issues. A complete
example of an operational problem is presented next. Similar
diagnostics and analyses were performed and documented for
all other identiﬁ ed items.
Lag Chiller Over-Cycling. As shown in Figure 1, a cus-
tomized trend plot (created from a template that allowed for
automatic data entry) revealed that the lag chiller was cycling
on and off—as often as four times an hour and more than 20
times per day. Excessive cycling causes added wear and tear,
increased maintenance and utility costs and often a need for
premature chiller repair.
Based on the trend data, the lag chiller was staging off
when the current on both chillers (CH2, CH3) fell below ap-
proximately 50% full-load amps (as speciﬁ ed in the design
documents and submitted by the controls contractor— most
likely with the unrealized expectation that this would be
“tuned” during the acceptance phase). At this condition, the
building cooling load was slightly greater than the capacity
of one chiller, which caused the lag chiller to be reenergized
upon the ensuing rise in secondary chilled water supply tem-
perature (CHWST). The applied simple solution was to lower
the cut-out setpoint from 50% to 45% full-load amps. Figure
2 shows the results of this change.
As shown, when one chiller could not handle the load as
indicated by a rise in secondary chiller water supply tem-
perature, the lag chiller staged on. When the current on both
chillers fell below 45% full-load amps, the lag chiller staged

off and stayed off until the load again exceeded the capacity
of one chiller. There was a single on/off cycle in getting the
lag chiller started on June 18, the cause of which cannot be
determined without further information. This indicates a pos-
sible need to continue watching chiller staging (perhaps using
more frequent sampling of additional points) in the event there
could be another problem that needs to be addressed.
Inspection of the chiller control program code also revealed
a number of timers and other chiller control variables that were
not readily available to the BAS operators. An easier method
for understanding and accessing chiller control logic was
needed to serve the long-term interests of facility operations.
The BAS contractor subsequently revised the CHW system
graphic at the BAS host computer to include all relevant CHW
system control variables. This greatly improved the ability for
facility operators to control the central CHW system.
Analytical techniques similar to the one given above were
designed and implemented for all of the major mechanical
systems involved in the process. Since the scope of this
article does not permit showing detailed results for all of
28
ASHRAE Journal
June 2006
the issues and improvements identi-
fied, two other examples are briefly
discussed next.
Frequent AHU Fan Static Pres-
sure Safety Shutdowns. Air-handling
unit (AHU) supply fans were tripping
off frequently on low (negative) static
pressure on the inlet (suction) side of
the vaneaxial fans. These fans remained
off until facility staff manually reset the
static pressure safety switches. Tenant
comfort was potentially compromised
and facility operations were interrupted.
Based on this better understanding
of what was initiating the shutdowns,
the facility staff temporarily solved
the problem by placing the mixed air
damper controls in economizer mode
(100% OA) through operator override at
the BAS host computer. While entirely
appropriate as a short-term spring/fall
ﬁ x, this caused unnecessary energy con-
sumption and increased cooling demand
on warmer days. Left uncorrected, this
condition also may have resulted in the
inability to meet cooling requirements
at peak summer conditions.
Using BAS trend data and ﬁ eld obser-
vations, the cause of the problem was
traced to a control algorithm that, when
economizer mode was off (main outdoor
air dampers closed, minimum dampers
open), the return air dampers modulated
closed to maintain minimum outdoor
airﬂ ow through the minimum outdoor
air damper. This had the intended effect
of “choking off ” the inlet to the supply
fan to increase negative static pressure
and increase minimum outdoor ﬂ ow.
However, the required negative static
pressure was well below the setpoint
of the static pressure safety switches,
causing the fans to trip off. (The mini-
mum outdoor air damper opening was
relatively small—likely too small for
proper operation.) This problem was
corrected by modifying the damper
control algorithm to include a minimum
stop on the main outdoor air damper.
This had the combined effect of elimi-
nating the safety trips and maintaining
minimum ventilation requirements
near design levels with lower fan en-
ergy consumption (lower static pressure
across the fan and appropriate use of
outdoor air).
Duct Static Pressure Too High. At
the time of occupancy, the blade pitch
on the air-handling unit vaneaxial supply
fans was being controlled to maintain
downstream duct static pressure (ap-
proximately two-thirds of the way down
the main trunk) at 1.5 in. w.g. (373.2 Pa)
(from our experience, a typical design/
controls starting setpoint).
Since the VAV terminal units were
designed to operate at a published 0.5
in. w.g. (124.4 Pa) and quite possibly
lower, an experiment was conducted to
measure the energy that could be saved
by reducing the duct static pressure, and
verify that tenant comfort would not
be compromised. Since the electrical
power monitoring system was not ready
at the time of the experiment, portable
electrical power meters were installed
on two of the AHUs. Fan power con-
sumption, duct static pressure, outdoor
air temperature and outdoor air ﬂ ow
were recorded for several weeks at 1.5
in. of water (373.2 Pa), followed by 1.0
in. w.g. (248.8 Pa).
The results, as expected, indicated
a projected annual fan energy sav-
ings of about 10% or $3,600 per year,
with no detected or reported impact
on VAV terminal unit operation (i.e.,
no terminals running at/near full-open
during normal operation) or tenant
comfort. The setpoint for duct static
pressure was subsequently changed to
1 in. w.g. (248.8 Pa) for all ﬁ ve air-
handling units (and may be able to be
lowered further).
Other results achieved during the post-
occupancy commissioning and optimiza-
tion process included:
• Detection and correction of lag boil-
er over-cycling, imprecise (elevated)
chilled water supply temperature
control, faulty AHU temperature
control, errant airﬂ ow measurement
calibration factors, loose damper
linkages, and failed or inaccurate
BAS sensors and actuators.
• Recommendations for better control
algorithms, including building static
pressure, economizer switchover,

29
Given budget impacts of 5 to 10% (which is conservatively
within the range reported in the literature and further sup-
ported by quantiﬁ ed measured impacts in this facility), the
projected savings are in the range of $0.13 to $0.27/gsf ($1.40
to $2.90/m2).
An example of a non-quantiﬁ ed beneﬁ t was correction of
equipment over-cycling for both the lag boiler and lag chiller as
presented in this report. It is difﬁ cult to quantify increased wear

heat recovery operation and the cooling tower bypass valve
were also made.
After the POCx process was complete, energy performance
testing was conducted by a group of agencies, including the
authors, in accordance with CFR Title 10 Part 435.1 Prior to
the test, the centralized power monitoring system was made
operational and provided enough resolution to measure actual
electrical load proﬁ les, peak demand and electrical energy
consumption by major end-use categories. Figure 3 shows the
summary results for the three-day test period. These and other
data can be combined to create relevant benchmarking metrics
to be compared with results for similar weather and operating
conditions on a continuous basis, if the information systems
are conﬁ gured properly and integrated with routine facility
management practices.
Discussion
While it is apparent that the post-occupancy commissioning
process conducted at the David Skaggs Research Center yielded
improvements in efﬁ ciency, operations and maintenance, useful
equipment life, and/or avoided occupant comfort problems,
some impacts are difﬁ cult to quantify. It would be speculation
to state how the identiﬁ ed problems would have been addressed
(if at all) and what operational, maintenance, repair and replace-
ment costs would have been in the absence of the process.
However, with this in mind, reasonable
estimates can be made.
In some cases, reasonably accurate
energy-efﬁ ciency savings can be deter-
mined where sufﬁ cient measured data
exist, such as the duct static pressure
reduction experiment discussed previ-
ously. However, most of the beneﬁ ts
are not directly measurable and are
best described qualitatively or in the
context of potential impacts on operat-
ing budgets.
For the David Skaggs Research
Center the annual utility costs, includ-
ing electricity, natural gas and water
have been projected to be $1.78 per
gross ft2 (gsf) ($19.16/m2) based on
original computer simulation projects
adjusted with utility bills for the ﬁ rst
three months of occupancy. The annual
operations and maintenance budget is
projected to be $1.13/gsf ($12.16/m2).
For major repairs and replacement,
GSA had a separate requisition process
(no funds are placed in escrow). There-
fore, the operational budget that could
be impacted by the post-occupancy
process (excluding major repairs and
replacement) is $2.91/gsf ($31.32/m2).
Advertisement formerly in this space.
30
ASHRAE Journal
ashrae.org
June 2006
and tear, associated extra maintenance and resulting occupant
discomfort, all arising from excessive cycling. It is also possible
the problem eventually may have been detected and corrected
through normal facility operations and maintenance. The main
beneﬁ t of the post-occupancy commissioning and optimization
process for this issue was that the problem was detected and
corrected sooner. It was a matter of prevention that one can say
was deﬁ nitely a good to do, but difﬁ cult to quantify.
The cost to implement the post-occupancy commissioning
and optimization at the David Skaggs Research Center during
a six-month period was approximately $0.10/gsf ($1.08/m2)
(approximately 12% of the total construction phase commis-
sioning cost). This included only the outsourced consulting
fees to set up the process, analysis of data on a weekly basis,
site visits as necessary, and interfacing with facility staff and
contractors for corrective action. Given the potential savings
discussed previously, the process likely paid for itself from
direct savings in less than one year.
The Future of Facility Information Systems
In general, facility information systems typically are under-
used, mainly due to the time, attention and level of sophistication
required to take advantage of the more advanced capabilities.
A case in point is that, after the six-month post-occupancy
commissioning activities were completed, it was discontinued.
No simple way existed to integrate the process with facility
operations because it required several staff-hours per week to
maintain. Technological advancements needed to make these
diagnostic techniques more widely used include:
• Automated data acquisition and analysis with “report by
exception” capabilities. This includes the ability to inte-
grate information from independent systems into a single
analysis framework;
• “Point-and-click” graphs and reports that allow quick
visualization of vital facility information, such as peak
demand proﬁ les and energy consumption per degree-day
and or other metrics; and
• A “user-friendly” POCx interface that allows facility staff
to easily create new diagnostics, graphs and reports to keep
up with changes in the facility and to various and changing
management information needs.
These and other capabilities are beginning to be offered
by both public and private organizations, including govern-
ment institutions, traditional building automation system
manufacturers and high-tech startup companies. It is not a
question of whether or not these new technologies will be
Advertisement formerly in this space.
June 2006
ASHRAE Journal
31
made more available and adopted. It
is a question of how fast. As invest-
ments are made on a trial basis and the
value of the information vs. the cost to
obtain and manage it becomes appar-
ent, those capabilities that prove useful
and cost-effective will essentially drive
the market. As the adoption and use of
these systems become more widespread,
costs are expected to fall correspond-
ingly. It is only a matter of time before
use of these systems and capabilities
for commissioning and building op-
eration becomes the rule rather than the
exception.2–7
Conclusions
• The post-occupancy commissioning
activities conducted at the David
Skaggs Research Center facilitated
timely correction of a number of
operational problems, increased
energy efﬁ ciency and generally im-
proved the quality of the facility, all
while being self-funding. Beneﬁ ts
included:
· Direct utility cost savings;
· Reduction in maintenance, repair
and/or replacement costs;
· Lowered burden and increased
productivity of facility staff;
· Avoided potential occupant com-
fort problems; and
· Benchmarking for use in ongoing
operational efﬁ ciency.
• The cost of implementing the
process was about $0.10/ft2 ($1.08/
m2). Expected savings were in the
range of $0.13 to $0.27/ft2 ($1.40
to $2.90/m2), which yielded a pro-
jected simple payback of less than
one year.
• Further advancements in facility
information technology will allow
the diagnostic techniques and asso-
ciated beneﬁ ts presented here, along
with new and emerging capabilities
and beneﬁ ts, to become an integral
aspect of facility operations.
Even with a well-built and compre-
hensively commissioned facility, an
opportunity exists for continuous com-
missioning and optimization. Facility
information systems offer the potential
to cost-effectively maintain and improve
upon comfort and efﬁ ciency levels as
the use of the facility changes and as
equipment and systems age.
Acknowledgments
We would like to thank the follow-
ing individuals for their assistance and
contribution to the success of the post-
occupancy commissioning and optimi-
zation process:
Ron Jakaitis, P.E., project manager,
U.S. General Services Administration,
Rocky Mountain Region (currently Chief
Facilities Logistics Div., NOAA); David
Clark, facility manager, LB&B Associ-
ates; Jerry Deall, senior project manager,
E-Cube; and Mandeep Singh, energy and
commissioning engineer, E-Cube.

Rx for Heath-Care HVAC | A Supplement to ASHRAE Journal

Health-Care HVAC
June 2006
Rx for Heath-Care HVAC | A Supplement to ASHRAE Journal
H13
Infection Control
In Hospitals
I
n the mid-1990s, the Australian state of Queensland undertook
a major redevelopment of a signiﬁ cant number of the hospitals
in the state. For the capital city of Brisbane, this included the
construction of extensive new inpatient, outpatient and clinical
services buildings. The construction program covered the majority
of tertiary services hospitals serving Brisbane and the state, with
most either totally or near totally rebuilt.
About the Author
Bill Drake, CPEng, is a mechanical engineer and
Queensland Manager of S2F Pty Ltd, Architects and
Engineers. He was the engineering representative
on the working committee and author of the engi-
neering services component of the 1997 edition of
the guidelines and advised on the revision for the
current 2002 edition.
By Bill Drake, CPEng
Hospital health care in Australia consists
of a large public hospital system providing
mostly free hospital services with a separate
private hospital system operating on a fee
for service basis. The public health system
in Queensland is the largest provider of
hospital services in the state and as such
the Queensland Health Department over-
sees the largest and most diverse hospital
building infrastructure in the state.
The Queensland Health Department
made a decision early in the redevel-
opment program to develop infection
control guidelines for construction and
refurbishment. The guidelines titled
“Building and Refurbishment—Infection
Control Guidelines” were ﬁ rst published
in 1997, and the current revised ver-
sion released in 2002.1 The guidelines,
although containing many relatively
standard practices including consolidation
of design requirements, was one of the
ﬁ rst guidelines relating to actual during-
construction practices published in Aus-
tralia. The guidelines included a speciﬁ c
operating room ventilation design which
was subject to computational ﬂ uid dynam-
ics (CFD) analysis in the decision process.
The guidelines also have been applied
to private hospitals in the state, via state
legislation that covers development and
construction of private hospitals.
The Guidelines
The guidelines cover infection control
in construction from a number of per-
spectives including:
• Basic principles and intent;
• Project planning and consultation;
• Construction practices; and
• Design, covering both functional planning requirements
such as number and location of hand basins as well as
more fundamental aspects such as air conditioning ﬁ lter
selection.
The term nosocomial infection is applied to infections which
are acquired and transmitted by patients within a hospital. By
way of indicating the extent and effect of nosocomial infec-
tions, The New England Journal of Medicine article “Infection
control—a problem for patient safety” stated (in relation to
the U.S.):
Currently, between 5 and 10 per cent of patients admitted
to acute care hospitals acquire one or more infections,
and the risks have steadily increased during recent de-
cades. These adverse events affect approximately 2 mil-
lion patients each year in the United States, result in some
90,000 deaths, and add an estimated $4.5 to $5.7 billion
per year to the costs of patient care. Infection control is
therefore a critical component of patient safety.2
Nosocomial infections cover a broad range of types and
causes, and as a result, the procedures, controls and systems
for prevention are also broad. Readers will, no doubt, have
a basic understanding of infection control in their everyday
lives. Unfortunately, patients in a hospital are typically at a
greater risk of infection than the general population due to
medical conditions.
An organism that has no adverse effect on a healthy individual
may be life-threatening to an ill patient suffering with a sup-
pressed immune system due to a preexisting medical condition
or medical treatment. Likewise, during surgery the procedures
expose areas of the body, normally protected, to attack from
multiple sources of infection.
Part of the management to prevent infections, therefore,
requires provision of buildings that provide:
• Barriers to infection transmission within the facility and
from external sources;
• Functional layouts that reduce opportunities for infection
transmission;
• Hygiene services to staff and patients for minimization of
transmission risk;
• Minimum opportunities to harbor infectious organisms;
and
• Facilities that can be readily cleaned and disinfected.
Added to this are the management practices and procedures
undertaken by the health-care professional and support staff.
Key areas in relation to HVAC covered in the guidelines
include infection control during construction, general design
considerations and speciﬁ c design requirements for areas such
as isolation rooms, operating rooms, etc. The development of
these HVAC aspects resulted from a mixture of existing general
government requirements on health projects, research on health
projects, research of other published text, articles, papers and
© 2006 American Society of Heating, Refrigerating and Air-Conditioning Engineers, Inc. (www.ashrae.org). Published in ASHRAE Journal (Vol. 48, June 2006).
For personal use only. Additional distribution in either paper or digital form is not permitted without ASHRAE’s permission.
or personal use only. Add
H14
Rx for Heath-Care HVAC | A Supplement to ASHRAE Journal
June 2006
Health-Care HVAC
June 2006
Rx for Heath-Care HVAC | A Supplement to ASHRAE Journal
H15
guidelines, some validation research and advice from health
professionals specializing in infection control.
Infection Control During Construction
The actual practices and procedures during construction were
identiﬁ ed as a major risk of introducing pathogens and/or provid-
ing suitable habitats for their propagation. The guidelines include
requirements for cleaning during construction and cleaning main-
tenance, as well as exclusion of weather and water entry. These
general requirements have in response created some prescriptive
requirements in the guidelines, with the most signiﬁ cant being:
• Ensuring ductwork is clean both internally and externally.
Ductwork is typically fully protected with covers during
transport and storage with all open ends of installed duct pro-
vided with temporary end covers. The temporary covers are
usually plastic sheeting taped on, with all ductwork inspected
prior to erection and internally cleaned if required;
• Similar practices are used for air-handling plants and other
air-system components;
• A full cleaning of all systems and concealed spaces before
being closed, including riser shafts, walls and ceilings, so
that the completed installation has all rubbish, dirt and
dust removed;
• Where construction work is within or adjoining existing
health facilities, barriers are required to prevent dirt and
dust travelling to non-construction areas. In addition, ﬁ l-
tered air exhaust is required to the construction area so that
it is at a negative pressure compared to adjacent areas. The
exhaust typically is ﬁ ltered since the discharge to outside
is frequently located at or above an external congregation
area or pedestrian trafﬁ cked area of the hospital; and
• Added to this is a general requirement that all construc-
tion projects implement; control, inspection and reporting
systems, which are speciﬁ c to the project.
Requirements and general philosophy are given for construc-
tion adjacent to areas remaining occupied and increased vigi-
lance is stressed for construction in relation to high risk areas
such as critical care, immunosuppressed patients (oncology,
transplant wards, etc.) and respiratory disease areas.
The previous requirements would appear to be common
sense for construction. However, evidence of outbreaks of
nosocomial aspergillosis (aspergillosis is a commonly occurring
fungus in the environment and typically only infects persons
already weakened by some other medical condition) was found
in various literature, where the actual construction work was
found to be the cause of the outbreak. Of the research papers
and textbooks reviewed in developing the guide, one text listed
12 instances of nosocomial aspergillosis outbreaks within the
United States between 1974 and 1989, affecting 95 individuals,
with the most common cause identiﬁ ed as infection resulting
from renovation construction works.3
General Design
The general HVAC design requirements include a mix of
design principles and a number of speciﬁ c requirements. These
requirements relate only to issues relevant to infection control,
as the guidelines are not intended to be a design manual for
health facilities beyond those aspects considered issues for
infection control.
Fundamentals of the general requirements are:
• Minimization of moisture and water in air-handling sys-
tems, with measures to prevent carryover and constant
high (>80%RH) humidity in ductwork;
• Prohibited use of water bath and spray type humidiﬁ ers;
• Positive pressurization of patient areas;
• Minimum outside air rate of 2 L/s per m2 (0.4 cfm/ft2)
of treatment/ward areas. Note that this is a minimum and
code requirements may mandate an increased rate in some
speciﬁ c areas;
• Use of ceiling plenums not permitted with all air transport
to be via ductwork.
• Supply and return air ductwork requirements include:
· No internal linings;
· Reasonable access provision to allow internal cleaning;
· Attenuators to have impervious linings, typically a high
strength plastic membrane; and
· Lint screen ﬁ lters on return air grilles. This was as
a result of high lint levels found in the return air of
existing systems. The lint was obviously from hos-
pital washed garments as the particular colour used
by different hospitals was evident in the lint found
in the ductwork.
• Good access for checking, maintenance and cleaning of
air-handling units. Typically requiring plant room mounted
air-handling units with good access for routine mainte-
nance and repairs; and
• Filter requirements are speciﬁ ed, ranging from high ef-
ﬁ ciency particulate air (HEPA) for operating rooms and
high efﬁ ciency for critical care areas to general efﬁ ciency
for general wards.
Designing HVAC systems that incorporate the previous has not
been difﬁ cult, except in relation to providing adequate noise attenu-
ation in-duct. The exclusion of acoustically absorptive duct linings
has increased the need for attenuators. However, added attenuator
impervious linings has resulted in selected attenuators being longer
in length and sometimes increased perimeter. Added to this, the
requirement for reasonable cleaning access dictates attenuator
installation locations that may be difﬁ cult to accommodate.
The net result has been an increase in required mechanical
plant room space to accommodate the attenuators. In plant
rooms with multiple air-handling units and subsequently many
attenuators, special consideration has been required to achieve a
well-coordinated arrangement as well as mounting and support
for the increased attenuator requirements.
Other than the previous issues, compliance with the general
design requirements has been relatively straightforward on
health projects since their inception.
Two areas speciﬁ cally dealt with in the guidelines are isola-
tion rooms and operating rooms. For these two important areas
there are speciﬁ c requirements for design and construction,
including HVAC requirements.
Isolation Rooms
The isolation room requirements cover both positive and nega-
tive pressure rooms, with the guideline precluding rooms that can
be switched between positive and negative. Thus, designated nega-
tive pressure room is designed and always operates as a negative
pressure room, with a positive pressure room designed and always
operated as a positive pressure room.
Key design features include:4,5
Positive Pressure Rooms
• Positive pressure achieved by a 10% excess of supply air
from a dedicated air-handling unit;
• Pressure differential gauge for visual check by staff;
• All outside air single-pass air systems;
• Dedicated air handler per room. However, grouping of
rooms is permitted with separate exhaust per room;
• Special requirements on exhaust system, including high
level air discharge and warning notices to maintenance
personnel;
• 12 air changes per hour on supply air as a minimum;
• Pressure differential gauge and warning alarm on loss of
pressure and system fault; and
• Exhaust system not required to be ﬁ ltered, except in situations
where briefed by the hospital as requiring such provision.
Operating Room
Obviously, infection control in the operating room environment
is the most challenging as the nature of surgery provides signiﬁ cant
infection risk. The other aspect of surgery is that infections will be
internal and difﬁ cult to treat. This is compounded by increasing
numbers of multi-resistant organisms occurring in health care fa-
cilities such as methicillin-resistant staphylococcus aureus (MRSA)
“golden staph” and vancomycin resistant enterococcus (VRE).
Hence, the operating room is a unique area, even within a
hospital in terms of the risk factors for a patient. Sources of
infection are multiple, including:
• The patient, self infection into the body from the patient’s
skin ﬂ akes and other particles on the body;
• The operating room staff, from skin ﬂ akes, expired air,
hair, etc.;
• The surgical instruments, wadding, etc. used in the
procedure;
• Clothing worn by operating room staff;
• The room and equipment used in the room;
• Air supplied to the room; and
• Inﬁ ltration air.
The HVAC systems within Queensland hospital operating
rooms, built in the 1970s, were generally of the all-outside air
Figure 1: Operating room—sectional view of air-distribution system.
• Warning system with pressure
monitoring for rooms where
briefed by the client hospital
as critical;
• Warning alarm on system fail-
ure; and
• 12 air changes per hour on
supply air as a minimum with
recirculation permitted and a
minimum outside air rate as
per code.
Negative Pressure Rooms
• Negative pressure achieved
by 10% excess of exhaust
airﬂ ow;
Advertisement formerly in this space.
H16
Rx for Heath-Care HVAC | A Supplement to ASHRAE Journal
June 2006
Health-Care HVAC
June 2006
Rx for Heath-Care HVAC | A Supplement to ASHRAE Journal
H17
type with HEPA or medium efﬁ ciency particulate air (MEPA)
ﬁ lters mounted either as terminal or at the air-handling unit and
air change rates of approximately 12 per hour. Air supply to the
room varied between ceiling diffusers and wall mounted side
blow. In the 1980s and early 1990s, the design parameters be-
came recirculation with approximately 50% outside air, terminal
ceiling mounted HEPA ﬁ lters and an air-change rate of 20 per
hour. Prior to the release of the guidelines in 1997, this was a
common standard for Queensland government hospitals.
A literature search was conducted to identify the then-
current practices, standards and research relevant to operating
room HVAC. A vast amount of material was uncovered, with
the major sources from the U.S., U.K. and some Australia. The
material covered, ranged from full research to more specula-
tive considerations, but few offered a recommended solution.
However, a number of papers showed a common direction and
a few making ﬁ rm recommendations.
The favored solutions appeared to be generally either a full
laminar ﬂ ow or a modiﬁ ed version using a smaller area of ﬁ lters
and lower airﬂ ow.6–9 From this research, it was decided that
the preferred solution was an adopted simple and small scale
laminar ﬂ ow style of system. The basics of the arrangement
being the use of four 1200 mm by 600 mm (4 ft by 2 ft) HEPA
ﬁ lter units ceiling mounted directly above the operating table.
Basic arrangement being:
• The HEPA units are arranged to provide a square donut,
with the center of the donut left for mounting lights or
light/pendant combinations;
• Exhausts are located at low levels;
• Return air as a combination of low level and ceiling-mount-
ed, with a 50/50 split between low and high level return
air. The low level exhaust and return acts as collectors of
particulates, with ceiling return air provided to return warm
air at the ceiling. The use of linear slot grilles was with the
intent to create a more even spread of air intake to prevent
producing disturbing currents and to also act as a barrier
to entrainment air by drawing in air before it could cause
a recirculating pattern. Validity of these assumptions was
not veriﬁ ed by analysis or in CFD modeling;
• A short drop skirt (Figure 1
(F
(
) as an option also was included
as this was considered to potentially improve overall per-
formance by effectively lowering the discharge point and
reducing the possible effects of inﬁ ltration into the discharge
airstream. This is an arrangement often used in full laminar
ﬂ ow systems installed to operating rooms; and
• Airﬂ ow rate set at 1700 L/s (3,600 cfm), air-change rate
was not referenced, as 20 air changes per hour would always
be exceeded with anticipated operating rooms sizes.
Figures 1 and 2
d
show the basic arrangement for this system.
It was decided that an obvious high conﬁ dence existed in the
air quality from the HEPA ﬁ lter bank and the ability to provide
suitable temperature control. However, the higher air quantity
above that required for normal sensible temperature and humid-
ity control requires special consideration at the air-handling unit,
depending on the prevailing local climate.
Three key factors were considered to require further inves-
tigation to achieve the necessary conﬁ dence to implement the
design into the guidelines. These were:
• Room air was not entrained into the clean airstream, which
may increase particulate occurrence at wound site;
• Air velocity at wound site would not exceed 0.2 m/s (39
fpm). Air velocity at wound site based on research data
suggested this should not exceed 0.2 m/s (39 fpm), so as to
ensure excessive drying at the wound did not occur; and
• Airﬂ ow pattern that directed ﬁ rst air (direct from HEPA
ﬁ lters) to the patient and spilled away from patient. The
requirement of spill away from the patient is to provide pro-
tection from particulates from the operating room staff.
To investigate these key factors, CFD was chosen as the
most appropriate tool. In conducting the CFD modeling,
it was decided to exclude the inﬂ uence of operating room
lights, staff and equipment. While all of these items will have
an undoubted effect (especially lights), variability of their
size and location is too numerous to take into account. It
also was considered that this simpliﬁ ed model would provide
adequate indicative results for the purpose of investigating
the three key factors.
The results of the CFD modeling were positive and supported
the use of the proposed system. The basic results against the
key investigation factors were:
• No signiﬁ cant entrainment of room air;
• Operating table (patient/wound) air velocity was below the
0.2 m/s (39 fpm) criteria; and
• First air was delivered to operating table (patient) and the airﬂ ow
pattern was deﬁ nitely outward, traveling away from patient.
Based on these CFD modeling results, the decision was made
for this design to be included in the infection guidelines. Figure
3 shows the airﬂ ow pattern and velocity output analysis from
the CFD modeling.
Since the guidelines were introduced in 1997, other organi-
zations and individuals have carried out further research and
investigation. Two papers of interest that indicate support for the
design contained in the guidelines and of distinct interest are:
• “Comparison of operating room ventilation systems on pro-
tection of the surgical site.”10,11 This paper is of particular
interest as it is an analytical study using airﬂ ow modeling
to compare different ventilation system types for supplying
air into and exhausting air from the operating room.
• “Comparative CFD analysis of hospital ward ventilation
systems on reducing cross infection rates.”12 This paper
includes a more extensive CFD study of an operating
room with a design very similar to that contained in the
Queensland Health “Building and Refurbishment—Infec-
tion Control Guidelines.” The results of the CFD study,
although only examining this one arrangement, support
the design in the guidelines.
Since the introduction of the guidelines in 1997, many facilities
have been designed and built, and practical experience has been
gained. The experience gained from the design, construction and
operation of these facilities has revealed the following:
• The guideline airﬂ ow rate of 1700 L/s (3,600 cfm) was found
to generate excessive noise. Reduction to not less than 1500
L/s (3,180 cfm) has proven to solve the noise problem;
• Simple smoke testing with smoke pencils at commissioning
using 1500 L/s indicated performance in terms of airﬂ ow
pattern comparable to the CFD modeling results;
• The detailed ﬁ tment of the four HEPA ﬁ lter modules and
center support light/pendant requires careful consideration
and detail design. This is due to the individual components
requiring mounting structure and the mounting unit for a
combination surgical light and services pendant can result
in an area with clustered mounting units and support fram-
ing all sharing a common zone;
• Skirts to the ﬁ lter group have rarely been used as surgical
lights, and services pendent units have made the use of
skirts difﬁ cult or impossible in many cases;
• Airﬂ ow velocity grids (measuring total airﬂ ow rate) com-
monly are used in supply air duct to enable airﬂ ow rate to
be maintained constant as ﬁ lters load, which also operates
to maintain pressure regimes for infection control between
operating room and other areas; and
• Local recirculation fans have not been used due to difﬁ cul-
ties with noise and maintenance access. The experience
with operating room laminar ﬂ ow units in Queensland
was not good in terms of acoustic performance, with
strong surgeon complaints of unacceptable noise levels.
The source of the excessive noise was from local recircu-
lation fans for the laminar ﬂ ow ﬁ lter units. The access to
the recirculation fans for maintenance required trade staff
entering the operating room or operating suite area, which
was considered undesirable for hygiene reasons.

ASHRAE Journal
June 2006
HANNOVER, Germany—Renewable and
efﬁ cient energy is as much a part of the
European landscape as the trains, bicycles,
and giant wind turbines in pastures.
So it seems ﬁ tting that organizers of Han-
nover Messe, described as the world’s most
important technology event, would adopt
energy as its “core theme” for 2006.
“Resources are declining and demand
is growing,” Art Paredes, president and
CEO of Hannover Fairs USA, told mem-
bers of a U.S. press delegation. “This
means that costs are growing and efﬁ cient
use of resources is a critical issue for
many industries.”
This focus played out in a three-day
Energy Dialogue where international
experts presented seminars on topics such
as combined heat and power (CHP). In
addition, an entire hall was dedicated to
companies displaying everything from
giant wind turbines and transmission
equipment for utilities to decentralized
options such as fuel cell, solar, wind and
biofuel technologies.
The only technology missing was nuclear
power. Paredes told U.S. media that the
exclusion was in deference to the 20th an-
h
niversary of the Chernobyl disaster, which
coincided with the third day of the show.
Still, there was more than could be
absorbed. In fuel cell technologies alone,
more than 100 companies exhibited, includ-
ing giants such as Honda and 3M. Much of
the equipment displayed were prototypes.
An example was a natural gas-powered
fuel cell generator from Viessmann that
provides both power and heat.
Rainer Dippel, Viessmann’s manager
of R&D and new technologies, said the
generator is designed to provide 5 kW of
thermal energy and 2 kW of power. It is
intended for the residential market and
should be available in ﬁ ve to 10 years.
Dippel said the cost per kW of this tech-
nology is six times less than a kW from
a centralized power plant, based on 2003
energy prices in Germany.
Dr. Isabel Kundler of PEMEAS, which
produces membranes and electrodes for
companies that build fuel cell stacks, said
fuel cell technology is still in its infancy,
and the barrier is high cost, but this cost
will come down as more fuel cell prod-
ucts enter the market.
She believes that the ﬁ rst big market
will be very small fuel cells for cell
phones and notebook computers because
this is where there is a need for additional
Energy ‘Core Theme’
At Hannover Messe
Hannover Notebook
Large Global Event
HANNOVER, Germany—The Han-
nover Messe trade show is a huge,
global event. The show held April
24 – 28 included 155,000 trade visi-
tors and 5,175 exhibitors, with 154
800 m2(1.6 million ft2) of net display
area. About 30% of visitors and 32%
of exhibitors came from outside of
Germany. Collectively, exhibitors
represented 66 different countries.
The size is partly because the show
combines 10 trade shows, each dedi-
cated to a speciﬁ c industrial technol-
ogy. The segmentation helps visitors
devote their time to their interests,
whether that be factory automation,
microtechnology, the digital fac-
tory, facility management or pipeline
technology.
Hannover Messe also is a political
and technical event. Its importance
is illustrated by its opening speakers,
German Chancellor Angela Merkel
and India Prime Minister Manmohan
Singh, who chided Germany about
alleged trade barriers.
Fairground History
HANNOVER, Germany—The Han-
nover Messe trade show is named
for its fairground, Hannover Messe.
The facility is the world’s largest fair-
ground with 496 000 m2 (5.3 million
ft2) of indoor exhibit space, 58 000 m2
(624,000 ft2) of open-air space in 27
halls and pavilions. It also includes
44 restaurants, shops, banks, pharma-
cies, travel and car rental agencies,
mass transit rail stations, and a major
convention center complex.
The fairground opened Aug. 16,
1947, on the site of a German munitions
plant that was destroyed in World War II.
The development was an initiative of the
British occupying forces, in consultation
with U.S. forces, to help Germany be-
come economically independent again
through exporting its own goods.
A wind turbine for
a utility plant has
considerable interest
on the opening
day of Hannover
Messe. Energy was
a “core theme” of
this year’s show
and hundreds of
companies exhibited
renewable energy
options.
See Energy, Page 8

ASHRAE Journal
June 2006
energy supply and ﬂ exibility. “Our vision
is to meet that need,” she said.
Presently, fuel cells are used mostly to
extend battery life and to provide power
in remote locations. An example is the
UPS (uninterruptible power supply) of-
fered by American Power Conversion
(APC). Pyong W. Pak of APC said this
technology is used in mission critical
applications where a UPS system might
need to provide temporary power for
more than two hours, and use of a diesel
generator is not feasible, usually due to
weight or space considerations.
Another example is the 100 W battery
recharger and mobile generator from
Voller Energy. The handheld, hydrogen-
powered fuel cell system is mainly used
for leisure (camping) and military applica-
tions, and at remote construction sites.
The fastest-growing renewable energy
source is wind, according to Bruce Douglas,
CEO of the European Wind Energy Asso-
ciation. Douglas said the global growth is
about 25% a year and wind power is now
meeting the needs of 100 million people.
“The fact that wind power is free and
clean is economically and environmen-
tally signiﬁ cant,” said Douglas. “But the
more fundamental point… is that the cost
of the electricity is ﬁ xed, once the plant
has been built.”
The wind-technology exhibitors on
the show ﬂ oor largely represented major,
mostly German companies displaying
large turbines. However, one of the more
interesting products was a small wind
converter that uses lifting pressure to
accelerate the wind.
Daniel Scheffer of Energy-Age Wind
said the noise-free converter can be placed
near a residence and produces power and
warm water. The company is ﬁ eld-testing
prototypes and plans to develop products
from 1 kW to 150 kW. The products will
be produced and distributed by partners in
Hamburg and Shanghai.
Solar power was represented by com-
panies supplying both decentralized
photovoltaic (PV) systems to supple-
ment grid power and concentrated solar
power (CSP) systems to replace grid
power. Prof. Dr. Robert Pitz-Paal told
the audience at Energy Dialogue that both
technologies presently need subsidies to
run through their experience curves. He
believes that PV and CSP technologies
will be in competition by 2030.
Hannover Notebook
Cooler Wins Award
HANNOVER, Germany—An open
display cooler that uses carbon dioxide
(R744) received the European Commis-
sion’s ProCool Award for eco-efﬁ ciency
during the opening day of Hannover
Messe. The award is sponsored by ma-
jor food and beverage companies.
The Easy Reach cooler from Frigo-
glass uses CO2 in the single-stage tran-
scritical cycle, said Spiros Masouras,
Member ASHRAE, group product
development manager for Frigoglass.
“This means,” said Masouras, “that
the heat extraction from the product in
the evaporator is done through typical
expansion below critical point, whereas
heat rejection to the ambient is done
through a gas cooler in which the refrig-
erant is in supercritical phase.”
Masouras said Easy Reach incorpo-
rates several innovative components,
including a new reciprocating com-
pressor for CO2 from Tecumseh USA,
a self-adjusting expansion valve from
Danfoss, high efﬁ ciency gas cooler
motors from SMEN and an intelligent
electronic control from Thyratron.
Masouras (L) with Easy Reach cooler.
Energy-Age Wind power system.
Using heat from the
exhaust and jacket
water can increase
energy efﬁ ciency
of a gas generator
from 45% to 85%,
according to Sanjeev
Puri of Green Power
International. In a
program at Energy
Dialogue, Puri
presented nine case
studies where recovered
heat is used to provide
heat, power and
cooling. The diagram
here is for the U.S.
Embassy in New Delhi.
Today, the fairground hosts about
eight major industry shows a year, in
addition to about 25 smaller events.
Hannover Messe is second in size to
the giant CeBIT show for the informa-
tion and communications industries.
It also hosts the IKK Hannover refrig-
eration show.

Huntair, CleanPak Join CES
CHASKA, Minn.—Nortek has acquired
the assets of Huntair® and CleanPak™
International, both providers of cus-
tom airﬂ ow management systems. The
companies will becomes subsidiaries of
the CES Group, a subsidiary of Nortek
that includes Governair®, Mammoth®,
Temtrol®, Venmar CES™, Ventrol®,
WEBCO™ and Eaton-Williams®. Hunt-
air and CleanPak both produce products
for critical industrial environments and
large commercial and institutional ap-
plications.
CEC Approves Energy-Storage AC
SACRAMENTO, Calif.—The California
Energy Commission (CEC) has approved
the Ice Bear energy-storage air condition-
er from Ice Energy as an optional com-
pliance measure for the latest revision
of California’s Title 24 building energy-
efﬁ ciency standards. Studies show the
Ice Bear module reduces peak weighted
electric energy up to 54% when compared
to conventional air conditioners. Ice Bear
was selected as an AHR Expo Innovation
Awards winner in 2004. This competition
is judged by ASHRAE members.
SEMCO Opens Plant in Texas
COLUMBIA, Mo.—SEMCO Duct &
Acoustical Products Inc. has opened
a manufacturing facility in Arlington,
Texas to support the Southwest and
Gulf Coast regions. This new 60,000
ft2 (5600 m2) facility will produce
manifolded and custom duct, and also
serve as a distribution point.

ATLANTA—Siemens Energy and Au-
tomation has acquired Controlotron, a
company that makes liquid and gas ﬂ ow
Japan Tightening Standard
TOKYO—Japanese manufacturers
will have to reduce power consump-
tion by home-use air-conditioners by
20% by ﬁ scal year 2010, according to
a report in the Japanese Air-Condition-
ing and Refrigeration News (JARN).
Similar regulations will be imposed
on refrigerators and ﬂ uorescent lamps
by spring 2007. The regulations are
needed to meet emission targets set
under the Kyoto Protocol. The increase
in AC efﬁ ciency follows a 40% in-
crease in efﬁ ciency that was required
in 1998, effective 2004. JARN said
the new requirements might force
some manufacturers to withdraw from
certain product sectors. It said others
might seek R&D joint ventures.

Advertisement formerly in this space.
By Kurt Roth, Ph.D., Associate Member ASHRAE;
John Dieckmann, P.E., Member ASHRAE; and
James Brodrick, Ph.D., Member ASHRAE
Natural and
Hybrid
Ventilation
This is the thirty-third column inspired by a DOE report
covering energy-saving HVAC&R technologies.
M
ost commercial buildings use mechanical systems to
ventilate and cool commercial buildings. In contrast,
a building with natural ventilation (NV) relies on
natural forces, primarily wind and thermal buoyancy, to move
air to ventilate and cool buildings. The two basic approaches to
NV are cross and stack ventilation.1,2 In both cases, the build-
ing designs allow for sufﬁ cient volume of outdoor air (OA)
to ﬂ ow through building spaces. NV buildings typically have
more complex façade and window systems, such as operable
(including automated) windows, dedicated air intakes, and roof
vents to effectively control building airﬂ ows.
Cross-ventilated buildings rely upon outdoor wind forces
to generate a pressure gradient from one side of the building
to another that drives OA through open windows (or other air
intake devices) on the high-pressure side of the building and out
of openings on the low-pressure side. In contrast, NV buildings
based on the stack effect often feature multistory chimney-like
spaces such as atria, specially designed heat stacks, and double-
skin façades.3–6 Heat generated inside the building (e.g., by
lights, people, and ofﬁ ce equipment) and from the sun warms
a column of air, causing it to rise and exit near or at the top of
the buildings. As the warm air rises, it draws additional air into
the lower part of the building to replace the rising air.
Hybrid ventilation (HV) systems, also known as mixed-mode
systems, have the infrastructure for both NV and conventional
air-conditioning systems. When possible, they use NV to pro-
vide space cooling and OA ventilation, and only operate air
conditioners when the NV system cannot meet space cooling
requirements.
Although all buildings before the 20th century were naturally
ventilated and cooled, very few conditioned buildings in the U.S.
currently use NV or HV. Recently, NV and HV have become
Advertisement formerly in this space.
© 2006 American Society of Heating, Refrigerating and Air-Conditioning
© 2006 American Society of Heating Refrigerating and Air-Conditioning
Engineers, Inc. (www.ashrae.org). Published in ASHRAE Journal (Vol. 48,
June 2006). For personal use only. Additional distribution in either paper or
digital form is not permitted without ASHRAE’s permission.
H38
Rx for Heath-Care HVAC | A Supplement to ASHRAE Journal
June 2006
more common outside of the U.S., notably in the cooler climates
of northern Europe and the U.K.4,5,7 Driving this renaissance
are two advantages of NV systems, reductions in ventilation and
cooling energy consumption and potential for improved indoor
air quality. Relative to conventional systems, NV can reduce
ventilation energy consumption because it uses natural forces
to move air instead of blowers and fans. Large volumes of OA
ﬂ owing through NV buildings also can reduce cooling energy
consumption by supplanting the need for cooling during periods
when the OA temperature is below the indoor air temperature
and humidity levels are acceptable. In some cases, NV buildings
introduce signiﬁ cantly higher levels of OA than economizers.1,4
As such, they can achieve greater cooling energy savings than
economizers and, when used in conjunction with sufﬁ cient
building mass, can also enable more extensive use of night
pre-cooling of buildings. Furthermore, research suggests that
occupants of NV buildings adapt to and can tolerate a greater
range of indoor temperatures, well beyond ASHRAE-deﬁ ned
comfort zones.8 This enables higher indoor air temperature
setpoints and, thus, additional cooling energy savings.
Because OA quality usually exceeds that of indoor air, NV
potentially improves IAQ. Some studies suggest that this,
in turn, can also reduce the incidence of sick building syn-
drome,9 increase occupant satisfaction and, possibly, worker
productivity.1,10,11
Energy-Saving Potential
Studies suggest that NV-only systems can provide acceptable
comfort throughout the year in very limited portions of the U.S.,
primarily in areas near the Paciﬁ c coast that have moderate
temperatures and low humidity.4,12 Outside of these regions, NV
cannot, by itself, maintain acceptable indoor environmental con-
ditions for signiﬁ cant periods due to temperature and humidity
levels.4 Consequently, the energy-saving potential assessment
and market factors discussions focus on HV systems.
Few studies have evaluated energy-saving potential of HV in
the U.S. To date, all of these studies have limitations. Emmerich,
et al.,4 carried out simulations comparing HV performance and
conventional systems conditioning a ﬁ ve-story, 46,000 ft2 (4273
m2) ofﬁ ce building in ﬁ ve different U.S. climates during Feb-
ruary, April, and July. They found that HV could dramatically
reduce (>50%) fan energy consumption in all cities, except dur-
ing colder months in the colder climates. The study also found
major reductions (~50%+) in cooling loads in all but the hot
and humid climates. On the other hand, HV moderately (~10%)
increased heating loads in the colder climates.
Another simulation-based study compared the annual energy
performance of HV to a conventional system in 40 U.S. cities.
They found that HVAC energy consumption decreased in all but
the hottest and most humid climates, with an average savings
of approximately 10%.12
The U.S. energy-saving potential for HV is difﬁ cult to
quantify from available data. A preliminary estimate is that
HV could reduce U.S. commercial building HVAC energy
consumption by an average of approximately 10%. This
number masks large geographical variations in total and by
component (cooling, heating, and ventilation) savings, includ-
ing potential increases in some regions and components. Un-
like many energy-efﬁ ciency measures, NV and HV are whole
building approaches that cannot be readily retroﬁ tted into a
larger portion of buildings. This limits their applicability to
the existing building stock.
Market Factors
Although HV has become accepted in some regions of the
world, it has negligible market share for U.S. commercial
buildings.4 Key barriers to greater use of HV in the U.S. in-
clude unfamiliarity with this approach, climatic limitations,
design challenges, ﬁ re code concerns, and implementation
challenges.
If a team does know about HV and wants to build an HV
building, they face several additional challenges. Because suc-
cessful HV buildings require strong integration of the overall
building design with climate control systems, the systems are
more challenging to design and effectively implement than
conventional systems. Designs must consider many factors,
including prevailing local winds, temperature, and humidity
over the course of the year, air distribution throughout the
Key barriers to greater use of hybrid ventilation in the U.S.
include unfamiliarity with these approaches, climatic limitations,
design challenges, ﬁ re code concerns, and implementation
challenges.
building, and the building’s thermal mass.3,4 Unfortunately,
few solid design and analysis data are available, and there is
a general lack of adequate design tools and methodologies.
In particular, designers need to understand airﬂ ows inside the
building to ensure proper airﬂ ow distribution and enable effec-
tive airﬂ ow control. Although coupled building airﬂ ow-thermal
simulations can provide this information, they typically are
costly and complex to perform.3
Furthermore, the U.S. building ventilation code, ANSI/
ASHRAE Standard 62.1, Ventilation for Acceptable Indoor
Air Quality, compromises the energy-saving potential of HV.
Speciﬁ cally, it requires a building to meet ventilation require-
ments as either an NV or mechanically ventilated building,
but does not allow the building to satisfy the standard for its
operational mode at a given point of time.
For example, if the designers select the NV design method for an
HV building, the building must always have the OA intakes open
during occupancy, even if the mechanical system operates and
provides sufﬁ cient airﬂ ow. During hot and humid or cold periods
where the HV building would logically use mechanical ventilation,
this increases space conditioning loads. Conversely, an HV system
designed to meet the mechanical ventilation design criteria must:
• Provide the minimum OA ventilation levels during oc-
cupancy via mechanical ventilation; or
• Use natural ventilation but verify, per the IAQ Procedure
of Standard 62, that the concentrations of all relevant
contaminants of concern lie within acceptable levels.7
Given the general confusion and legal concerns about prop-
erly implementing the IAQ Procedure, most designers likely
would have the mechanical system provide the prescribed OA
levels during occupancy. This reduces or eliminates the ventila-
tion energy savings.
NV and HV buildings also encounter challenges related to
ﬁ re codes because they are designed to enable large airﬂ ow
volumes between spaces. Although desirable from a ventilation
perspective, this raises smoke and ﬁ re propagation concerns,
which complicate design and implementation of the HV or NV
system.3,5,13 The associated project risk likely would deter many
teams from pursuing HV and NV. Finally, an effective natural
or hybrid ventilation system requires integration of the building
and HVAC system designs. In turn, this depends upon effective
collaboration between architects, building designers, and HVAC
designers and installers. Such a collaborative design approach
is uncommon for U.S. commercial buildings.2,3,13
Assuming a good design, effective implementation of NV
and HV is another challenge due to the uniqueness and greater
complexity of these systems. NV and HV systems actually
can increase energy consumption if poorly designed or imple-
mented.1 To provide the intended OA to all spaces, NV and
HV buildings need to have low inﬁ ltration levels.3,4 They also
require well-implemented control systems to dynamically co-
ordinate OA intakes (automated windows, dampers, etc.) with
the mechanical systems as outdoor and building conditions
change.3,5 Building owners and operators also have concerns
about the feasibility of maintaining the more sophisticated
controls, as well as the general maintenance required for the
airﬂ ow dampers and powered windows.4,5 Other potential issues
requiring attention include outdoor noise propagation through
air intakes,1,13 insufﬁ cient treatment of low-quality OA, and
occupant discomfort caused by indoor airﬂ ows.4

northwest buildings.” ASHRAE Journal 48(1):47 – 52.
